topics.title: Principles of Renewable Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of Renewable Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Renewable Energy** is defined as energy derived from natural sources or processes that are replenished at a rate faster than or equal to the rate at which they are consumed. According to the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** syllabus for the course *OEEET613 – Renewable Energy Systems*, renewable energy systems are those energy conversion technologies that harness the continuously available energy fluxes originating from solar, geothermal, gravitational, and biochemical processes occurring on Earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The fundamental distinction between a *renewable* and a *non-renewable* energy resource lies in the **replenishment timescale**. A resource is classified as renewable only if its **regeneration time is comparable to (or less than) the rate of human consumption**, not merely if it exists in nature.

### Classification of Energy Resources

Energy resources are broadly bifurcated into two categories based on their replenishment characteristics:

| Category | Definition | Typical Examples | Replenishment Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Non-Renewable (Conventional)** | Finite stock resources that deplete with use and cannot be regenerated within a human timescale. | Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium) | Millions of years |
| **Renewable (Non-Conventional)** | Flow resources that are continuously replenished by natural processes. | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal, Wave, OTEC | Seconds to days |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **water tank (representing Earth's total energy resources)** connected to a **small inlet pipe (the natural replenishment rate)** and a **large outlet tap (human consumption rate)**.

- If you open the outlet tap wider than the inlet pipe can fill, the tank eventually empties. This is the situation with **non-renewable fossil fuels**.
- If you keep the outlet flow *at most equal* to the inlet flow, the tank level stays constant indefinitely. This is the essence of a **sustainable renewable energy system**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Explanation:** Renewable energy is like earning *interest* from a savings account — as long as you don't withdraw more than the interest earned, your principal (the Earth's natural energy flux) remains intact for future generations.

## 1.3 Primary Physical Drivers of Renewable Energy

The vast majority of renewable energy on Earth originates from a single source — the **Sun**. The KTU curriculum groups renewable energy sources according to their fundamental physical driver:

1. **Direct Solar Radiation** → Solar Photovoltaic, Solar Thermal, Solar Architecture.
2. **Indirect Solar (Atmospheric / Oceanic Cycles)** → Wind Energy, Hydropower, Wave Energy, Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC), Biomass.
3. **Gravitational (Lunar–Solar)** → Tidal Energy.
4. **Terrestrial (Internal Earth Heat)** → Geothermal Energy.
5. **Biochemical** → Biomass, Biogas, Biofuels.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exam:** If asked to list the *ultimate* source of most renewables, the answer is the **Sun** — except for **Tidal** (Sun + Moon) and **Geothermal** (radioactive decay of Earth's core).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following constants and units are essential for solving KTU numerical problems in this module:

- **Solar Constant (Gsc)** = **1367 W/m²** (or 1361 W/m² as per latest NASA measurements).
- **Standard Solar Irradiance (Air Mass 1.5)** = **1000 W/m²** (used for PV module rating).
- **1 kWh** = **3.6 × 10⁶ J (Joules)** = **860 kcal (kilocalories)**.
- **1 Toe (Tonne of Oil Equivalent)** = **41.868 GJ** = **11.63 MWh** (used for national energy statistics).
- **Earth's rotational axis tilt** = **23.45°** (responsible for seasonal variation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Energy Replenishment Principle

The **scientific foundation** of renewable energy rests on the **Law of Conservation of Energy** and the concept of **Energy Flux Density**. For any renewable resource to be classified as sustainable, the following inequality must hold for all time $t$:

$$
R_{\text{regen}}(t) \;\geq\; R_{\text{consumption}}(t)
$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{regen}}(t)$ = Natural rate of energy replenishment (W or J/s).
- $R_{\text{consumption}}(t)$ = Rate at which humans extract and convert that energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Even "renewable" sources can be depleted unsustainably. For example, groundwater (a form of stored solar energy via the hydrological cycle) can be pumped out faster than rainfall recharges aquifers, leading to depletion.

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Renewable Energy Sources

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes understanding the **interconnection hierarchy** between renewables:

$$
\text{Solar Radiation} \;\longrightarrow\; \begin{cases} \text{Direct: PV, Thermal, CSP} \\ \text{Indirect: Wind, Hydro, Wave, OTEC, Biomass} \end{cases}
$$

$$
\text{Gravitational (Sun + Moon)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Tidal Energy}
$$

$$
\text{Geothermal (Earth's Core)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Geothermal Energy}
$$

## 2.3 Solar Energy Fundamentals — Foundation for Module 1

Since Module 1 of OEEET613 establishes solar energy principles as a foundation, the following analytical framework is critical.

### 2.3.1 The Solar Constant ($G_{sc}$)

The **Solar Constant** is the rate at which solar electromagnetic radiation is received *outside the Earth's atmosphere*, on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays, at the Earth's mean distance from the Sun (1 Astronomical Unit).

$$
G_{sc} = 1367 \; \text{W/m}^2
$$

### 2.3.2 Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

The solar radiation incident on a horizontal surface at the top of the atmosphere varies with the day of the year due to the changing Earth–Sun distance.

$$
G_{on} = G_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360 n}{365}\right)
$$

Where:
- $G_{on}$ = Extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface (W/m²).
- $G_{sc}$ = Solar constant (1367 W/m²).
- $n$ = Day of the year (1 = Jan 1, 365 = Dec 31).

### 2.3.3 Declination Angle ($\delta$)

The **declination angle** is the angle between the Earth–Sun line and the equatorial plane. It varies from **+23.45°** (Summer Solstice) to **−23.45°** (Winter Solstice).

$$
\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]
$$

### 2.3.4 Hour Angle ($\omega$)

The hour angle converts local solar time into the angular displacement of the Sun. It is **0° at solar noon**, **−15° per hour before noon**, and **+15° per hour after noon**.

$$
\omega = 15^\circ \times (\text{Solar Time in hours} - 12)
$$

### 2.3.5 Angle of Incidence ($\theta_z$)

The **zenith angle** $\theta_z$ is the angle between the Sun's rays and the local vertical (normal to a horizontal surface).

$$
\cos \theta_z = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega
$$

Where $\phi$ = Latitude of the location (positive in Northern Hemisphere).

### 2.3.6 Sunset Hour Angle ($\omega_s$)

The hour angle at which the Sun sets (zenith angle = 90°).

$$
\cos \omega_s = -\tan \phi \tan \delta
$$

### 2.3.7 Day Length ($N$)

The total number of daylight hours.

$$
N = \frac{2}{15} \omega_s = \frac{2}{15} \cos^{-1}(-\tan \phi \tan \delta)
$$

### 2.3.8 Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation ($H_o$)

The total daily (24-hour) solar radiation received on a horizontal surface at the top of the atmosphere.

$$
H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \; G_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360 n}{365}\right) \left[\cos \phi \cos \delta \sin \omega_s + \frac{2\pi \omega_s}{360} \sin \phi \sin \delta\right]
$$

The unit of $H_o$ is **J/m²/day** (or converted to **kWh/m²/day** by dividing by $3.6 \times 10^6$).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table for last-minute KTU exam revision.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Unit | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar Constant | $G_{sc} = 1367$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Reference for extraterrestrial flux |
| 2 | Earth–Sun Distance Correction | $G_{on} = G_{sc}\!\left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360n}{365}\right)$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | Daily ET radiation on horizontal surface |
| 3 | Declination Angle | $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$ | degrees | Seasonal sun position |
| 4 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15^\circ(T_{solar} - 12)$ | degrees | Time-to-angle conversion |
| 5 | Zenith Angle | $\cos \theta_z = \sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega$ | dimensionless | Solar geometry |
| 6 | Sunset Hour Angle | $\cos \omega_s = -\tan \phi \tan \delta$ | degrees | Day length calculation |
| 7 | Day Length | $N = \frac{2}{15}\omega_s$ | hours | Daylight duration |
| 8 | Daily ET Radiation | $H_o = \frac{86400}{\pi} G_{on}\!\left[\cos \phi \cos \delta \sin \omega_s + \frac{\pi \omega_s}{180}\sin \phi \sin \delta\right]$ | $\text{J/m}^2/\text{day}$ | Daily insolation |
| 9 | Energy Conversion | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$ | — | Unit conversion |
| 10 | Replenishment Criterion | $R_{\text{regen}} \geq R_{\text{consumption}}$ | — | Sustainability check |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall:** The factor inside the cosine of the **declination angle** formula is $\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)$, **NOT** $\frac{360}{365}(n - 81)$. The "284" constant arises from the spring equinox reference and is a common source of marks lost in Part B numericals.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles established in this module form the analytical basis for:

- **Sizing of Solar PV Plants** — Engineers use $H_o$ and ground-based measurements to estimate monthly average daily insolation ($\bar{H}$) for any latitude.
- **Architectural Design** — Daylight availability (Day Length $N$) governs window placement, overhang design, and HVAC load reduction.
- **Agriculture** — Greenhouse orientation and crop selection depend on declination and zenith angle.
- **Power System Planning** — Load curves are matched with the **Capacity Utilisation Factor (CUF)** derived from daily insolation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface ($G_{on}$)

### Objective
To derive the expression for instantaneous solar radiation incident on a horizontal surface located outside the Earth's atmosphere, accounting for the elliptical nature of Earth's orbit.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The basic inverse-square law gives the radiation intensity at a distance $r$ from the Sun.

$$
G_r = \frac{L_\odot}{4\pi r^2}
$$

Where $L_\odot$ is the solar luminosity and $r$ is the instantaneous Earth–Sun distance.

**Step 2:** The solar constant is defined as the radiation intensity at the mean Earth–Sun distance $r_0 = 1 \text{ AU}$:

$$
G_{sc} = \frac{L_\odot}{4\pi r_0^2} = 1367 \; \text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 3:** The ratio of actual intensity to the solar constant is:

$$
\frac{G_r}{G_{sc}} = \frac{r_0^2}{r^2}
$$

**Step 4:** The Earth–Sun distance $r$ varies as Earth moves in its elliptical orbit. Using the eccentricity $e = 0.0167$ and the day angle $\theta = \frac{2\pi n}{365}$:

$$
\frac{r_0^2}{r^2} \approx 1 + 2 e \cos\theta
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $e = 0.0167$ and $\theta = \frac{2\pi n}{365}$:

$$
\frac{r_0^2}{r^2} \approx 1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}
$$

**Step 6:** Therefore, the extraterrestrial radiation on a surface **normal** to the Sun's rays is:

$$
G_{on,n} = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)
$$

**Step 7:** To obtain the radiation on a **horizontal** surface, multiply by the cosine of the zenith angle $\theta_z$:

$$
G_{on} = G_{on,n} \cos\theta_z = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\cos\theta_z
$$

**Final Result (Horizontal Surface, Top of Atmosphere):**

$$
\boxed{\,G_{on} = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\cos\theta_z\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Zenith Angle Equation ($\cos\theta_z$)

### Objective
To derive the fundamental solar geometry equation relating latitude, declination, hour angle, and zenith angle.

### Setup
Consider the celestial sphere with three key points: the **observer (O)**, the **Sun (S)**, and the **zenith point (Z)**. The zenith angle $\theta_z$ is the angular distance from Z to S.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Using the **spherical law of cosines** applied to the triangle ZOS on the celestial sphere:

$$
\cos\theta_z = \cos(90^\circ - \phi)\cos(90^\circ - \delta) + \sin(90^\circ - \phi)\sin(90^\circ - \delta)\cos\omega
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\cos(90^\circ - x) = \sin x$ and $\sin(90^\circ - x) = \cos x$:

$$
\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega\,}
$$

This is one of the **most heavily tested equations** in KTU Module 1 numericals.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation ($H_o$)

### Objective
To integrate the instantaneous extraterrestrial horizontal radiation over the day-length period to obtain the daily total.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface is:

$$
G_{on}(t) = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\cos\theta_z(t)
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the zenith angle expression:

$$
G_{on}(t) = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\left[\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega(t)\right]
$$

**Step 3:** Convert hour angle to time. The hour angle changes at 15° per hour:

$$
\omega = \frac{\pi}{12}(t_{solar} - 12) \quad \Rightarrow \quad dt = \frac{12}{\pi}d\omega
$$

**Step 4:** The daily radiation is the integral from sunrise ($\omega = -\omega_s$) to sunset ($\omega = +\omega_s$):

$$
H_o = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} G_{on}(t) \cdot \frac{12}{\pi} d\omega
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and separate the two terms:

$$
H_o = \frac{12}{\pi} G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right) \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \left[\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega\right] d\omega
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the integral. The first term integrates as $\sin\phi \sin\delta \cdot (2\omega_s)$. The second term integrates to zero by symmetry of $\cos\omega$:

$$
\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \sin\phi \sin\delta \, d\omega = 2\omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta
$$

$$
\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega \, d\omega = \cos\phi \cos\delta \left[\sin\omega\right]_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} = 2\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s
$$

**Step 7:** Substituting back, with $\frac{12}{\pi} \times 24 = \frac{86400}{\pi}$ when $\omega_s$ is in degrees (multiplied by $\pi/180$):

$$
\boxed{\,H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\left[\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi \omega_s}{360}\sin\phi \sin\delta\right]\,}
$$

Unit: **J/m²/day** (when $G_{sc}$ is in W/m²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Note:** When using $H_o$ in **kWh/m²/day**, the leading coefficient becomes $\frac{24}{\pi}$ instead of $\frac{86400}{\pi}$. The choice of unit is the most common mistake students make in Part B numericals.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Question)

**Problem:** Calculate the daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface at latitude $\phi = 13^\circ\text{N}$ (Trivandrum, Kerala) on **March 21** (Equinox, $n = 80$). Use $G_{sc} = 1367 \; \text{W/m}^2$.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute the Eccentricity Correction Factor for $n = 80$:

$$
E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 \times 80}{365} = 1 + 0.033 \cos(78.90^\circ) = 1 + 0.033 \times 0.1936 = 1.0064
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Declination Angle for $n = 80$:

$$
\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 80)\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(364)\right]
$$

$$
\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin(358.85^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \times (-0.0179) \approx -0.42^\circ
$$

This is consistent with the spring equinox (Sun directly over the equator).

**Step 3:** Compute the Sunset Hour Angle:

$$
\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta = -\tan(13^\circ)\tan(-0.42^\circ) = -(0.2309)(-0.00733) = 0.00169
$$

$$
\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(0.00169) \approx 89.90^\circ \approx 90^\circ
$$

**Step 4:** Compute Day Length:

$$
N = \frac{2}{15}\omega_s = \frac{2}{15}\times 90 = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

This matches the equinox expectation of 12 hours of daylight everywhere on Earth.

**Step 5:** Compute $H_o$ in J/m²/day:

$$
H_o = \frac{86400}{\pi}(1367)(1.0064)\left[\cos(13^\circ)\cos(-0.42^\circ)\sin(90^\circ) + \frac{\pi(90)}{180}\sin(13^\circ)\sin(-0.42^\circ)\right]
$$

$$
H_o = \frac{86400}{\pi}(1375.75)\left[(0.9744)(0.9999)(1.000) + (1.5708)(0.2250)(-0.00733)\right]
$$

$$
H_o = (27510.6)(1375.75)\left[0.9743 - 0.00259\right]
$$

$$
H_o \approx 37.85 \times 10^6 \; \text{J/m}^2/\text{day} \approx 37.85 \; \text{MJ/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to kWh/m²/day:

$$
H_o = \frac{37.85 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} \approx 10.51 \; \text{kWh/m}^2/\text{day}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** This result is the **theoretical maximum** daily insolation. Ground-level measurements in Trivandrum typically give $H \approx 5\text{–}6 \; \text{kWh/m}^2/\text{day}$ due to atmospheric attenuation, cloud cover, and the fact that the measurement is on a horizontal surface (not tracking the Sun).

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Renewable vs Non-Renewable

| Parameter | Non-Renewable (Fossil) | Renewable |
|---|---|---|
| **Replenishment Time** | $10^6$ – $10^8$ years | Seconds to years |
| **Carbon Footprint (gCO₂eq/kWh)** | 400 – 1000 | 5 – 50 (lifecycle) |
| **Initial Capital Cost** | Moderate to High | High (declining) |
| **Operating Cost** | High (fuel) | Low (no fuel) |
| **Energy Density (W/m²)** | High (concentrated) | Low (diffuse) |
| **Grid Stability** | Dispatchable | Variable (needs storage) |
| **Environmental Impact** | High (emissions, mining) | Low (visual, land use) |
| **Sustainability Index** | Unsustainable | Sustainable (if managed) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Energy Resources — Hierarchical Tree

The following Mermaid diagram presents the complete KTU-aligned classification hierarchy for energy resources, emphasizing how all renewables trace back to one of three ultimate physical drivers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[ENERGY RESOURCES]
    nodeB[NON RENEWABLE FOSSIL AND NUCLEAR]
    nodeC[RENEWABLE NATURAL FLUXES]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD[COAL]
    nodeB --> nodeE[PETROLEUM OIL AND GAS]
    nodeB --> nodeF[NUCLEAR URANIUM]
    nodeC --> nodeG[DIRECT SOLAR DRIVER]
    nodeC --> nodeH[INDIRECT SOLAR DRIVER]
    nodeC --> nodeI[GRAVITATIONAL DRIVER]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[GEOTHERMAL DRIVER]
    nodeG --> nodeK[SOLAR PHOTOVOLTAIC PV]
    nodeG --> nodeL[SOLAR THERMAL]
    nodeG --> nodeM[CONCENTRATED SOLAR POWER CSP]
    nodeH --> nodeN[WIND ENERGY]
    nodeH --> nodeO[HYDROPOWER]
    nodeH --> nodeP[WAVE AND TIDAL]
    nodeH --> nodeQ[BIOMASS AND BIOGAS]
    nodeH --> nodeR[OCEAN THERMAL OTEC]
    nodeI --> nodeS[TIDAL BARRAGE]
    nodeI --> nodeT[TIDAL STREAM]
    nodeJ --> nodeU[GEOTHERMAL POWER]
    nodeJ --> nodeV[GROUND SOURCE HEAT PUMP]
```

## 4.2 Solar Geometry Schematic — Functional Architecture Flow

Because drawing a celestial sphere with three-dimensional angles is not well-suited for Mermaid syntax, the following block-level functional architecture represents the **computational pipeline** for solar position calculation, which is the core analytical workflow students must master.

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[INPUT LOCATION DATA LATITUDE PHI]
    node2[INPUT DAY OF YEAR N]
    node3[INPUT SOLAR TIME T]
    node4[COMPUTE DECLINATION DELTA]
    node5[COMPUTE ECCENTRICITY FACTOR E0]
    node6[COMPUTE HOUR ANGLE OMEGA]
    node7[COMPUTE ZENITH ANGLE THETAZ]
    node8[COMPUTE SUNSET HOUR ANGLE OMEGAS]
    node9[COMPUTE DAY LENGTH N HOURS]
    node10[COMPUTE INSTANTANEOUS GON]
    node11[INTEGRATE OVER DAY]
    node12[OUTPUT DAILY EXTRATERRESTRIAL HO]
    node1 --> node7
    node1 --> node8
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node2 --> node10
    node3 --> node6
    node4 --> node7
    node4 --> node8
    node5 --> node10
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node10
    node8 --> node9
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
```

```mermaid
graph LR

    nodeB[COMPARISON TEST]

    nodeE[UNSUSTAINABLE IF R REGEN LT R CONSUMPTION]

    nodeE --> nodeF

```

## 4.4 Energy Conversion Path — Solar to Electrical

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[SUN NUCLEAR FUSION CORE]
    nodeB[PHOTONS EMITTED ISOTROPICALLY]
    nodeC[EXTRATERRESTRIAL RADIATION 1367 W PER M2]

    nodeE[TERRESTRIAL GLOBAL RADIATION 1000 W PER M2]

```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`
**Define renewable energy. List any four renewable energy resources with their ultimate source.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Renewable energy is defined as the energy obtained from natural sources that are replenished at a rate equal to or greater than the rate of their consumption. These energy flows are essentially inexhaustible on a human timescale.

**Four Renewable Energy Resources and Their Ultimate Sources:**

| Resource | Ultimate Source |
|---|---|
| Solar Energy | Sun (nuclear fusion) |
| Wind Energy | Sun (indirect, via atmospheric pressure gradients) |
| Hydropower | Sun (indirect, via hydrological cycle) |
| Geothermal Energy | Earth's internal heat (radioactive decay) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Word Count Target:** 80 – 100 words for a 3-mark answer. Including a small table earns full marks for presentation as per KTU 2024 valuation pattern.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`
**Explain the difference between conventional and non-conventional energy sources with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Conventional (Non-Renewable) | Non-Conventional (Renewable) |
|---|---|---|
| **Replenishment** | Finite, millions of years to form | Continuous, within human timescale |
| **Environmental Impact** | High CO₂ emissions, pollutants | Low emissions, eco-friendly |
| **Examples** | (i) Coal, (ii) Natural Gas | (i) Solar PV, (ii) Wind Energy |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Module Internal Choice is mandatory — you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the expression for the daily extraterrestrial radiation $H_o$ on a horizontal surface at the top of the atmosphere. State all assumptions clearly.**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Earth orbits the Sun in a circular path (small eccentricity correction is added separately).
2. The solar constant is uniform across the day.
3. The Sun's rays are parallel at the Earth's distance from the Sun.
4. There is no atmospheric attenuation (radiation is at the "top of atmosphere").
5. The declination angle is constant during a single day.

**Derivation Steps:**

**Step 1:** Write the instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface:

$$
G_{on}(t) = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\cos\theta_z
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the cosine zenith angle expression:

$$
\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega
$$

**Step 3:** Convert time to hour angle: $dt = \frac{12}{\pi}d\omega$, with the sun rising at $\omega = -\omega_s$ and setting at $\omega = +\omega_s$.

**Step 4:** Integrate over the day-length period:

$$
H_o = \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} G_{on}(t) \cdot \frac{12}{\pi} \, d\omega
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and separate the two terms inside the integral:

$$
H_o = \frac{12}{\pi} G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right) \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \left[\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega\right] d\omega
$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$
\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \sin\phi \sin\delta \, d\omega = 2\omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta
$$

$$
\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega \, d\omega = 2\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s
$$

**Step 7:** Multiply by the constant factor and convert to seconds (multiply by 3600 for $\omega$ in degrees to seconds):

$$
H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\left[\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi \omega_s}{360}\sin\phi \sin\delta\right]
$$

**Step 8:** Mention the unit: **J/m²/day** (or kWh/m²/day if divided by $3.6 \times 10^6$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Forgetting the unit conversion factor $\frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi}$. A common student mistake is to write $\frac{24}{\pi}$, which is only correct when the result is desired in kWh/m²/day and $G_{sc}$ is in kW/m².

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**For a location at latitude $\phi = 10^\circ\text{N}$, calculate the declination angle and sunset hour angle on the summer solstice (n = 172). Take $G_{sc} = 1367 \; \text{W/m}^2$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Declination Angle**

$$
\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 172)\right] = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(456)\right]
$$

$$
\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin(449.59^\circ) = 23.45^\circ \sin(89.59^\circ) \approx 23.45^\circ \times 0.99999 \approx 23.45^\circ
$$

*(As expected for the summer solstice, $\delta$ equals the maximum value of $23.45^\circ$.)*

**Step 2: Calculate Sunset Hour Angle**

$$
\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta = -\tan(10^\circ)\tan(23.45^\circ)
$$

$$
\cos\omega_s = -(0.1763)(0.4335) = -0.07644
$$

$$
\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.07644) = 94.38^\circ
$$

**Step 3: Day Length**

$$
N = \frac{2}{15}\omega_s = \frac{2}{15}(94.38) = 12.58 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: Extraterrestrial Horizontal Radiation**

For an equinox-like setup at low latitude, $H_o$ can be approximated. However, since the question only asks for $\delta$ and $\omega_s$, the calculation stops at Step 3.

**Final Answers:**
- $\delta \approx 23.45^\circ$
- $\omega_s \approx 94.38^\circ$
- $N \approx 12.58$ hours

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A location at $10^\circ$N experiences only **0.58 hours (35 minutes)** of additional daylight compared to a 12-hour day at the equinox — typical of equatorial regions where seasonal day-length variation is small.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the term "Solar Constant". What is its value and on what factors does the extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface depend?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Solar Constant:** The solar constant is the rate at which the Sun's energy is received per unit area by a surface placed *perpendicular* to the Sun's rays *at the top of the Earth's atmosphere*, at the **mean Earth–Sun distance** of 1 Astronomical Unit (AU).

**Value:** $G_{sc} = 1367 \; \text{W/m}^2$ (also commonly written as $1.367 \; \text{kW/m}^2$ or $1361 \; \text{W/m}^2$ as per recent NASA SORCE satellite data).

**Factors on which Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface Depends:**

1. **Day of the year (n):** Due to the elliptical orbit of the Earth, the Earth–Sun distance varies, modifying the incident flux by approximately ±3.3%.

2. **Latitude of the location ($\phi$):** Higher latitudes receive radiation at a steeper angle, spreading the same energy over a larger area ($\cos\theta_z$ effect).

3. **Solar declination angle ($\delta$):** A function of the day of the year, governing the seasonal variation in sun position.

4. **Hour angle ($\omega$):** The sun's position changes throughout the day, varying $\theta_z$ continuously from sunrise to sunset.

5. **Sunset hour angle ($\omega_s$):** Governs the day length, which determines the total daily energy received.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
G_{on} = G_{sc}\left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right)\cos\theta_z
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface at the top of the atmosphere at a location is $G_{on} = 1320 \; \text{W/m}^2$ on day $n = 100$. Estimate the solar zenith angle for that instant. Take $G_{sc} = 1367 \; \text{W/m}^2$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the Eccentricity Correction Factor for $n = 100$:

$$
E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 \times 100}{365} = 1 + 0.033 \cos(98.63^\circ)
$$

$$
E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \times (-0.1495) = 1 - 0.00493 = 0.9951
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange the extraterrestrial radiation equation to solve for $\cos\theta_z$:

$$
G_{on} = G_{sc} \cdot E_0 \cdot \cos\theta_z
$$

$$
\cos\theta_z = \frac{G_{on}}{G_{sc} \cdot E_0} = \frac{1320}{1367 \times 0.9951} = \frac{1320}{1360.30} = 0.9704
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Zenith Angle:

$$
\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.9704) \approx 13.99^\circ \approx 14^\circ
$$

**Step 4: Engineering Interpretation:**

A zenith angle of $14^\circ$ indicates the Sun is high in the sky (close to the zenith), which is typical for a location near the equator during midday, or for a location closer to the sub-solar point on that day.

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Check:** At the equinox at the equator, the noon zenith angle is approximately $0^\circ$. The value of $14^\circ$ here is reasonable for early-April sun position in a low-latitude location.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in KTU Module 1 Numericals (Based on 2023–2024 Evaluation Reports):**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing degrees and radians in the declination and sunset hour angle calculations. Always keep angles in **degrees** for the formulas shown, and convert using $\pi/180$ only at the final integration step.
>
> 2. **Day-of-Year Off-by-One:** January 1 is $n = 1$, **NOT** $n = 0$. Using $n = 0$ in the eccentricity correction is a 1-mark deduction trap.
>
> 3. **Latitude Sign Convention:** Northern latitudes are positive ($\phi > 0$), Southern latitudes are negative ($\phi < 0$). Students often forget the sign convention for declination.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert $\omega_s$ to Hours:** Dividing $\omega_s$ by 15 to get the day length is a step students frequently skip, losing 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Mixing up the two $\cos\theta_z$ formulas:** For a **tilted** surface, the formula includes additional terms. Make sure you are using the **horizontal surface** version unless the question explicitly states a tilted collector.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Principles of Renewable Energy**

- **Renewable Energy** is energy from sources replenished at a rate ≥ consumption rate.
- **Three ultimate drivers:** Sun, Gravitation (Sun + Moon), Earth's internal heat (Geothermal).
- **Solar Constant:** $G_{sc} = 1367 \; \text{W/m}^2$ — defined at the top of the atmosphere, perpendicular to the Sun's rays, at 1 AU.
- **Eccentricity Correction:** $E_0 = 1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360n}{365}$ — accounts for Earth's elliptical orbit.
- **Declination Angle:** $\delta = 23.45^\circ \sin\!\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$ — varies seasonally.
- **Hour Angle:** $\omega = 15^\circ(T_{solar} - 12)$ — 0° at solar noon, ±15° per hour.
- **Zenith Angle:** $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ — most tested formula.
- **Sunset Hour Angle:** $\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta$ — required for day length.
- **Day Length:** $N = \frac{2}{15}\omega_s$ in hours.
- **Daily ET Radiation ($H_o$):** Combines all of the above in a single integral; expressed in J/m²/day or kWh/m²/day.
- **Sustainability Test:** $R_{\text{regen}} \geq R_{\text{consumption}}$ for all $t$.
- **Energy Units:** 1 kWh = $3.6 \times 10^6$ J = 860 kcal.
- **Equinox Special Case:** $n = 80$ (March 21) and $n = 264$ (Sept 23) give $\delta = 0^\circ$ and $N = 12$ hours everywhere on Earth.
- **Solstice Special Cases:** $n = 172$ (Summer Solstice, $\delta = +23.45^\circ$) and $n = 355$ (Winter Solstice, $\delta = -23.45^\circ$).
- **Atmospheric Attenuation:** Real ground-level $H \ll H_o$ due to scattering, absorption, clouds, and pollution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For KTU 2024 ESE Module 1, allocate **at least 40% of preparation time** to numerical problems involving solar geometry and extraterrestrial radiation. These are guaranteed to appear as 7-mark sub-questions in Part B.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Wind Energy: Properties of Wind, Availability of Wind Energy in India, Wind Velocity and Power from Wind (Numerical Problems)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Energy and Sustainable Development — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sustainable Development (KTU Board Definition):**
> *"Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."*
> — **Brundtland Report (WCED, 1987), Our Common Future**

In the context of **KTU OEEET613**, **Energy and Sustainable Development** refers to the integration of energy generation, distribution, and consumption practices that ensure long-term ecological balance, economic viability, and social equity — without depleting natural resources or degrading the environment for posterity.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🌍

Imagine a household drawing a fixed monthly salary from a bank account. If the family spends **less than** what they earn and saves the rest, the account grows and survives for generations. But if they spend **more than** they earn (and dip into capital), the account will eventually be exhausted.

Earth's natural resources (coal, oil, gas) are the **"capital"** of the planet. **Fossil fuels** are like withdrawing from this capital; **renewable energy** (solar, wind, hydro) is like living off the **"monthly interest"** (continuous incoming energy from the Sun). Sustainable development is simply **living off the interest**, never the capital.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Sustainable Development (Triple Bottom Line):**
> 1. **Environmental Sustainability** — Conservation of resources & ecosystems.
> 2. **Economic Sustainability** — Affordable, reliable energy for industrial growth.
> 3. **Social Sustainability** — Universal energy access, equity, and health.

## 1.2 Fundamentals of Energy

**Energy** is the capacity to do work or produce change, measured in **Joules (J)** or **kWh**.

### Standard Energy Units (KTU 2024 Mandatory Recall)

| Unit | Symbol | Equivalent in Joules |
|---|---|---|
| Joule | J | 1 J |
| Kilojoule | kJ | 10³ J |
| Calorie | cal | 4.184 J |
| Kilowatt-hour | kWh | 3.6 × 10⁶ J |
| Tera Joule | TJ | 10¹² J |
| Mega Tonne of Oil Equivalent | Mtoe | 4.1868 × 10¹⁶ J |
| Barrel of Oil Equivalent | boe | 5.8 × 10⁹ J |

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Energy** = Energy found in nature **before** any human conversion (coal, sunlight, wind).
> **Secondary Energy** = Energy after conversion from primary sources (electricity, gasoline).
> **Final Energy** = Energy delivered to the end-user (electricity at the socket).
> **Useful Energy** = Energy actually performing the desired service (light, motion, heat).

## 1.3 Social Implications of Energy

The **social implications** of energy are the cascading effects of energy generation, distribution, and use on human civilization. These are high-weightage concepts in KTU Part A questions.

| Implication | Description |
|---|---|
| **Energy Poverty** | Lack of access to modern, affordable, reliable energy. Affects **~770 million** people globally (IEA, 2022). |
| **Public Health** | Indoor air pollution from biomass burning causes ~**3.8 million** premature deaths/year (WHO). |
| **Energy Security** | Reliance on imported fossil fuels creates geopolitical vulnerability. |
| **Climate Change** | Fossil-fuel CO₂ emissions drive global warming (**~36.8 GtCO₂ in 2022**). |
| **Rural Electrification** | Decentralized renewables empower off-grid villages, boosting education & GDP. |
| **Gender Equity** | Women disproportionately affected by energy poverty (fuel collection burden). |
| **Industrial Growth** | Energy is the backbone of GDP — every 1% increase in energy access raises GDP by ~**0.67%** in developing nations. |

## 1.4 Worldwide Renewable Energy Availability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Renewable Energy (KTU Definition):** Energy collected from resources that are **naturally replenished** on a human time scale and do not run out.

### Global Renewable Resource Potential (Approximate Theoretical)

| Resource | Annual Global Potential | Practical Recoverable |
|---|---|---|
| **Solar** | 23,000 TW (or 1.575 × 10⁵ TWh/yr on land) | ~ 1,200 EJ/year |
| **Wind** | ~ 1,200 TW theoretical | ~ 200 EJ/year practical |
| **Hydro** | ~ 16 TW theoretical | ~ 7–8 TW practical |
| **Biomass** | ~ 100 TW theoretical | ~ 5–10 TW sustainable |
| **Geothermal** | ~ 12 TW (heat) | ~ 2 TW practical |
| **Tidal / Wave / OTEC** | ~ 2 TW combined | < 1 TW near-term |

> [!NOTE]
> The total world energy consumption in 2023 was approximately **~ 595 EJ** (≈ 165,000 TWh). Solar alone could theoretically supply this demand many times over — the bottleneck is **capture efficiency and storage**, not resource scarcity.

### Installed Global Renewable Capacity (End of 2023, IRENA)

| Source | Capacity (GW) | YoY Growth |
|---|---|---|
| Hydropower | 1,412 | +1.9% |
| Wind | 1,017 | +13.9% |
| Solar PV | 1,419 | +32.4% |
| Bioenergy | 150 | +3.2% |
| Geothermal | 15.4 | +3.0% |
| **Total RE** | **~ 4,048 GW** | **+14%** |

## 1.5 Renewable Energy Availability in India

India is geographically blessed with abundant renewable potential across all major sources.

### State-wise Resource Snapshot (MNRE Estimates)

| Resource | Estimated Potential in India |
|---|---|
| Solar (incident radiation) | **~ 748 GWp** (assuming 3% of wasteland) |
| Wind (at 100 m hub height) | **~ 302 GW** |
| Small Hydro (≤ 25 MW) | **~ 21 GW** |
| Biomass / Bagasse Cogeneration | **~ 25 GW** |
| Geothermal | **~ 10 GW** |
| Tidal | **~ 8 GW** (Gulf of Kutch, Gulf of Cambay) |
| Wave | **~ 40 GW** (along 7,500 km coastline) |
| OTEC | **~ 180 GW** (deep tropical waters) |

### Installed RE Capacity in India (as of early 2024)

| Source | Installed Capacity (GW) |
|---|---|
| Solar Power | ~ 81.5 |
| Wind Power | ~ 47.0 |
| Biomass / Bagasse | ~ 10.2 |
| Small Hydro | ~ 4.9 |
| Waste-to-Energy | ~ 0.6 |
| **Total RE (excluding large hydro)** | **~ 144 GW** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India's Targets (Panchamrit & Net-Zero 2070, COP26 Glasgow, Nov 2021):**
> 1. **500 GW** of non-fossil fuel electricity capacity by 2030.
> 2. **50%** of installed power from non-fossil sources by 2030.
> 3. Reduce carbon intensity by **45%** (vs 2005 levels) by 2030.
> 4. Achieve **net-zero emissions by 2070**.
> 5. **1 GtCO₂** cumulative carbon reduction by 2030.

## 1.6 Types of Renewable Energy — Overview

Renewables are classified based on the **source of origin**:

### A. Solar Energy
- **Direct Solar PV** — Photovoltaic cells convert sunlight → electricity.
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)** — Mirrors focus sunlight to heat a fluid → steam → turbine.

### B. Wind Energy
- Kinetic energy of moving air drives turbine blades → mechanical → electrical.

### C. Hydro Energy
- Potential/kinetic energy of falling/running water → turbine → electricity.
- **Large Hydro (> 25 MW)**, **Small Hydro (≤ 25 MW)**, **Pumped Storage**.

### D. Biomass / Bioenergy
- Organic matter (crop residue, wood, municipal waste, biogas) → combustion/fermentation → heat/power.

### E. Geothermal Energy
- Heat from Earth's interior (radioactive decay + residual primordial heat) → steam → turbine.

### F. Ocean Energy
- **Tidal Energy** — Gravitational pull of moon/sun on seawater.
- **Wave Energy** — Surface wave motion captured by buoys/oscillators.
- **OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion)** — Temperature gradient between warm surface & cold deep water (ΔT ≥ 20 °C).
- **Salinity Gradient Energy** — Pressure retarded osmosis / reverse electrodialysis.

### G. Hydrogen Energy (Green Hydrogen)
- Electrolysis of water powered by renewables → H₂ → fuel cell or combustion.

### H. Hybrid Systems
- PV-Wind, PV-Diesel, PV-Wind-Battery, etc.

## 1.7 Wind Energy — Core Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wind:** The bulk horizontal movement of air driven by differential heating of Earth's surface by the Sun. Wind energy is the **kinetic energy** of this moving air mass.

### Conceptual Analogy 🍃

Imagine a stream of water flowing past a paddle wheel. The faster the stream and the larger the paddle area, the more work the wheel does. **Wind is the "stream of air"**, and the **wind turbine blades are the "paddle wheel"**. The energy captured depends on (1) **air density** (how "thick" the air is), (2) the **swept area** (how big the blades are), and (3) the **cube of wind speed** — which is why even small increases in wind speed create enormous jumps in power.

## 1.8 Properties of Wind

| Property | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Wind Speed (V)** | Linear velocity of moving air | 3 – 25 m/s for power generation |
| **Wind Direction** | Compass bearing from which wind blows | N, NE, E, ... |
| **Air Density (ρ)** | Mass per unit volume of air | **1.225 kg/m³** (at 15 °C, 1 atm, sea level) |
| **Kinematic Viscosity (ν)** | Resistance to flow deformation | ~ 1.46 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s at 15 °C |
| **Turbulence Intensity (TI)** | Ratio of std. deviation to mean wind speed | 0.10 – 0.20 for utility-scale |
| **Wind Shear** | Change of wind speed with height | Modelled by power-law exponent α |
| **Wind Power Density (WPD)** | Power per unit area perpendicular to wind | 200 – 600 W/m² (good sites) |
| **Wind Class** | Categorical rating by WPD at hub height | Class 1 (poor) → Class 7 (superb) |
| **Capacity Factor** | Ratio of actual to rated energy output | 25 – 45% (onshore); 45 – 55% (offshore) |
| **Roughness Length (z₀)** | Surface terrain descriptor for ABL modelling | 0.0002 m (water) → 1.0 m (forest, cities) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU/IEA):**
> - Air Density ρ = **1.225 kg/m³**
> - Hub height: **10 m / 30 m / 50 m / 100 m**
> - Standard Atmosphere: T = **288.15 K (15 °C)**, P = **101.325 kPa**, g = 9.807 m/s².

### Variation of Air Density with Altitude and Temperature

$$
\rho = \frac{P}{R \cdot T} = \frac{1.225 \times 288.15}{T} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{h}{8435}\right)
$$

Where:
- $P$ = absolute pressure (Pa)
- $R$ = specific gas constant of dry air = **287.05 J/(kg·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $h$ = altitude (m)

### Wind Direction & Site Selection

KTU students must remember:
1. **Prevailing Wind Direction** — Predominant direction (e.g., SW Monsoon in Kerala).
2. **Wind Rose** — Graphical plot showing wind speed/direction frequencies.
3. **Wake Effect** — Reduction in wind speed downstream of a turbine (5 – 10% loss).
4. **Rough Terrain** — Increases turbulence → reduces power & accelerates fatigue.

## 1.9 Availability of Wind Energy in India

### Wind Power Potential (MNRE Re-assessment, 2017 @ 100 m hub height)

| Region / State | Estimated Potential (GW) |
|---|---|
| **Tamil Nadu** | ~ 71.5 (Muppandal wind farm cluster) |
| **Gujarat** | ~ 65.5 (Kutch, Samana) |
| **Karnataka** | ~ 39.3 (Chitradurga, Davangere) |
| **Maharashtra** | ~ 36.0 (Satara, Dhule) |
| **Rajasthan** | ~ 27.7 (Jaisalmer, Bikaner) |
| **Andhra Pradesh** | ~ 18.2 |
| **Kerala (Agali, Kanjikode)** | ~ 3.0 (potential) |
| **TOTAL India** | **~ 302 GW** |

### Top Wind Farms in India

| Wind Farm | State | Capacity (MW) |
|---|---|---|
| **Muppandal Wind Farm** | Tamil Nadu | 1,500 |
| **Jaisalmer Wind Park** | Rajasthan | 1,064 |
| **Kutch Wind Farm** | Gujarat | 800+ |
| **Brahmanvel & Dhule** | Maharashtra | 575 |
| **Tuppadahalli** | Karnataka | 200 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **First Wind Farm in India:** **Muppandal**, Kanyakumari, Tamil Nadu — commissioned in **1986**, capacity ~ 25 MW. (KTU Favourite Question!)
> **First Offshore Wind Project:** Proposed off Gujarat coast (~ 4 GW), tendered in 2022.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why is Sustainable Development Critical for Energy?

Modern civilization is built on three pillars of energy use: **electricity, transportation, and industrial process heat**. Historically, all three have been dominated by fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas). This creates four systemic problems:

1. **Climate Change** — CO₂ emissions (from coal, oil) and CH₄ (from gas/oil) trap long-wave radiation, raising global mean surface temperature. The Paris Agreement (2015) targets **1.5 °C** above pre-industrial.
2. **Finite Reserves** — Coal may last ~150 yrs, oil ~50 yrs, gas ~55 yrs at current consumption (BP Statistical Review).
3. **Geopolitical Instability** — Energy import dependency creates vulnerability (e.g., 1973 OPEC oil crisis).
4. **Local Pollution** — Particulate matter (PM2.5), SO₂, NOₓ cause ~7 million premature deaths/year (WHO, 2022).

Renewable energy breaks this triad by offering **infinite, indigenous, clean** power.

## 2.2 The Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) Framework — UN 2030 Agenda

There are **17 SDGs**; energy is explicitly captured in:

- **SDG 7:** Affordable and Clean Energy.
- **SDG 13:** Climate Action.

KTU frequently asks the linkage between energy and sustainability — remember the **3 Es**: **Energy + Equity + Environment**.

## 2.3 Classification of Energy Sources (Depth View)

### A. By Depletion Characteristic
- **Non-Renewable (Stock-limited):** Coal, Oil, Gas, Uranium.
- **Renewable (Flow-limited):** Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Ocean.

### B. By Carbon Emission Intensity
| Source | gCO₂eq / kWh |
|---|---|
| Coal | 820 – 1,200 |
| Oil | 650 – 950 |
| Natural Gas | 410 – 520 |
| Solar PV | 18 – 80 |
| Onshore Wind | 8 – 30 |
| Offshore Wind | 5 – 25 |
| Hydro | 3 – 30 |
| Geothermal | 25 – 120 |
| Biomass | 30 – 410 (depending on feedstock) |

### C. By Form
- **Primary:** Sunlight, wind, coal, gas.
- **Secondary:** Electricity, hydrogen, gasoline.

### D. By Maturity
- **Mature (commercial):** Hydro, Solar PV, Wind, Biomass.
- **Developing:** CSP, Geothermal, OTEC, Wave.
- **Emerging:** Tidal Stream, Salinity Gradient, Green H₂.

## 2.4 Wind Energy — Operating Principle

The wind turbine is essentially a **rotary air-mill**:

1. Wind strikes the aerofoil-shaped blades.
2. **Lift** (perpendicular to wind) and **drag** (parallel to wind) forces act on the blade.
3. Lift-to-drag ratio (**L/D**) > 50 in modern blades → blade rotates.
4. Hub connects to a low-speed shaft → gearbox (modern multi-MW turbines often use **direct-drive** PM generators, no gearbox) → high-speed shaft → **doubly-fed induction generator (DFIG)** or **PMSG**.
5. **Pitch control** (blade feathering) regulates power at high wind speeds.
6. **Yaw system** rotates nacelle into the wind.

### Power vs Wind Speed Curve (KTU Visual)

```
   Power
    ^
    |               ___________ Rated Power
    |              /
    |             /
    |            /
    |           /
    |          / 
    |         / 
    |        /
    |       /
    |______/
    |     /
    |____/_________________________________> Wind Speed
         V_ci    V_r              V_co
```

Where:
- $V_{ci}$ = **Cut-in Speed** (typically 3 – 4 m/s)
- $V_r$ = **Rated Speed** (typically 12 – 15 m/s)
- $V_{co}$ = **Cut-out Speed** (typically 25 m/s)

## 2.5 Mathematical Foundation of Wind Energy

### A. Kinetic Energy of a Wind Parcel

Consider a parcel of air of mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$. Its kinetic energy is:

$$
KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^{2}
$$

### B. Mass Flow Rate of Air Through Swept Area

If the turbine has a rotor diameter $D$ (radius $R$), the swept area is:

$$
A = \pi R^{2} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}
$$

In time $dt$, the air column passing through $A$ has volume $A \cdot V \cdot dt$, hence:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V \quad [\text{kg/s}]
$$

### C. Power Available in the Wind

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A V) V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power in Wind Equation — The Most Important Formula of This Module:**
> $$P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,V^{3}$$
> - $P_{\text{wind}}$ in Watts (W)
> - $\rho$ in kg/m³ (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, standard atmosphere)
> - $A$ in m²
> - $V$ in m/s

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Since $P \propto V^{3}$, **doubling the wind speed increases power 8-fold**. This is why even modest site selection differences in mean wind speed yield dramatic energy yield changes.

### D. Power Extracted by the Turbine

$$
P_{\text{turbine}} = C_{p} \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}
$$

Where $C_{p}$ = **Power Coefficient** (fraction of wind power captured).

### E. Betz Coefficient & Betz Limit (Albert Betz, 1919)

The maximum physically possible $C_{p}$ for an **ideal, disc-shaped, frictionless turbine** is **16/27 ≈ 0.5926 (≈ 59.3%)**. This is the **Betz Limit**.

$$
C_{p,\text{max}} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593
$$

$$
P_{\text{max,Betz}} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = \frac{8}{27} \rho A V^{3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept Check:** Why can we never extract 100% of wind energy?
> If we stopped the air completely behind the rotor, the air would pile up (impossible — continuity must hold). If we let the air leave at near-inlet velocity, we get zero extraction. The optimum occurs when the rear velocity is **1/3 of inlet velocity** (axial induction factor $a = 1/3$). This is the **Betz condition**.

### F. Power Coefficient in Terms of Induction Factor

$$
C_{p} = 4 a (1 - a)^{2}
$$

Where $a$ = **axial induction factor** = fractional reduction in wind speed at rotor.

Maximizing: $\dfrac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4 [(1-a)^{2} - 2a(1-a)] = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1/3$.

### G. Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)

$$
\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V}
$$

Where $\omega$ = rotor angular velocity (rad/s), $R$ = rotor radius (m), $V$ = wind speed.

### H. Wind Speed Variation with Height (Power Law / Hellmann)

$$
\frac{V_{h}}{V_{h_{0}}} = \left(\frac{h}{h_{0}}\right)^{\alpha}
$$

Where:
- $V_{h}$ = wind speed at height $h$
- $V_{h_0}$ = wind speed at reference height $h_0$
- $\alpha$ = **Hellmann exponent** (depends on terrain roughness)

| Terrain | α |
|---|---|
| Smooth, flat (sea, snow) | 0.10 |
| Open grassland | 0.15 |
| Cropland, hedges | 0.20 |
| Wooded / scattered obstacles | 0.25 – 0.30 |
| Small towns, suburbs | 0.30 |
| Cities, forests | 0.40 |

> [!NOTE]
> **1/7 Power Law (Open Terrain):** $\alpha \approx 1/7 \approx 0.143$, a commonly used standard in open-country conditions.

### I. Weibull Distribution (Wind Speed Frequency)

Probability of wind speed $V$ occurring:

$$
f(V) = \frac{k}{A} \left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{k-1} \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{V}{A}\right)^{k}\right]
$$

Where:
- $k$ = **shape parameter** (typically 1.5 – 3.0; $k = 2$ → Rayleigh distribution)
- $A$ = **scale parameter** (m/s)

Mean wind speed:

$$
\bar{V} = A \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{k}\right)
$$

Energy pattern factor:

$$
E_{pf} = \frac{\overline{V^{3}}}{\bar{V}^{3}} = \frac{\Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{3}{k}\right)}{\left[\Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{k}\right)\right]^{3}}
$$

Total annual energy in wind per unit area:

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \bar{V}^{3} E_{pf} \cdot T_{\text{year}}
$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER CHEAT SHEET — Print this and keep beside your calculator during the exam.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ | Available power in wind | W |
| 2 | $A = \pi R^{2} = \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$ | Swept area of rotor | m² |
| 3 | $P_{\text{turbine}} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ | Actual power captured | W |
| 4 | $C_{p,\text{max}} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz coefficient (max theoretical) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{\text{max,Betz}} = \tfrac{8}{27}\rho A V^{3}$ | Betz max power | W |
| 6 | $C_{p} = 4a(1-a)^{2}$ | Cp in terms of axial induction factor | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\lambda = \omega R / V$ | Tip speed ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $V_{h}/V_{h_0} = (h/h_0)^{\alpha}$ | Wind shear power law | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ | Angular speed from RPM | rad/s |
| 10 | $f(V) = \tfrac{k}{A}(V/A)^{k-1}\exp[-(V/A)^{k}]$ | Weibull PDF | 1/(m/s) |
| 11 | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | Mass flow rate of wind | kg/s |
| 12 | $E = P \cdot t$ | Energy from power over time | J or kWh |
| 13 | $1\ \text{m/s} = 3.6\ \text{km/h}$ | Velocity conversion | — |
| 14 | $\rho_{\text{standard}} = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ | Standard air density (15 °C, 1 atm, sea level) | kg/m³ |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Wind Energy Equations

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Wind Resource Assessment** | Sizing of wind farms; energy yield forecasting. |
| **Turbine Selection** | Matching rotor diameter + Cp curve to site wind regime. |
| **Economic LCOE Calculation** | LCOE = (LCC) / (Lifetime Energy Production). |
| **Offshore Wind Design** | Higher hub height → higher V³ → exponentially more energy. |
| **Wind-Hybrid Systems** | Combined PV-Wind sizing for off-grid microgrids. |
| **Wake Loss Modelling** | Jensen/PARK models use wind speed deficit (Bastankhah, 2016). |
| **Power Grid Stability** | Variability analysis & forecasting using Weibull distribution. |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Power in the Wind (Full Exhaustive Derivation)

### Starting Premise
A wind stream with velocity $V$ passes perpendicularly through a circular area $A$.

### Step 1: Define the Mass of Air Passing per Second

Consider a slug of air with length $V \cdot dt$ (the distance covered in time $dt$) and cross-sectional area $A$. The volume of this slug is $V \cdot A \cdot dt$. Multiplying by air density $\rho$:

$$
dm = \rho \cdot V \cdot A \cdot dt
$$

Differentiating mass with respect to time gives mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{dm}{dt} = \rho A V \quad [\text{kg/s}]
$$

**Explanation:** The faster the air ($V \uparrow$), the denser it is ($\rho \uparrow$), and the larger the opening ($A \uparrow$), the more kilograms of air pass per second.

### Step 2: Apply Kinetic Energy Principle

The kinetic energy of any moving mass $m$ at velocity $V$ is:

$$
KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^{2}
$$

### Step 3: Energy Passing per Unit Time = Power

For the air mass flowing in time $dt$:

$$
dKE = \frac{1}{2} (dm) V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A V \, dt) V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} \, dt
$$

Dividing both sides by $dt$:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{dKE}{dt} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}
$$

**Conclusion:** Power available in wind is proportional to the cube of velocity.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Betz Limit (16/27) — Full Derivation

### Setup
Consider an ideal disc-shaped rotor with:
- Inlet wind velocity $V_1$ (upstream, undisturbed)
- Velocity at rotor plane $V_2$
- Outlet (wake) velocity $V_3$ (downstream)

Define **axial induction factor** $a$:

$$
a = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{V_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_2 = V_1 (1 - a)
$$

From momentum conservation, the velocity in the wake decays linearly:

$$
V_3 = V_1 (1 - 2a)
$$

### Step 1: Mass Flow Through Rotor

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A V_2 = \rho A V_1 (1 - a)
$$

### Step 2: Force on the Rotor (Newton's 2nd Law)

$$
F = \dot{m} (V_1 - V_3) = \rho A V_1 (1 - a) \cdot [V_1 - V_1(1 - 2a)]
$$

Simplifying the bracket:

$$
V_1 - V_1(1 - 2a) = V_1 \cdot 2a = 2 a V_1
$$

Hence:

$$
F = \rho A V_1 (1 - a) \cdot 2 a V_1 = 2 \rho A V_1^{2} a (1 - a)
$$

### Step 3: Power Extracted by the Rotor

Power = Force × velocity at rotor plane:

$$
P_{\text{rotor}} = F \cdot V_2 = 2 \rho A V_1^{2} a (1 - a) \cdot V_1 (1 - a)
$$

$$
P_{\text{rotor}} = 2 \rho A V_1^{3} a (1 - a)^{2}
$$

### Step 4: Power Available in Undisturbed Wind

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_1^{3}
$$

### Step 5: Power Coefficient

$$
C_{p} = \frac{P_{\text{rotor}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = \frac{2 \rho A V_1^{3} a (1 - a)^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A V_1^{3}}
$$

$$
C_{p} = 4 a (1 - a)^{2}
$$

### Step 6: Maximize $C_{p}$ with Respect to $a$

$$
\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4 \left[ (1 - a)^{2} + a \cdot 2 (1 - a) (-1) \right]
$$

$$
\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4 (1 - a)^{2} - 8 a (1 - a) = 4 (1 - a) \left[ (1 - a) - 2a \right] = 4 (1 - a)(1 - 3a)
$$

Setting $\dfrac{dC_{p}}{da} = 0$:

$$
1 - a = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 1 \ (\text{trivial, zero power})
$$
$$
1 - 3a = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \tfrac{1}{3}
$$

Substituting $a = 1/3$ into $C_{p}$:

$$
C_{p,\text{max}} = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \tfrac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \tfrac{4}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{9} = \tfrac{16}{27}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{p,\text{Betz}} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593}
$$

### Step 7: Maximum Power Extracted

$$
P_{\text{max}} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_1^{3} = \frac{8}{27} \rho A V_1^{3}
$$

**Conclusion:** No turbine (real or ideal) can extract more than **59.3 %** of the wind's kinetic energy. Modern turbines achieve $C_{p,\text{max}} \approx 0.45 - 0.50$.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Hellmann Power Law

Wind speed increases with height due to reduced ground friction. From boundary layer theory, the wind profile is logarithmic:

$$
V(h) = \frac{u_{*}}{\kappa} \ln\!\left(\frac{h}{z_{0}}\right)
$$

Where $u_{*}$ = friction velocity, $\kappa \approx 0.41$ (von Kármán), $z_{0}$ = roughness length.

For simplicity, an empirical **power law** is widely used:

$$
\frac{V_{h}}{V_{h_0}} = \left(\frac{h}{h_0}\right)^{\alpha}
$$

Where $\alpha$ is determined by:

$$
\alpha = \frac{\ln(V_{h}/V_{h_0})}{\ln(h/h_0)}
$$

**Example Application:** If $V_{10} = 5\ \text{m/s}$ and $\alpha = 0.143$ (open terrain), what is $V_{50}$?

$$
V_{50} = 5 \cdot \left(\frac{50}{10}\right)^{0.143} = 5 \cdot (5)^{0.143} = 5 \cdot e^{0.143 \ln 5} = 5 \cdot e^{0.143 \cdot 1.6094} = 5 \cdot e^{0.2301} \approx 6.29\ \text{m/s}
$$

Power ratio at hub 50 m vs 10 m:

$$
\frac{P_{50}}{P_{10}} = \left(\frac{V_{50}}{V_{10}}\right)^{3} = \left(\frac{6.29}{5}\right)^{3} = 1.258^{3} \approx 1.99
$$

**Conclusion:** Raising hub from 10 m to 50 m nearly **doubles** available wind power — a critical design insight.

---

## 3.4 SOLVED NUMERICAL PROBLEMS (KTU Board Pattern)

---

### **PROBLEM 1 — Basic Power in Wind [3 Mark Weightage Pattern, KTU 2023]**

> **Question:** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **80 m**. The wind speed at hub height is **12 m/s**. Air density is **1.225 kg/m³**. Calculate the power available in the wind. If the turbine has a power coefficient of **0.40**, find the electrical power output.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Swept Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (80)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6400}{4} = 1600 \pi \approx 5026.55\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Power in Wind:**

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot (12)^{3}
$$

Compute $12^{3} = 1728$. Then:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 1728
$$

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = 0.6125 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 1728
$$

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = 3080.74 \cdot 1728 \approx 5{,}323{,}521\ \text{W} \approx 5.32\ \text{MW}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Turbine Output Power:**

$$
P_{\text{turbine}} = C_{p} \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.40 \cdot 5.32\ \text{MW} \approx 2.13\ \text{MW}
$$

**Result:** $P_{\text{wind}} \approx 5.32\ \text{MW}$; $P_{\text{electrical}} \approx 2.13\ \text{MW}$.

---

### **PROBLEM 2 — Betz Maximum Power [KTU 2022 University Exam Type]**

> **Question:** A wind turbine of rotor diameter **40 m** operates at a hub-height wind speed of **10 m/s**. Calculate the **maximum possible power** extractable (Betz limit) at $\rho = 1.25\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Swept Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 1600}{4} = 400 \pi \approx 1256.64\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Power in Wind:**

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.25 \cdot 1256.64 \cdot (10)^{3}
$$

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = 0.625 \cdot 1256.64 \cdot 1000 = 785.4 \cdot 1000 = 785{,}400\ \text{W} = 785.4\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Betz Limit:**

$$
P_{\text{max,Betz}} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot 785.4 = 0.5926 \cdot 785.4 \approx 465.4\ \text{kW}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
P_{\text{max,Betz}} = \frac{8}{27} \rho A V^{3} = \frac{8}{27} \cdot 1.25 \cdot 1256.64 \cdot 1000
$$

$$
= 0.2963 \cdot 1.25 \cdot 1256.64 \cdot 1000 = 0.2963 \cdot 1{,}570{,}800 \approx 465{,}418\ \text{W} \approx 465.4\ \text{kW}
$$

**Result:** Maximum theoretical extractable power ≈ **465.4 kW**.

---

### **PROBLEM 3 — Velocity Variation with Height [KTU Frequently Asked]**

> **Question:** The wind speed at a height of **10 m** is measured as **6 m/s**. Calculate the wind speed at **50 m** if the terrain roughness exponent $\alpha = 1/7$. Also compute the percentage increase in available wind power.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply Power Law:**

$$
V_{50} = V_{10} \cdot \left(\frac{50}{10}\right)^{1/7} = 6 \cdot (5)^{1/7}
$$

Compute $(5)^{1/7}$:

$$
\ln(5) = 1.6094 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{7} \cdot 1.6094 = 0.2299 \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{0.2299} = 1.2585
$$

$$
V_{50} = 6 \cdot 1.2585 \approx 7.55\ \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Power Ratio:**

$$
\frac{P_{50}}{P_{10}} = \left(\frac{V_{50}}{V_{10}}\right)^{3} = (1.2585)^{3} \approx 1.994 \approx 1.99
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Increase:**

$$
\% \text{ increase} = (1.99 - 1) \times 100\% \approx 99\%
$$

**Result:** Wind speed at 50 m is **7.55 m/s**; available wind power increases by **~ 99 %** by raising the hub from 10 m to 50 m.

---

### **PROBLEM 4 — Tip Speed Ratio & Rotor RPM [KTU Module Test]**

> **Question:** A wind turbine has rotor diameter **50 m**, operating at wind speed **10 m/s**. The blade tip speed is to be maintained at a **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) of 7**. Calculate (a) the rotor angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s), (b) the rotor rotational speed $N$ in RPM.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rotor Radius:**

$$
R = \frac{D}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular Velocity from TSR Definition:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega = \frac{\lambda V}{R} = \frac{7 \cdot 10}{25} = \frac{70}{25} = 2.8\ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to RPM:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N = \frac{60 \, \omega}{2 \pi} = \frac{60 \cdot 2.8}{2 \pi}
$$

$$
N = \frac{168}{6.2832} \approx 26.74\ \text{RPM}
$$

**Result:** $\omega \approx 2.8\ \text{rad/s}$, $N \approx 26.74\ \text{RPM}$.

---

### **PROBLEM 5 — Annual Energy from a Wind Farm [KTU Higher Order / Numerical]**

> **Question:** A wind farm has **10 identical turbines**, each with rotor diameter **60 m**, installed at a site with annual mean wind speed of **8 m/s** at hub height. The wind follows a Weibull distribution with shape factor $k = 2$. Assume $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $C_{p} = 0.40$, and overall drivetrain + generator efficiency $\eta = 0.85$. Calculate the annual energy generated by the farm in MWh.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Swept Area of Each Turbine:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 3600}{4} = 900 \pi \approx 2827.43\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Pattern Factor for $k = 2$ (Rayleigh):**

For Rayleigh distribution:

$$
\bar{V} = A \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\right) = A \cdot \Gamma(1.5) = A \cdot 0.8862
$$

Energy pattern factor:

$$
E_{pf} = \frac{\Gamma(1 + 1.5)}{[\Gamma(1.5)]^{3}} = \frac{\Gamma(2.5)}{(0.8862)^{3}} = \frac{1.3293}{0.6958} \approx 1.910
$$

(Recall $\Gamma(2.5) = 1.3293$, $\Gamma(1.5) = 0.8862$.)

**Step 3 — Mean Cube of Wind Speed:**

$$
\overline{V^{3}} = E_{pf} \cdot \bar{V}^{3} = 1.910 \cdot (8)^{3} = 1.910 \cdot 512 \approx 977.92\ \text{(m/s)}^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Mean Power per Turbine:**

$$
\bar{P}_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A \overline{V^{3}} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 2827.43 \cdot 977.92
$$

$$
= 0.6125 \cdot 2827.43 \cdot 977.92 \approx 612.5 \cdot 2{,}765{,}362 \approx 1.694 \times 10^{9}\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\approx 1694\ \text{kW} = 1.694\ \text{MW}
$$

**Step 5 — Actual Electrical Power (per turbine):**

$$
\bar{P}_{\text{el}} = C_{p} \cdot \eta \cdot \bar{P}_{\text{wind}} = 0.40 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 1.694 \approx 0.5759\ \text{MW}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Power for 10 Turbines:**

$$
\bar{P}_{\text{farm}} = 10 \cdot 0.5759 \approx 5.759\ \text{MW}
$$

**Step 7 — Annual Energy Output:**

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = \bar{P}_{\text{farm}} \cdot T_{\text{year}} = 5.759\ \text{MW} \cdot 8760\ \text{h/year}
$$

$$
E_{\text{annual}} \approx 50{,}447\ \text{MWh/year} \approx 50.4\ \text{GWh/year}
$$

**Result:** Annual energy ≈ **50,447 MWh/year (≈ 50.4 GWh)**.

---

### **PROBLEM 6 — Effect of Air Density on Power [KTU Conceptual]**

> **Question:** A wind turbine produces **750 kW** at standard conditions ($\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$). What will be the power output at a high-altitude site where $\rho = 0.96\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$, assuming wind speed is the same?

**Solution:**

Since $P \propto \rho$, the ratio is:

$$
\frac{P_{\text{alt}}}{P_{\text{std}}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{alt}}}{\rho_{\text{std}}} = \frac{0.96}{1.225} \approx 0.7837
$$

$$
P_{\text{alt}} = 0.7837 \cdot 750 \approx 587.7\ \text{kW}
$$

**Result:** Output drops to **~ 587.7 kW** — a **~ 21.6 % reduction** due to thinner air.

---

### **PROBLEM 7 — Comparison Across Sites (Kerala vs Rajasthan)**

> **Question:** Two wind sites — **A (Kerala coast, $\rho = 1.225$, $V = 5\ \text{m/s}$)** and **B (Rajasthan, $\rho = 1.10$, $V = 7.5\ \text{m/s}$)** — have identical turbines with $D = 60\ \text{m}$. Find the ratio of power in wind, $\frac{P_B}{P_A}$.

**Solution:**

$$
\frac{P_B}{P_A} = \frac{\rho_B V_B^{3}}{\rho_A V_A^{3}} = \frac{1.10 \cdot (7.5)^{3}}{1.225 \cdot (5)^{3}}
$$

Compute cubes:

$$
(7.5)^{3} = 421.875 \quad ; \quad (5)^{3} = 125
$$

$$
\frac{P_B}{P_A} = \frac{1.10 \cdot 421.875}{1.225 \cdot 125} = \frac{464.06}{153.125} \approx 3.03
$$

**Result:** Site B delivers **~ 3.03 times** the power of Site A — illustrating why inland arid sites with higher wind speed often outperform coastal sites despite lower air density.

---

## 3.5 ANALYTICAL COMPARISON MATRIX (KTU Viva / Revision)

| Aspect | $V = 5\ \text{m/s}$ | $V = 10\ \text{m/s}$ | $V = 15\ \text{m/s}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V^{3}$ | 125 | 1000 | 3375 |
| Power Ratio | 1× (ref) | **8×** | **27×** |
| Increase % | — | +700% | +2600% |

| Aspect | Altitude 0 m | Altitude 1000 m | Altitude 3000 m |
|---|---|---|---|

| Power Ratio | 1× | 0.91× | 0.74× |

| Aspect | Smooth Sea | Grassland | Suburbs |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hellmann α | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.30 |

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Classification of Energy Sources

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[ENERGY SOURCES]:::root --> B1[NON-RENEWABLE]
    A1 --> B2[RENEWABLE]:::highlight

    B1 --> C1[Fossil Fuels]

    C1 --> D1[Coal]

    C1 --> D3[Natural Gas]

    B2 --> E1[Solar Energy]
    B2 --> E2[Wind Energy]
    B2 --> E3[Hydro Energy]

    B2 --> E5[Geothermal]
    B2 --> E6[Ocean Energy]
    B2 --> E7[Hydrogen Energy]

    E6 --> F1[Tidal Energy]
    E6 --> F2[Wave Energy]
    E6 --> F3[OTEC]
```
