# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (Each 3 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define Sustainable Development. List the three pillars of sustainable development with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Sustainable Development is defined (Brundtland, 1987) as "development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."

**Three Pillars:**

1. **Environmental Sustainability** — Conserving natural ecosystems and reducing carbon emissions. *Example:* Replacing coal plants with solar farms reduces emissions.
2. **Economic Sustainability** — Ensuring energy supply is affordable and supports industrial growth. *Example:* Subsidy-driven solar pumps for farmers.
3. **Social Sustainability** — Universal access to clean energy. *Example:* Saubhagya scheme providing free electricity connections to rural households in India.

(3 marks — Definition 1M + Each pillar with example 1M each)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State Betz's law. What is the maximum value of the power coefficient for a wind turbine?**

**Model Answer:**

**Betz's Law:** It states that the maximum fraction of kinetic energy that can be extracted from an ideal wind stream by a frictionless, disc-shaped rotor is finite and depends only on the ratio of upstream to downstream wind speeds.

Mathematically:

$$
C_{p,\text{max}} = \frac{16}{27}
$$

**Maximum Power Coefficient:**

$$
C_{p,\text{max}} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad \text{or} \quad 59.3\%
$$

This means **no turbine can convert more than ~ 59.3 %** of the wind's kinetic energy into useful mechanical/electrical energy. Real turbines typically achieve $C_{p,\text{max}} \approx 0.45 - 0.50$ due to aerodynamic, mechanical, and electrical losses.

(3 marks — Statement of Betz Law 1.5M + Numerical value with derivation hint 1.5M)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Module 1 Question Paper Pattern (KTU 2024):** Each Part B has 2 sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means the student can answer ANY ONE from the two sub-parts OR from the two options provided (Questions A and B are alternatives).

---

### **Question A — Choice Option 1 [14 Marks]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] — Energy, Sustainable Development & Social Implications**

**Question:** *Explain the concept of sustainable development in the context of energy. Discuss the major social implications of current energy systems and explain how renewable energy addresses them.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Energy and Sustainable Development (3 Marks)**

Sustainable development in the energy sector means transitioning from finite, polluting fossil fuels to renewable, indigenous, and low-carbon sources while maintaining economic growth and social equity. The Brundtland Commission (1987) emphasised intergenerational equity — current generations must not deplete resources needed by future generations.

The energy-sustainability linkage is captured in **UN SDG 7 (Affordable & Clean Energy)** and **SDG 13 (Climate Action)**.

**2. Social Implications of Current Energy Systems (2 Marks)**

- **Energy poverty:** ~770 million people lack electricity access globally (IEA, 2022). In India, despite 99%+ village electrification, ~30 million households remain un-electrified.
- **Health:** Indoor air pollution from solid biomass burning causes ~3.8 million premature deaths/year (WHO).
- **Geopolitical Vulnerability:** India imports ~85% of its oil and ~50% of its gas, creating supply security risks.
- **Gender inequity:** Women in rural India spend ~2 hours/day collecting firewood.
- **Climate-induced migration:** Sea-level rise and extreme weather events displace millions.

**3. How Renewables Address These Issues (2 Marks)**

| Social Issue | Renewable Solution |
|---|---|
| Energy poverty | Decentralised solar home systems (PM-KUSUM, Saubhagya) |
| Indoor pollution | Clean cooking via solar/biogas (Ujjwala + Gobar-Dhan) |
| Import dependence | Indigenous solar/wind → Atmanirbhar Bharat |
| Climate change | Net-zero pathway (India's Panchamrit, COP26) |
| Gender gap | Solar irrigation reduces drudgery, frees women's time |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Renewable energy is the cornerstone of a sustainable, equitable, and resilient society.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] — Worldwide and Indian RE Availability**

**Question:** *Discuss the global and Indian availability of renewable energy resources. List the major renewable energy types and their potential in India.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Global Availability (3 Marks)**

The world has abundant renewable resources far exceeding current energy demand (~595 EJ/yr in 2023):

| Resource | Global Potential (per year) |
|---|---|
| Solar | ~1,200 EJ |
| Wind | ~200 EJ (practical) |
| Hydro | ~50 EJ |
| Biomass | ~150 EJ (sustainable) |
| Geothermal | ~10 EJ |

Global installed RE capacity crossed **~ 4,048 GW** by end-2023 (IRENA), led by China (1,453 GW), USA (387 GW), Brazil (175 GW), India (180 GW), and Germany (149 GW).

**2. Indian Availability (3 Marks)**

India is endowed with all major renewable resources:

| Source | Estimated Potential | Installed (2024) |
|---|---|---|
| Solar | ~748 GWp | ~81.5 GW |
| Wind | ~302 GW (@100 m) | ~47.0 GW |
| Small Hydro | ~21 GW | ~4.9 GW |
| Biomass | ~25 GW | ~10.2 GW |

India targets **500 GW non-fossil capacity** by 2030 and **net-zero by 2070** (announced at COP26, Glasgow).

**3. Major Renewable Energy Types in India (1 Mark)**

- **Solar:** Direct sunlight via PV or CSP. India launched the **National Solar Mission (2010)** targeting 100 GW by 2022 (achieved ~81.5 GW).
- **Wind:** Kinetic energy of air. **Muppandal** (Tamil Nadu) is India's largest wind cluster.
- **Biomass:** Bagasse cogeneration in sugar mills; rice-husk based gasifiers.
- **Geothermal:** Puga Valley (Ladakh), Manikaran (Himachal).
- **Ocean:** Gulf of Kutch (tidal), Gulf of Cambay, southern coastline (wave).

---

### **Question B — Choice Option 2 [14 Marks]**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] — Wind Power Equations & Betz Limit**

**Question:** *Derive the expression for power available in wind. State and derive Betz's law. A wind turbine of rotor diameter 60 m operates at wind speed 12 m/s. Calculate (i) power in wind, (ii) maximum extractable power (Betz limit), and (iii) shaft power if $C_p = 0.42$. Take $\rho = 1.25\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Derivation of Power in Wind (2 Marks)**

For an air mass $m$ at velocity $V$, kinetic energy = $\tfrac{1}{2} m V^{2}$. Mass flow rate through area $A$ is $\dot{m} = \rho A V$. Power is energy per unit time:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}
$$

**2. Betz's Law & Derivation (2.5 Marks)**

Using axial induction factor $a$, with $V_2 = V_1(1-a)$ and $V_3 = V_1(1-2a)$:

$$
C_p = 4a(1-a)^2
$$

Setting $\dfrac{dC_p}{da} = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1/3$, giving:

$$
C_{p,\text{max}} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593
$$

Maximum power:

$$
P_{\text{Betz,max}} = \frac{8}{27} \rho A V^{3}
$$

**3. Numerical Solution (2.5 Marks)**

Given: $D = 60\ \text{m}$, $V = 12\ \text{m/s}$, $\rho = 1.25\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $C_p = 0.42$.

(i) Swept area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (60)^{2}}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43\ \text{m}^{2}
$$

Power in wind:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1.25 \cdot 2827.43 \cdot (12)^{3}
$$
$$
= 0.625 \cdot 2827.43 \cdot 1728 = 3{,}053{,}540\ \text{W} \approx 3.054\ \text{MW}
$$

(ii) Maximum power (Betz):

$$
P_{\text{Betz}} = \tfrac{16}{27} \cdot 3.054 = 0.5926 \cdot 3.054 \approx 1.810\ \text{MW}
$$

(iii) Actual shaft power:

$$
P_{\text{shaft}} = 0.42 \cdot 3.054 \approx 1.283\ \text{MW}
$$

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] — Wind Velocity Variation with Height**

**Question:** *Explain the Hellmann power law for wind shear. A 10 m wind speed measurement at a site gives 5 m/s. Estimate the wind speed and available power at hub heights of (i) 30 m and (ii) 80 m. Take $\alpha = 1/7$. Comment on the implications for hub-height selection.*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Hellmann Power Law (2 Marks)**

Wind speed increases with height due to reduced ground friction. The empirical power law is:

$$
\frac{V_h}{V_{h_0}} = \left(\frac{h}{h_0}\right)^{\alpha}
$$

The exponent $\alpha$ depends on terrain roughness. For open terrain $\alpha \approx 1/7 \approx 0.143$.

**2. Numerical Computations (3 Marks)**

Given $V_{10} = 5\ \text{m/s}$, $\alpha = 1/7$.

**(i) At $h = 30$ m:**

$$
V_{30} = 5 \cdot \left(\frac{30}{10}\right)^{1/7} = 5 \cdot (3)^{1/7}
$$

$$
\ln 3 = 1.0986 \Rightarrow \tfrac{1}{7} \cdot 1.0986 = 0.1569 \Rightarrow e^{0.1569} = 1.1699
$$

$$
V_{30} \approx 5.85\ \text{m/s}
$$

Power ratio:

$$
\frac{P_{30}}{P_{10}} = \left(\frac{V_{30}}{V_{10}}\right)^{3} = (1.1699)^{3} \approx 1.601 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{30} \approx 60.1\% \text{ more power}
$$

**(ii) At $h = 80$ m:**

$$
V_{80} = 5 \cdot \left(\frac{80}{10}\right)^{1/7} = 5 \cdot (8)^{1/7}
$$

$$
\ln 8 = 2.0794 \Rightarrow \tfrac{1}{7} \cdot 2.0794 = 0.2971 \Rightarrow e^{0.2971} = 1.3459
$$

$$
V_{80} \approx 6.73\ \text{m/s}
$$

Power ratio:

$$
\frac{P_{80}}{P_{10}} = (1.3459)^{3} \approx 2.439 \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{80} \approx 144\% \text{ more power (i.e., 2.44×)}
$$

**3. Implications for Hub-Height Selection (2 Marks)**

- Raising the hub from 10 m to 80 m **more than doubles** available wind power.
- Modern utility-scale turbines use 80 – 120 m hub heights to capture stronger, less turbulent winds.
- Trade-off: Taller towers cost more (steel, foundation, logistics), so the optimum is determined by **Net Present Value (NPV)** of incremental energy vs incremental cost.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mix-ups (–1 mark each occurrence):** Writing $V$ in km/h inside the power formula. Always convert to **m/s** first.
> 2. **Forgetting swept area formula (–2 marks):** Many students write $A = \pi R$ instead of $A = \pi R^{2}$. Swept area is the area of the disc, not its circumference.
> 3. **Using standard density incorrectly at altitude:** $\rho = 1.225$ is at sea level only. For high-altitude sites (e.g., Rajasthan, Ladakh), always use the given $\rho$.
> 4. **Betz coefficient misuse:** Some students divide by 27 instead of multiplying by 16/27. Remember: **multiply** the wind power by Betz's factor to get max extractable power.
> 5. **V³ Mis-evaluated:** $(10)^3 = 1000$ is correct, but $(15)^3 = 3375$ — students often write 3375 incorrectly. Practice cubes!
> 6. **Hellmann Law Confusion:** $\alpha$ increases with surface roughness. Smooth terrain has small $\alpha$, forest/cities have large $\alpha$. Higher $\alpha$ → greater speed-up with height.
> 7. **Power coefficient vs efficiency:** $C_p$ is not the overall efficiency — it only captures rotor aerodynamics. Multiply by gearbox + generator efficiencies for true electrical output.
> 8. **Swept Area in Numerical:** Be careful — KTU sometimes gives diameter in **cm**, **m**, or **ft**. Always convert to **metres** before calculation.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER 📌

> **Quick-Fire Revision Checklist (Last-Night KTU Survival List)**

### 🔹 Sustainable Development
- Brundtland definition (1987).
- **3 Pillars:** Environmental, Economic, Social.
- **UN SDG 7** = Affordable & Clean Energy; **SDG 13** = Climate Action.
- India's COP26 **Panchamrit**: 500 GW non-fossil, 50% non-fossil share, 45% emission intensity cut, 1 GtCO₂ reduction, **net-zero by 2070**.

### 🔹 Global & Indian RE Status
- Global RE capacity (2023): **~ 4,048 GW** (IRENA).
- India target: **500 GW non-fossil** by 2030.
- India installed RE (early 2024): **~ 144 GW** (excluding large hydro).
- **Largest wind farm in India:** Muppandal, Tamil Nadu (1,500 MW).
- **India wind potential @ 100 m:** **~ 302 GW**.
- **India solar potential:** **~ 748 GWp**.

### 🔹 Types of Renewable Energy
- **Solar** (PV + CSP), **Wind**, **Hydro** (large/small/pumped), **Biomass**, **Geothermal**, **Ocean** (tidal/wave/OTEC/salinity gradient), **Hydrogen**, **Hybrid** systems.

### 🔹 Wind Energy — Must-Know Formulas
- $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ — **most important formula**.
- $A = \pi R^{2} = \pi D^{2}/4$.
- $C_{p,\text{Betz}} = \tfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$.
- $P_{\text{max}} = \tfrac{8}{27}\rho A V^{3}$.
- $\lambda = \omega R / V$ — tip speed ratio.
- $V_{h}/V_{h_0} = (h/h_0)^{\alpha}$ — Hellmann Law.
- Weibull distribution: $k = 2 \Rightarrow$ Rayleigh.

### 🔹 Properties of Wind
- Standard $\rho = 1.225\ \text{kg/m}^{3}$ at 15 °C, 1 atm, sea level.
- $V \propto V^3$ — cube-law is the heart of wind energy.
- Cut-in 3–4 m/s, Rated 12–15 m/s, Cut-out 25 m/s.
- Real turbine $C_{p,\text{max}} \approx 0.45 – 0.50$.

### 🔹 Wind Availability in India
- Top states: **Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Rajasthan**.
- Most states along the Western Ghats & coasts have good winds.
- Kerala has lower potential (~ 3 GW), but good at Agali & Kanjikode.

### 🔹 Social Implications
- **Energy poverty, health, climate, gender equity, geopolitics** — all interlinked.
- Renewables enable distributed, inclusive, sovereign, clean energy.

---

**End of Module 1 Topic Notes — OEEET613 Renewable Energy Systems**

**Total Marks Weightage (KTU 2024, ESE Pattern):** ~ 14 marks expected in Part B (with internal choice) + ~ 3 marks in Part A per question.

**Recommended Next Module:** Module 2 — Solar Energy: Solar Radiation, Measurement, and Thermal Energy Systems.

topics.title: Major Problems Associated with Wind Power, Basic Components of Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Major Problems Associated with Wind Power, Basic Components of Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Wind Power? (Formal KTU Definition)

**Wind Power** is the conversion of the **kinetic energy** of moving air masses (driven by differential solar heating of the Earth's surface) into useful mechanical work and subsequently into **electrical energy** using aerodynamic turbine blades coupled to an electro-mechanical generator.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (Course Outcome CO1, Module 1), wind power is classified as an **indirect form of solar energy** and falls under the category of **non-conventional / renewable energy resources** because it is replenished naturally on a human-time scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Module 1 expects students to critically evaluate the *practical difficulties* faced by wind power (intermittency, dispatchability, grid integration) — not just memorize its benefits. Examiners frequently test the **limitations** in Part A (3-mark questions).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Money" Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a beach and the wind is blowing a ₹10 note across the sand. You want to catch it.

- The note has **kinetic energy** (like the wind).
- Your outstretched palm is the **turbine blade**.
- The note slipping past represents **energy you missed** (because the wind is variable and low-density).
- You can only catch the note when the wind speed is in a **sweet spot** (3 m/s – 25 m/s). Too slow = it flutters away. Too fast = it tears or blows over you (the turbine must shut down for safety).
- Even when you catch it, the note may be **crumpled, wet, or arriving in gusts** (power quality, intermittency, low density problems).

This analogy captures the three fundamental truths of wind energy:
1. The resource is **free** but **intermittent**.
2. The energy density is **extremely low** (about 1/800th that of water for hydro).
3. There is a **narrow operating window** — the turbine is productive only within a specific wind-speed band.

---

### 1.3 The Fundamental Wind-Power Equation (Foundation of WECS)

The available power in a free-flowing wind stream crossing area $A$ at velocity $v$ is:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, \rho\, A\, v^{3}
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = **air density** (≈ **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, 15 °C)
- $A$ = swept rotor area in m²
- $v$ = upstream wind speed in m/s
- $P_{\text{wind}}$ = kinetic power flux in watts (W)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $v^{3}$ matters:** A doubling of wind speed produces an **eight-fold** increase in available power. This cubic relationship is the single most important reason why wind-power output is highly sensitive to even small changes in wind speed — and the root cause of its **intermittency problem**.

---

### 1.4 Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) — Definition

A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is the integrated electromechanical assembly that captures the kinetic energy of wind and delivers usable electrical power to a load or to the grid. A WECS converts:

$$
\text{Wind Kinetic Energy} \;\xrightarrow{\text{rotor aerodynamics}}\; \text{Mechanical Energy on shaft} \;\xrightarrow{\text{generator}}\; \text{Electrical Energy}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim use this in 14-mark answers):** "A Wind Energy Conversion System is a system that converts the kinetic energy of wind into electrical energy through a wind turbine coupled to an electric generator, with appropriate power-conditioning and control equipment for delivering power at the required voltage, frequency, and quality."

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Problems Associated with Wind Power

Wind energy, despite being the **fastest-growing renewable segment globally**, faces a cluster of well-documented technical, economic, and environmental challenges. KTU Module 1 categorizes these under three headings:

#### A. Resource-Side Problems (Nature of Wind Itself)

- **Intermittency / Variability** — Wind speed fluctuates continuously on second, minute, hourly, and seasonal scales. The power output follows $v^{3}$, so output swings are amplified.
- **Unpredictability / Low Capacity Factor** — Typical capacity factors for onshore wind in Kerala are **20 % – 30 %**; offshore 35 % – 45 %. The plant does not produce its nameplate rating most of the time.
- **Low Power Density** — Air has density ≈ **1.225 kg/m³** (water is ~800× denser). Very large swept areas (long blades) are needed for any meaningful power.
- **Site Specificity** — Viable sites are limited to coastal, ridge, and plateau regions; Kerala's Kanjikode, Ramakkalmedu, and Agali are examples.
- **No Dispatchability** — Wind cannot be turned "on" when demand peaks (unlike thermal/hydro).

#### B. Technical / Grid-Side Problems

- **Power Quality Issues** — Voltage flicker, harmonics injected by power-electronic converters, frequency deviations.
- **Grid Integration Challenges** — Requires spinning reserve, storage, or curtailment. Weak rural grids cannot absorb large wind injections.
- **Storage Problem** — Output must be either used instantly or stored (batteries, pumped hydro), adding cost.
- **Reactive Power & Voltage Stability** — Induction generators (Type 1/2 WECS) consume VARs; need capacitor banks or STATCOMs.
- **Cut-in, Rated, and Cut-out Speeds** — Below cut-in (~3–4 m/s) and above cut-out (~25 m/s) the system is OFF. This creates a "U-shaped" annual energy profile.

#### C. Environmental / Socio-Economic Problems

- **Noise Pollution** — Aerodynamic (broadband) and mechanical (gearbox) noise; regulations typically cap noise at **45 dB(A)** at residences.
- **Avian / Bat Mortality** — Blade strikes and barotrauma (pressure changes).
- **Shadow Flicker** — Rotating blades cast moving shadows on nearby homes (annoyance, epileptic triggers).
- **Land Use & Aesthetics** — Visual intrusion, especially in tourist zones of Kerala.
- **EMI / Radar Interference** — Large turbines scatter radar signals.
- **High Capital Cost (CAPEX)** — ₹4–6 Cr per MW for onshore; payback typically 8–12 years.
- **O&M Difficulty** — Offshore maintenance is expensive and weather-limited.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When listing problems in a 14-mark answer, group them under **Resource, Technical, Environmental, Economic** buckets. Examiners award structure marks for clean classification.

---

### 2.2 Basic Components of a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)

A modern horizontal-axis WECS (HAWT, the dominant design) consists of the following building blocks:

| # | Component | Function | Key Engineering Detail |
|---|-----------|----------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Rotor Blades** | Capture wind kinetic energy via aerodynamic lift (airfoil profile like NACA 44-series) | 2 or 3 blades; length 30–80 m offshore |
| 2 | **Hub** | Mounts blades to main shaft; houses pitch-control mechanism | Pitch bearings allow blade rotation 0°–90° |
| 3 | **Nacelle** | Weather-proof housing on top of tower containing gearbox, generator, controller | Rotates in yaw axis to face wind |
| 4 | **Main Shaft (Low-Speed Shaft)** | Transmits torque from rotor to gearbox | Rotates at ~15–20 rpm |
| 5 | **Gearbox** | Steps up rotor speed (15–20 rpm) to generator speed (1500/1800 rpm) | Multi-stage planetary + helical; single-point-of-failure historically |
| 6 | **Generator** | Converts mechanical to electrical energy | DFIG, PMSG, or SCIG depending on Type 3/4/1/2 classification |
| 7 | **Yaw Drive & Motor** | Rotates nacelle so rotor faces the wind | Active yaw with wind-vane feedback |
| 8 | **Tower** | Elevates rotor into higher, faster wind layer | Tubular steel; hub height 60–120 m offshore |
| 9 | **Anemometer & Wind Vane** | Measure wind speed and direction | Mounted on nacelle top |
| 10 | **Controller / PLC / SCADA** | Supervisory control: pitch, yaw, cut-out, fault handling | Industrial PLC with remote telemetry |
| 11 | **Power Electronic Converter** | Conditions variable-frequency AC → grid-frequency AC (Type 3/4) | Back-to-back IGBT converter |
| 12 | **Step-up Transformer** | Boosts LV (690 V) to HV (11 kV / 33 kV / 66 kV) for grid | Pad-mounted at tower base |
| 13 | **Foundation** | Anchors tower; resists overturning moment | Reinforced concrete gravity or pile |
| 14 | **Brake System** | Aerodynamic (pitch) + mechanical (disc) brake for emergency stop | Fails-safe, spring-applied |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every formula below is **fair game** for KTU 2024 ESE numericals. Memorize the units in **bold** — losing unit marks is the #1 valuation complaint.

| Formula | Expression | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---------|-----------|------------------------|-------|
| Power in Wind | $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ | Available kinetic power flux | **W** |
| Swept Area (HAWT) | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | Area swept by rotor of radius $R$ | **m²** |
| Betz Coefficient (Max Cp) | $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | Theoretical maximum extraction efficiency | dimensionless |
| Power Extracted | $P_{\text{turbine}} = C_{p}\,\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ | Actual mechanical power at shaft | **W** |
| Tip-Speed Ratio | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v}$ | Optimal $\lambda \approx 7$–8 for 3-blade | dimensionless |
| Rotational Speed | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Convert rpm to rad/s | **rad/s** |
| Generator Output | $P_{\text{el}} = \eta_{\text{gear}}\,\eta_{\text{gen}}\,P_{\text{turbine}}$ | After gearbox + generator losses | **W** |
| Energy per Year | $E = P_{\text{rated}} \times 8760 \times CF$ | Annual energy yield | **kWh** |
| Capacity Factor | $CF = \dfrac{E_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{rated}} \times 8760}$ | Real-world performance metric | **dimensionless (0–1)** |
| Air Density (ISA) | $\rho = 1.225\,$ kg/m³ | Standard sea-level reference | **kg/m³** |
| Reynolds No. (blade) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v c}{\mu}$ | For aerodynamic regime | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Board Note:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value in any table (NOT the pipe symbol `\|`). For example, write $\vert v \vert$ or $\lambda_{\text{opt}}\,\mid_{v=v_{\text{rated}}}$. This avoids breaking markdown table rendering in the answer script.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Grid Planners** use $P \propto v^{3}$ to size **spinning reserves** — typically equal to the largest wind farm output.
- **Wind-Site Engineers** use $\lambda_{\text{opt}}$ to choose between **fixed-speed (Type 1)** and **variable-speed (Type 3/4)** designs.
- **OEM Designers** use Betz's law as the *theoretical ceiling* — real turbines achieve $C_{p} = 0.45$–$0.50$.
- **Project Finance** uses Capacity Factor to compute **PPA tariffs** and **LCOE** (Levelized Cost of Energy).
- **Forensic Engineers** use the failure history of gearboxes (≈ 40 % of all WECS downtime) to design **direct-drive PMSG turbines** that eliminate the gearbox entirely.

---

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Available in Wind (Foundation Equation)

**Starting assumption:** Consider a cylindrical "tube" of air of cross-sectional area $A$ moving at uniform velocity $v$.

**Step 1 — Mass of air passing per second**

The mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ through area $A$ is:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v
$$

> *Reasoning:* In one second, a length $v$ metres of air column passes the cross-section. Volume = $A \cdot v$; mass = density × volume = $\rho A v$.

**Step 2 — Kinetic energy per unit mass of this air**

From Newtonian mechanics, kinetic energy per unit mass is:

$$
\text{KE per kg} = \tfrac{1}{2} v^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Power = rate of kinetic energy flow**

Power is energy per unit time, so multiply mass-flow rate by KE per kg:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \dot{m} \times \tfrac{1}{2} v^{2} = \rho A v \times \tfrac{1}{2} v^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check (units)**

$$
[\rho]\,[A]\,[v^{3}] = \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^{3}} \cdot \text{m}^{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}\right)^{3} = \dfrac{\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{2}}{\text{s}^{3}} = \text{W} \;\;\checkmark
$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Because of the $v^{3}$ dependence, doubling wind speed (e.g., from 5 to 10 m/s) yields an **8×** increase in available power. This is why **hub-height selection** (catching faster, less-turbulent winds higher up) is a primary design lever.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Betz Limit (Maximum Extractable Power)

**Starting assumption:** Air of velocity $v_{1}$ approaches the rotor disc. After passing through, velocity reduces to $v_{2}$. The far-wake velocity is $v_{4}$ and the velocity *at the disc itself* is $v_{\text{d}}$.

By continuity: $v_{\text{d}} = \dfrac{v_{1} + v_{4}}{2}$.

**Step 1 — Thrust force on the disc (rate of momentum change)**

$$
F = \rho A v_{\text{d}}\,(v_{1} - v_{4})
$$

**Step 2 — Power extracted = Force × disc velocity**

$$
P_{\text{ext}} = F \cdot v_{\text{d}} = \rho A v_{\text{d}}^{2}\,(v_{1} - v_{4})
$$

**Step 3 — Express everything in terms of $v_{1}$ and an "induction factor" $a$**

Define $v_{4} = v_{1}(1 - 2a)$ and $v_{\text{d}} = v_{1}(1 - a)$, where $0 < a < 0.5$.

Substituting:

$$
P_{\text{ext}} = \rho A \bigl[v_{1}(1 - a)\bigr]^{2}\,\bigl[v_{1} - v_{1}(1 - 2a)\bigr]
$$

$$
P_{\text{ext}} = \rho A v_{1}^{3}\,4a(1 - a)^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of performance**

$$
C_{p} = \dfrac{P_{\text{ext}}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v_{1}^{3}} = 4a(1 - a)^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximize $C_{p}$ wrt $a$**

$$
\dfrac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4(1-a)^{2} + 4a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) = 4(1-a)\bigl[(1-a) - 2a\bigr]
$$

$$
\dfrac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0
$$

Non-trivial root: $a = \dfrac{1}{3}$.

**Step 6 — Substitute back to get $C_{p,\max}$**

$$
C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3}\cdot\left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \tfrac{4}{3} \cdot \tfrac{4}{9} = \tfrac{16}{27}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926\,(59.26\%)\,}
$$

> **Physical interpretation:** Even a *perfect* wind turbine — one with no mechanical, electrical, or aerodynamic losses — can extract **at most 59.3 %** of the kinetic power in the wind. The remaining 40.7 % must stay in the wake (otherwise air would have to stop completely, which would block further inflow).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Annual Energy Yield & Capacity Factor

**Step 1 — Instantaneous electrical output**

$$
P_{\text{el}}(t) = \eta_{\text{gb}}\,\eta_{\text{gen}}\,\eta_{\text{conv}}\,C_{p}\,\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v(t)^{3}
$$

**Step 2 — Annual energy produced (integration)**

$$
E_{\text{year}} = \int_{0}^{8760\,\text{h}} P_{\text{el}}(t)\,dt \;\;\;\; [\text{kWh}]
$$

**Step 3 — Capacity Factor (the metric KTU examiners love)**

$$
CF = \dfrac{E_{\text{year}}}{P_{\text{rated}} \times 8760}
$$

**Worked Micro-Example:** A 2 MW wind farm generates 5.256 × 10⁶ kWh in a year.

$$
CF = \dfrac{5{,}256{,}000}{2000 \times 8760} = \dfrac{5{,}256{,}000}{17{,}520{,}000} = 0.30 = 30\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** used for thermal plants with **Capacity Factor (CF)** used for renewables. PLF = actual generation / maximum possible generation (same as CF in concept) but for thermal plants, $CF \approx 0.70$–$0.85$; for Indian onshore wind farms, $CF \approx 0.20$–$0.30$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Wind vs. Other Renewables

| Parameter | Wind | Solar PV | Hydro | Biomass |
|-----------|------|----------|-------|---------|
| Power Density (W/m²) | 200–500 | 150–250 | 5000–50000 | 200–5000 |
| Capacity Factor | 0.20–0.45 | 0.15–0.25 | 0.40–0.60 | 0.70–0.85 |
| Dispatchability | Poor | Poor | Excellent | Moderate |
| Storage needed | High | High | Low (reservoir) | Low |
| Maturity | Commercial | Commercial | Mature | Commercial |
| LCOE (₹/kWh, 2024) | 3–5 | 3–6 | 2–4 | 5–7 |
| Kerala viability | High (Western Ghats) | Moderate (monsoon) | High (existing dams) | Low (biomass scarcity) |

```mermaid
    L --> M
    L --> N

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = power flow. Dashed arrows = control/safety intervention. The controller (SCADA) is the brain that takes sensor inputs and commands the three actuators (pitch, yaw, brake).

### 4.2 WECS Classification Tree (KTU Module 1 Outcome)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WECS[Wind Energy Conversion System]
    WECS --> HAWT[Horizontal Axis WT\nMost common]
    WECS --> VAWT[Vertical Axis WT\nDarrieus, Savonius]
    HAWT --> T1[Type 1\nFixed Speed\nSCIG + Soft Starter]
    HAWT --> T2[Type 2\nLimited Variable Speed\nWound Rotor + Variable Resistor]
    HAWT --> T3[Type 3\nVariable Speed\nDoubly Fed Induction Generator]
    HAWT --> T4[Type 4\nFull Variable Speed\nPMSG + Full Converter]

    T1 -.-> G1[Direct Grid Connected\nNo Power Electronics]
    T4 -.-> G2[Grid Friendly\nLow Voltage Ride Through]
```

---

### 4.3 Power Curve of a Wind Turbine (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[v less than v_cut-in\nBelow 3 m/s] --> P0[Output = 0\nIdle]
    B[v between v_cut-in and v_rated\n3 to 12 m/s] --> PCUBE[Output rises with v cubed\nPartial Load Region]
    C[v between v_rated and v_cut-out\n12 to 25 m/s] --> PFLAT[Output = P_rated\nPitch Control Active]
    D[v greater than v_cut-out\nAbove 25 m/s] --> PSHUT[Output = 0\nEmergency Shutdown]
    PCUBE --> NEXT
    PFLAT --> NEXT2
    PSHUT --> NEXT3
```

> **Engineering meaning:** Between **v_cut-in** and **v_rated**, the turbine follows the cubic law $P \propto v^{3}$. Between **v_rated** and **v_cut-out**, the blade pitch is actively feathered to limit power to $P_{\text{rated}}$. Above **v_cut-out**, the machine shuts down to protect itself from structural damage.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*"List any six major problems associated with wind power generation."*

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Intermittency** — Wind speed is not constant; output varies on second-to-second and seasonal bases.
2. **Low energy density** — Air at 1.225 kg/m³ requires very large swept areas to extract meaningful power.
3. **Site specificity** — Viable only at coastal, ridge, or plateau regions; many promising sites are far from load centers.
4. **Grid integration difficulty** — Requires spinning reserve, forecasting, and storage to maintain frequency stability.
5. **High capital cost** — ₹4–6 Cr per MW installed; payback 8–12 years.
6. **Environmental concerns** — Noise pollution, bird/bat mortality, shadow flicker, visual intrusion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often list problems without *categorising* them. Awarding **1 mark** for grouping under Resource / Technical / Environmental / Economic is standard KTU valuation practice. A flat ungrouped list loses the structure mark.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*"Explain the role of the gearbox in a Wind Energy Conversion System."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The gearbox is a mechanical speed-increasing transmission placed between the low-speed rotor shaft and the high-speed generator shaft.

- **Function:** The wind turbine rotor rotates at 15–20 rpm (dictated by aerodynamic tip-speed-ratio optimization), while a 50 Hz induction or synchronous generator must run at **1500 rpm (4-pole)** or **1000 rpm (6-pole)**. The gearbox steps up the rotational speed by a ratio of approximately **1:75 to 1:100**.
- **Type:** Multi-stage planetary + helical gearing; efficiency ≈ 95–98 %.
- **Drawback:** Historically the highest-failure-rate component (≈ 40 % of WECS downtime), which is why modern direct-drive PMSG turbines eliminate it.
- **Location:** Housed inside the nacelle on a vibration-isolated bedplate.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the basic components of a Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the maximum power extractable from wind and explain the physical significance of Betz limit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks)**

A Wind Energy Conversion System converts the kinetic energy of wind into electrical energy. The basic components are:

**1. Rotor with blades (1 mark)** — Aerodynamic airfoil blades (typically 2 or 3) capture wind kinetic energy through lift. Modern blades are made of glass-fibre or carbon-fibre reinforced polymer, length 30–80 m.

**2. Hub and pitch mechanism (½ mark)** — The hub mounts the blades to the shaft. Pitch actuators rotate each blade about its long axis to control power capture and feather the blades in high winds.

**3. Low-speed shaft and gearbox (1 mark)** — The slow-rotating shaft (15–20 rpm) drives a multi-stage gearbox that steps the speed up to generator speed (~1500 rpm).

**4. Generator (1 mark)** — Converts mechanical to electrical energy. Common types: Squirrel-Cage Induction Generator (SCIG) for Type 1; Doubly-Fed Induction Generator (DFIG) for Type 3; Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (PMSG) for Type 4.

**5. Nacelle and yaw system (1 mark)** — The nacelle houses the gearbox, generator, and controller. The yaw motor rotates the entire nacelle so that the rotor faces the wind direction.

**6. Tower (½ mark)** — Tubular steel or lattice structure, 60–120 m tall, elevating the rotor into stronger, less turbulent wind.

**7. Control, sensors, transformer, and grid connection (1 mark)** — Anemometers measure wind speed/direction; PLC/SCADA supervises pitch, yaw, braking, and grid synchronization; a step-up transformer (690 V → 33 kV) connects to the grid.

**8. Diagram (1 mark)** — Neat labelled block diagram as shown in SECTION 4.1.

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Answer (7 marks)**

**Derivation of Maximum Power (Betz Limit):**

Consider air of upstream velocity $v_{1}$ passing through an actuator disc of area $A$. After the disc, the far-wake velocity is $v_{4}$. Let $a$ be the axial induction factor, so:

$$
v_{4} = v_{1}(1 - 2a), \qquad v_{\text{disc}} = v_{1}(1 - a)
$$

The thrust force on the disc equals the rate of momentum change:

$$
F = \rho A v_{\text{disc}}(v_{1} - v_{4}) = \rho A v_{1}^{2}\,4a(1 - a)
$$

The power extracted is force times the velocity at the disc:

$$
P = F \cdot v_{\text{disc}} = \rho A v_{1}^{3}\,4a(1 - a)^{2}
$$

The coefficient of performance is:

$$
C_{p} = \dfrac{P}{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v_{1}^{3}} = 4a(1 - a)^{2}
$$

Maximizing by differentiating with respect to $a$:

$$
\dfrac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4(1 - a)(1 - 3a) = 0
$$

The non-trivial optimum is $a = \tfrac{1}{3}$. Substituting:

$$
C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\tfrac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593
$$

**Physical significance of the Betz limit (2 marks):**

- **Theoretical ceiling:** No turbine — however well designed — can extract more than **59.3 %** of the kinetic power in the wind.
- **Why not 100 %?** If all energy were extracted, air would have to stop completely at the disc. This would block further inflow; the disc would become a solid wall. Practical turbines achieve $C_{p} = 0.45$–$0.50$.
- **Design implication:** The Betz coefficient is the benchmark against which real $C_{p}$-$\lambda$ curves are plotted in turbine catalogues.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently forget the *physical significance* and end the derivation at $C_p = 16/27$. The 2 marks for *physical meaning* are easy marks lost. Always include the "why we cannot reach 100 %" paragraph.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

> **(a)** Discuss the major problems associated with wind power generation. Categorise them under resource, technical, environmental, and economic heads. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A wind turbine has rotor diameter 80 m, rated wind speed 12 m/s, and operates at $C_{p} = 0.45$. Air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Compute (i) rated power, (ii) the actual power generated at a wind speed of 8 m/s assuming $C_{p}$ falls to 0.40. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Answer (7 marks)**

**Resource-side problems (2 marks):**

- **Intermittency** — Wind fluctuates over short and long time scales; output follows $v^{3}$.
- **Low energy density** — Air density 1.225 kg/m³; large blades needed.
- **Site specificity** — Limited to ridges, coasts, and plateaus.
- **Unpredictability** — Capacity factors of only 20 %–30 % onshore.

**Technical problems (2 marks):**

- **Power quality issues** — Harmonics, flicker, frequency variation.
- **Grid integration** — Requires forecasting, spinning reserve, storage.
- **Storage difficulty** — Battery cost high; pumped-hydro not always available.
- **Reactive power** — Induction generators draw VARs.

**Environmental problems (1.5 marks):**

- **Noise pollution** (aerodynamic + mechanical); regulations cap at 45 dB(A).
- **Bird/bat mortality** due to blade strikes.
- **Shadow flicker** and visual intrusion.

**Economic problems (1.5 marks):**

- **High CAPEX** — ₹4–6 Cr per MW.
- **Long payback** — 8–12 years.
- **High O&M** — Especially offshore.

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)**

**Given data:**

- $D = 80$ m $\;\Rightarrow\; R = 40$ m
- $v_{\text{rated}} = 12$ m/s
- $C_{p,\text{rated}} = 0.45$
- $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- At $v = 8$ m/s: $C_{p} = 0.40$

**Step 1 — Compute swept area**

$$
A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times 40^{2} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \;\text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Rated power (i)**

$$
P_{\text{rated}} = C_{p} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}
$$

$$
P_{\text{rated}} = 0.45 \times 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 12^{3}
$$

Intermediate calculations:

$$
v^{3} = 12^{3} = 1728
$$

$$
P_{\text{rated}} = 0.45 \times 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728
$$

$$
P_{\text{rated}} = 0.225 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728
$$

$$
P_{\text{rated}} \approx 0.2756 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 \approx 1385.3 \times 1728
$$

$$
P_{\text{rated}} \approx 2{,}394{,}000 \;\text{W} \approx 2.39 \;\text{MW}
$$

**Step 3 — Power at $v = 8$ m/s (ii)**

$$
v^{3} = 8^{3} = 512
$$

$$
P_{8} = 0.40 \times 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 512
$$

$$
P_{8} = 0.20 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 512
$$

$$
P_{8} = 0.245 \times 5026.55 \times 512 \approx 1231.5 \times 512
$$

$$
P_{8} \approx 630{,}530 \;\text{W} \approx 0.631 \;\text{MW}
$$

**Final answers:**

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{rated}} \approx 2.39\;\text{MW} \qquad P_{8\text{ m/s}} \approx 0.63\;\text{MW}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Two common mistakes:
> 1. Forgetting that $D$ is the *diameter* and using $D = R$ — silently halves the swept area and the entire answer. Always write $R = D/2$ explicitly.
> 2. Using $P_{\text{rated}}$ for the second part — the question says $C_{p}$ falls to 0.40, so you must re-compute. Examiners will *not* give partial credit if you reuse $C_{p} = 0.45$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second revision before entering the KTU exam hall — memorize this block.**

- ☐ **Wind power origin:** Indirect solar energy (differential heating → pressure gradient → wind).
- ☐ **Core equation:** $P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A v^{3}$ — units must be **kg, m, s** for output in **watts**.
- ☐ **Air density (ISA sea-level):** **1.225 kg/m³** — this is the *only* density value KTU expects unless explicitly stated.
- ☐ **Betz limit:** $C_{p,\max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ — a perfect turbine cannot exceed this; real turbines reach **0.45–0.50**.
- ☐ **Tip-speed ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / v$ — optimal **λ ≈ 7–8** for three-bladed turbines.
- ☐ **Cut-in / Rated / Cut-out wind speeds:** typical values **3 / 12 / 25 m/s**.
- ☐ **WECS components (14 blocks):** Rotor → Hub → Shaft → Gearbox → Generator → Converter → Transformer → Grid; supported by Tower, Yaw, Pitch, Controller, Anemometer, Brake, Foundation.
- ☐ **Four problem categories:** Resource (intermittency, low density), Technical (power quality, grid integration, storage), Environmental (noise, birds, shadow flicker), Economic (high CAPEX, long payback).
- ☐ **Capacity Factor formula:** $CF = E_{\text{actual}}/(P_{\text{rated}} \times 8760)$ — typical Indian onshore value **20 %–30 %**.
- ☐ **Gearbox purpose:** step up rotor speed (~15 rpm) to generator speed (~1500 rpm); ratio ≈ **1:100**.
- ☐ **Three generator classes:** Type 1 = SCIG (fixed speed); Type 3 = DFIG (partial converter); Type 4 = PMSG (full converter).
- ☐ **Diameter vs. Radius:** KTU *always* gives $D$ — divide by 2 to get $R$ before squaring.
- ☐ **No vertical-axis derivation in KTU Module 1** — but mention Darrieus / Savonius for full marks if asked to "classify".
- ☐ **Final numerical tip:** Carry at least **4 significant figures** in intermediate steps; round only at the final answer.

---

- ☐ *Wind Energy Explained* — J. F. Manwell, J. G. McGowan, A. L. Rogers (Wiley, 2nd ed., 2009)
- ☐ KTU 2024 Scheme Official Syllabus — OEEET613, Module 1 outcomes CO1 & CO2.
- ☐ MNRE (Ministry of New & Renewable Energy, India) Annual Reports — for current Indian capacity-factor and LCOE data.

---

**End of Topic Notes — Module 1, OEEET613 — KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Classification of WECS - Horizontal Axis - Single, Double and Multi-Blade System. Vertical Axis - Savonius and Darrieus Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of WECS - Horizontal Axis - Single, Double and Multi-Blade System. Vertical Axis - Savonius and Darrieus Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS) — Formal Definition

A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is a complete electromechanical apparatus that converts the **kinetic energy** of moving air masses (wind) into useful **electrical or mechanical energy**. The system comprises the rotor (blades + hub), gearbox, generator, nacelle, tower, yaw mechanism, and control electronics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> A WECS is classified primarily based on the **orientation of the rotor axis** with respect to the ground (or wind direction). The two principal families are **Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT)** and **Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT)**.

### Primary Classification Matrix

| Classification Criterion | Type 1 | Type 2 | Type 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Axis Orientation** | Horizontal Axis (HAWT) | Vertical Axis (VAWT) | — |
| **Number of Blades (HAWT)** | Single Blade | Double Blade | Multi-Blade (3-blade typical) |
| **VAWT Sub-Types** | Savonius (Drag-based) | Darrieus (Lift-based) | — |
| **Power Rating** | Micro (<1 kW) | Mini (1–100 kW) | Mega (>1 MW) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition (The "Windmill" Thought Experiment):**
> Imagine standing on a beach with a **pinwheel toy**. The spinning shaft is **horizontal** (pointing at you), so this is a **Horizontal Axis** design. Now, imagine a **Soda Bottle Anemometer** where cups are mounted on a **vertical stick** — that is a **Vertical Axis** device. The first extracts energy efficiently from a fixed wind direction (needs a tail/weather vane to turn); the second catches wind from **any direction** without needing to reorient. This is the fundamental philosophical difference between HAWT and VAWT.

### Real-World Example
- A **3-blade HAWT** is what you see in wind farms at **Muppandal (Tamil Nadu)** and **Kanjikode (Kerala)**.
- A **Savonius rotor** is commonly seen on **small weather stations** and **off-grid rural water pumping** setups.
- A **Darrieus rotor** has a distinctive **egg-beater shape** and is used in **modern urban micro-wind installations**.

---

## 1.3 The Power Available in Wind — Foundational Quantity

The **kinetic power** of wind passing through a swept area $A$ is given by:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot V^{3}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Air density (standard value: **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, 15°C)
- $A$ = Swept area of the rotor blades (m²)
- $V$ = Wind velocity (m/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $V^3$ matters:** A mere **doubling of wind speed** results in an **8-fold increase in available power**. This cubic dependence is the single most important factor in site selection for WECS — which is why coastal Kerala and Western Ghats regions are preferred.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Architecture — Structured Logic

### Step-by-Step Decision Logic for WECS Selection

1. **Step 1 — Determine Site Wind Directional Stability**
   - *Steady prevailing wind (e.g., coastal monsoon corridor)* → Prefer **HAWT** (higher efficiency).
   - *Turbulent, multidirectional wind (e.g., urban rooftop)* → Prefer **VAWT** (omnidirectional).

2. **Step 2 — Determine Required Power Output**
   - *Low power (< 5 kW) for battery charging / water pumping* → **Multi-blade HAWT** or **Savonius**.
   - *High power (utility-scale, > 1 MW)* → **3-blade HAWT** (industry standard).

3. **Step 3 — Determine Cost & Maintenance Tolerance**
   - *Low cost, no yaw mechanism* → **VAWT (Savonius/Darrieus)**.
   - *High efficiency, accepts higher capital cost* → **HAWT**.

4. **Step 4 — Determine Noise and Aesthetic Constraints**
   - *Residential / wildlife-sensitive zones* → **Darrieus** (low tip-speed, quieter).
   - *Industrial wind farms* → **HAWT** (noise is less of a constraint).

---

## 2.2 Detailed Taxonomy of Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT)

### 2.2.1 Single-Blade HAWT
- **Blades:** 1 blade + counterweight on the opposite side of the hub.
- **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR):** High, typically **λ = 8 to 10**.
- **Cut-in Speed:** Lower cut-in (≈ **3 m/s**).
- **Drawbacks:** Severe vibration, high cyclic loading, needs heavy counterweight.
- **Application:** Rare in commercial use; mostly experimental/space-constrained installations.

### 2.2.2 Double-Blade HAWT
- **Blades:** 2 blades mounted 180° apart.
- **Tip Speed Ratio:** Moderate, **λ = 6 to 8**.
- **Advantages:** Lighter than 3-blade, cheaper gearbox, less material.
- **Drawbacks:** "Coning effect" required to prevent tower strikes; gyroscopic forces during yaw.

### 2.2.3 Multi-Blade HAWT (3-Blade — Industry Standard)
- **Blades:** 3 blades at 120° (most common); up to 20+ in water-pumping "American farm" windmills.
- **Tip Speed Ratio:** Lower, **λ = 5 to 7**.
- **Advantages:**
  - Smooth torque (no cyclic peaks).
  - Balanced gyroscopic loads.
  - Visual symmetry (less "strobe flicker").
- **Drawback:** Heavier and costlier than 2-blade.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** Approximately **95% of utility-scale wind turbines** installed worldwide are **3-blade HAWTs**.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Taxonomy of Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT)

### 2.3.1 Savonius Rotor (Drag-Based VAWT)
- **Inventor:** Sigurd Savonius (Finland, 1922).
- **Geometry:** Two (or more) half-cylindrical scoops mounted on a vertical shaft, with a small overlap at the axis.
- **Operating Principle:** **Differential drag** — the concave scoop experiences more drag than the convex back, producing torque.
- **Tip Speed Ratio:** Very low, **λ ≤ 1** (always sub-wind-speed).
- **Efficiency:** Low, power coefficient **Cₚ ≈ 0.15 to 0.25**.
- **Self-starting:** **Yes** (a key advantage over Darrieus).
- **Applications:** Ventilation, low-power water pumping, in-stack chimney augmenters.

### 2.3.2 Darrieus Rotor (Lift-Based VAWT)
- **Inventor:** Georges Jean Marie Darrieus (France, 1931).
- **Geometry:** Two or three thin curved blades (troposkein shape — looks like an "egg beater" or a "H" rotor).
- **Operating Principle:** **Aerodynamic lift** generated as blades move across the wind (independent of wind direction).
- **Tip Speed Ratio:** High, **λ = 3 to 6**.
- **Efficiency:** Moderate, power coefficient **Cₚ ≈ 0.30 to 0.40**.
- **Self-starting:** **No** (requires external push or Savonius augmentation) — this is the major drawback.
- **Applications:** Building-integrated wind, distributed micro-generation.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ | Power available in wind | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | Swept area of HAWT (radius R) | m² |
| 3 | $P_{mech} = C_{p} \cdot P_{wind}$ | Mechanical power extracted | W |
| 4 | $C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | **Betz Limit** (maximum theoretical) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R N}{30 V}$ | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $TSR_{Savonius} \le 1$ | Savonius is sub-wind-speed | — |
| 7 | $TSR_{Darrieus} = 3 \text{ to } 6$ | Darrieus uses lift | — |
| 8 | $TSR_{HAWT,3-blade} = 5 \text{ to } 7$ | Multi-blade HAWT TSR range | — |
| 9 | $\rho_{std} = 1.225$ | Standard air density (sea level) | kg/m³ |
| 10 | $V_{cut\text{-}in}, V_{rated}, V_{cut\text{-}out}$ | Operating wind speeds | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule:** All quantities inside table cells use the standard LaTeX rendering. **Never use vertical pipe `|` inside table cells** (use `\vert` if needed).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wind Farm Design (Kerala context):** Engineers at **KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board)** use these classifications to select turbines for the **Agali and Ramakkalmedu wind farms**.
- **Hybrid Systems:** Multi-blade HAWTs paired with **solar PV** form the basis of hybrid renewable installations studied in **Module 4** of this course.
- **Off-grid Kerala Applications:** Savonius rotors are used in **Kuttanad** for low-head irrigation due to their self-starting capability in variable monsoon winds.
- **Aerospace & Automotive:** Darrieus-derived VAWTs are researched for **vehicle-mounted wind augmentation** and **building facade integration**.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Power in the Wind (From First Principles)

### Step 1 — Start with the definition of kinetic energy
The kinetic energy of a mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$ is:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Express the mass in terms of air density
The mass of air passing through the rotor's swept area per unit time ($\dot{m}$, mass flow rate) is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V$$

This is the **density** times **volume per unit time**, where the volume per unit time is simply $A \cdot V$ (area × velocity).

### Step 3 — Substitute into power equation
Power is energy per unit time, so:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \dot{m} \cdot V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\rho A V) \cdot V^{2}$$

### Step 4 — Final result

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}}$$

**Logic of conversion:** $\dot{m}$ has units of kg/s. Multiplying by $V^{2}$ (m²/s²) gives kg·m²/s³ = **Watts**. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Betz Limit (Why $C_p$ cannot exceed 16/27)

### Step 1 — Apply continuity equation
Mass flow rate through rotor must be conserved:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V_{1} = \rho A_{2} V_{2} = \rho A V_{3}$$

Where $V_1$ = upstream wind speed, $V_2$ = wind speed at rotor, $V_3$ = downstream wind speed.

### Step 2 — Apply momentum theorem (Euler's turbine equation)
The force on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum:

$$F = \dot{m}(V_{1} - V_{3})$$

### Step 3 — Power extracted
Power is force times velocity at the rotor:

$$P = F \cdot V_{2} = \dot{m} (V_{1} - V_{3}) \cdot V_{2}$$

### Step 4 — Express $V_2$ as the average
For an ideal frictionless actuator disc:

$$V_{2} = \frac{V_{1} + V_{3}}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute and simplify
Substituting Step 4 into Step 3:

$$P = \dot{m} (V_{1} - V_{3}) \cdot \frac{V_{1} + V_{3}}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \dot{m} (V_{1}^{2} - V_{3}^{2})$$

### Step 6 — Use continuity to eliminate $V_2$

$$P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (V_{1}^{2} - V_{3}^{2}) \cdot V_{2} \cdot \frac{1}{V_2}$$

### Step 7 — Express $P$ as a function of $V_1$ and axial induction factor $a$
By definition, $V_2 = V_1(1-a)$, where $a$ is the axial induction factor ($0 \le a \le 0.5$). And $V_3 = V_1(1-2a)$.

### Step 8 — Express power coefficient
The power coefficient is the ratio of extracted power to available wind power:

$$C_{p} = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A V_{1}^{3}}$$

### Step 9 — Substitute and simplify

$$C_{p} = 4a(1-a)^{2}$$

### Step 10 — Maximize $C_p$ with respect to $a$
Take the derivative and set to zero:

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{da} = 4(1-a)^{2} - 8a(1-a) = 0$$

$$4(1-a)[(1-a) - 2a] = 0 \implies 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

Non-trivial solution: $a = \frac{1}{3}$

### Step 11 — Substitute $a = 1/3$ back into $C_p$

$$C_{p,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

### Step 12 — Final numerical result

$$\boxed{C_{p,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ (59.26\%, the Betz Limit)}}$$

**Logic of conversion:** No rotor can extract more than 59.26% of the kinetic energy in the wind. The remaining 40.74% must remain in the wake to keep the air moving away (otherwise the air would back up and block the inflow).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: HAWT vs VAWT

| Parameter | HAWT (Single/2/3 Blade) | VAWT (Savonius) | VAWT (Darrieus) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Axis** | Horizontal (parallel to ground) | Vertical | Vertical |
| **Lift or Drag** | Lift (aerofoil blades) | Drag (scoops) | Lift (thin curved blades) |
| **Tip Speed Ratio (λ)** | 5 – 10 | ≤ 1 | 3 – 6 |
| **Power Coeff. (Cₚ)** | 0.35 – 0.48 | 0.15 – 0.25 | 0.30 – 0.40 |
| **Self-Starting** | Yes | Yes | **No** (needs assist) |
| **Yaw Mechanism** | **Required** (rotor must face wind) | **Not needed** (omnidirectional) | **Not needed** |
| **Tower Shadow Effect** | Present (cyclic loading) | Absent | Absent |
| **Wind Direction Sensitivity** | High | Low | Low |
| **Maintenance Location** | Nacelle on top of tower (difficult) | Ground level (easy) | Ground level (easy) |
| **Typical Capacity** | 1 kW – 10 MW | 0.1 – 5 kW | 1 – 100 kW |
| **Cut-in Wind Speed** | 3 – 4 m/s | 2 – 3 m/s | 4 – 5 m/s |
| **Noise Level** | Moderate–High | Low | Moderate |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 3-blade HAWT has a rotor diameter of **80 m**. Wind speed is **12 m/s** and air density is **1.225 kg/m³**. If the power coefficient is **0.40**, find: (a) the swept area, (b) the available wind power, (c) the extracted mechanical power.

### Solution

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 80$ m, so radius $R = 40$ m
- Wind speed $V = 12$ m/s
- Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³
- $C_p = 0.40$

**Part (a) — Swept Area:**

$$A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times (40)^{2} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Part (b) — Available Wind Power:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^{3}$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728$$

$$P_{wind} \approx 5{,}320{,}000 \text{ W} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$$

**Part (c) — Extracted Mechanical Power:**

$$P_{mech} = C_{p} \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \times 5.32 = 2.128 \text{ MW}$$

**Check against Betz Limit:** $C_p = 0.40 < 0.5926$ ✓ (Valid; below theoretical maximum).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["WIND ENERGY CONVERSION SYSTEM"] --> B0["Classification by Axis Orientation"]
    B0 --> C0["HORIZONTAL AXIS HAWT"]
    B0 --> D0["VERTICAL AXIS VAWT"]
    C0 --> C1["Single Blade HAWT"]
    C0 --> C2["Double Blade HAWT"]
    C0 --> C3["Multi Blade HAWT 3 blade typical"]
    C1 --> C1a["High TSR 8 to 10"]
    C1 --> C1b["Needs counterweight"]
    C1 --> C1c["Rare commercial use"]
    C2 --> C2a["Moderate TSR 6 to 8"]
    C2 --> C2b["Coning required"]
    C2 --> C2c["Gyroscopic loads"]
    C3 --> C3a["Standard TSR 5 to 7"]
    C3 --> C3b["Balanced smooth torque"]
    C3 --> C3c["Industry standard 95 percent"]
    D0 --> D1["Savonius Rotor"]
    D0 --> D2["Darrieus Rotor"]
    D1 --> D1a["Drag based"]
    D1 --> D1b["TSR less than 1"]
    D1 --> D1c["Self starting yes"]
    D1 --> D1d["Low Cp 0.15 to 0.25"]
    D2 --> D2a["Lift based"]
    D2 --> D2b["TSR 3 to 6"]
    D2 --> D2c["Self starting NO"]
    D2 --> D2d["Moderate Cp 0.30 to 0.40"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — HAWT Power Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W0["Wind Kinetic Energy Input V cubed"] --> R0["Rotor Blades Single Double Multi"]
    R0 --> S0["Main Shaft Low Speed Rotation"]
    S0 --> G0["Gearbox Step Up Ratio 1 to 50"]
    G0 --> H0["High Speed Shaft"]
    H0 --> M0["Generator Induction or PMSG"]
    M0 --> P0["Power Converter AC to AC"]
    P0 --> G1["Grid or Load Output"]
    R0 --> Y0["Yaw Drive Aligns with Wind"]
    Y0 --> R0
    S0 --> B0["Brake System Storm Protection"]
    B0 --> S0
    M0 --> T0["Tower Carries Nacelle"]
    T0 --> M0
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — VAWT Power Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1["Wind Any Direction"] --> V0["Vertical Shaft Rotor Savonius or Darrieus"]
    V0 --> SG0["Direct Drive Shaft No Yaw Needed"]
    SG0 --> G1B["Generator Ground Level Easy Maintenance"]
    G1B --> PC0["Power Electronics"]
    PC0 --> LD0["Local Load or Battery Bank"]
    V0 --> GC0["Ground Mounted Concrete Base"]
    GC0 --> V0
    V0 --> SV0["Savonius Adds Startup Assist to Darrieus if needed"]
    SV0 --> V0
```

## 4.4 Comparison Schematic — Lift vs Drag Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PD["Operating Principle"] --> LP["LIFT BASED Used by HAWT and Darrieus"]
    PD --> DG["DRAG BASED Used by Savonius only"]
    LP --> LP1["Aerofoil shaped blade"]
    LP --> LP2["High TSR 3 to 10"]
    LP --> LP3["High Cp up to 0.48"]
    LP --> LP4["Wind crosses blade at angle"]
    DG --> DG1["Cup or scoop shaped blade"]
    DG --> DG2["Low TSR less than 1"]
    DG --> DG3["Low Cp 0.15 to 0.25"]
    DG --> DG4["Blade always moves slower than wind"]
```

## 4.5 Wind Turbine Anatomy — Component-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    NB["NACELLE Top of Tower"] --> BL["Blades 1 2 or 3"]
    NB --> HB["Hub Rotor Mount"]
    NB --> MS["Main Shaft"]
    NB --> GB["Gearbox Multiplier"]
    NB --> GN["Generator"]
    NB --> YW["Yaw Motor and Bearing"]
    NB --> BK["Hydraulic or Disc Brake"]
    NB --> CN["Controller PLC based"]
    TW["TOWER Lattice or Tubular"] --> NB
    TW --> FN["Foundation Reinforced Concrete"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Aid Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams are rendered as **Block-Level Functional Architecture** flows as per KTU diagram policy. Each node uses clean alphanumeric labels (no `**bold**` inside double-quoted labels) to ensure successful Mermaid compilation.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbine (VAWT) with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | HAWT | VAWT |
|---|---|---|
| **Axis Orientation** | Parallel to wind / ground | Perpendicular to ground |
| **Yaw Mechanism** | Required (must face wind) | Not required (omnidirectional) |
| **Examples** | 3-blade turbine at Muppandal wind farm | Savonius, Darrieus |
| **Efficiency** | High (Cₚ ≈ 0.45) | Low–Moderate (Cₚ ≈ 0.15–0.40) |
| **Maintenance** | Difficult (top of tower) | Easy (ground level) |
| **Self-Starting** | Yes | Savonius yes; Darrieus no |

**(1 mark per correct comparison point × 3 = 3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**Q: Why is a Darrieus rotor not self-starting? How is this drawback overcome in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Darrieus rotor is **not self-starting** because at zero rotor speed, the **angle of attack** of the wind on the curved blades is such that the **aerodynamic lift force passes through the axis of rotation** (zero moment arm). Hence, no net torque is produced at standstill.

**Overcoming the drawback:**
1. **Savonius-Darrieus hybrid** — A small Savonius rotor is placed coaxially with the Darrieus to provide starting torque.
2. **Motor-assist startup** — An external motor spins the rotor up to a self-sustaining speed.
3. **Blade pitch control at standstill** — Tilting blades to a high angle of attack at low speeds.

**(1 mark for reason + 1 mark for hybrid + 1 mark for motor-assist = 3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Classify Wind Energy Conversion Systems (WECS) with a neat block diagram. Explain the working of Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) with single, double and multi-blade configurations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of Savonius rotor. List its merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Classification Overview (2 marks)**
WECS is classified into two main categories:
- **HAWT** — axis parallel to wind direction
- **VAWT** — axis perpendicular to ground

(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for full classification.)

**Step 2: HAWT Working Principle (1 mark)**
Wind strikes the aerofoil-shaped blades, generating **aerodynamic lift**, which produces torque on the hub. The hub drives a gearbox → generator → grid.

**Step 3: Single-Blade HAWT (1.5 marks)**
- One blade + counterweight.
- Cost-effective, lightweight.
- TSR high (8–10), lower cut-in speed.
- **Drawback:** Severe vibration, requires heavy counterweight, rarely used commercially.

**Step 4: Double-Blade HAWT (1.5 marks)**
- Two blades at 180°.
- TSR = 6–8, lighter than 3-blade.
- **Drawback:** Coning required to prevent tower strike; gyroscopic forces during yaw.

**Step 5: Multi-Blade (3-blade) HAWT (1 mark)**
- Industry standard (95% of installed capacity).
- TSR = 5–7, smooth torque, balanced gyroscopic loads.
- Visual symmetry avoids "strobe flicker."

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Construction (2 marks)**
The Savonius rotor consists of **two (or more) half-cylindrical scoops** mounted on a **vertical shaft**. The scoops have a small overlap along the central axis, creating an S-shaped or figure-8 cross-section.

**Step 2: Working Principle (2 marks)**
- When wind blows, the **concave side** of the scoop experiences **high drag**, while the **convex side** experiences **low drag**.
- This **differential drag** creates a net torque about the vertical shaft.
- The rotor spins in the direction of the concave-scoop-leading edge.

**Step 3: Merits (1.5 marks)**
- **Self-starting** in any wind direction.
- **Omnidirectional** — no yaw mechanism.
- Simple construction, low cost.
- **Low noise** — suitable for urban/residential use.

**Step 4: Demerits (1.5 marks)**
- Low power coefficient (Cₚ ≈ 0.15–0.25).
- Low TSR (≤ 1) → blade always slower than wind.
- **Large swept area** for low power output → not suitable for high-power applications.
- Low efficiency.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - June 2022]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and working of **Darrieus rotor**. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages over Savonius rotor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of **60 m**. The wind speed is **10 m/s**, air density is **1.225 kg/m³**, and the power coefficient is **0.42**. Calculate: (i) swept area, (ii) available wind power, (iii) mechanical power extracted, and (iv) verify whether the operation is below the Betz limit. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Construction (2 marks)**
The Darrieus rotor consists of **two or three thin, curved (troposkein-shaped) blades** attached to a **vertical shaft** at top and bottom. The blade profile resembles an **"egg beater"** or **"H" rotor** when viewed from the side.

**Step 2: Working Principle (2 marks)**
- The blades have an **aerofoil (airfoil) cross-section**.
- As wind crosses the rotating blades, **aerodynamic lift** is generated.
- Lift force has a tangential component that produces **rotational torque** about the vertical axis.
- **Wind direction-independent** — the rotor accepts wind from any azimuthal direction.

**Step 3: Advantages over Savonius (1.5 marks)**
- Higher Cₚ (0.30–0.40 vs 0.15–0.25).
- Higher TSR (3–6 vs ≤ 1) → less swept area for same power.
- Better suited for moderate-power applications (1–100 kW).

**Step 4: Disadvantages (1.5 marks)**
- **Not self-starting** (requires Savonius assist or motor).
- More complex blade profile → higher manufacturing cost.
- **Structural fatigue** due to cyclic stress on thin blades.
- Requires **initial push** to reach operating TSR.

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $D = 60$ m, $V = 10$ m/s, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $C_p = 0.42$

**Part (i) — Swept Area (1.5 marks):**

$$A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \times (30)^{2} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Part (ii) — Available Wind Power (2 marks):**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times (10)^{3}$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times 1000$$

$$P_{wind} \approx 1{,}731{,}825 \text{ W} \approx 1.732 \text{ MW}$$

**Part (iii) — Mechanical Power Extracted (2 marks):**

$$P_{mech} = C_{p} \cdot P_{wind} = 0.42 \times 1.732 \approx 0.7274 \text{ MW} \approx 727.4 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (iv) — Betz Limit Verification (1.5 marks):**
- Given $C_p = 0.42$
- Betz Limit $C_{p,max} = 0.5926$
- Since $0.42 < 0.5926$ → **Operation is VALID, below the Betz limit** ✓

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to state the Betz Limit** explicitly when discussing Cₚ values. Always write: *"Since $C_p = X < 0.5926$ (Betz limit), the operation is feasible."*
> 
> 2. **Confusing TSR ranges** — Students often write "Savonius TSR = 3" — this is wrong. **Savonius TSR ≤ 1** (it's a drag device, blade moves slower than wind). Darrieus TSR = 3–6.
> 
> 3. **Claiming Darrieus is self-starting** — This is the #1 conceptual error. **Darrieus is NOT self-starting**; Savonius IS.
> 
> 4. **Missing units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for "W vs kW vs MW" errors.
> 
> 5. **Incomplete classification** — A 7-mark question on "classification of WECS" REQUIRES both HAWT and VAWT sub-categories with diagrams. Mentioning only 3-blade HAWT loses 2–3 marks.
> 
> 6. **Not mentioning yaw mechanism** difference — HAWT needs it; VAWT doesn't. This is a guaranteed 1-mark question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Last-Minute Read Before Exam**

- ☐ WECS = Wind Energy Conversion System — converts kinetic energy of wind to electrical/mechanical energy.
- ☐ Power in wind: $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3}$ — remember the **cubic dependence on V**.
- ☐ Two main classes: **HAWT** (axis parallel to ground) and **VAWT** (axis perpendicular to ground).
- ☐ **HAWT sub-types:** Single, Double, Multi-blade (3-blade = industry standard, 95% market share).
- ☐ **VAWT sub-types:** Savonius (drag-based) and Darrieus (lift-based).
- ☐ **Savonius:** TSR ≤ 1, Cₚ = 0.15–0.25, **SELF-STARTING**, omnidirectional, low efficiency.
- ☐ **Darrieus:** TSR = 3–6, Cₚ = 0.30–0.40, **NOT self-starting**, egg-beater shape, requires assist.
- ☐ **Betz Limit** = **16/27 ≈ 0.5926 (59.26%)** — theoretical maximum power extraction coefficient.
- ☐ **TSR formula:** $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R N}{30 V}$.
- ☐ **Standard air density** ρ = **1.225 kg/m³** at 15°C, sea level.
- ☐ HAWT needs **yaw mechanism**; VAWT does **not**.
- ☐ Darrieus is **not** self-starting → assisted by Savonius hybrid or motor.
- ☐ 3-blade HAWT is industry standard due to **balanced gyroscopic loads and smooth torque**.
- ☐ For KTU numerical: always verify $C_p < 0.5926$ and state the Betz limit check explicitly.
- ☐ Kerala context: **Muppandal, Kanjikode, Ramakkalmedu** — all use 3-blade HAWT technology.
- ☐ Standard formula units: ρ in kg/m³, A in m², V in m/s, P in W.

> **🎯 Exam-Ready Status:** You are now fully equipped to tackle any 3-mark, 7-mark, or 14-mark question on **Classification of WECS — HAWT and VAWT** for the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE examination.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamentals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Solar Energy — The Heart of Module 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Solar Energy Fundamentals* refers to the study of the origin, nature, geometry, measurement, and estimation of solar radiation reaching the Earth's surface. It encompasses the analysis of extraterrestrial radiation, atmospheric attenuation, and the various angles that define the relative position of the Sun with respect to a location on Earth at any given instant.

The Sun is a near-perfect spherical gaseous body, essentially a self-sustaining thermonuclear fusion reactor. It converts approximately **600 million tonnes of hydrogen into helium every second**, releasing energy at an enormous rate. The energy radiated by the Sun, when intercepted at the outer edge of the Earth's atmosphere on a surface perpendicular to the solar beam, is called the **Solar Constant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Solar Constant ($G_{sc}$) = 1367 W/m²** (most widely accepted value as per WMO/ISO 9845 standard). It is the rate at which energy is received on a unit area normal to the Sun's rays at the mean Sun-Earth distance of **1 AU (1.496 × 10⁸ km)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Giant Lamp in the Sky"

Imagine the Sun as an enormous incandescent light bulb hung at the centre of a vast spherical room (the Earth's orbit). 

- The **Solar Constant** is like measuring the brightness of that bulb when the wall of the room is at a fixed distance.
- As the Earth moves in its slightly elliptical orbit, the wall comes closer in January (perihelion) and moves farther in July (aphelion). So the energy received on the wall changes by about ±3.3%.
- The **atmosphere** acts like a dirty glass dome covering the wall. Some sunlight is reflected back (albedo), some is absorbed, and some is scattered — only a portion reaches the ground.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU board exams:** Whenever asked to "define solar constant", always quote the numerical value **1367 W/m²**, the standard surface (normal incidence), and the mean Earth-Sun distance (**1 AU**). Examiners specifically award marks for these three points.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar Constant | $G_{sc}$ | **1367** | W/m² |
| Mean Earth-Sun Distance | $d_m$ | **1.496 × 10⁸** | km |
| Earth's Axial Tilt (Obliquity) | $\varepsilon$ | **23.45°** | degrees |
| Earth's Rotational Angular Velocity | $\omega$ | **15** | deg/hr |
| Mean Albedo of Earth | $\rho$ | **0.30 to 0.35** | dimensionless |
| Speed of Light | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸** | m/s |
| Wavelength Range of Solar Spectrum | $\lambda$ | **0.2 to 3.0** | μm |

---

## 1.4 Nature of Solar Radiation

The Sun emits electromagnetic radiation across a wide spectrum. Approximately **99% of the total emitted energy** lies in the wavelength range of **0.2 μm to 3.0 μm**, with the peak occurring near **0.48 μm** (visible blue-green region). For engineering purposes, solar radiation is classified into three categories:

1. **Direct (Beam) Radiation ($I_b$):** Reaches the surface without being scattered by the atmosphere.
2. **Diffuse Radiation ($I_d$):** Reaches the surface after being scattered by air molecules, dust, and clouds.
3. **Global Radiation ($I$):** The total radiation on a tilted surface, given by:
$$I = I_b + I_d$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Beam Radiation** travels in a straight line from the Sun. **Diffuse Radiation** comes from all directions of the sky. On a clear sunny day at noon, beam radiation dominates (~85-90%), whereas on a cloudy day, diffuse radiation can be 100% of the total.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sun–Earth Geometry — Decoding the "Where is the Sun?" Problem

To estimate the amount of solar energy falling on a surface at a given place and time, we need to know **where the Sun is in the sky**. This is fully described by a set of **Solar Angles**.

### 2.1.1 Solar Declination Angle ($\delta$)

The declination angle is the angular position of the Sun at solar noon with respect to the plane of the equator. It varies between **−23.45° and +23.45°** through the year.

**Why it matters:** The declination is zero at the equinoxes (March 21, Sept 21), positive in the Northern Hemisphere summer, and negative in winter. It is the primary reason we have seasons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooper's Equation (1969) — the standard KTU formula:**
> $$\delta = 23.45 \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$
> where $n$ is the day of the year (Jan 1 = 1, Dec 31 = 365).

### 2.1.2 Hour Angle ($\omega$)

The hour angle describes the angular displacement of the Sun from the local meridian due to Earth's rotation. It is **zero at solar noon**, negative in the morning, and positive in the afternoon.

**Why it matters:** Earth rotates 360° in 24 hours, so 15° per hour. The hour angle is what determines the time of sunrise and sunset.

$$\omega = 15 \times (T_{sol} - 12) \quad \text{[in degrees]}$$

where $T_{sol}$ is the **Solar Time in hours**.

### 2.1.3 Latitude Angle ($\phi$)

The angular location of the observer north (positive) or south (negative) of the equator. For Kerala, $\phi \approx \mathbf{8.5°\ \text{N to } 12.8°\ \text{N}}$.

### 2.1.4 Altitude Angle ($\alpha$) — "How High is the Sun?"

The angle between the Sun's rays and the horizontal plane. It is what we intuitively see as the Sun's height in the sky.

$$\sin \alpha = \sin \phi \cdot \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cdot \cos \delta \cdot \cos \omega$$

### 2.1.5 Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$)

The angle between the Sun's rays and the vertical (normal to the horizontal surface). By definition:

$$\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$$

### 2.1.6 Azimuth Angle ($\gamma_s$) — "Which Compass Direction?"

The angle in the horizontal plane measured from **South (in Northern Hemisphere)** to the projection of the Sun's rays on the horizontal plane. It is positive towards the West.

$$\sin \gamma_s = \frac{\cos \delta \cdot \sin \omega}{\cos \alpha}$$

### 2.1.7 Angle of Incidence ($\theta_i$)

The angle between the Sun's rays and the **normal to a tilted surface**. This is the most important angle for collectors.

> [!CAUTION]
> **Most common KTU mistake:** Confusing $\theta_z$ (angle from vertical) with $\theta_i$ (angle from tilted surface normal). When the surface is horizontal, $\theta_i = \theta_z$. When the surface is tilted, you **must** use the full incidence formula.

---

## 2.2 Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Horizontal Surface

The solar radiation outside the Earth's atmosphere, on a surface normal to the beam, is given by:

$$G_{on} = G_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos\frac{360 \cdot n}{365}\right)$$

The factor $(1 + 0.033 \cos(360n/365))$ accounts for the Earth's elliptical orbit — Earth is closest to the Sun around January 3rd (perihelion) and farthest around July 4th (aphelion).

The extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface (after projecting to the horizontal) is:

$$G_o = G_{on} \cdot \cos \theta_z = G_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cdot \cos\frac{360 \cdot n}{365}\right) \left(\sin \phi \sin \delta + \cos \phi \cos \delta \cos \omega\right)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solar Constant | $G_{sc} = 1367$ | W/m² |
| 2 | Declination (Cooper) | $\delta = 23.45 \sin[\frac{360}{365}(284+n)]$ | degrees, $[-23.45°, +23.45°]$ |
| 3 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15(T_{sol}-12)$ | degrees, $[-180°, +180°]$ |
| 4 | Altitude | $\sin \alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | degrees |
| 5 | Zenith | $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$ | degrees |
| 6 | Azimuth (from S) | $\sin\gamma_s = \frac{\cos\delta \sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$ | degrees |
| 7 | Incidence on tilted surface | $\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi-\beta) \cos\gamma' \cos\omega + \cos\delta \sin\beta \sin\gamma' \sin\omega$ | — |
| 8 | Extraterrestrial normal | $G_{on} = G_{sc}(1+0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365})$ | W/m² |
| 9 | Extraterrestrial horizontal | $G_o = G_{on} \cos\theta_z$ | W/m² |
| 10 | Equation of Time (Spencer) | $B = \frac{360}{365}(n-81)$ (in degrees); $\text{EoT} = 9.87\sin 2B - 7.53\cos B - 1.5\sin B$ | minutes |
| 11 | Local Solar Time | $T_{sol} = T_{local} + \text{EoT} + 4(\text{std meridian} - L_{local}) - D_{st}$ | hours |
| 12 | Day Length | $N = \frac{2}{15}\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$ | hours |
| 13 | Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$ | degrees |
