| 14 | Daily Extraterrestrial Radiation | $H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} G_{sc}\left(1+0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)\left[\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi \omega_s}{360}\sin\phi \sin\delta\right]$ | J/m²/day |
| 15 | Clearness Index | $K_T = \frac{H}{H_o}$ | dimensionless, 0 to 0.75 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU exams:** Memorize formulas **2, 3, 6, 8, 9, and 12** by heart. These six are the most frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) problems.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar PV Plant Design:** Engineers use the declination and hour angle to compute the optimum tilt angle of photovoltaic modules, ensuring maximum annual energy yield.
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP):** Tracking systems use $\theta_i$ to actively orient mirrors so that the sun's rays always strike normal to the collector surface.
- **Building Energy Simulation:** Tools like EnergyPlus, PVsyst, and SAM use these exact equations as their foundational geometry module.
- **Solar Cookers & Water Heaters:** Manufacturers in Kerala (latitude ~10°N) use $\phi \approx 10°$ and adjust $\beta$ to $\phi - 15°$ for winter and $\phi + 15°$ for summer use.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cooper's Declination Angle

### Starting Point — Why does $\delta$ vary?

The Earth's rotational axis is tilted at **23.45°** with respect to the plane of its orbit (the ecliptic). As the Earth orbits the Sun, this tilt causes the Sun's apparent position to oscillate above and below the equatorial plane.

### Step 1: Define the angular position in the orbit

The day of the year $n$ (1 to 365) can be converted to an angular position in the orbit:

$$\theta_{orbit} = \frac{360}{365}(n - 1) \quad \text{[in degrees]}$$

### Step 2: Identify the zero-crossing

The declination is **zero at the spring equinox** (around March 21). The spring equinox occurs on approximately day $n = 81$. So we shift the argument by 81 days to make the sine function zero at the equinox.

### Step 3: Apply the maximum tilt amplitude

The amplitude of variation in declination equals the Earth's axial tilt, 23.45°. Therefore:

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left(\theta_{orbit}^{shifted}\right)$$

### Step 4: Substitute and simplify

Substituting the shifted angular position:

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n - 1) - \frac{360}{365}(81 - 1)\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n - 81)\right]$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \cdot \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(n + 284)\right]$$

**This is Cooper's formula**, since $(n-1) - 80 = n - 81$ and $(n-1) - 80 = n + 284 - 365$, both are equivalent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic check:** For $n = 81$ (March 21 equinox), the argument becomes $\frac{360}{365}(81+284) = \frac{360}{365} \times 365 = 360°$, and $\sin 360° = 0$. Hence $\delta = 0$ ✓.
> For $n = 172$ (June 21 summer solstice), the argument becomes $\frac{360}{365}(172+284) = \frac{360}{365} \times 456 = 449.7° \approx 89.7°$, and $\sin 89.7° \approx 1$. Hence $\delta \approx +23.45°$ ✓.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Angle of Incidence on a Tilted Surface

### Step 1: Define coordinate systems

Let us set up two reference frames:
- **Equatorial (terrestrial) frame:** Origin at the observer. $z$-axis points to the zenith, $x$-axis to the South, $y$-axis to the East.
- **Surface frame:** Origin at the observer. $z'$-axis is the normal to the tilted surface.

### Step 2: Express the Sun vector in the equatorial frame

The unit vector pointing from the observer to the Sun:

$$\vec{S} = (\cos\alpha \sin\gamma_s,\ \cos\alpha \cos\gamma_s,\ \sin\alpha)$$

### Step 3: Express the surface normal in the equatorial frame

For a surface tilted at angle $\beta$ from the horizontal, with surface azimuth $\gamma$ (measured from South, positive to West), the normal is:

$$\vec{n} = (\sin\beta \cos\gamma,\ -\sin\beta \sin\gamma,\ \cos\beta)$$

### Step 4: Compute the dot product

The angle of incidence $\theta_i$ is defined as the angle between $\vec{S}$ and $\vec{n}$:

$$\cos\theta_i = \vec{S} \cdot \vec{n}$$

$$\cos\theta_i = \cos\alpha \sin\gamma_s \sin\beta \cos\gamma - \cos\alpha \cos\gamma_s \sin\beta \sin\gamma + \sin\alpha \cos\beta$$

### Step 5: Substitute $\alpha$ and $\gamma_s$ from the spherical-trigonometric identities

Using:
- $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$
- $\cos\alpha \sin\gamma_s = -\cos\delta \sin\omega$ (with sign convention for South-positive)
- $\cos\alpha \cos\gamma_s = \sin\delta \cos\phi - \cos\delta \sin\phi \cos\omega$

Substituting all three into the dot product:

$$\cos\theta_i = (-\cos\delta \sin\omega)(\sin\beta \cos\gamma) - (\sin\delta \cos\phi - \cos\delta \sin\phi \cos\omega)(\sin\beta \sin\gamma) + (\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega)(\cos\beta)$$

### Step 6: Group terms by $\sin\delta$ and $\cos\delta$

After careful algebraic expansion and regrouping:

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta_i =\ & \sin\delta \sin\phi \cos\beta \\
& - \sin\delta \cos\phi \sin\beta \sin\gamma \\
& + \cos\delta \cos\phi \cos\beta \cos\omega \\
& + \cos\delta \sin\phi \sin\beta \cos\gamma \cos\omega \\
& - \cos\delta \sin\beta \cos\gamma \sin\omega
\end{aligned}}$$

This is the **complete incidence angle formula** used in all KTU derivations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case 1 (Horizontal surface, $\beta = 0$):** All $\sin\beta$ terms vanish, leaving $\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin\phi + \cos\delta \cos\phi \cos\omega = \sin\alpha = \cos\theta_z$ ✓
>
> **Special Case 2 (South-facing surface, $\gamma = 0$):** All $\sin\gamma$ terms vanish, simplifying to:
> $$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\omega$$
> This is the **most-tested form** in KTU 14-mark problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Day Length and Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle

The Sun rises when $\alpha = 0$ (Sun is on the horizon). Setting the altitude equation to zero:

$$\sin\alpha = 0 = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s$$

### Step 1: Solve for $\cos\omega_s$

$$\cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega_s = -\sin\phi \sin\delta$$

$$\cos\omega_s = -\frac{\sin\phi \sin\delta}{\cos\phi \cos\delta} = -\tan\phi \tan\delta$$

### Step 2: Solve for $\omega_s$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta) \quad \text{[in degrees]}$$

### Step 3: Compute day length

Since the Sun moves 15° per hour and crosses the meridian at noon, the total day length is twice the time from sunrise to noon:

$$N = \frac{2 \cdot \omega_s}{15} = \frac{2}{15}\cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta) \quad \text{[in hours]}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick fact for Kerala (Trivandrum, $\phi = 8.5°$):** Day length varies only between approximately **11.5 hours and 12.5 hours** through the year — much less variation than at higher latitudes, which is why Kerala enjoys very stable solar insolation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Equation of Time (Spencer's Formula)

The Earth orbits the Sun in an **ellipse**, and the orbit is tilted by about 1.5° from the plane of the equator. The combined effect causes the **apparent solar day** to differ from the **mean solar day** by up to ±16 minutes.

Define the day angle:

$$B = \frac{360}{365}(n - 81) \quad \text{[in degrees]}$$

The Equation of Time, in **minutes**, is given by Spencer's series:

$$\boxed{\text{EoT} = 9.87 \sin(2B) - 7.53 \cos B - 1.5 \sin B}$$

This correction is essential for converting standard clock time to local solar time before computing the hour angle.

$$T_{sol} = T_{clock} + \text{EoT} + 4 \cdot (\text{Standard Meridian} - L_{local}) - D_{st}$$

The factor **4 minutes per degree** comes from the fact that Earth rotates 1° in 4 minutes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Sun–Earth Coordinate System

The following diagram maps the geometric relationship between the major solar angles defined in Section 2.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[SUN POSITION IN SKY] --> B[Solar Declination DELTA]
    A --> C[Hour Angle OMEGA]
    A --> D[Altitude Angle ALPHA]
    A --> E[Azimuth Angle GAMMA_S]
    A --> F[Zenith Angle THETA_Z]

    B --> G[Depends on Day of Year n]
    C --> G
    C --> H[Time of Day T_SOL]

    D --> I[Sun HEIGHT above horizon]
    F --> I

    D --> J[Receiver SURFACE NORMAL]
    E --> J
    F --> K[Horizontal Surface Reference]
    J --> L[ANGLE OF INCIDENCE THETA_I]
    K --> L

    G --> M[COOPER FORMULA]
    H --> N[15 deg per hour]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style L fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Solar Radiation Path Through the Atmosphere

This schematic shows how the solar constant gets attenuated as it passes through the atmosphere before reaching a tilted collector.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[SUN - Source of Radiation] -->|G_sc equals 1367 W per m2| B[Outer Atmosphere TOA]

    B --> C{Solar Constant Normal G_on}
    C -->|Geometric projection| D[Extraterrestrial Horizontal G_o]

    B -->|Atmospheric Attenuation| E[Atmospheric Effects]
    E --> E1[Scattering by air molecules]
    E --> E2[Absorption by O3 H2O CO2]
    E --> E3[Reflection by clouds]

    E1 --> F[Diffuse Radiation I_d]
    E2 --> G[Lost to space]
    E3 --> G

    F --> H[Tilted Collector Surface]
    C -->|Direct Beam| I[Direct Beam Radiation I_b]
    I --> H

    H --> J[Global Radiation on Surface I equals I_b plus I_d]
    G --> K[Albedo Reflection back to space]
    D -.->|Comparison reference| L[Ground Level Insolation]

    style A fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style J fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style G fill:#808080,stroke:#FFF,color:#FFF
    style L fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture for Solar Radiation Measurement

For topics that involve physical instrument layouts, we use a functional block flow as a substitute for line drawings.

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[Sunlight Incident] --> SENSOR1[Pyranometer - Global Horizontal]
    INPUT --> SENSOR2[Pyrheliometer - Direct Normal via SHADOW BAND]
    INPUT --> SENSOR3[Shaded Pyranometer - Diffuse Only]

    SENSOR1 --> AMP1[Signal Amplifier]
    SENSOR2 --> AMP2[Signal Amplifier]
    SENSOR3 --> AMP3[Signal Amplifier]

    AMP1 --> ADC1[Analog to Digital Converter]
    AMP2 --> ADC2[Analog to Digital Converter]
    AMP3 --> ADC3[Analog to Digital Converter]

    ADC1 --> LOGGER[Data Logger - 1 minute average]
    ADC2 --> LOGGER
    ADC3 --> LOGGER

    LOGGER --> CALC[Processor - Calculates K_T, H_o, I_b, I_d]
    CALC --> DISPLAY[Digital Display Monitor]
    CALC --> STORAGE[SD Card Storage]
    CALC --> NETWORK[Cloud Server Upload]

    style INPUT fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style LOGGER fill:#4169E1,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style CALC fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style NETWORK fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Classification of Solar Angles

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((SOLAR ANGLES))
    TIME BASED
      Declination DELTA
      Hour Angle OMEGA
      Equation of Time
    OBSERVER BASED
      Latitude PHI
      Longitude L
    SUN POSITION
      Altitude ALPHA
      Zenith THETA_Z
      Azimuth GAMMA_S
    SURFACE BASED
      Tilt BETA
      Surface Azimuth GAMMA
      Angle of Incidence THETA_I
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Solar Constant. Mention its standard value. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The Solar Constant is defined as the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area on a surface placed **perpendicular to the Sun's rays** at the **outer edge of the Earth's atmosphere**, at the **mean Earth–Sun distance** of one astronomical unit (1 AU = 1.496 × 10⁸ km).

**Standard value:** $G_{sc} = \mathbf{1367\ W/m^2}$ (as per the World Meteorological Organization).

> [!NOTE]
> The value varies by about ±1–2% over the 11-year solar cycle, but for KTU exam purposes, **1367 W/m²** is the accepted figure.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between Beam, Diffuse, and Global Solar Radiation. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Beam Radiation ($I_b$) | Diffuse Radiation ($I_d$) | Global Radiation ($I$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Solar radiation that reaches the Earth's surface **directly from the Sun** without scattering | Solar radiation that reaches the surface **after being scattered** by atmosphere, clouds, dust | Total radiation on a surface (sum of beam + diffuse) |
| **Direction** | Unidirectional (parallel rays) | Comes from **all sky directions** | Combination of both |
| **Dominance** | Clear sky, midday | Cloudy sky, sunrise/sunset | Throughout the day |
| **Magnitude (noon, clear)** | ~85–90% of total | ~10–15% of total | 100% of total |

Mathematical relation: $\boxed{I = I_b + I_d}$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, an internal choice is mandatory.** Candidates must answer **either Question A or Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain in detail the various solar angles used in solar energy estimation. Derive the equation for the angle of incidence on a tilted surface. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

The various solar angles are:

1. **Declination angle ($\delta$):** Angular position of the Sun at solar noon with respect to the equatorial plane. Varies from −23.45° to +23.45°.

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

2. **Hour angle ($\omega$):** Angular displacement of the Sun from the local meridian. $\omega = 15(T_{sol} - 12)$.

3. **Latitude ($\phi$):** Angular location of the observer north/south of the equator.

4. **Altitude angle ($\alpha$):** Angle of the Sun above the horizontal.

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

5. **Zenith angle ($\theta_z$):** Angle of the Sun from the vertical. $\theta_z = 90° - \alpha$.

6. **Azimuth angle ($\gamma_s$):** Compass direction of the Sun, measured from South.

$$\sin\gamma_s = \frac{\cos\delta \sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$$

7. **Surface tilt angle ($\beta$):** Angle of the surface from the horizontal.

8. **Surface azimuth angle ($\gamma$):** Compass direction the surface faces, from South.

9. **Angle of incidence ($\theta_i$):** Angle between Sun's rays and the normal to the tilted surface.

**Derivation of Incidence Angle (as in Section 3.2, Steps 1–6):**

The Sun vector in the equatorial frame is:

$$\vec{S} = (\cos\alpha \sin\gamma_s,\ \cos\alpha \cos\gamma_s,\ \sin\alpha)$$

The surface normal vector in the equatorial frame:

$$\vec{n} = (\sin\beta \cos\gamma,\ -\sin\beta \sin\gamma,\ \cos\beta)$$

The angle between the two vectors:

$$\cos\theta_i = \vec{S} \cdot \vec{n}$$

$$\cos\theta_i = \cos\alpha \sin\gamma_s \sin\beta \cos\gamma - \cos\alpha \cos\gamma_s \sin\beta \sin\gamma + \sin\alpha \cos\beta$$

Substituting the identities for $\sin\alpha$, $\cos\alpha \sin\gamma_s$, and $\cos\alpha \cos\gamma_s$:

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
\cos\theta_i =\ & \sin\delta \sin\phi \cos\beta \\
& - \sin\delta \cos\phi \sin\beta \sin\gamma \\
& + \cos\delta \cos\phi \cos\beta \cos\omega \\
& + \cos\delta \sin\phi \sin\beta \cos\gamma \cos\omega \\
& - \cos\delta \sin\beta \cos\gamma \sin\omega
\end{aligned}}$$

#### (b) A solar collector is located at Trivandrum (8.5°N) on March 21. The surface is tilted at 15° from the horizontal and faces due South. Calculate the angle of incidence at 10:00 AM local solar time. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Given:**
- $\phi = 8.5°$
- Date: March 21 → $n = 80$
- $\beta = 15°$, $\gamma = 0°$ (South-facing)
- $T_{sol} = 10:00$ AM

**Step 1: Compute declination**

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 80)\right] = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360 \times 364}{365}\right] = 23.45 \sin(358.85°) = 23.45 \times (-0.0205) \approx -0.48° \approx 0°$$

(The spring equinox on March 21 gives $\delta \approx 0°$.)

**Step 2: Compute hour angle**

$$\omega = 15 \times (10 - 12) = 15 \times (-2) = -30°$$

(Negative indicates morning, as expected.)

**Step 3: Apply the south-facing incidence formula**

For $\gamma = 0$:

$$\cos\theta_i = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega$$

Substituting $\delta \approx 0°$:

$$\cos\theta_i = 0 + (1) \cdot \cos(8.5° - 15°)\cos(-30°)$$

$$\cos\theta_i = \cos(-6.5°) \cdot \cos(-30°)$$

$$\cos\theta_i = 0.9936 \times 0.8660 = 0.8605$$

**Step 4: Compute $\theta_i$**

$$\theta_i = \cos^{-1}(0.8605) = \mathbf{30.65°}$$

**Final Answer:** The angle of incidence at 10:00 AM local solar time on March 21 is approximately **30.65°**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Pyranometer and a Pyrheliometer. How are they used to measure global, beam, and diffuse radiation? (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Pyranometer:**

A pyranometer is an instrument used to measure the **total (global) solar radiation** falling on a horizontal surface. It uses a **thermopile** (a series of thermocouples) as the sensing element. The hot junctions are coated with **black absorbent paint**, and the cold junctions are shielded and kept at ambient temperature. The temperature difference between the hot and cold junctions generates a voltage proportional to the incident radiation.

A **glass dome** protects the thermopile from wind and convective losses while allowing solar radiation (0.3–3 μm) to pass through.

- With the **dome present and surface horizontal** → measures **global radiation** ($I = I_b + I_d$).
- With the **dome present and a shadow band/ring** blocking the Sun → measures **diffuse radiation** ($I_d$).
- The beam component is then computed as: $I_b = I - I_d$.

**2. Pyrheliometer:**

A pyrheliometer measures the **direct (beam) solar radiation** at normal incidence. It consists of a thermopile mounted inside a long tube with a narrow aperture, allowing **only the direct rays from the Sun** (within a half-angle of about 2.5° to 5°) to reach the sensor. The instrument is mounted on a **sun tracker** so that it continuously points at the Sun.

**Working Principle:** Same thermopile principle as pyranometer. The voltage output is calibrated in W/m².

**3. Computation:**

$$\boxed{I_b = I - I_d}$$

where:
- $I$ is measured by the pyranometer (no shadow band).
- $I_d$ is measured by the pyranometer with a shadow band/ring.

This indirect method is preferred because pyrheliometers require expensive sun-tracking mounts and are not always available.

#### (b) Define Clearness Index and Diffuse Fraction. Estimate the daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface at Trivandrum (8.5°N) on April 1. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Given:**
- $\phi = 8.5°$
- Date: April 1 → $n = 91$

**Definitions (2 marks):**

- **Clearness Index ($K_T$):** The ratio of the **monthly average daily global radiation** on a horizontal surface ($H$) to the **monthly average daily extraterrestrial radiation** on a horizontal surface ($H_o$).
$$K_T = \frac{H}{H_o}$$

$K_T$ ranges from 0 (totally cloudy) to about 0.75 (very clear sky in arid regions).

- **Diffuse Fraction ($K_d$):** The ratio of the **daily diffuse radiation** ($H_d$) to the **daily global radiation** ($H$) on a horizontal surface.
$$K_d = \frac{H_d}{H}$$

**Step 1: Compute declination**

$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 91)\right] = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360 \times 375}{365}\right] = 23.45 \sin(369.86°)$$

$$369.86° - 360° = 9.86°, \quad \sin(9.86°) = 0.1712$$

$$\delta = 23.45 \times 0.1712 = 4.015°$$

**Step 2: Compute sunset hour angle**

$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta = -\tan(8.5°) \tan(4.015°)$$

$$\tan(8.5°) = 0.1493, \quad \tan(4.015°) = 0.0702$$

$$\cos\omega_s = -(0.1493)(0.0702) = -0.01048$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.01048) = 90.6°$$

**Step 3: Compute daily extraterrestrial radiation**

$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} G_{sc}\left(1+0.033\cos\frac{360n}{365}\right)\left[\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi \omega_s}{360}\sin\phi \sin\delta\right]$$

Compute the correction factor:
$$1 + 0.033\cos\left(\frac{360 \times 91}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(89.75°) = 1 + 0.033 \times 0.00218 = 1.0001 \approx 1.000$$

Compute $\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s$:
$$\cos(8.5°)\cos(4.015°)\sin(90.6°) = 0.9888 \times 0.9975 \times 1.0000 \approx 0.9863$$

Compute $\frac{2\pi \omega_s}{360} \sin\phi \sin\delta$:
$$\frac{2\pi \times 90.6}{360} \sin(8.5°)\sin(4.015°) = (1.581) \times 0.1478 \times 0.0700 = 0.01635$$

Sum in brackets:
$$0.9863 + 0.01635 = 1.00265$$

Now:
$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \times 1367 \times 1.000 \times 1.00265$$

$$H_o = 27544.4 \times 1367 \times 1.00265 = 3.766 \times 10^7 \ \text{J/m}^2\text{/day}$$

Convert to MJ/m²/day:
$$H_o \approx \mathbf{37.66\ MJ/m^2/day} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad 10.46\ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/day}$$

**Final Answer:** The daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface at Trivandrum on April 1 is approximately **37.66 MJ/m²/day**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in Module 2 "Fundamentals":**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Hour Angle:** Forgetting that $\omega$ is **negative before solar noon** and **positive after solar noon**. Many students incorrectly treat it as always positive.
> 2. **Using 360 vs 365 days:** Some use 360 in the denominator. The correct divisor is **365.25** (or 365 with Cooper's formula). Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per incorrect usage.
> 3. **Wrong declination sign:** Forgetting to apply the +284 shift in Cooper's formula. This shifts the curve so that $\delta = 0$ on March 21.
> 4. **Confusing $\theta_i$ with $\theta_z$:** When the surface is tilted, the angle of incidence is **NOT** the zenith angle. You must use the full incidence formula with $\beta$ and $\gamma$ terms.
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units:** The declination formula gives degrees if you put 360, but **radians** if you put $2\pi$. KTU uses the **360 version** — be consistent.
> 6. **Solar time vs Standard time:** Failing to apply the Equation of Time correction. For 14-mark numerical problems, this can shift the hour angle by 1–2 hours, giving completely wrong answers.
> 7. **Direction of Azimuth:** KTU uses the convention where azimuth is measured **from South, positive to West**. Some textbooks use from North. State the convention explicitly in the answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Solar Constant** = **1367 W/m²** at **1 AU**, normal incidence. (Memorize verbatim.)
- **Cooper's Declination:** $\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$. Always add 284, not 81.
- **Hour Angle:** $\omega = 15(T_{sol} - 12)$ — negative in morning, zero at noon, positive in afternoon.
- **Altitude–Zenith Pair:** $\alpha + \theta_z = 90°$. Use $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$.
- **Incidence Angle (Tilted Surface):** Always use the full 5-term formula. The 3-term version applies **only** for south-facing surfaces.
- **Sunrise/Sunset:** $\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta$. Day length is $\frac{2\omega_s}{15}$ hours.
- **Global = Beam + Diffuse:** $I = I_b + I_d$. A pyranometer measures $I$ alone, and with a shadow band, it measures $I_d$.
- **Pyrheliometer** needs a **sun tracker** and measures only $I_b$ at normal incidence.
- **Clearness Index $K_T$** lies between 0 and ~0.75. It is the most important metric for assessing solar resource at a site.
- **Clearness vs Diffuse Fraction:** $K_T$ decreases on cloudy days → $K_d$ increases (more diffuse).
- **Equation of Time** can shift the apparent solar noon from 12:00 clock time by **up to ±16 minutes** — this is critical for solar tracker design.
- **Earth's orbital eccentricity correction:** $(1 + 0.033 \cos(360n/365))$ — peaks at perihelion (Jan 3), minimum at aphelion (Jul 4).
- **For Kerala ($\phi \approx 10°$ N):** The day length variation is very small (~11.5–12.5 h), and the Sun stays close to the zenith year-round — ideal for solar energy harvesting.
- **For KTU numerical problems,** always show **all intermediate steps**. Marks are awarded step-by-step, not just for the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solar Radiation** refers to the electromagnetic energy emitted by the Sun that propagates through space and reaches the Earth's atmosphere and surface. It is the primary source of nearly all renewable energy systems on Earth (excluding tidal and geothermal). Quantitatively, it is measured as irradiance (W/m²) or insolation (Wh/m² or MJ/m²).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEET613 - Renewable Energy Systems)**, solar radiation is rigorously classified into three primary components reaching a terrestrial surface:

1. **Beam (Direct) Radiation ($I_b$):** Solar radiation received from the Sun without having been scattered by the atmosphere; travels in a straight line from the solar disk.
2. **Diffuse Radiation ($I_d$):** Solar radiation received from the Sun after being scattered by atmospheric molecules, aerosols, and clouds; arrives from all directions of the sky dome.
3. **Ground-Reflected Radiation ($I_r$):** Solar radiation reflected from the ground surface onto a tilted receiver.

The total radiation incident on a surface is termed **Global Radiation ($G$)**, which is the sum of beam, diffuse, and (if tilted) reflected components:

$$G = I_b + I_d + I_r$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The *Solar Constant* ($G_{sc}$) and *Solar Geometry* (declination, hour angle, altitude, zenith, azimuth) form the foundational kernel for Module 2. Any numerical problem on solar energy systems MUST begin with these parameters.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

**Analogy — The Cosmic Bonfire and Earth's Tilted Umbrella:**

Imagine the Sun as a giant, continuously burning bonfire located 149.6 million km away. The heat (radiation) traveling from this bonfire hits the Earth's atmosphere. Now imagine holding an umbrella (a solar collector/PV panel) — the amount of heat you catch depends on:

- **How big the bonfire appears** (depends on the Earth-Sun distance → varies with the day of the year).
- **The angle at which you hold the umbrella** (depends on the time of day, season, and your latitude → *solar geometry*).
- **How thick the smoke is between you and the bonfire** (depends on atmospheric path length, clouds, dust → *air mass and atmospheric attenuation*).

If you hold the umbrella flat (parallel to the ground), the heat is most concentrated at noon when the Sun is directly overhead. Tilt the umbrella to face the Sun, and you catch more beam radiation. Hold it horizontally, and you catch diffuse radiation from the entire sky plus any ground reflection.

> [!NOTE]
> The Earth receives only about **1.74 × 10¹⁷ W** of solar power — this is more than the entire instantaneous power consumption of human civilization by ~10,000 times. Even capturing a tiny fraction of this in Kerala's latitude (~8°–12° N) makes solar energy highly viable.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Solar Constant ($G_{sc}$):** **1367 W/m²** — the rate at which solar energy strikes a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the outer edge of the Earth's atmosphere, at the mean Earth-Sun distance.
- **Mean Earth-Sun Distance:** **1 AU = 1.496 × 10¹¹ m**
- **Solar Spectral Peak:** ~0.48 μm (visible green-yellow region)
- **Solar Disc Angular Diameter:** ~0.53°
- **Standard AM (Air Mass) Spectrum:** **AM 1.5** = Sun at 48.2° from zenith; defines the standard test condition for PV modules (1000 W/m²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ unless a problem explicitly states otherwise. The WMO 1981 value of 1361 W/m² may also be accepted but is less common in KTU answer keys.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solar Geometry — The Sun's Apparent Position

The Sun's position relative to an observer at any location on Earth is defined by a set of angles. Mastering these is essential because *every solar energy calculation* begins with them.

### 2.1.1 Declination Angle ($\delta$)
The angular position of the Sun at solar noon with respect to the equatorial plane. It varies from −23.45° to +23.45° throughout the year.

**Cooper's Equation (Most commonly used in KTU):**

$$\delta = 23.45^\circ \times \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right]$$

where $n$ = day of the year (1 = Jan 1, 365 = Dec 31).

### 2.1.2 Hour Angle ($\omega$)
The angular displacement of the Sun from the local meridian due to Earth's rotation. **+15° per hour** before solar noon, **−15° per hour** after solar noon.

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (T_{solar} - 12)$$

where $T_{solar}$ is the solar time in hours.

### 2.1.3 Latitude ($\phi$)
Angular location north (+) or south (−) of the equator. For Kerala: $\phi \approx 8^\circ$ to $12^\circ$ N.

### 2.1.4 Altitude Angle ($\alpha$)
The angle between the horizontal and the line to the Sun.

$$\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

### 2.1.5 Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$)
Angle from the vertical (zenith) to the Sun. By definition: $\theta_z + \alpha = 90^\circ$.

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

### 2.1.6 Azimuth Angle ($\gamma$)
The angle measured from North to the projection of the Sun's rays on the horizontal plane (positive clockwise). For KTU, the standard convention:

$$\sin\gamma = \frac{\cos\delta \sin\omega}{\cos\alpha}$$

$$\cos\gamma = \frac{\sin\alpha \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha \cos\phi}$$

### 2.1.7 Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle ($\omega_s$)
Setting $\alpha = 0$ in the zenith equation:

$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan\phi \tan\delta$$

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$$

### 2.1.8 Solar Time ($T_{solar}$)

$$T_{solar} = T_{standard} + 4(L_{st} - L_{loc}) + E_o \text{ (minutes)}$$

where:
- $L_{st}$ = standard meridian for the time zone (e.g., 82.5° E for IST)
- $L_{loc}$ = local longitude
- $E_o$ = **Equation of Time correction** in minutes:

$$E_o = 9.87\sin(2B) - 7.53\cos(B) - 1.5\sin(B)$$

with $B = \frac{360}{365}(n - 81)$ (in degrees).

## 2.2 Air Mass (AM)

The ratio of the path length of solar rays through the atmosphere to the path length if the Sun were at the zenith.

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z}$$

At sea level with Sun at zenith, $AM = 1$. At $\theta_z = 48.2°$, $AM = 1.5$ (the standard PV testing condition).

A more rigorous empirical form (Kasten & Young 1989):

$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos\theta_z + a(90^\circ - \theta_z + b)^{-c}}$$

with $a = 0.50572$, $b = 6.07995°$, $c = 1.6364$.

## 2.3 Extraterrestrial Radiation ($H_o$)

Daily extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface:

$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600 \times G_{sc}}{\pi} \left[1 + 0.033\cos\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)\right] \times \left[\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{2\pi \omega_s}{360}\sin\phi \sin\delta\right]$$

Units: J/m²/day. The first bracket represents the eccentricity correction ($E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$).

## 2.4 Beam Radiation on a Tilted Surface

The **tilt factor** $R_b$ relates beam radiation on a tilted surface to that on a horizontal surface:

$$R_b = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z}$$

where $\theta$ is the **angle of incidence** on the tilted surface:

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\omega$$

For a south-facing surface in the Northern Hemisphere at tilt angle $\beta$.

**Total radiation on a tilted surface:**

$$I_T = I_b R_b + I_d \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) + \rho I \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

where $\rho$ is the ground reflectivity (typically 0.2 for grass/soil).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **#** | **Parameter / Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Remarks** |
|:-----:|:-------------------------|:------------|:---------------------|
| 1 | Solar Constant | $G_{sc} = 1367$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 2 | Eccentricity Factor | $E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365)$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Declination Angle | $\delta = 23.45 \sin[360(284+n)/365]$ | degrees |
| 4 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15(T_{solar} - 12)$ | degrees |
| 5 | Sunrise/Sunset Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$ | degrees |
| 6 | Altitude Angle | $\sin\alpha = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$ | degrees |
| 7 | Zenith Angle | $\theta_z = 90^\circ - \alpha$ | degrees |
| 8 | Azimuth Angle | $\sin\gamma = \cos\delta \sin\omega / \cos\alpha$ | degrees |
| 9 | Air Mass | $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Equation of Time | $E_o = 9.87\sin(2B) - 7.53\cos B - 1.5\sin B$ | minutes |
| 11 | Daily Extraterrestrial $H_o$ | $\frac{24 \cdot 3600 \cdot G_{sc}}{\pi} E_o [\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + (\pi\omega_s/180)\sin\phi\sin\delta]$ | $\text{J/m}^2/\text{day}$ |
| 12 | Angle of Incidence (tilted) | $\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\omega$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Tilt Factor | $R_b = \cos\theta / \cos\theta_z$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Global Radiation (tilted) | $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d (1+\cos\beta)/2 + \rho I (1-\cos\beta)/2$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| 15 | Clear-Sky Beam Model | $I_b = A \cdot e^{-k \cdot AM}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ (Angstrom) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Utility:** Every grid-tied solar PV plant in Kerala (latitude ~10° N) is designed using these geometric relations. The optimal **tilt angle** for annual energy maximization in Kerala is approximately $\beta_{opt} \approx \phi - 10°$ (slightly less than latitude to account for monsoon diffuse radiation dominance).

## 2.6 Clear-Sky Radiation Models (For Examiner Reference)

**Hottel's Model (for beam clear-sky):**

$$I_{b,clear} = G_{sc} \cdot E_o \cdot 0.91^{(AM)^{0.758}}$$

**Liu & Jordan Model:**

$$\frac{I_d}{I} = 0.271 - 0.2939 \cdot \frac{I_b}{I_o}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hour Angle and Time Relationship

**Starting Premise:** The Earth rotates 360° in 24 hours, so it rotates at:

$$\text{Rate} = \frac{360^\circ}{24 \text{ h}} = 15^\circ/\text{hour}$$

**At Solar Noon:** The Sun crosses the local meridian → $\omega = 0°$.

**At Sunrise/Sunset:** $\omega = \pm \omega_s$.

**General Form:**

$$\omega = 15^\circ \times (T_{solar} - 12)$$

This derivation is fundamental. Memorize the **+15°/hr** convention — KTU examiners often test this with a simple time-to-angle conversion.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cosine of Zenith Angle from First Principles

**Setup:** Consider a 3D coordinate system with origin at the observer. The Sun's position vector makes angle $\theta_z$ with the zenith (local vertical).

**Using the dot product** between the Sun's unit vector $\hat{S}$ and the zenith unit vector $\hat{Z} = (0,0,1)$:

$$\cos\theta_z = \hat{S} \cdot \hat{Z}$$

The Sun's unit vector in terms of latitude $\phi$, declination $\delta$, and hour angle $\omega$ can be decomposed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_x &= \cos\delta \cos\omega \sin\phi - \sin\delta \cos\phi \\
S_y &= -\cos\delta \sin\omega \\
S_z &= \sin\delta \sin\phi + \cos\delta \cos\phi \cos\omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the zenith is along +z:

$$\cos\theta_z = S_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

**Step-by-Step Conversion Logic:**
1. The Sun's apparent position on the celestial sphere depends on Earth's rotation (hour angle $\omega$), Earth's tilt (declination $\delta$), and observer's location (latitude $\phi$).
2. The unit vector pointing TO the Sun has components derived from spherical trigonometry.
3. The z-component (vertical) equals $\cos\theta_z$ because zenith = +z direction.
4. Substituting yields the standard **cosine zenith angle** identity used universally.

## 3.3 Derivation: Extraterrestrial Daily Radiation $H_o$ (Full Derivation)

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Extraterrestrial Radiation on a Surface Normal to Sun's Rays:**

At a given day $n$, the Earth-Sun distance correction gives:

$$G_{on} = G_{sc} \cdot E_o = G_{sc}\left[1 + 0.033\cos\left(\frac{360n}{365}\right)\right]$$

This is the radiation on a surface *perpendicular* to the solar beam.

**Step 2 — Project onto a Horizontal Surface:**

The horizontal component is $G_{on} \cos\theta_z$:

$$G_o = G_{sc} \cdot E_o \cdot \cos\theta_z$$

Substituting $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$:

$$G_o = G_{sc} \cdot E_o (\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Over a Day:**

The hourly radiation is integrated from sunrise ($\omega = -\omega_s$) to sunset ($\omega = +\omega_s$). Since $dt = d\omega / 15$ (in hours), and 1 hour = 3600 seconds:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_o &= \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} G_o \cdot \frac{3600}{15} \, d\omega \\
&= \frac{3600 \cdot G_{sc} \cdot E_o}{15} \int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} (\sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega) \, d\omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \sin\phi \sin\delta \, d\omega &= 2\omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta \\
\int_{-\omega_s}^{+\omega_s} \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega \, d\omega &= 2\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s
\end{aligned}
$$

(Since $\cos\omega$ is an even function, its integral is symmetric.)

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_o &= \frac{3600 \cdot G_{sc} \cdot E_o}{15} \left[2\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + 2\omega_s \sin\phi \sin\delta\right] \\
&= \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \cdot G_{sc} \cdot E_o \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

(Note: $\frac{2 \times 3600}{15} = 480 \text{ s/deg} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{2\pi} \text{ s/rad}$)

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600 \cdot G_{sc}}{\pi} \cdot E_o \left[\cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi\omega_s}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta\right]}$$

Units: **J/m²/day**. To convert to kWh/m²/day, divide by **3.6 × 10⁶**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Find the declination, sunset hour angle, and daily extraterrestrial radiation $H_o$ on a horizontal surface on **March 21** ($n = 80$) at a location with latitude $\phi = 10°$ N.

**Step 1 — Day number:** $n = 31 (\text{Jan}) + 28 (\text{Feb}) + 21 = 80$ ✓

**Step 2 — Declination:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 80)\right] \\
&= 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360 \times 364}{365}\right] \\
&= 23.45 \sin[358.85^\circ] \\
&= 23.45 \times (-0.01920) \\
&\approx -0.45^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

(Negligible — March 21 is the **Spring Equinox**, so $\delta \approx 0$ as expected.)

**Step 3 — Sunset Hour Angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega_s &= \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta) \\
&= \cos^{-1}[-\tan(10^\circ) \times \tan(-0.45^\circ)] \\
&= \cos^{-1}[-0.1763 \times (-0.00785)] \\
&= \cos^{-1}[0.001385] \\
&\approx 89.92^\circ \approx 90^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

So at the equinox, daylight lasts **12 hours** at all latitudes. ✓

**Step 4 — Eccentricity Factor:**

$$E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos\left(\frac{360 \times 80}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(78.9^\circ) = 1 + 0.033(0.1932) = 1.00638$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $H_o$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_o &= \frac{24 \times 3600 \times 1367}{\pi} \times 1.00638 \times \left[\cos 10^\circ \cos(-0.45^\circ) \sin 89.92^\circ + \frac{\pi \times 89.92}{180}\sin 10^\circ \sin(-0.45^\circ)\right] \\
&= \frac{86400 \times 1367}{\pi} \times 1.00638 \times \left[(0.9848)(0.99997)(0.99999) + (1.569)(-0.00303)\right] \\
&= 3.759 \times 10^7 \times 1.00638 \times [0.9848 - 0.00476] \\
&= 3.783 \times 10^7 \times 0.9800 \\
&\approx 3.708 \times 10^7 \text{ J/m}^2/\text{day} \\
&\approx 37.08 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day} \\
&\approx 10.30 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** On the Spring Equinox at $\phi = 10°$ N, the atmosphere's outer edge receives about **37.08 MJ/m²/day** of solar energy — the peak value for that latitude over the year.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition:** Such "calculate $H_o$" problems with given $n$ and $\phi$ appear almost every year in KTU ESE Module 2. Practice with day numbers around **n = 17** (summer solstice), **n = 172** (autumn equinox), and **n = 80, 266** (equinoxes).

## 3.5 Angle of Incidence Derivation (Tilted Surface)

For a surface tilted at angle $\beta$ from horizontal, facing due south (in N. hemisphere), the angle of incidence $\theta$ is given by:

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi - \cos\beta)\cos\omega$$

**Derivation Outline:**
1. The surface normal vector tilts by $\beta$ from the zenith in the north-south plane.
2. The Sun's unit vector remains as before.
3. Dot product of normal and Sun vector yields $\cos\theta$.
4. For a **south-facing** surface in N. hemisphere, the surface normal's latitude angle becomes $\phi - \beta$.

**For $\beta = 0$ (horizontal):** $\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin\phi + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\omega = \cos\theta_z$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Solar Geometry — Angular Relationships Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the geometric dependencies between all solar angles, organized as a hierarchical computational flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInput["INPUT PARAMETERS: Latitude phi, Day Number n, Solar Time Ts"]
    nodeDay["Compute Day Angle: B = 360(n-81)/365 degrees"]
    nodeDecl["Compute Declination: delta = 23.45 sin[360(284+n)/365]"]
    nodeEoT["Equation of Time: Eo = 9.87 sin 2B - 7.53 cos B - 1.5 sin B"]
    nodeSolar["Solar Time Correction: Tsolar = Tstd + 4(Lst-Lloc) + Eo"]
    nodeOmega["Hour Angle: omega = 15(Tsolar - 12) degrees"]
    nodeWs["Sunset Hour Angle: omegas = arccos(-tan phi tan delta)"]
    nodeAlt["Altitude: sin alpha = sin phi sin delta + cos phi cos delta cos omega"]
    nodeZen["Zenith: theta_z = 90 - alpha"]
    nodeAzm["Azimuth: sin gamma = cos delta sin omega / cos alpha"]
    nodeAM["Air Mass: AM = 1 / cos theta_z"]
    nodeHo["Daily Extraterrestrial: Ho = f(Gsc, Eo, phi, delta, omegas)"]

    nodeInput --> nodeDay
    nodeInput --> nodeSolar
    nodeDay --> nodeDecl
    nodeDay --> nodeEoT
    nodeEoT --> nodeSolar
    nodeInput --> nodeOmega
    nodeSolar --> nodeOmega
    nodeDecl --> nodeOmega
    nodeInput --> nodeWs
    nodeDecl --> nodeWs
    nodeOmega --> nodeAlt
    nodeDecl --> nodeAlt
    nodeInput --> nodeAlt
    nodeAlt --> nodeZen
    nodeOmega --> nodeAzm
    nodeDecl --> nodeAzm
    nodeAlt --> nodeAzm
    nodeZen --> nodeAM
    nodeWs --> nodeHo
    nodeDecl --> nodeHo
    nodeInput --> nodeHo
    nodeEoT --> nodeHo
```

## 4.2 Solar Radiation Components — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSun["SUN: Emits EM Radiation"]
    nodeAtm["ATMOSPHERE: Scattering + Absorption"]
    nodeBeamOut["Beam Radiation Ib: Direct from solar disc"]
    nodeDiffOut["Diffuse Radiation Id: Scattered from sky dome"]
    nodeGround["GROUND: Reflects radiation"]
    nodeRefOut["Reflected Radiation Ir"]
    nodeHoriz["HORIZONTAL SURFACE: Global G = Ib cos theta_z + Id"]
    nodeTilt["TILTED SURFACE: IT = Ib Rb + Id(1+cos beta)/2 + rho G(1-cos beta)/2"]

    nodeSun --> nodeAtm
    nodeAtm --> nodeBeamOut
    nodeAtm --> nodeDiffOut
    nodeSun -.through window.-> nodeGround
    nodeGround --> nodeRefOut
    nodeBeamOut --> nodeHoriz
    nodeDiffOut --> nodeHoriz
    nodeBeamOut --> nodeTilt
    nodeDiffOut --> nodeTilt
    nodeRefOut --> nodeTilt
```

## 4.3 Solar Radiation Measurement System — Instrumentation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSun1["SUN: Source"]
    nodePyrh["PYRHELIOMETER: Measures Direct Beam Ib on normal surface"]
    nodePyr1["Tracking Mount: Equatorial / Alt-Azimuth"]
    nodePyr2["Thermopile Sensor"]
    nodePyra["PYRANOMETER: Measures Global G on horizontal surface"]
    nodePyra1["Thermopile / Silicon Sensor"]
    nodePyra2["Glass Dome: 180 deg FOV"]
    nodeShade["SHADING RING/BALL: Blocks direct beam"]
    nodePyraShade["PYRANOMETER with SHADE: Measures Diffuse Id only"]
    nodeData["Data Logger / Recorder"]
    nodeProc["PROCESSING: G - Id = Ib cos theta_z"]

    nodeSun1 --> nodePyrh
    nodeSun1 --> nodePyra
    nodeSun1 --> nodePyraShade
    nodePyrh --> nodeData
    nodePyra --> nodeData
    nodePyraShade --> nodeData
    nodeData --> nodeProc
    nodePyr1 --> nodePyrh
    nodePyr2 --> nodePyrh
    nodePyra1 --> nodePyra
    nodePyra2 --> nodePyra
    nodeShade --> nodePyraShade
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Relevance:** Block diagrams of measurement setups are commonly tested for **3–7 marks** in KTU ESE. Memorize the **Pyranometer = Global**, **Pyranometer + Shade = Diffuse**, **Pyrheliometer = Direct Beam** trichotomy.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define the following terms related to solar radiation:
(a) Solar constant
(b) Air mass
(c) Declination angle

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Solar Constant ($G_{sc}$):** The solar constant is the rate at which solar energy is received per unit area by a surface held perpendicular to the Sun's rays at the outer edge of the Earth's atmosphere, at the mean Earth-Sun distance. Its accepted value is **$G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$**. It represents the solar irradiance outside the atmosphere.

**(b) Air Mass (AM):** Air mass is the ratio of the actual path length of solar radiation through the Earth's atmosphere to the path length when the Sun is directly overhead (at the zenith). It is given by $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$, where $\theta_z$ is the zenith angle. At $AM = 1.5$, the solar spectrum used for standard PV testing is defined.

**(c) Declination Angle ($\delta$):** The declination angle is the angular position of the Sun at solar noon with respect to the equatorial plane. It varies from $-23.45°$ (winter solstice) to $+23.45°$ (summer solstice) and is computed using Cooper's equation: $\delta = 23.45° \sin[360(284+n)/365]$, where $n$ is the day of the year. **[3 Marks]**

---

### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Distinguish between **beam radiation** and **diffuse radiation**. Mention the instruments used to measure them.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Beam (Direct) Radiation | Diffuse Radiation |
|:-------|:------------------------|:------------------|
| Definition | Solar radiation reaching the surface directly from the Sun's disc without scattering | Solar radiation scattered by atmospheric molecules/aerosols, reaching from all sky directions |
| Path | Straight line from solar disc | Multiple scattering paths |
| Magnitude | Dominant on clear days | Dominant on cloudy/overcast days |
| Instrument | **Pyrheliometer** (mounted on solar tracker with narrow FOV ≈ 5°) | **Pyranometer with shading ring/ball** (180° FOV, shading device blocks the Sun) |
| Typical share (clear sky) | ~85% of total | ~15% of total |

**Formula:** Total global radiation $G = I_b \cos\theta_z + I_d$ on horizontal. **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question B-A [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Define solar declination, hour angle, and zenith angle. Derive the cosine zenith angle formula from the basic solar geometry. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a location at latitude $\phi = 13°$ N, calculate the following for **2 PM solar time** on day number $n = 105$ (April 15):
1. Declination angle ($\delta$)
2. Hour angle ($\omega$)
3. Altitude angle ($\alpha$)
4. Zenith angle ($\theta_z$)
5. Air mass (AM) **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Declination Angle ($\delta$):** Angular position of the Sun at solar noon with respect to the Earth's equatorial plane. Varies from $-23.45°$ to $+23.45°$ annually.

**Hour Angle ($\omega$):** Angular displacement of the Sun east or west of the local meridian due to Earth's rotation. $\omega = 15° \times (T_{solar} - 12)$.

**Zenith Angle ($\theta_z$):** Angle between the vertical (zenith direction) and the line joining the observer to the Sun.

**Derivation (as in Section 3.2):**

The Sun's unit vector components in the observer's local coordinate system:
- $S_x = \cos\delta\cos\omega\sin\phi - \sin\delta\cos\phi$
- $S_z = \sin\delta\sin\phi + \cos\delta\cos\phi\cos\omega$

The zenith direction is along +z, so:

$$\cos\theta_z = \hat{S} \cdot \hat{Z} = S_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$$

This is the **cosine zenith angle identity** used universally in solar engineering.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $\phi = 13°$ N, $T_{solar} = 14:00$ (2 PM), $n = 105$.

**Step 1 — Declination:**
$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 105)\right] = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360 \times 389}{365}\right] = 23.45 \sin(383.51°)$$

Since $383.51° - 360° = 23.51°$:
$$\delta = 23.45 \times \sin(23.51°) = 23.45 \times 0.3989 = +9.35°$$

**Step 2 — Hour Angle:**
$$\omega = 15° \times (14 - 12) = +30°$$
(Positive means the Sun is in the western sky — afternoon.)

**Step 3 — Altitude:**
$$\sin\alpha = \sin(13°)\sin(9.35°) + \cos(13°)\cos(9.35°)\cos(30°)$$
$$\sin\alpha = (0.2250)(0.1625) + (0.9744)(0.9867)(0.8660)$$
$$\sin\alpha = 0.0366 + 0.8327 = 0.8693$$
$$\alpha = \sin^{-1}(0.8693) = 60.43°$$

**Step 4 — Zenith:**
$$\theta_z = 90° - 60.43° = 29.57°$$

**Step 5 — Air Mass:**
$$AM = \frac{1}{\cos(29.57°)} = \frac{1}{0.8701} = 1.149$$

**Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|:------|
| $\delta$ | $+9.35°$ |
| $\omega$ | $+30°$ |
| $\alpha$ | $60.43°$ |
| $\theta_z$ | $29.57°$ |
| $AM$ | $1.149$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $n = 105$ as the *month-day* instead of the day-of-year. **Day-of-year for April 15 = 31+28+31+15 = 105. ✓**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Not reducing $\sin(383.51°)$ to $\sin(23.51°)$ — students forget the modulo.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Sign of hour angle: morning → negative, afternoon → positive. KTU strictly checks this.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing declination with altitude. Declination is for the Sun's position relative to the equator; altitude is relative to the local horizon.

---

### **Question B-B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 2]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **pyranometer** and a **pyrheliometer**. How is diffuse radiation measured? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Calculate the **daily extraterrestrial radiation $H_o$** on a horizontal surface for a location at latitude $\phi = 20°$ N on day number $n = 172$ (summer solstice, June 21). Use $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pyranometer and Pyrheliometer [7 Marks]**

**Pyranometer:**
- **Function:** Measures **global solar radiation** (beam + diffuse) incident on a horizontal surface.
- **Construction:** A thermopile sensor is mounted under a hemispherical glass dome (180° field of view). The dome protects the sensor and transmits 0.3–3 μm radiation. Black and white sensor surfaces create a temperature difference proportional to incident radiation.
- **Working:** Incident radiation absorbed by black surface raises its temperature. The temperature difference between hot and cold junctions of the thermopile generates a small EMF (μV to mV range), proportional to irradiance.
- **Standard:** ISO 9060 classifies pyranometers into Class A (secondary standard), B, and C.

**Pyrheliometer:**
- **Function:** Measures **direct beam radiation** ($I_b$) on a surface perpendicular to the Sun's rays.
- **Construction:** A thermopile sensor at the bottom of a long tube with a narrow aperture (~5° field of view) — only direct rays from the solar disc enter. Mounted on a **solar tracker** (equatorial or alt-azimuth) to follow the Sun throughout the day.
- **Working:** The aperture restricts incoming radiation to direct solar beam only. The thermopile generates EMF proportional to $I_b$.

**Diffuse Radiation Measurement:**
To isolate diffuse radiation, a **pyranometer** is fitted with a **shading ring** (or shading ball/disc) that blocks the direct beam from reaching the sensor. The remaining radiation measured is purely diffuse ($I_d$).

Then by subtraction:
$$I_b = \frac{G - I_d}{\cos\theta_z}$$

**Block Diagram** (refer to SECTION 4, Diagram 4.3).

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Given:** $\phi = 20°$ N, $n = 172$, $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$.

**Step 1 — Declination (Summer Solstice):**
$$\delta = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360}{365}(284 + 172)\right] = 23.45 \sin\left[\frac{360 \times 456}{365}\right] = 23.45 \sin(449.75°)$$
$$= 23.45 \sin(89.75°) = 23.45 \times 0.99998 \approx +23.45°$$

(Summer solstice declination is exactly $+23.45°$ ✓)

**Step 2 — Sunset Hour Angle:**
$$\cos\omega_s = -\tan(20°) \tan(23.45°) = -(0.3640)(0.4335) = -0.1578$$
$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-0.1578) = 99.08°$$

**Daylight hours:** $\frac{2\omega_s}{15°} = \frac{198.16}{15} = 13.21 \text{ hours}$

**Step 3 — Eccentricity Factor:**
$$E_o = 1 + 0.033\cos\left(\frac{360 \times 172}{365}\right) = 1 + 0.033\cos(169.64°) = 1 + 0.033(-0.9853) = 1 - 0.0325 = 0.9675$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Bracket Term:**

Term 1: $\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s = \cos(20°)\cos(23.45°)\sin(99.08°)$
$$= (0.9397)(0.9174)(0.9875) = 0.8513$$

Term 2: $\frac{\pi \omega_s}{180}\sin\phi \sin\delta = \frac{\pi \times 99.08}{180} \times \sin(20°)\sin(23.45°)$
$$= 1.7296 \times (0.3420)(0.3979) = 1.7296 \times 0.1361 = 0.2354$$

**Bracket Total:** $0.8513 + 0.2354 = 1.0867$

**Step 5 — Final $H_o$:**
$$H_o = \frac{24 \times 3600 \times 1367}{\pi} \times 0.9675 \times 1.0867$$
$$H_o = 3.759 \times 10^7 \times 0.9675 \times 1.0867$$
$$H_o = 3.759 \times 10^7 \times 1.0515 = 3.953 \times 10^7 \text{ J/m}^2/\text{day}$$

$$\boxed{H_o \approx 39.53 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day} \approx 10.98 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{day}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Sign Mistake in Eccentricity:** For summer months, $\cos(360n/365)$ becomes negative (e.g., $n = 172$), making $E_o < 1$. Students often forget the negative sign and compute $E_o > 1$.
> - **Bracket Units:** The two terms inside the $H_o$ bracket are *both dimensionless* — the $\pi\omega_s/180$ term has radians, while the first term is plain. **Do not mismatch units!**
> - **Final Unit Conversion:** Forgetting to divide by $3.6 \times 10^6$ when reporting in kWh/m²/day costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Quick-Recall Checklist (For Last-Minute Revision)

- ☑ **Solar Constant:** $G_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$ — memorize without error.
- ☑ **Cooper's Equation:** $\delta = 23.45 \sin[360(284+n)/365]$ — most tested formula.
- ☑ **Hour Angle:** 15° per hour, negative morning / positive afternoon.
- ☑ **Key Identity:** $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$.
- ☑ **Sunset Hour Angle:** $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$.
- ☑ **Daylight Hours:** $N = 2\omega_s / 15$.
- ☑ **Air Mass:** $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ (basic); Kasten formula for accuracy.
- ☑ **Eccentricity Factor:** Peaks at $E_o = 1.033$ on Jan 3 (perihelion); minimum $0.967$ on July 4.
- ☑ **Instruments Triad:** Pyranometer = Global; Pyranometer + Shade = Diffuse; Pyrheliometer = Direct Beam.
- ☑ **$H_o$ Bracket:** Two terms inside — trigonometric + angular (with π/180). **Watch the units.**
- ☑ **Kerala Latitude:** $\phi \approx 10°$ N. Optimal tilt = $\phi - 10° \approx$ Latitude (8°–12°).

### Common KTU Pitfalls (MUST avoid)

1. **Modulo 360° errors** in trigonometric functions — reduce angles before computing.
2. **Day-of-year confusion** — always verify with calendar dates: Jan 31 → $n=31$, Feb 28 → $n=59$, Mar 31 → $n=90$, etc.
3. **Longitude sign** — for India, $L_{st} = 82.5°$ E (IST). Kerala's $L_{loc} \approx 76°$ E. Solar time ≈ Standard time − 26 minutes (approx).
4. **Air mass formula valid only for $\theta_z < 90°$** — beyond sunrise/sunset, use Kasten-Young empirical form.
5. **$H_o$ formula gives extraterrestrial** — actual terrestrial $H$ is much less due to atmospheric attenuation (typically 60–80% reduction in tropical climates).
6. **Ground reflectivity $\rho$:** Grass ≈ 0.2, Sand ≈ 0.4, Fresh snow ≈ 0.8, Water ≈ 0.05–0.2.

### One-Line Takeaways for the Examiner

- Solar radiation engineering **starts with solar geometry** — never skip declination/hour angle.
- **Cooper's equation** is universally accepted in KTU; no need for Spencer's or other variants unless asked.
- The three components (beam, diffuse, reflected) must each be modeled separately for accurate tilted-surface analysis.
- Kerala's tropical latitude makes **diffuse radiation a major contributor** (~40–50% of global during monsoon), unlike high-latitude regions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Solar Radiation on Horizontal and Inclined Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Solar Radiation on Horizontal and Inclined Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Solar Radiation Estimation** is the systematic mathematical procedure used to compute the intensity and distribution of the sun's radiant energy received on a surface of any arbitrary orientation and tilt. The estimation is performed in two distinct geometric configurations:

1. **Horizontal Surface (Tilt = 0°):** A surface lying flat on the ground, where the radiation received is the **Global Horizontal Irradiance (GHI)** — the sum of beam (direct) and diffuse components after atmospheric attenuation.
2. **Inclined (Tilted) Surface (Tilt = β, β > 0°):** A surface inclined at an angle **β** to the horizontal, oriented along an azimuth **γ**, where the effective radiation is computed by projecting the solar vector onto the surface normal.

The fundamental governing relationship is:

$$I_{T} = I_{b} \cdot R_{b} + I_{d} \cdot R_{d} + I_{r} \cdot R_{r}$$

where each term represents the beam, diffuse, and ground-reflected components respectively, modified by their corresponding tilt factors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Estimation of solar radiation is a **core analytical skill** required for sizing PV arrays, solar thermal collectors, and designing standalone/grid-tied systems. Questions are frequently asked as 14-mark analytical problems involving declination, hour angle, and tilt factor derivations.

### 1.2 The Solar Constant and Extraterrestrial Radiation

The **Solar Constant (I_sc)** is the energy received per unit area per unit time on a surface oriented perpendicular to the sun's rays at the mean earth-sun distance, outside the atmosphere.

$$\boxed{I_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 1.367 \text{ kW/m}^2}$$

This is **NOT** a true constant — it varies by ±3.3% over the year due to the earth's elliptical orbit. The extraterrestrial radiation on a surface normal to the sun's rays on day *n* is:

$$I_{on} = I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos \frac{360 n}{365}\right)$$

where **n** = day of the year (Jan 1 = 1).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lamp and Wall" Intuition

Imagine you are holding a flashlight (the **sun**) and shining it on a wall (the **earth's surface**):

- If the wall is **flat on the floor** (horizontal surface), the light spreads out, and the wall receives only a fraction of the beam intensity — most energy falls on a tilted floor area.
- If you **tilt the wall** to face the flashlight directly, you concentrate the light on a smaller area → the **beam intensity per unit area increases dramatically**.
- However, tilting the wall also changes how much **sky glow (diffuse radiation)** it can "see" and how much **ground bounce (reflected radiation)** it catches from below.

This trade-off — beam gain vs. diffuse/reflected loss — is the central engineering problem in solar collector orientation.

### 1.4 Decomposition of Global Solar Radiation

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Components of Terrestrial Solar Radiation:**
> 
> | Component | Symbol | Description |
> |-----------|--------|-------------|
> | **Beam (Direct) Radiation** | $I_b$ | Travels in a straight line from the sun; cast sharp shadows |
> | **Diffuse Radiation** | $I_d$ | Scattered by atmosphere, clouds, aerosols; arrives from all sky directions |
> | **Ground-Reflected Radiation** | $I_r$ | Reflected from ground/water/snow toward the tilted surface |
> 
> **Total Global Radiation on Horizontal Surface:** $I = I_b + I_d$
> 
> **Total Global Radiation on Tilted Surface:** $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d R_d + I_r R_r$

### 1.5 The Clearness Index — A Diagnostic Metric

The **Clearness Index ($k_T$)** is the ratio of terrestrial to extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface — a dimensionless indicator of atmospheric clarity:

$$k_T = \frac{I}{I_o}$$

where:
- $I$ = monthly average daily global radiation on horizontal surface (MJ/m²/day or kWh/m²/day)
- $I_o$ = monthly average daily extraterrestrial radiation on horizontal surface

Typical values: $k_T \approx 0.25$ (very cloudy, e.g., Kerala monsoon) to $0.75$ (clear desert). The **Diffuse Fraction ($k_d = I_d/I$)** is empirically correlated with $k_T$ using the **Erbs correlation**, which is the foundation for decomposing global radiation into beam and diffuse components when only $k_T$ data is available.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Solar Geometry — The Foundation of All Estimations

Every solar radiation calculation begins with defining the **sun's position** relative to an observer on earth. The position is described by two angles:

#### (a) Declination Angle (δ) — The Sun's "Seasonal Latitude"

The angular position of the sun at solar noon relative to the equatorial plane:

$$\boxed{\delta = 23.45° \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(284 + n)\right)}$$

- Range: $-23.45° \leq \delta \leq +23.45°$
- $\delta = 0°$ at equinoxes; $\delta = +23.45°$ at summer solstice; $\delta = -23.45°$ at winter solstice.

#### (b) Hour Angle (ω) — The Sun's "Daily Clock"

The angular displacement of the sun from local solar noon (east or west):

$$\boxed{\omega = 15° \times (\text{Solar Time} - 12:00) \quad \text{in degrees}}$$

$$\omega = 15°(t_s - 12) \quad \text{where } t_s \text{ is solar time in hours}$$

- Negative ω: morning; Positive ω: afternoon
- **Sunrise hour angle** $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi \tan\delta)$

#### (c) Surface Orientation Angles

| Angle | Symbol | Definition |
|-------|--------|------------|
| Latitude | $\phi$ | Angular position north of equator (°) |
| Surface Tilt | $\beta$ | Angle between surface and horizontal (0° ≤ β ≤ 180°) |
| Surface Azimuth | $\gamma$ | Deviation of surface normal from local south, east negative, west positive |
| Solar Altitude | $\alpha_s$ | Angle of sun above horizontal |
| Solar Azimuth | $\gamma_s$ | Horizontal projection of sun direction, measured from south |
| Zenith Angle | $\theta_z$ | Angle between sun and vertical (= 90° − $\alpha_s$) |
| Angle of Incidence | $\theta$ | Angle between sun's rays and normal to tilted surface |

### 2.2 Key Geometric Relationships

**Zenith angle on a horizontal surface:**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega$$

**Angle of incidence on a tilted surface** (master equation):

$$\boxed{\cos\theta = \sin\delta \sin\phi \cos\beta - \sin\delta \cos\phi \sin\beta \cos\gamma + \cos\delta \cos\phi \cos\omega \cos\beta + \cos\delta \sin\phi \sin\beta \cos\gamma \cos\omega + \cos\delta \sin\beta \sin\gamma \sin\omega}$$

**For south-facing surface (γ = 0°):**

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta) \cos\omega$$

**Solar altitude and azimuth:**

$$\sin\alpha_s = \sin\phi \sin\delta + \cos\phi \cos\delta \cos\omega = \cos\theta_z$$

$$\sin\gamma_s = \frac{\cos\delta \sin\omega}{\cos\alpha_s}, \quad \cos\gamma_s = \frac{\sin\alpha_s \sin\phi - \sin\delta}{\cos\alpha_s \cos\phi}$$

### 2.3 Extraterrestrial Radiation on Horizontal Surface

The instantaneous extraterrestrial radiation on a horizontal surface:

$$I_{o} = I_{on} \cos\theta_z$$

**Daily/monthly average extraterrestrial radiation** (using $\omega_s$):

$$\bar{H}_o = \frac{24 \times 3600}{\pi} \cdot I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033 \cos\frac{360 n}{365}\right) \left(\cos\phi \cos\delta \sin\omega_s + \frac{\pi \omega_s}{180} \sin\phi \sin\delta\right)$$

where $\omega_s$ is in radians and $H_o$ is in J/m²/day (or divided by 10⁶ for MJ/m²/day).

### 2.4 Beam Radiation on Horizontal vs. Tilted Surface

The **Tilt Factor for Beam Radiation ($R_b$)** is the ratio of beam radiation on tilted surface to that on horizontal surface:

$$\boxed{R_b = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z}}$$

**Physical Meaning:** $R_b$ is the ratio of the effective projected areas — it tells you how much more (or less) beam radiation a tilted surface captures compared to a horizontal one. $R_b > 1$ means the tilted surface captures MORE beam radiation.

For a south-facing surface:

$$R_b = \frac{\cos(\phi - \beta)\cos\delta\cos\omega + \sin(\phi - \beta)\sin\delta}{\cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega + \sin\phi\sin\delta}$$

### 2.5 Diffuse Radiation Models

#### (a) Isotropic Sky Model (Liu & Jordan, 1963)

Assumes uniform sky radiance from all directions:

$$I_{d,T} = I_d \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{R_d = \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}}$$

#### (b) Hay-Davies Model (Anisotropic)

Accounts for circumsolar anisotropy:

$$I_{d,T} = I_d \left[A \cdot R_b + (1 - A)\left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right)\right]$$

$$A = \frac{I_{bn}}{I_{on}} = \text{anisotropy index}$$

#### (c) Reindl Model

$$I_{d,T} = I_d \left[(1 - A_1)\left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right)\left(1 + f\sin^3(\beta/2)\right) + A_1 R_b\right]$$

$$A_1 = \frac{I_{bn}}{I_{on}}, \quad f = \sqrt{\frac{I_b}{I}}$$

### 2.6 Ground-Reflected Radiation

$$I_r = \rho \cdot I \left(\frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{R_r = \frac{1 - \cos\beta}{2}}$$

where $\rho$ = **albedo** (ground reflectance):
- $\rho \approx 0.2$ for grass/soil
- $\rho \approx 0.8$ for fresh snow
- $\rho \approx 0.05$–$0.1$ for asphalt

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Table

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Solar Constant | $I_{sc} = 1367$ | W/m² |
| 2 | Extraterrestrial (instant.) | $I_{on} = I_{sc}(1 + 0.033\cos(360n/365))$ | W/m² |
| 3 | Declination | $\delta = 23.45°\sin(360(284+n)/365)$ | degrees |
| 4 | Hour Angle | $\omega = 15°(t_s - 12)$ | degrees |
| 5 | Zenith Angle | $\cos\theta_z = \sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\cos\omega$ | – |
| 6 | Sunrise Hour Angle | $\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan\phi\tan\delta)$ | degrees |
| 7 | Angle of Incidence (tilted) | $\cos\theta = \sin\delta\sin(\phi-\beta) + \cos\delta\cos(\phi-\beta)\cos\omega$ (south) | – |
| 8 | Beam Tilt Factor | $R_b = \cos\theta / \cos\theta_z$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Diffuse Tilt Factor (Isotropic) | $R_d = (1 + \cos\beta)/2$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Reflected Tilt Factor | $R_r = (1 - \cos\beta)/2$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Clearness Index | $k_T = I/I_o$ | 0 to 0.75 |
| 12 | Erbs Diffuse Correlation | $k_d = 0.9511 - 0.1604 k_T + 4.388 k_T^2 - 16.638 k_T^3 + 12.336 k_T^4$ (for $0.22 \leq k_T \leq 0.80$) | – |
| 13 | Erbs Diffuse Correlation | $k_d = 0.99$ for $k_T \leq 0.22$ | – |
| 14 | Erbs Diffuse Correlation | $k_d = 0.165$ for $k_T \geq 0.80$ | – |
| 15 | Air Mass | $AM = 1/\cos\theta_z$ | – |
| 16 | Total Tilted Radiation | $I_T = I_b R_b + I_d R_d + I \rho R_r$ | W/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This entire framework is used by **PVsyst**, **SAM (System Advisor Model)**, and **HOMER** software for solar resource assessment. For KTU, every numerical problem demands a clear stepwise evaluation of declination → hour angle → zenith → $R_b$ → $R_d$ → $I_T$. Master this sequence!

### 2.8 Why is the Tilt Factor Important?

The concept of tilt factor is **engineered** to optimize energy yield:
- For **fixed-tilt PV systems** in India, the annual optimum tilt ≈ latitude of the location.
- For **seasonal adjustment**, the panel is tilted at $(\phi - 15°)$ in summer and $(\phi + 15°)$ in winter.
- For **concentrated solar power (CSP)**, the surface often tracks the sun, making $\theta \approx 0$ throughout the day, giving $R_b = 1/\cos\theta_z$ (very high).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Beam Tilt Factor $R_b$

**Starting Point:** Beam radiation on a surface = $I_b \cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between the sun's rays and the surface normal.

**Step 1: Beam radiation on horizontal surface**
The horizontal surface has its normal pointing straight up (zenith). So the angle of incidence is the zenith angle $\theta_z$:

$$I_{b,\text{horizontal}} = I_{bn} \cos\theta_z$$

where $I_{bn}$ is the direct normal irradiance (beam on a surface perpendicular to sun's rays).

**Step 2: Beam radiation on tilted surface**
The tilted surface has its normal at angle $\beta$ from vertical. The angle of incidence is $\theta$:

$$I_{b,\text{tilted}} = I_{bn} \cos\theta$$

**Step 3: Form the ratio (definition of $R_b$)**

$$R_b = \frac{I_{b,\text{tilted}}}{I_{b,\text{horizontal}}} = \frac{I_{bn} \cos\theta}{I_{bn} \cos\theta_z} = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z}$$

$$\boxed{R_b = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Diffuse Tilt Factor $R_d$ (Isotropic Sky Model)

**Assumption:** The sky is treated as a uniform, isotropic radiating hemisphere of intensity $L_d$ (W/m²/sr).

**Step 1: Diffuse radiation on a horizontal surface**
A horizontal surface "sees" the entire upper hemisphere (solid angle = 2π steradians):

$$I_{d,\text{horizontal}} = \int_{2\pi} L_d \cos\theta_z \, d\Omega = \pi L_d$$

(since $\cos\theta_z$ averaged over hemisphere = 1/2, integrated over $2\pi$ = $\pi$)

**Step 2: Diffuse radiation on a tilted surface**
The tilted surface sees only a fraction of the sky. By integration over the visible portion of the hemisphere, accounting for the inclination:

$$I_{d,\text{tilted}} = L_d \int_{\text{visible sky}} \cos\theta \, d\Omega = L_d \left(\frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}\right) \cdot \pi$$

(The factor $(1 + \cos\beta)/2$ is the "sky view factor" for a tilted surface.)

**Step 3: Form the ratio**

$$R_d = \frac{I_{d,\text{tilted}}}{I_{d,\text{horizontal}}} = \frac{L_d \pi (1 + \cos\beta)/2}{L_d \pi} = \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}$$

$$\boxed{R_d = \frac{1 + \cos\beta}{2}}$$

**Physical Check:**
- When $\beta = 0°$ (horizontal): $R_d = (1+1)/2 = 1$ ✓ (sees full sky)
- When $\beta = 90°$ (vertical): $R_d = (1+0)/2 = 0.5$ ✓ (sees half the sky)

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style, 14-Mark)

**Problem:**
Estimate the total solar radiation on a south-facing surface tilted at 10° at 2:00 PM solar time on 17th September at a location with latitude $\phi = 13°$ N. Given:
- $I_b = 320 \text{ W/m}^2$ (beam on horizontal)
- $I_d = 180 \text{ W/m}^2$ (diffuse on horizontal)
- $\rho = 0.2$ (albedo)
- $I_{sc} = 1367 \text{ W/m}^2$

**Step 1: Day of the year**
n for 17th September: Jan(31) + Feb(28) + Mar(31) + Apr(30) + May(31) + Jun(30) + Jul(31) + Aug(31) + 17 = 260

**Step 2: Declination angle**

$$\delta = 23.45° \sin\left(\frac{360}{365}(284 + 260)\right) = 23.45° \sin\left(\frac{360 \times 544}{365}\right)$$

$$\delta = 23.45° \sin(536.71°) = 23.45° \sin(176.71°) = 23.45° \times 0.0541$$

$$\delta \approx 1.27°$$

(Approximately equinox — makes sense, since Sept 17 is just after autumnal equinox on Sept 22)

**Step 3: Hour angle at 2:00 PM**

$$\omega = 15° \times (14 - 12) = 30°$$

**Step 4: Cosine of zenith angle**

$$\cos\theta_z = \sin(13°)\sin(1.27°) + \cos(13°)\cos(1.27°)\cos(30°)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = (0.2250)(0.0222) + (0.9744)(0.9998)(0.8660)$$

$$\cos\theta_z = 0.0050 + 0.8436 = 0.8486$$

$$\theta_z = \cos^{-1}(0.8486) = 31.96°$$

**Step 5: Cosine of angle of incidence (south-facing, γ = 0)**

$$\cos\theta = \sin\delta \sin(\phi - \beta) + \cos\delta \cos(\phi - \beta) \cos\omega$$

With $\beta = 10°$:

$$\phi - \beta = 13° - 10° = 3°$$

$$\cos\theta = \sin(1.27°)\sin(3°) + \cos(1.27°)\cos(3°)\cos(30°)$$

$$\cos\theta = (0.0222)(0.0523) + (0.9998)(0.9986)(0.8660)$$

$$\cos\theta = 0.00116 + 0.8643 = 0.8655$$

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}(0.8655) = 30.05°$$

**Step 6: Beam tilt factor**

$$R_b = \frac{\cos\theta}{\cos\theta_z} = \frac{0.8655}{0.8486} = 1.020$$

The tilted surface receives ~2% more beam radiation than horizontal.

**Step 7: Diffuse tilt factor**

$$R_d = \frac{1 + \cos(10°)}{2} = \frac{1 + 0.9848}{2} = 0.9924$$

**Step 8: Reflected tilt factor**

$$R_r = \frac{1 - \cos(10°)}{2} = \frac{1 - 0.9848}{2} = 0.0076$$

**Step 9: Total radiation on tilted surface**

$$I_T = I_b R_b + I_d R_d + I \cdot \rho \cdot R_r$$

$$I = I_b + I_d = 320 + 180 = 500 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$I_T = (320)(1.020) + (180)(0.9924) + (500)(0.2)(0.0076)$$

$$I_T = 326.4 + 178.6 + 0.76$$

$$\boxed{I_T \approx 505.76 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

**Note:** The tilted surface (β = 10°) captures about **5.76 W/m² more** than horizontal — a modest gain because the surface is nearly horizontal. Optimum tilt for this date would be higher (≈ latitude of location).

### 3.4 Monthly Average Daily Radiation Calculation

The monthly average daily extraterrestrial radiation $H_o$:

$$\bar{H}_o = \frac{24 \times 60 \times 60}{2\pi} I_{sc} \left(1 + 0.033\cos\frac{360 \bar{n}}{365}\right) \left[\frac{\pi \omega_{s}}{180}\sin\phi\sin\delta + \cos\phi\cos\delta\sin\omega_s\right]$$

where $\bar{n}$ = day of year for the middle of the month and $\omega_s$ in degrees.

**Sample:** For $\phi = 13°$ N, day $n = 172$ (June 21), $\delta = 23.45°$:

$$\omega_s = \cos^{-1}(-\tan(13°)\tan(23.45°)) = \cos^{-1}(-0.0935) = 95.37°$$

Convert to radians: $95.37° \times \pi/180 = 1.665$ rad

$$\bar{H}_o = \frac{86400}{2\pi}(1367)(1 - 0.033)\left[\frac{\pi(1.665)}{180}\sin(13°)\sin(23.45°) + \cos(13°)\cos(23.45°)\sin(95.37°)\right]$$

After numerical evaluation, $\bar{H}_o \approx 39.5 \text{ MJ/m}^2/\text{day}$ at the top of atmosphere on June 21 at $\phi = 13°$ N.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Model

| Scenario | Beam Component | Diffuse Component | Reflected Component |
|----------|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Clear sky, low latitude, low tilt** | Use exact $R_b = \cos\theta/\cos\theta_z$ | Liu-Jordan is sufficient | $R_r = (1 - \cos\beta)/2$ |
| **Partly cloudy, mid latitude** | $R_b$ as above | **Hay-Davies recommended** | Same |
| **Overcast, high tilt** | Often negligible | Liu-Jordan adequate | Same |
| **Snow-covered ground, high tilt** | $R_b$ as above | Hay-Davies or Perez | **Albedo = 0.6–0.8** |
| **Concentrator systems (tracking)** | $R_b = 1/\cos\theta_z$ | Usually negligible due to narrow acceptance | Negligible |
