Steam drives the turbine-generator; the cooled salt returns to the cold tank (~290 °C) for re-circulation. **Role of Molten Salt (2 marks):** - High specific heat (1.55 kJ/kg·K) → compact storage. - Wide liquid range (220–565 °C) → enables high steam temperatures. - Allows **dispatchable** solar power — generation even at night. #### 📘 Model Solution for Part (b): **Principle (2 marks):** Conversion of sunlight directly into electricity by the **photovoltaic effect** in a semiconductor p-n junction. **Advantages (3 marks):** - No moving parts → silent, low maintenance. - Long life (~25–30 years), modular scalability (mW to GW). - Direct sunlight-to-electricity conversion — no fuel cost. - Environmentally clean, no emissions during operation. - Can be integrated into buildings (BIPV). **Disadvantages (1 mark):** - High initial capital cost. - Low conversion efficiency (15–22 %). - Output depends on sunlight (intermittent, weather-sensitive). - Requires large land area for utility-scale. - Manufacturing involves toxic chemicals (Cd, Pb in some types). **Applications (1 mark):** - Rooftop residential and commercial power. - Spacecraft and satellite power (since 1958). - Solar water pumps for agriculture. - Street lighting, calculators, watches. - Off-grid rural electrification, telecom towers. - Megawatt-scale solar farms (e.g., Pavagada, Bhadla). --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / COMMON PITFALLS:** > 1. **Diode equation sign error:** Most students forget the minus sign and write $I = I_L + I_0[...]$, losing 2 marks. Remember: photo-current *opposes* the diode forward current. > 2. **Confusing V_oc with V_mp:** V_oc is the **open-circuit** voltage, NOT the operating voltage. Operating point is at V_mp < V_oc. > 3. **Block diagram with arrows:** KTU examiners *strictly* deduct marks if arrows are missing or wrongly directed. Always label every block and arrow. > 4. **Units mismatch:** When computing η, irradiance must be in **W/m²** and area in **m²**, NOT cm². This mistake alone costs 1 mark. > 5. **Missing "n" (ideality factor):** KTU expects the **ideality factor** in the diode equation, not the simplified form with "1" — don't drop n. > 6. **Solar tower vs. solar dish confusion:** Solar tower has a *single central receiver* on a tower; a solar dish has a *single parabolic dish* per unit. Do not interchange. > 7. **Applications list:** Examiners expect at least 3-4 specific applications with industries. Generic answers like "renewable energy" are penalized. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Preparation):** ### 🔹 Solar Tower - [ ] Concentrating Solar Power (CSP) using **heliostats + central receiver + molten salt**. - [ ] Concentration ratio: **600 – 1500 suns**; Receiver temperatures up to **565 °C**. - [ ] Working fluid: **Molten salt (NaNO₃ + KNO₃)**, water-steam, or air. - [ ] Advantages: **High efficiency (30–40 %), thermal energy storage, dispatchable power**. - [ ] Limitations: Requires **high DNI** (>2000 kWh/m²/yr); high land and water use. - [ ] Examples: **Ivanpah (USA, 392 MW), Gemasolar (Spain, 19.9 MW with 24-hr storage)**. ### 🔹 Photovoltaic Effect - [ ] Discovered by **Edmond Becquerel, 1839**. - [ ] Requires photon energy **hν ≥ E_g** (E_g,Si ≈ **1.12 eV**, λ_c ≈ **1.11 μm**). - [ ] Diode equation: $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$. - [ ] **V_oc decreases** with temperature; **I_sc increases** with irradiance. ### 🔹 PV Classification - [ ] **Stand-alone** (off-grid) — with/without battery. - [ ] **Grid-connected** (on-grid) — with/without battery, uses net-metering. - [ ] **Hybrid** — combined with diesel, wind, or biomass. ### 🔹 Key Performance Metrics - [ ] **Fill Factor (FF) = P_max / (V_oc × I_sc)** — typical 0.6–0.85. - [ ] **Efficiency η = FF × V_oc × I_sc / (G × A)** — typical 15–22 %. - [ ] **Thermal voltage V_T = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K** — memorize. ### 🔹 PV Advantages - [ ] No moving parts → low maintenance. - [ ] Long life (~25–30 yrs), modular, silent. - [ ] Direct conversion (no heat-engine losses). - [ ] Scalable from mW to GW. ### 🔹 PV Disadvantages - [ ] High initial cost; Low efficiency. - [ ] Weather/intermittent; Needs storage for night. - [ ] Manufacturing is energy-intensive; some toxic materials. ### 🔹 PV Applications - [ ] Rooftop residential, BIPV. - [ ] Spacecraft and satellites. - [ ] Solar water pumps (agriculture). - [ ] Street lighting, calculators, watches. - [ ] Telecom towers, off-grid rural electrification. - [ ] Megawatt-scale solar farms (Pavagada, Bhadla — India). > 🏁 **END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — Solar Tower & Photovoltaic Systems**

topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Introduction # 🌬️ MODULE 3 — INTRODUCTION TO WIND ENERGY SYSTEMS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wind Energy** is the kinetic energy of moving air masses that can be converted into useful forms of mechanical work or, more commonly, electrical energy through a device called a **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** or **Wind Turbine Generator System (WTGS)**. A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is defined (per IEC 61400 standards and KTU 2024 syllabus terminology) as the complete integrated assembly comprising: - A **Rotor** (blades + hub) that extracts kinetic energy from the wind - A **Drivetrain** (gearbox, shafts, couplings) that transfers rotational energy - A **Generator** (synchronous, induction, or PMSG) that converts mechanical to electrical energy - A **Nacelle** that houses the drivetrain and generator - A **Tower** that elevates the rotor to favorable wind regimes - A **Yaw & Control System** that orients and regulates the turbine ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "River of Air" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine standing in a flowing river holding a paddle wheel. The faster the river flows and the larger the wheel you hold, the more energy you can extract. Now imagine the **atmosphere as an invisible, slow-moving river of air** above us. The wind is simply this "river" in motion due to pressure differences caused by solar heating. A wind turbine is just a giant paddle wheel placed in this aerial river — but unlike a paddle wheel, modern airfoil-shaped blades can extract **up to 59.3%** of the kinetic energy passing through them (this magic number is called the **Betz Limit**). ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following benchmark values are used universally in KTU textbook and examiner solutions: | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Air density (sea level, 15°C) | **ρ = 1.225** | kg/m³ | | Betz Limit (max Cp) | **16/27 ≈ 0.5926** | dimensionless | | Swept area of HAWT | **A = πR²** | m² | | Kinetic energy formula | **KE = ½mv²** | Joules | | Gravitational acceleration | **g = 9.81** | m/s² | ### 1.4 Why Wind Energy Matters in 2024+ > [!IMPORTANT] > - Global installed wind capacity crossed **~1000 GW** in 2024. > - India ranks **4th globally** in installed wind capacity (~47 GW as of 2024). > - Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, Gujarat, and Rajasthan are the highest wind potential states. > - The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE) targets **140 GW of installed wind capacity by 2030**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Origin of Wind — The "Why" Behind Moving Air Wind originates from the **uneven heating of the Earth's surface by the Sun**, which generates horizontal and vertical pressure gradients in the atmosphere. The flow of air from high-pressure zones to low-pressure zones is what we call wind. **Core driving equation — Pressure Gradient Force per unit mass:** $$f_{pg} = -\frac{1}{\rho_{air}} \cdot \frac{dp}{dx}$$ Where: - $f_{pg}$ = pressure gradient acceleration (m/s²) - $\rho_{air}$ = air density (kg/m³) - $dp/dx$ = pressure gradient in the horizontal direction (Pa/m) > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** Meteorologists use this to forecast wind speed, which is critical for wind farm site selection, load forecasting, and grid balancing in KSEB (Kerala State Electricity Board) operations. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Energy Extraction from Wind 1. **Wind Strikes the Rotor:** Air moving at velocity $v$ approaches the turbine's swept area $A = \pi R^2$. 2. **Momentum Transfer:** The rotor blades (acting as airfoils) extract a portion of the air's momentum. 3. **Pressure Differential:** Air pressure drops in front of the disc (suction side) and rises behind it. 4. **Energy Conversion:** The rotor converts the kinetic energy drop into rotational mechanical energy. 5. **Electrical Generation:** The generator (typically Induction Generator or PMSG) converts mechanical energy to electricity. 6. **Grid/Load Supply:** Output is conditioned and fed to the grid via a Power Electronic Converter (PEC). ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **THE GOLDEN FORMULAS — Memorize these for KTU ESE 2024 onwards.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3$ | Total available kinetic power in wind | W (Watts) | | 2 | $A = \pi R^2$ | Swept area of Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) rotor | m² | | 3 | $E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of an air parcel of mass $m$ | J (Joules) | | 4 | $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot t$ | Mass flow rate of air through swept area in time $t$ | kg | | 5 | $P_{mech} = C_p \cdot P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_p \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3$ | Actual mechanical power extracted by turbine | W | | 6 | $C_p = \frac{P_{mech}}{P_{wind}}$ | Power coefficient (efficiency of extraction) | dimensionless | | 7 | $C_{p, max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | **Betz Limit** — theoretical maximum | dimensionless | | 8 | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{\pi R n}{v}$ | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | dimensionless | | 9 | $v_{cut\text{-}in} < v < v_{cut\text{-}out}$ | Operating wind speed window | m/s | | 10 | $E_{annual} = P_{rated} \cdot CF \cdot 8760$ | Annual energy generation | kWh/year | Where $CF$ is the **Capacity Factor** (typically 0.20–0.40 for onshore, 0.40–0.60 for offshore). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always write $v^3$ as **"v-cubed"** in derivations. Many students lose marks by forgetting that the power in wind is **CUBIC** in velocity — doubling wind speed increases power by **8 times**. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Wind Resource Assessment:** Engineers use the $P \propto v^3$ relationship to estimate site economics. A site with 7 m/s mean wind produces **2.7× more power** than one with 5 m/s. - **Turbine Sizing:** The formula guides blade radius selection. Doubling $R$ quadruples $A$, thus quadruples the extractable power. - **Energy Yield Prediction:** Used in bankable feasibility reports under MNRE/SECI tendering for IPPs (Independent Power Producers). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Power Available in the Wind ### Problem Statement Derive the expression for the total kinetic power available in a stream of wind passing through a swept area $A$ at velocity $v$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Start with the definition of kinetic energy.** The kinetic energy of an air parcel of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$ is: $$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$ **Step 2 — Express the mass of air passing through area A in time t.** In time $t$, the volume of air that crosses area $A$ is: $$V_{air} = A \cdot v \cdot t$$ Therefore, the mass of this air parcel is: $$m = \rho \cdot V_{air} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot t$$ **Step 3 — Substitute mass into the kinetic energy expression.** $$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} (\rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot t) \cdot v^2$$ Simplifying: $$E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot t$$ **Step 4 — Convert energy to power by dividing by time t.** Since power is the rate of energy transfer, $P = E/t$: $$P_{wind} = \frac{E_{k}}{t} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3 \cdot t}{t}$$ $$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot A \cdot v^3}$$ **Units check:** - $\rho$ in kg/m³ - $A$ in m² - $v^3$ in (m/s)³ = m³/s³ - Product: kg/m³ × m² × m³/s³ = kg·m²/s³ = **W (Watts)** ✓ --- ## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Betz Limit (Maximum Theoretical Cp) ### Problem Statement Show that the maximum fraction of wind kinetic power that an ideal turbine can extract is $C_{p, max} = 16/27$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation (Actuator Disc Model) **Step 1 — Set up the actuator disc model.** Consider an ideal rotor as a uniform "actuator disc" that slows the wind from upstream velocity $v_1$ to downstream velocity $v_2$ without adding rotational swirl. The velocity at the disc plane is the average: $$v_{disc} = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Mass flow rate through the disc.** $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v_{disc} = \rho \cdot A \cdot \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Thrust force on the disc equals momentum change.** By Newton's 2nd Law (rate of change of momentum): $$F = \dot{m} (v_1 - v_2) = \rho \cdot A \cdot \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} \cdot (v_1 - v_2)$$ Using the difference of squares identity $(v_1+v_2)(v_1-v_2) = v_1^2 - v_2^2$: $$F = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$ **Step 4 — Power extracted = Force × Disc velocity.** $$P_{extracted} = F \cdot v_{disc} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Write Cp as ratio of extracted power to available wind power.** The available wind power (upstream) is $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3$. So: $$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3}$$ Simplifying: $$C_p = \frac{(v_1^2 - v_2^2)(v_1 + v_2)}{2 v_1^3} = \frac{(v_1 - v_2)(v_1 + v_2)^2}{2 v_1^3}$$ **Step 6 — Introduce the induction factor 'a' for substitution.** Let $a = \frac{v_1 - v_{disc}}{v_1}$, so that $v_{disc} = v_1(1-a)$ and $v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$. Substitute $v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$ into the Cp expression: $$C_p = \frac{[v_1 - v_1(1-2a)] \cdot [v_1 + v_1(1-2a)]^2}{2 v_1^3}$$ $$C_p = \frac{(2a \cdot v_1) \cdot (2 - 2a)^2 \cdot v_1^2}{2 v_1^3}$$ $$C_p = \frac{4 a (1 - a)^2 v_1^3}{2 v_1^3}$$ $$C_p = 2 a (1 - a)^2 = 4a^3 - 4a^2 + 2a$$ **Step 7 — Maximize Cp by differentiating with respect to a and setting to zero.** $$\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)^2 + 2a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) = 0$$ $$4(1-a)^2 - 4a(1-a) = 0$$ $$4(1-a)[(1-a) - a] = 0$$ $$4(1-a)(1 - 2a) = 0$$ Solutions: $a = 1$ (trivial, no flow) or $a = 1/3$. **Step 8 — Substitute a = 1/3 back into Cp expression.** $$C_{p, max} = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{8}{27}$$ Wait — let me recheck. The **power** extracted is to the **upstream** wind power through area $A$: $$C_p = \frac{P_{extracted}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A v_1^3}$$ After substitution and simplification (re-doing carefully): $$C_p = 4a(1-a)^2$$ At $a = 1/3$: $$C_{p, max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$ $$\boxed{C_{p, max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ (Betz Limit)}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This 59.26% is the absolute theoretical maximum.** No real turbine can ever exceed it due to thermodynamic and fluid mechanical constraints. Modern utility-scale wind turbines achieve $C_p \approx 0.45$–$0.50$. --- ## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Annual Energy Generation The annual energy output of a wind turbine is: $$E_{annual} = P_{rated} \times CF \times 8760 \text{ hours}$$ Where: - $P_{rated}$ = rated (maximum) power output of the turbine in **kW** - $CF$ = Capacity Factor (ratio of actual to maximum possible output) - $8760$ = number of hours in a year > [!NOTE] > **Plain English:** This is like calculating how much money a salaried employee brings home vs. their maximum possible salary if they worked every single hour of the year. The Capacity Factor accounts for low-wind days, maintenance shutdowns, and grid curtailment. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a WECS The following diagram maps the complete functional flow of a Wind Energy Conversion System from ambient wind to grid injection. This is the type of architecture KTU examiners expect in 14-mark questions on "Explain the working of a WECS." ```mermaid flowchart TB A[AMBIENT WIND
Kinetic Energy Source] --> B[ROTOR BLADES
Airfoil Section] B --> C[HUB ASSEMBLY
Mechanical Coupling] C --> D[LOW SPEED SHAFT
Rotor Side] D --> E[GEARBOX
Speed Multiplier] E --> F[HIGH SPEED SHAFT
Generator Side] F --> G[GENERATOR
DFIG or PMSG] G --> H[POWER ELECTRONIC CONVERTER
AC-DC-AC Link] H --> I[TRANSFORMER
Step-up to Grid Voltage] I --> J[GRID / LOAD
Electrical Output] K[CONTROL SYSTEM
Yaw and Pitch Regulation] -.-> B K -.-> G L[ANEMOMETER and WIND VANE] --> K M[BRAKE SYSTEM
Mechanical Safety] -.-> C M -.-> F ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Wind Turbine Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart LR A[WIND TURBINES] --> B[AXIS OF ROTATION] A --> C[POWER OUTPUT] A --> D[LOCATION] B --> E[HAWT
Horizontal Axis] B --> F[VAWT
Vertical Axis] E --> G[Upwind] E --> H[Downwind] F --> I[Darrieus] F --> J[Savonius] C --> K[Micro
less than 1 kW] C --> L[Small
1 to 100 kW] C --> M[Medium
100 kW to 1 MW] C --> N[Large or Utility Scale
above 1 MW] D --> O[Onshore] D --> P[Offshore] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Wind Speed vs. Power Output Operating Zones ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Cut-in Speed
3 to 4 m/s] --> B[Operating Region
CP Maximized] B --> C[Rated Wind Speed
12 to 14 m/s] C --> D[Constant Rated Power
Pitch Control Active] D --> E[Cut-out Speed
25 m/s] E --> F[Shutdown
Turbine Stops] style A fill:#90EE90 style B fill:#FFD700 style C fill:#FFA500 style D fill:#FF6347 style E fill:#DC143C style F fill:#696969 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the chart:** Modern pitch-regulated turbines hold rated power constant between rated and cut-out wind speeds by **feathering** the blades (turning them edge-on to the wind). Above cut-out speed, the turbine shuts down to prevent structural damage from gales. ## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Energy Conversion Chain in WECS ```mermaid flowchart LR A[KINETIC ENERGY
of Air Mass] --> B[MECHANICAL ENERGY
Rotor Rotation] B --> C[ELECTRICAL ENERGY
AC at Variable Frequency] C --> D[GRID COMPATIBLE AC
50 Hz Fixed Frequency] D --> E[END USES
Grid Supply or Local Load] A -.-> F[Formula: 1/2 m v squared]:::note B -.-> G[Formula: T omega]:::note C -.-> H[Formula: V I cos phi]:::note D -.-> I[Converter and Transformer Stage]:::note classDef note fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **Define the term "Wind Energy Conversion System" and list its main components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is an integrated assembly of components that converts the kinetic energy of wind into electrical energy. Its main components are: 1. **Rotor (blades + hub)** — extracts kinetic energy from the wind 2. **Nacelle** — houses the gearbox, generator, and control systems 3. **Tower** — elevates the rotor to higher wind speeds 4. **Gearbox / Drivetrain** — steps up rotor RPM to generator RPM 5. **Generator** — converts mechanical to electrical energy (DFIG, PMSG, or SCIG) 6. **Yaw and Pitch Control System** — orients the turbine and regulates power capture 7. **Power Electronic Converter (PEC)** — conditions output to grid-compatible AC > [!NOTE] > **Mark split:** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for naming 4–5 components + 1 mark for correct technical terms used. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **What is the Betz Limit? Why is it considered the theoretical maximum for wind energy extraction?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Betz Limit** is the maximum fraction of the kinetic energy in wind that an ideal turbine can extract, given by: $$C_{p, max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ (or 59.26%)}$$ It is the theoretical maximum because: 1. The **Actuator Disc Model** derivation (using momentum and continuity equations) shows that extraction greater than 59.26% would require the air behind the disc to have **negative kinetic energy**, which is physically impossible. 2. If the rotor were 100% efficient, the air would have to come to a complete stop — but the air must still flow away to allow more air to enter, hence the 59.26% cap. 3. Real turbines achieve $C_p$ between **0.35 and 0.50** due to additional losses like blade tip losses, swirl losses, and mechanical drivetrain inefficiencies. > [!NOTE] > **Mark split:** 1 mark for stating the value (16/27 or 0.593) + 1 mark for physical reasoning + 1 mark for real-world $C_p$ range. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]` **(a) Derive the expression for the total kinetic power available in wind. State the assumptions made.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Assumptions:** 1. Wind is a steady, incompressible, uniform horizontal flow. 2. Air density $\rho$ is constant at 1.225 kg/m³ (standard sea-level). 3. The wind stream tube has uniform velocity across the rotor swept area. 4. No friction or heat losses; flow is along a single horizontal streamline. **Derivation:** **Step 1:** Kinetic energy of an air parcel of mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$: $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$ **Step 2:** Mass of air flowing through swept area $A$ in time $t$: $$m = \rho \cdot A \cdot v \cdot t$$ **Step 3:** Substituting: $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} (\rho A v t) v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 t$$ **Step 4:** Power = Energy / time: $$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3}$$ This is the total available kinetic power in the wind stream. **Real-world interpretation:** A turbine with 80 m diameter rotor ($A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times 40^2 = 5027 \text{ m}^2$) at wind speed 12 m/s and $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ would have $P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5027 \times 12^3 \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$ available in the wind. --- **(b) With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of a Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT) based WECS. What is the role of the Power Electronic Converter (PEC) in such systems?** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram Description:** ``` Wind → Rotor Blades → Hub → Low-Speed Shaft → Gearbox → High-Speed Shaft → Generator (DFIG/PMSG) → Power Electronic Converter (PEC) → Transformer → Grid/Load ``` **Working Principle:** 1. **Wind** strikes the **rotor blades** which are shaped as aerodynamic airfoils. The lift force causes rotation. 2. The **hub** transmits this rotation to the **low-speed shaft** (typically 15–25 RPM for utility-scale turbines). 3. The **gearbox** steps up the rotation to ~1500 RPM suitable for the generator. 4. The **generator** (squirrel-cage induction, doubly-fed induction, or PMSG) converts mechanical energy to variable-frequency AC. 5. The **Power Electronic Converter (PEC)** — consisting of an **AC-DC rectifier**, **DC link capacitor**, and **DC-AC inverter** — converts this variable-frequency AC into **grid-compatible 50 Hz AC**. 6. The **transformer** steps up the voltage to transmission levels. 7. The **control system** continuously monitors wind speed, direction, and grid conditions to optimize output via **pitch control** (blade feathering) and **yaw control** (nacelle rotation). **Role of the Power Electronic Converter (PEC):** | Function | Explanation | |---|---| | **Frequency Conversion** | Wind produces variable-frequency AC; PEC converts it to fixed 50 Hz grid frequency. | | **Voltage Regulation** | Maintains output voltage within grid code limits during wind fluctuations. | | **Reactive Power Control** | Provides or absorbs VARs to support grid voltage stability. | | **Soft Start Capability** | Limits inrush currents during turbine startup. | | **Fault Ride-Through (FRT)** | Helps the turbine stay connected during brief grid faults (per Indian Electricity Grid Code). | | **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** | Adjusts generator torque to operate at optimal tip-speed ratio and extract maximum $C_p$. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention **MPPT** as a function of the PEC. This is a **2-mark loss** in a 14-mark answer. Also, do NOT confuse **"soft start"** (a PEC feature) with the **"no-load to full-load startup procedure"** of the turbine. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]` **(a) Explain the Betz Limit in detail. Using the actuator disc theory, derive the expression for maximum power coefficient $C_{p, max} = 16/27$.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Conceptual Background:** The Betz Limit, derived by German physicist Albert Betz in 1919, establishes that no wind turbine can convert more than **59.26%** of the kinetic energy in wind into useful mechanical work. The remaining energy is either: - Left in the downstream wake (kinetic energy the wind carries away) - Lost as friction, turbulence, and swirl **Actuator Disc Theory — Step-by-Step Derivation:** **Step 1 — Setup:** Consider an ideal rotor modeled as a uniform **actuator disc** of area $A$. Wind enters at upstream velocity $v_1$ and exits at downstream velocity $v_2$, with disc-plane velocity $v_d = (v_1 + v_2)/2$. **Step 2 — Mass flow rate:** $$\dot{m} = \rho A v_d = \rho A \cdot \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Thrust force (from momentum conservation):** $$F = \dot{m}(v_1 - v_2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2)$$ **Step 4 — Power extracted:** $$P_{ext} = F \cdot v_d = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (v_1^2 - v_2^2) \cdot \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Power coefficient:** $$C_p = \frac{P_{ext}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^3} = \frac{(v_1^2 - v_2^2)(v_1 + v_2)}{2 v_1^3} = \frac{(v_1 - v_2)(v_1 + v_2)^2}{2 v_1^3}$$ **Step 6 — Introduce induction factor $a$ where $v_2 = v_1(1-2a)$:** $$C_p = \frac{2a v_1 \cdot (2 - 2a)^2 v_1^2}{2 v_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$ **Step 7 — Maximize:** $\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-2a) = 0 \Rightarrow a = 1/3$ **Step 8 — Substitute:** $$C_{p, max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \boxed{\frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926}$$ --- **(b) A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m and operates at a wind speed of 12 m/s. The air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate: (i) The total power available in the wind, (ii) The maximum mechanical power extractable (using Betz Limit), and (iii) The actual mechanical power extracted if $C_p = 0.40$.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Rotor diameter $D = 80$ m, so $R = 40$ m - Wind speed $v = 12$ m/s - Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ - $C_p = 0.40$ (actual) **Step 1 — Swept area:** $$A = \pi R^2 = \pi \times (40)^2 = 1600\pi = 5026.55 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Total power in wind:** $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^3$$ $$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728$$ $$\boxed{P_{wind} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum extractable power (Betz Limit):** $$P_{max} = C_{p, max} \times P_{wind} = 0.5926 \times 5.32 \text{ MW}$$ $$\boxed{P_{max} \approx 3.15 \text{ MW}}$$ **Step 4 — Actual power extracted ($C_p = 0.40$):** $$P_{actual} = 0.40 \times 5.32 \text{ MW}$$ $$\boxed{P_{actual} \approx 2.13 \text{ MW}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. Forgetting to convert diameter to **radius** (using $D$ instead of $R$ in $\pi R^2$) — **−1 mark**. > 2. Using $v^2$ instead of $v^3$ in the power formula — **−2 marks** (most critical). > 3. Not writing the final units (MW) — **−0.5 mark**. > 4. Rounding off too early in multi-step problems — propagate values till the final step. --- ## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING > [!WARNING] > **Common Reasons for Mark Deductions in Wind Energy Questions:** > 1. **Cubic velocity confusion:** Power in wind is proportional to $v^3$, NOT $v^2$. Writing $v^2$ leads to 0 marks for the derivation. > 2. **Density unit mismatch:** Forgetting $\rho$ or using 1000 kg/m³ (water density) instead of 1.225 kg/m³ (air density). > 3. **Swept area calculation error:** Using $\pi D^2$ instead of $\pi R^2$ — silently doubles the result. > 4. **Missing numerical units:** Always end every numerical answer with proper units (W, kW, MW, kWh). > 5. **No mention of assumptions:** In derivation questions, state assumptions explicitly at the start to secure full marks. > 6. **Confusing $C_p$ and $\eta$:** $C_p$ is the aerodynamic power coefficient; overall system efficiency $\eta$ is always less than $C_p$ due to gearbox and generator losses. --- ## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — The 12 Golden Points to Remember:** 1. **Wind Energy = Kinetic Energy of Air** — derived from solar heating-driven pressure gradients. 2. **WECS** = Wind Energy Conversion System — full assembly from rotor to grid. 3. **Available wind power:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3$ — **memorize this cube!** 4. **Swept area for HAWT:** $A = \pi R^2$ (NOT $\pi D^2$). 5. **Power extracted:** $P_{mech} = \frac{1}{2} C_p \rho A v^3$. 6. **Betz Limit:** $C_{p, max} = 16/27 \approx 0.5926$ — the 59.26% theoretical maximum. 7. **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR):** $\lambda = \omega R / v$ — used for control optimization. 8. **Operating zones:** Cut-in (3–4 m/s) → Operating → Rated (12–14 m/s) → Constant power → Cut-out (25 m/s) → Shutdown. 9. **Turbine types:** HAWT (dominant in industry) vs VAWT (Darrieus, Savonius). 10. **Generator types:** SCIG, DFIG (most common), PMSG (offshore trend). 11. **PEC role:** AC-DC-AC conversion, MPPT, FRT, VAR support, soft start. 12. **Indian context:** India targets 140 GW wind by 2030 (MNRE); Kerala has low-to-moderate wind potential; Tamil Nadu and Gujarat lead.

topics.title: Principle of Biomass Energy Generation - Biofuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle of Biomass Energy Generation - Biofuels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Biomass** is defined as any organic, non-fossil material of recent biological origin that can be used as a fuel or converted into energy carriers through physical, chemical, thermal, or biological conversion processes. It includes crop residues, forestry wastes, wood, agricultural by-products, animal manure, municipal solid waste (biodegradable fraction), aquatic plants, and energy crops deliberately cultivated for energy generation.

**Biofuels** are gaseous, liquid, or solid fuels produced from biomass feedstocks through various conversion technologies. They are broadly classified as **primary biofuels** (used in unprocessed form, e.g., firewood, wood chips) and **secondary biofuels** (processed into upgraded forms such as bioethanol, biodiesel, biogas, and bio-char).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must be able to distinguish clearly between **biochemical** (anaerobic digestion, fermentation), **thermochemical** (combustion, pyrolysis, gasification, liquefaction), and **physico-chemical** (transesterification, oil extraction) conversion routes. Examiner questions almost always open with a comparison of these pathways.

## 1.2 The Four Generations of Biofuels (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Generation | Feedstock Example | Technology | Status |
|------------|-------------------|-----------|--------|
| 1st Gen | Sugarcane, corn, soybean, palm | Fermentation, transesterification | Commercial |
| 2nd Gen | Lignocellulose (straw, wood waste) | Advanced hydrolysis, pyrolysis | Pilot / Pre-commercial |
| 3rd Gen | Algae (micro/macro) | Photosynthesis, hydrothermal | R&D |
| 4th Gen | Engineered algae / synthetic | Genetic engineering, solar-to-fuel | Lab scale |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Stored Sunlight Bank"

Think of biomass as **Nature's rechargeable battery**. The Sun pours energy onto a field of sugarcane every day, but the plant cannot use the entire dose at once. So it **stores the surplus in sugar molecules** (C₆H₁₂O₆) through photosynthesis, just like a mobile phone stores charge in its lithium cells. When we burn sugar, ethanol, or methane produced from it, we are essentially **withdrawing that stored solar energy with interest**, but releasing the carbon that was only recently absorbed from the atmosphere. This makes biomass the only renewable fuel that is **carbon-cycle neutral over its growth period**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Memorise:**
> - Standard calorific value of dry wood: **16–20 MJ/kg**
> - Calorific value of methane (CH₄): **55.5 MJ/kg** (or 37.5 MJ/m³ at NTP)
> - Stoichiometric ratio for complete combustion of methane: **1 CH₄ : 2 O₂ → 1 CO₂ : 2 H₂O**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure (NTP) for gaseous fuels: **101.325 kPa, 20 °C**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Biochemical Conversion Pathway

### 2.1.1 Anaerobic Digestion (Biogas Generation)

Anaerobic digestion (AD) is the **four-stage microbial degradation** of wet biomass in the absence of oxygen. The four stages are:

1. **Hydrolysis** – Complex polymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) by extracellular enzymes.
2. **Acidogenesis** – Monomers are converted into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), H₂, and CO₂ by acidogenic bacteria.
3. **Acetogenesis** – VFAs and alcohols are converted into acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria.
4. **Methanogenesis** – Acetate, H₂, and CO₂ are converted into CH₄ and CO₂ by methanogenic archaea.

**Overall stoichiometric equation:**

$$C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2$$

**Theoretical methane yield per kg of glucose:**

From the balanced equation, **1 mole (180 g) of glucose → 3 moles (48 g) of CH₄**

Therefore, the theoretical CH₄ yield per kg of glucose is **0.267 kg CH₄/kg glucose**.

### 2.1.2 Fermentation (Bioethanol Production)

Saccharomyces cerevisiae ferments hexose sugars to ethanol and CO₂:

$$C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 2 C_2 H_5 OH + 2 CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -92 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Theoretical ethanol yield** from glucose = **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** (by mass).

## 2.2 Thermochemical Conversion Pathway

| Process | Atmosphere | Temperature | Main Product |
|---------|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Combustion | Excess air (oxidising) | 700–1200 °C | Heat, flue gas, ash |
| Pyrolysis | Inert (N₂) | 300–700 °C | Bio-char, bio-oil, syngas |
| Gasification | Sub-stoichiometric O₂ | 700–900 °C | Syngas (CO + H₂) |
| Hydrolysis Liquefaction | Steam / high pressure | 300–350 °C | Bio-oil |

**Syngas composition (typical air-blown gasifier):** 20% CO, 15% H₂, 10% CO₂, 2% CH₄, balance N₂.

## 2.3 Physicochemical Conversion — Transesterification (Biodiesel)

Biodiesel (FAME – Fatty Acid Methyl Esters) is produced by reacting a triglyceride with a short-chain alcohol (methanol) in the presence of a catalyst (NaOH or KOH):

$$Triglyceride + 3 CH_3 OH \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH}} 3 R-COO-CH_3 + Glycerol$$

**Stoichiometric mole ratio** = **1 : 3 (oil : methanol)**. In practice, **6 : 1 to 12 : 1** is used to drive equilibrium towards the product side.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THE CHEAT SHEET — Memorise the table below. At least 60% of KTU numericals are solved from these equations alone.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $CV_{biomass} = m_f \cdot HHV_{fuel}$ | $m_f$ = mass of fuel (kg), HHV in MJ/kg | Energy content of solid biomass |
| 2 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{m_f \cdot CV}$ | Energy balance across boiler | Boiler efficiency |
| 3 | $Y_{CH_4} = 0.267 \cdot m_{glucose}$ | kg CH₄ produced per kg glucose | Anaerobic digester sizing |
| 4 | $Y_{ethanol} = 0.511 \cdot m_{glucose}$ | kg ethanol per kg glucose | Fermenter design |
| 5 | $\rho_{biogas} \approx 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at NTP | Density of typical biogas (60% CH₄) | Volume-to-mass conversion |
| 6 | $m_{biodiesel} = 3 \cdot M_{FAME} \cdot n_{triglyceride}$ | M = molar mass (g/mol), n = moles | Theoretical biodiesel yield |
| 7 | $Yield\,\% = \dfrac{m_{FAME,actual}}{m_{oil,feed}} \times 100$ | Percentage yield | Lab-scale optimisation |
| 8 | $\text{Stoichiometric O}_2 = 1.07 \cdot m_{CH_4} + 4.31 \cdot m_{H_2} + \dots$ | Empirical combustion eqn. | Air supply design for burner |
| 9 | $E_{biogas} = V \cdot CV_{biogas}$ | E in MJ, V in m³, CV ≈ 22 MJ/m³ | Power-generation estimation |
| 10 | $P_{electrical} = \eta_{gen} \cdot \dot{V} \cdot CV$ | $\eta_{gen}$ = 30–40% for IC engine | Sizing a biogas genset |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** An engineer designing a 500 kW biogas plant in Kerala must size the digester volume, gas-cleaning system, and engine. All of these calculations trace back to the **3 moles of CH₄ per mole of sugar** stoichiometry and the **22 MJ/m³ calorific value of biogas**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Analysis

## 3.1 Derivation — Theoretical Methane Yield from Glucose

**Step 1 — Write the balanced anaerobic equation**

$$C_6 H_{12} O_6 \longrightarrow 3 CH_4 + 3 CO_2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate molecular weights**

$$M_{glucose} = 6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 72 + 12 + 96 = 180 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$M_{CH_4} = 12 + 4(1) = 16 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Apply stoichiometry per mole of glucose**

$$\text{Moles of } CH_4 = 3 \times n_{glucose}$$

$$\text{Mass of } CH_4 = 3 \times 16 = 48 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4 — Normalise to 1 kg of glucose**

$$Y_{CH_4} = \frac{48 \text{ g } CH_4}{180 \text{ g glucose}} = 0.2667 \text{ kg CH}_4 \text{ per kg glucose}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to volume at NTP (PV = nRT, T = 293 K, P = 101.325 kPa)**

$$V = \frac{nRT}{P} = \frac{(48/16)(8.314)(293)}{101.325} = 0.72 \text{ m}^3 \text{ CH}_4 \text{ per kg glucose}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Biogas Plant Sizing

**Problem (KTU-style):** A community kitchen in a Kerala village generates **100 kg/day** of food waste with average composition equivalent to glucose. The waste has **85% moisture content** (wet basis) and **90% biodegradable volatile solids** (dry basis). Calculate:

(a) Daily CH₄ production in m³ at NTP.
(b) Electrical energy that can be generated per day assuming generator efficiency of 35%.
(c) Daily CO₂ equivalent avoided (assume 1 m³ CH₄ offsets 2.75 kg CO₂-eq).

> Use $Y_{CH_4} = 0.72 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg glucose}$ and $CV_{CH_4} = 37.5 \text{ MJ/m}^3$.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Determine mass of biodegradable dry solids (glucose equivalent)**

$$m_{dry} = 100 \times (1 - 0.85) = 15 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$m_{glucose} = 15 \times 0.90 = 13.5 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Daily methane volume**

$$V_{CH_4} = 13.5 \times 0.72 = 9.72 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

> **Step 2 Explanation:** This follows directly from the derivation in §3.1, applied to the actual mass of biodegradable solids available per day.

**Step 3 — Daily energy available in CH₄**

$$E_{CH_4} = V_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4} = 9.72 \times 37.5 = 364.5 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 4 — Daily electrical energy output**

$$E_{electrical} = 364.5 \times 0.35 = 127.6 \text{ MJ/day}$$

$$E_{electrical,kWh} = \frac{127.6}{3.6} = 35.4 \text{ kWh/day}$$

> **Step 4 Explanation:** 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ. The factor 0.35 represents the typical overall electrical conversion efficiency of a biogas-fuelled internal combustion engine + generator set.

**Step 5 — CO₂-equivalent avoided daily**

$$m_{CO_2,avoided} = 9.72 \times 2.75 = 26.7 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq/day}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Biofuel Conversion Routes

> [!NOTE]
> **The matrix below is the most-tested comparison in KTU Module 3. Memorise the energy efficiency and the dominant product column.**

| Parameter | Combustion | Pyrolysis | Gasification | Anaerobic Digestion | Fermentation |
|-----------|------------|-----------|--------------|---------------------|--------------|
| Operating T (°C) | 700–1200 | 300–700 | 700–900 | 30–55 (mesophilic) | 25–35 |
| Atmosphere | Air | Inert N₂ | Limited O₂ | Strictly anaerobic | Anaerobic |
| Energy efficiency | 60–80% | 70–80% | 70–85% | 30–50% | 40–60% |
| Main product | Heat | Bio-oil, char | Syngas | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) | Ethanol |
| Feedstock | Dry biomass | Dry biomass | Dry biomass | Wet biomass | Sugar/starch |
| Best for | Boilers | Liquid fuel | Power gas | Distributed plants | Transport fuel |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Biofuel Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK] --> B1[Agricultural Residue]
    A1 --> B2[Forestry Waste]
    A1 --> B3[Animal Manure]
    A1 --> B4[Municipal Solid Waste]
    A1 --> B5[Energy Crops]

    B1 --> C1[Solid Biofuels]
    B2 --> C1
    B5 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Firewood]
    C1 --> D2[Charcoal]
    C1 --> D3[Pellets and Briquettes]

    B3 --> C2[Gaseous Biofuels]
    B4 --> C2
    C2 --> D4[Biogas]
    C2 --> D5[Syngas]

    B1 --> C3[Liquid Biofuels]
    B5 --> C3
    C3 --> D6[Bioethanol]
    C3 --> D7[Biodiesel FAME]
    C3 --> D8[Bio-oil from Pyrolysis]
```

## 4.2 Biomass-to-Energy Conversion Routes (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FEED[Biomass Feedstock] --> SORT{Dry or Wet?}

    SORT -- Dry --> THERMO[Thermochemical Route]
    SORT -- Wet --> BIO[Biochemical Route]

    THERMO --> COMB[Combustion]
    THERMO --> PYR[Pyrolysis]
    THERMO --> GAS[Gasification]

    COMB --> HEAT[Heat and Steam]
    PYR --> BOIL[Bio-oil and Char]
    GAS --> SYN[Syngas CO and H2]

    BIO --> AD[Anaerobic Digestion]
    BIO --> FER[Fermentation]

    AD --> BG[Biogas CH4 and CO2]
    FER --> ET[Bioethanol C2H5OH]

    BG --> ENG[IC Engine Genset]
    SYN --> GT[Gas Turbine]
    HEAT --> BOILER[Steam Turbine]
    BOIL --> REFIN[Refining]
    ET --> BLEND[Blending with Petrol]
    REFIN --> BD[Biodiesel FAME]
    BD --> BLEND2[Blending with Diesel]

    ENG --> POW[Electrical Power]
    GT --> POW
    BOILER --> POW
    BLEND --> TRAN[Transport Fuel]
    BLEND2 --> TRAN
```

## 4.3 Anaerobic Digestion — Four-Stage Microbial Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Hydrolysis] -- VFAs and Sugars --> S2[Stage 2 Acidogenesis]
    S2 -- Acetate and H2 and CO2 --> S3[Stage 3 Acetogenesis]
    S3 -- Acetic Acid --> S4[Stage 4 Methanogenesis]
    S4 --> PROD[Biogas CH4 60 percent and CO2 40 percent]

    S1 -. Cellulose and Proteins .-> S1
    S2 -. VFAs .-> S2
    S3 -. Syntrophic bacteria .-> S3
    S4 -. Methanogenic archaea .-> S4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]
**Differentiate between first-generation and second-generation biofuels with two suitable examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

First-generation biofuels are produced from **edible biomass** (food crops) using well-established, commercial conversion technologies. They directly compete with the food supply chain and have a poor land-use ratio. Examples: **bioethanol from sugarcane molasses** and **biodiesel from soybean oil or palm oil**.

Second-generation biofuels are produced from **non-edible lignocellulosic biomass** such as agricultural residues (rice straw, bagasse), forestry waste, and dedicated energy crops (switchgrass). They require advanced pre-treatment (acid/enzymatic hydrolysis) or thermochemical processing. Examples: **cellulosic ethanol from rice straw** and **biodiesel from waste cooking oil or Jatropha**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Examiner Insight:** For full marks, always state the *advantage* of 2nd-gen biofuels (no food-vs-fuel conflict) and the *challenge* (costly pre-treatment).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]
**List the four stages of anaerobic digestion and identify the bacteria/archaea responsible for the final stage.**

**Model Answer:**

The four stages of anaerobic digestion are:
1. **Hydrolysis** – polymeric compounds (carbohydrates, proteins, fats) decomposed into monomers.
2. **Acidogenesis** – monomers converted into volatile fatty acids by acidogenic bacteria.
3. **Acetogenesis** – VFAs converted into acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂ by acetogenic bacteria.
4. **Methanogenesis** – acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂ converted into CH₄ and CO₂ by **methanogenic archaea** (e.g., *Methanobacterium*, *Methanococcus*).

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module 3 ESE questions carry 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Answer any ONE full question. The two alternatives below are stylistically representative of actual KTU papers.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a biomass gasifier**. List the typical composition of producer gas and its calorific value.

**(b)** A community biogas plant processes **200 kg/day** of kitchen waste with 80% moisture and 88% volatile solids. Calculate (i) the daily methane yield in m³ at NTP, (ii) the daily electrical energy in kWh assuming 30% generator efficiency, and (iii) the carbon-credit equivalent in kg CO₂/day.

#### Model Solution to (a)

A **biomass gasifier** is a closed, refractory-lined reactor in which solid biomass is partially oxidised in a limited supply of air/oxygen to produce a combustible gas mixture called **producer gas** (syngas). The unit operations are:

1. **Drying zone** (100–200 °C) – moisture evaporated.
2. **Pyrolysis zone** (200–600 °C) – biomass devolatilised to char + vapours.
3. **Reduction (or gasification) zone** (600–1000 °C) – char reacts with CO₂ and H₂O:

$$C + CO_2 \longrightarrow 2 CO \quad \text{(Boudouard reaction)}$$

$$C + H_2 O \longrightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \text{(Water-gas reaction)}$$

4. **Combustion (oxidation) zone** (1000–1400 °C) – exothermic reaction sustains process heat:

$$C + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2$$

**Typical producer gas composition:** CO 20–22%, H₂ 12–18%, CH₄ 2–5%, CO₂ 8–10%, N₂ 50–55%.

**Calorific value of producer gas:** **4.5–6.0 MJ/m³** (much lower than natural gas because of N₂ dilution).

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Mass of volatile solids (VS) per day**

$$m_{VS} = 200 \times (1 - 0.80) \times 0.88 = 200 \times 0.20 \times 0.88 = 35.2 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Daily methane volume** (using 0.72 m³ CH₄ per kg VS, same as glucose equivalent)

$$V_{CH_4} = 35.2 \times 0.72 = 25.34 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — Daily energy in CH₄**

$$E = 25.34 \times 37.5 = 950.3 \text{ MJ/day}$$

**Step 4 — Daily electrical energy**

$$E_{el} = 950.3 \times 0.30 = 285.1 \text{ MJ/day} = \frac{285.1}{3.6} = 79.2 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 5 — Carbon-credit equivalent**

$$m_{CO_2} = 25.34 \times 2.75 = 69.7 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{day}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]

**(a)** Describe the **transesterification process** for biodiesel production. State the role of the catalyst and explain why excess methanol is used.

**(b)** 1000 kg of waste cooking oil (density 920 kg/m³, average molecular weight 884 g/mol) reacts with 200 kg of methanol (M = 32 g/mol) in the presence of NaOH catalyst. Calculate (i) the stoichiometric methanol required, (ii) the percentage excess methanol used, and (iii) the theoretical yield of biodiesel (FAME, average M = 296 g/mol).

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Transesterification** is the chemical reaction in which an ester (vegetable oil / triglyceride) reacts with a short-chain alcohol (methanol or ethanol) in the presence of a **homogeneous alkali catalyst** (NaOH or KOH) to produce **fatty acid methyl esters (FAME)** — i.e., biodiesel — and **glycerol** as a by-product.

**Overall reaction:**

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3 CH_3 OH \xrightleftharpoons[\text{NaOH}]{} 3 FAME + Glycerol$$

**Role of the catalyst:** NaOH dissociates in methanol to form **methoxide ion (CH₃O⁻)**, which is the active nucleophile. It catalyses the stepwise substitution of glycerol with three methyl groups, dramatically increasing the reaction rate (from days to 60–90 minutes) at mild temperatures (50–65 °C).

**Why excess methanol is used:** The reaction is **reversible (equilibrium-limited)**. Le Chatelier's principle dictates that pushing the reactant side (methanol) drives the equilibrium towards FAME formation, increasing the conversion of oil to biodiesel. Typical practice uses **6 : 1 to 12 : 1** molar ratio (methanol to oil) against the stoichiometric 3 : 1.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Moles of oil**

$$n_{oil} = \frac{1000 \times 1000}{884} = 1131.2 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric methanol required** (3 mol per mol oil)

$$n_{MeOH,stoich} = 3 \times 1131.2 = 3393.7 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{MeOH,stoich} = 3393.7 \times 32 \times 10^{-3} = 108.6 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Moles of methanol actually used**

$$n_{MeOH,actual} = \frac{200 \times 1000}{32} = 6250 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage excess methanol**

$$\%\,\text{excess} = \frac{6250 - 3393.7}{3393.7} \times 100 = 84.2\%$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical biodiesel yield** (3 mol FAME per mol oil)

$$n_{FAME} = 3 \times 1131.2 = 3393.7 \text{ mol}$$

$$m_{FAME} = 3393.7 \times 296 \times 10^{-3} = 1004.5 \text{ kg}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing moles and mass** in transesterification: Always convert kg → mol first using molecular weight.
> 2. **Forgetting the 3 : 1 stoichiometry** when calculating methanol/oil.
> 3. **Using density incorrectly** in oil calculations — use the *molar mass*, not the density, to find moles of triglyceride.
> 4. **Writing the wrong catalyst** — only NaOH or KOH for alkali-catalysed transesterification; acid catalysis uses H₂SO₄ but is much slower.
> 5. **Omitting the role of glycerol** as a marketable by-product (used in pharma and cosmetics).

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- [ ] **Biomass** = recent (non-fossil) organic material; essentially **stored solar energy** via photosynthesis.
- [ ] **Biofuels** are classified as primary (wood, chips) and secondary (ethanol, biodiesel, biogas, bio-oil, syngas).
- [ ] **Four generations** of biofuels — 1st (food crops), 2nd (lignocellulose), 3rd (algae), 4th (engineered organisms).
- [ ] **Anaerobic digestion** has **4 stages** — Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis.
- [ ] Methanogenesis is performed by **archaea**, not bacteria.
- [ ] **Stoichiometric reaction:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 3 CH₄ + 3 CO₂.
- [ ] **Theoretical CH₄ yield** = 0.72 m³/kg glucose at NTP; **theoretical ethanol yield** = 0.511 g/g glucose.
- [ ] **Transesterification** of triglyceride with methanol gives 3 FAME + 1 glycerol. **Stoichiometric ratio 1 : 3**.
- [ ] **Catalyst:** NaOH or KOH (alkali); reaction temperature 50–65 °C; 60–90 minutes.
- [ ] **Thermochemical routes:** combustion (heat), pyrolysis (bio-oil + char), gasification (syngas).
- [ ] **Producer gas CV** ≈ 4.5–6.0 MJ/m³; **biogas CV** ≈ 22 MJ/m³; **biomethane CV** ≈ 37.5 MJ/m³.
- [ ] **Calorific values:** firewood 16–20 MJ/kg; charcoal 28–30 MJ/kg; biodiesel 37–40 MJ/kg; bioethanol 26.8 MJ/kg.
- [ ] **Carbon-neutral** cycle because the CO₂ released was recently absorbed by the plant.
- [ ] **Generator efficiency** typically 30–35% for biogas-fuelled IC engines.
- [ ] **Energy conversion** in S.I. units: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ.
- [ ] **Common numerical pattern in KTU:** find CH₄ volume, then energy, then kWh output, then CO₂ avoided.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biomass Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biomass Resources # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Biomass** refers to any organic matter derived from living or recently living biological organisms — primarily plant-based and animal-based materials — that can be converted into useful forms of energy, predominantly heat, electricity, and transportation fuels. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, biomass is explicitly classified as a **renewable, indigenous, and carbon-neutral** energy resource because the carbon dioxide released during its combustion/energy conversion was previously absorbed from the atmosphere during the growth phase of the plant through photosynthesis. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Standardised):** *"Biomass is the biodegradable fraction of products, waste and residues from agriculture (including vegetal and animal substances), forestry and related industries, as well as the biodegradable fraction of industrial and municipal waste."* ## 1.2 The Photosynthetic Origin (The Bio-Energy Link) The entire bioenergy chain is rooted in photosynthesis. The fundamental photosynthetic reaction is: $$ 6CO_2 + 6H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{Sunlight + Chlorophyll}} C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2 $$ Here, the incident **solar radiation** acts as the *primary energy input*. Green plants use this solar energy to convert low-energy **inorganic molecules** (CO₂ and H₂O) into high-energy **organic molecules** (carbohydrates such as glucose, cellulose, lignin, etc.). This stored chemical energy is the basis of all biomass-derived fuels. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Solar Battery" of Nature > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a plant as a **natural, rechargeable solar battery**. > - The **solar panel** = the leaf (chlorophyll absorbs sunlight). > - The **charging process** = photosynthesis (solar energy is stored in chemical bonds). > - The **stored electricity** = chemical energy locked in carbohydrates, oils, lignin. > - **Discharge** = burning biomass, fermentation, or gasification to release the stored solar energy on demand. Unlike a true battery, this "biological battery" is **rechargeable infinitely** (sustainably), and the **charging input is free sunlight**. This is precisely why biomass is considered *renewable*. ## 1.4 Why Biomass is Considered Carbon-Neutral | Process Step | Carbon Exchange | |---|---| | Plant growth (photosynthesis) | Absorbs CO₂ from atmosphere | | Combustion / Conversion of biomass | Releases CO₂ to atmosphere | | Net atmospheric CO₂ change | Approximately **zero** (closed carbon cycle) | > [!WARNING] > **Important Clarification:** Carbon-neutrality assumes *sustainable harvesting* (replanting rate ≥ consumption rate). Unsustainable deforestation breaks the cycle and turns biomass into a net CO₂ source. ## 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Photosynthetic efficiency** of natural vegetation: **≈ 1–2 %** of incident solar radiation. - **Average calorific value of dry biomass:** **≈ 16–20 MJ/kg**. - **Energy content of 1 tonne of dry biomass** ≈ **5,500 kWh** of thermal energy. - **World's largest biomass producers:** Brazil, USA, China, India. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Biomass Resources (KTU Mandatory) Biomass is classified into **five major categories** as per the KTU 2024 energy syllabus: ### 2.1.1 Category 1 — Woody Biomass Solid lignocellulosic material from forest trees, shrubs, and dedicated energy plantations (e.g., *Eucalyptus*, *Poplar*, *Casuarina*, *Bamboo*). - **Examples:** Firewood, sawdust, wood chips, pellets, charcoal. - **Typical calorific value:** **18–20 MJ/kg** (oven-dry basis). ### 2.1.2 Category 2 — Agricultural Residues (Agro-Residues) By-products of crop cultivation and processing. - **Field residues:** Rice straw, wheat straw, sugarcane bagasse, maize stover, coconut fronds. - **Process residues:** Rice husk, bagasse, groundnut shell, coconut shell, olive cake. - **Typical calorific value:** **14–17 MJ/kg**. ### 2.1.3 Category 3 — Aquatic Biomass Plant matter grown in water bodies; grows very fast, no land competition. - **Examples:** Water hyacinth, algae (*Spirulina*, microalgae), seaweed. - **Microalgae productivity:** Can be **10–30 times higher** than terrestrial crops per unit area. ### 2.1.4 Category 4 — Animal Wastes & Municipal Solid Waste (MSW) - **Animal waste:** Cattle dung, poultry litter, pig manure, slaughterhouse waste. - **MSW organic fraction:** Kitchen waste, paper, yard trimmings, sewage sludge. - **Note:** These are predominantly used for **biogas production** via anaerobic digestion. ### 2.1.5 Category 5 — Energy Crops (Dedicated Bioenergy Crops) Crops cultivated *exclusively* for energy generation. - **Sugar/starch crops:** Sugarcane, sweet sorghum, corn (for ethanol). - **Oil-seed crops:** Jatropha, soybean, rapeseed, palm oil (for biodiesel). - **Lignocellulosic energy crops:** Switchgrass, Miscanthus, *Pongamia*. ## 2.2 Properties of Biomass — The Four Pillars of Biomass Characterisation The quality and suitability of a biomass fuel is judged by four essential analyses: ### (A) Proximate Analysis Determines the **gross composition** of fuel in terms of four components. | Component | Symbol | Significance | |---|---|---| | Moisture Content | **M** | Reduces CV; causes smoke & energy loss | | Volatile Matter | **VM** | Gases released on heating; governs combustion | | Ash Content | **A** | Non-combustible mineral residue | | Fixed Carbon | **FC** | Solid combustible carbon remaining after volatiles escape | > **Constraint (mass balance):** M + VM + A + FC = **100 %** ### (B) Ultimate Analysis Determines the **elemental composition** in mass percentage. | Element | Symbol | Typical Range (biomass) | |---|---|---| | Carbon | C | 40 – 50 % | | Hydrogen | H | 5 – 7 % | | Oxygen | O | 35 – 45 % | | Nitrogen | N | 0.1 – 2 % | | Sulphur | S | < 0.5 % | | Ash | – | 1 – 10 % | ### (C) Calorific Value (CV) / Heating Value The **energy released per unit mass** of fuel during complete combustion. $$ \text{Higher Heating Value (HHV)} = \text{Latent heat of vaporisation of water in flue gas} + \text{Lower Heating Value (LHV)} $$ ### (D) Ash Melting Point Critical for boiler design — if ash fuses at low temperatures, it causes **slagging and fouling**. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **single most important** reference for solving any numerical problem on biomass. Memorise it thoroughly. | # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - h_f \cdot \left( \dfrac{9H + M}{100} \right)$ | Relationship between HHV and LHV; $h_f$ = latent heat of vaporisation of water = **2,442 kJ/kg** (at 25 °C) | MJ/kg | | 2 | $\text{HHV} = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A$ | **Dulong's formula** for HHV from ultimate analysis (C, H, S, O, N, A in mass %) | MJ/kg | | 3 | $\text{Combustion efficiency}, \eta_c = \dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_a} \times 100$ | For a biomass-fired boiler; $T_1$ = furnace temp, $T_2$ = flue gas temp, $T_a$ = ambient temp | % | | 4 | $\text{Air-Fuel ratio, AFR} = \dfrac{11.6C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35S}{100}$ | Stoichiometric AFR per kg of fuel | kg air/kg fuel | | 5 | $\text{Energy output} = m \times \text{LHV}$ | Thermal energy from burning $m$ kg of fuel | MJ | | 6 | $\text{Energy from biogas} = V \times \text{calorific value of methane} (35.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3)$ | Biogas assumed ~60 % CH₄ → ≈ **21.5 MJ/m³** | MJ | | 7 | $\text{Photochemical efficiency}, \eta_p = \dfrac{\text{Energy in biomass}}{\text{Solar energy incident on crop area}} \times 100$ | Typically 1–2 % for most biomass crops | % | | 8 | $\text{Energy ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Energy obtained}}{\text{Energy invested in cultivation}}$ | Net energy gain must be > 1 for sustainability | Dimensionless | | 9 | $\text{Ash \%} = \dfrac{m_{\text{ash}}}{m_{\text{dry fuel}}} \times 100$ | Standard ash measurement formula | % | | 10 | $\text{HHV}_{\text{biomass (approx)}} = 0.3536 \, C + 1.0477 \, H$ | Simplified empirical HHV for woody biomass | MJ/kg | > **Critical Note on Pipes in Tables:** The original formulation uses the absolute value symbol. We have replaced all `|` symbols with `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table parser happy. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Biomass Characterisation - **Power plant design** (boilers, gasifiers): requires ash %, moisture %, VM, CV to size the combustor and decide on firing technology. - **Pellet/briquette manufacturing:** requires CV ≥ **16 MJ/kg**, moisture ≤ **12 %**, ash ≤ **3 %**. - **Biofuel trade & pricing:** international contracts specify CV (HHV) on dry ash-free (DAF) basis. - **Emissions policy:** sulphur & nitrogen contents from ultimate analysis govern NOₓ and SOₓ control costs. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Biomass Combustion **Problem statement:** Derive the theoretical (stoichiometric) oxygen requirement for complete combustion of a biomass fuel having the ultimate analysis (by mass) as: C = 42 %, H = 6 %, O = 38 %, S = 0.5 %, N = 0.5 %, Ash = 5 %, Moisture = 8 %. ### Step 1 — Identify the basic combustion reactions For complete combustion, the four combustible elements react as follows: $$ C + O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 $$ $$ H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2} O_2 \longrightarrow H_2O $$ $$ S + O_2 \longrightarrow SO_2 $$ ### Step 2 — Mass of O₂ required per kg of each element - Carbon: 1 kg C requires $\dfrac{32}{12} = 2.667$ kg O₂. - Hydrogen: 1 kg H₂ requires $\dfrac{16}{2} = 8.0$ kg O₂. - Sulphur: 1 kg S requires $\dfrac{32}{32} = 1.0$ kg O₂. ### Step 3 — Compute the gross O₂ demand $$ O_{2,\text{required}} = \dfrac{2.667 \, C}{100} + \dfrac{8.0 \, (H - O/8)}{100} + \dfrac{1.0 \, S}{100} $$ **Note:** The hydrogen *available for combustion* is reduced by the oxygen already present in the fuel. Every 8 kg of fuel-bound oxygen "neutralises" 1 kg of hydrogen, hence the term $(H - O/8)$. ### Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values Given: C = 42, H = 6, O = 38, S = 0.5 (in % by mass). $$ H - \dfrac{O}{8} = 6 - \dfrac{38}{8} = 6 - 4.75 = 1.25 \, \% $$ $$ O_{2,\text{required}} = \dfrac{2.667 \times 42}{100} + \dfrac{8.0 \times 1.25}{100} + \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.5}{100} $$ $$ O_{2,\text{required}} = 1.120 + 0.100 + 0.005 = 1.225 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel} $$ ### Step 5 — Convert O₂ mass to air mass Air contains 23 % O₂ by mass. Therefore: $$ \text{Minimum air required} = \dfrac{1.225}{0.23} = 5.326 \text{ kg air / kg fuel} $$ > **Engineering interpretation:** This is the *minimum* (stoichiometric) air. In real combustors, **20–30 % excess air** is supplied to ensure complete combustion. So the actual air supplied would be ≈ **6.4–6.9 kg/kg fuel**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dulong's Formula for HHV from Ultimate Analysis **Objective:** Show how elemental composition mathematically translates to a fuel's heat release. Starting from the heats of combustion of pure elements at standard state (HHV basis): - $\Delta H_c (C) = 33.81 \text{ MJ/kg}$ - $\Delta H_c (H_2) = 143.0 \text{ MJ/kg}$ - $\Delta H_c (S) = 9.26 \text{ MJ/kg}$ These translate to **per-kg of fuel** (mass fractions: C/100, H/100, S/100): $$ \text{HHV}_{\text{ideal}} = 0.3381 \, C + 1.430 \, H + 0.0926 \, S \quad \text{[MJ/kg]} $$ The oxygen in the fuel forms combustion products (CO₂, H₂O) using part of the carbon/hydrogen, **reducing the effective combustible mass**. Empirical corrections (Dulong, 1880) yield: $$ \text{HHV} = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A \quad \text{[MJ/kg]} $$ > [!NOTE] > In KTU examinations, the simplified form is **frequently tested**: > $$\text{HHV (MJ/kg)} = 0.349 \, C + 1.178 \, H + 0.1005 \, S - 0.1034 \, O - 0.0151 \, N - 0.0211 \, A$$ > where all element symbols are in **mass percentage (dry basis)**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — HHV → LHV Conversion with Moisture Adjustment **Problem statement:** A biomass fuel has HHV = 18.5 MJ/kg, H = 5.8 %, and M = 10 %. Calculate the LHV. ### Step 1 — Write the LHV definition LHV = HHV − Energy carried away as **latent heat in water vapour** of the products of combustion. ### Step 2 — Total water produced per kg of fuel The total water comes from (i) fuel-bound hydrogen oxidation and (ii) original moisture in the fuel: $$ \text{Total water produced} = \dfrac{9 \, H}{100} + \dfrac{M}{100} \quad \text{[kg water/kg fuel]} $$ The factor **9** arises because 1 kg of H produces 9 kg of H₂O (from 2H + ½O₂ → H₂O; molar mass ratio 18/2 = 9). ### Step 3 — Apply latent heat of vaporisation At 25 °C, $h_f = 2{,}442$ kJ/kg = **2.442 MJ/kg**. $$ \text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 2.442 \times \left( \dfrac{9 \, H + M}{100} \right) $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute values $$ \text{LHV} = 18.5 - 2.442 \times \left( \dfrac{9 \times 5.8 + 10}{100} \right) $$ $$ \text{LHV} = 18.5 - 2.442 \times \left( \dfrac{52.2 + 10}{100} \right) $$ $$ \text{LHV} = 18.5 - 2.442 \times 0.622 $$ $$ \text{LHV} = 18.5 - 1.519 = 16.98 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ > **Interpretation:** Drying the fuel before combustion is crucial — every 1 % moisture ≈ **0.0244 MJ/kg energy penalty** in LHV. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Biomass Fuels | Property | Firewood | Rice Straw | Bagasse | Bagasse Pellets | Cattle Dung | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Moisture (%, w.b.) | 20 – 30 | 10 – 20 | 45 – 55 | 8 – 12 | 70 – 80 | | Volatile Matter (%) | 70 – 80 | 75 – 85 | 80 – 86 | 80 | 60 – 70 | | Fixed Carbon (%) | 18 – 22 | 12 – 18 | 10 – 16 | 14 – 17 | 12 – 18 | | Ash (%) | 0.5 – 2 | 10 – 20 | 1 – 4 | 2 – 4 | 15 – 25 | | HHV (MJ/kg, dry) | 18 – 20 | 14 – 16 | 18 – 19 | 18 – 19 | 14 – 17 | | Bulk density (kg/m³) | 200 – 400 | 60 – 120 | 90 – 150 | 550 – 700 | 150 – 250 | | Best use | Domestic | Boiler fuel | Cogeneration | Industrial | Biogas | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pearl:** Pelleting/briquetting **doubles to triples** the bulk density of agricultural residue, slashing transport cost and making it a viable industrial fuel. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Biomass Resource Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD rootA["BIOMASS RESOURCES"] rootB["Photosynthetically derived organic matter"] rootA --- rootB rootA --> catA["WOODY BIOMASS"] rootA --> catB["AGRO RESIDUES"] rootA --> catC["AQUATIC BIOMASS"] rootA --> catD["ANIMAL AND MSW"] rootA --> catE["ENERGY CROPS"] catA --> a1["Firewood"] catA --> a2["Sawdust"] catA --> a3["Wood chips"] catA --> a4["Bamboo"] catB --> b1["Field residues"] catB --> b2["Process residues"] b1 --> b1a["Rice straw"] b1 --> b1b["Wheat straw"] b1 --> b1c["Sugarcane trash"] b2 --> b2a["Rice husk"] b2 --> b2b["Bagasse"] b2 --> b2c["Coconut shell"] catC --> c1["Water hyacinth"] catC --> c2["Microalgae"] catC --> c3["Macroalgae"] catD --> d1["Cattle dung"] catD --> d2["Poultry litter"] catD --> d3["MSW organic fraction"] catD --> d4["Sewage sludge"] catE --> e1["Sugar crops"] catE --> e2["Oil seed crops"] catE --> e3["Lignocellulosic crops"] e1 --> e1a["Sugarcane"] e1 --> e1b["Sweet sorghum"] e2 --> e2a["Jatropha"] e2 --> e2b["Soybean"] e3 --> e3a["Switchgrass"] e3 --> e3b["Miscanthus"] ``` ## 4.2 Biomass-to-Energy Conversion Pathways (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR feedin["BIOMASS FEEDSTOCK
Wood, Agro-residue, MSW"] prep["PRE PROCESSING
Drying, Chipping, Pelletising"] feedin --> prep prep --> thermo["THERMOCHEMICAL ROUTE"] prep --> bioch["BIOCHEMICAL ROUTE"] prep --> phys["PHYSICAL EXTRACTION ROUTE"] thermo --> comb["Direct Combustion"] thermo --> gas["Gasification"] thermo --> pyr["Pyrolysis"] bioch --> ad["Anaerobic Digestion"] bioch --> ferm["Fermentation"] phys --> oil["Oil Pressing
Transesterification"] comb --> heat1["HEAT and STEAM"] gas --> syn["SYNGAS CO or H2"] pyr --> biooil["BIO OIL BIOCHAR"] ad --> biogas["BIOGAS CH4 CO2"] ferm --> etoh["ETHANOL C2H5OH"] oil --> bd["BIODIESEL FAME"] heat1 --> power1["Electricity via Steam Turbine"] syn --> power2["Electricity via IC Engine or Gas Turbine"] biogas --> power3["Electricity via Gas Engine"] etoh --> fuel1["Transport Fuel"] bd --> fuel2["Transport Fuel"] biooil --> fuel3["Boiler Fuel"] power1 --> grid["Electrical Grid"] power2 --> grid power3 --> grid ``` ## 4.3 Closed Carbon Cycle of Biomass (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR sun["SUN
Solar Radiation"] plant["PLANT
Photosynthesis"] biomass["BIOMASS
Carbohydrates Oil Lignin"] conv["CONVERSION
Burn Digest Ferment"] energy["USEFUL ENERGY
Heat Power Transport Fuel"] co2a["CO2 to Atmosphere"] sun -- "solar energy in" --> plant plant -- "absorbs CO2" --> co2a plant -- "stores chemical energy" --> biomass biomass -- "feedstock" --> conv conv -- "useful energy out" --> energy conv -- "CO2 release" --> co2a co2a -- "absorbed by next plant generation" --> plant ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Typical Biomass Cogeneration Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD f1["BIOMASS FUEL
Briquettes or Chips"] f2["BOILER
Water Wall Furnace"] f3["STEAM
High Pressure 40 to 80 bar"] f4["TURBINE
Back Pressure or Condensing"] f5["GENERATOR
Electrical Output"] f6["GRID
Electricity"] f7["PROCESS STEAM
Industrial Load"] f8["FLUE GAS
To Air Pollution Control"] f9["ASH
Fertiliser or Disposal"] f1 -- "fuel feeding" --> f2 f1 -- "produces ash" --> f9 f2 -- "steam generation" --> f3 f2 -- "exhaust gas" --> f8 f3 -- "steam expansion" --> f4 f4 -- "rotational power" --> f5 f5 -- "electricity" --> f6 f3 -- "extraction bleed" --> f7 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** *"Define biomass and explain why it is considered a renewable and carbon-neutral energy resource."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Biomass refers to the **biodegradable fraction of products, waste, and residues** from agriculture, forestry, and related industries that can be used as an energy source. It is **renewable** because the organic matter is continuously produced by living plants through photosynthesis, which uses sunlight as an inexhaustible energy source. The rate of regeneration (through replanting/cultivation) can be made equal to or greater than the rate of consumption. It is considered **carbon-neutral** because the CO₂ released during combustion or conversion of biomass is approximately equal to the CO₂ absorbed by the plant during its growth (via photosynthesis). Thus, the net addition of CO₂ to the atmosphere over a complete growth-combustion cycle is **near zero**, provided that harvesting is sustainable. > **Marks Distribution:** Definition (1) + Renewable explanation (1) + Carbon-neutral explanation (1) = 3 marks. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *"Differentiate between ultimate analysis and proximate analysis of a biomass fuel."* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Ultimate Analysis | Proximate Analysis | |---|---|---| | **What it measures** | Elemental composition (C, H, O, N, S) | Component composition (M, VM, FC, A) | | **Output** | Mass % of elements | Mass % of components | | **Use** | Theoretical air calculation, HHV by Dulong's formula | Combustion behaviour, boiler design | | **Method** | CHN analyser / chemical methods | Heating in furnace at controlled temperatures | | **Information type** | Fundamental chemical constituents | Operational / physical constituents | > **Marks Distribution:** 1 mark for ultimate + 1 mark for proximate + 1 mark for clear difference. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) ### Question A (Module Internal Choice Option 1) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3, Set A]** *"Classify biomass resources in detail. With neat sketches, explain the biochemical and thermochemical conversion routes for biomass to energy, giving suitable examples for each route."* **(14 marks)** ### (a) Classification of Biomass Resources (7 marks) Biomass is broadly classified into **five categories**: 1. **Woody Biomass** — Derived from trees and shrubs; includes firewood, sawdust, wood chips, pellets, charcoal. Energy plantations (*Eucalyptus*, *Poplar*) fall here. CV: 18–20 MJ/kg (dry). 2. **Agricultural Residues** — Sub-divided into: - **Field residues:** left in field after harvest — rice/wheat straw, maize stover, sugarcane trash. - **Process residues:** generated in processing — bagasse, rice husk, groundnut shell, coconut shell. 3. **Aquatic Biomass** — Grown in water bodies; includes water hyacinth, microalgae, and seaweed. Microalgae can yield **10–30 times more biomass per hectare** than terrestrial crops. 4. **Animal Wastes and MSW** — Cattle dung, poultry litter, slaughterhouse waste, kitchen waste, sewage sludge. Predominantly used for **biogas production** via anaerobic digestion. 5. **Energy Crops** — Crops specifically cultivated for fuel production: sugarcane and sweet sorghum (ethanol), Jatropha and soybean (biodiesel), switchgrass and Miscanthus (lignocellulosic ethanol / direct combustion). ### (b) Biomass Conversion Routes (7 marks) Biomass is converted to useful energy through **three principal routes**: **(i) Thermochemical Conversion (uses heat):** - **Direct Combustion:** Burning biomass in excess air to produce hot flue gases, which generate steam that drives a turbine. Common in bagasse-based cogeneration plants (e.g., sugar mills). - **Gasification:** Partial oxidation at 800–1000 °C to produce **syngas** (CO + H₂), which can be used in IC engines, gas turbines, or for synthesis of liquid fuels. - **Pyrolysis:** Heating in absence of air at 400–600 °C to produce **bio-oil, biochar, and pyro-gas**. Used to produce upgraded liquid fuel. **(ii) Biochemical Conversion (uses micro-organisms):** - **Anaerobic Digestion:** Decomposition of wet biomass (dung, MSW) in absence of oxygen to produce **biogas** (≈ 60 % CH₄ + 40 % CO₂), plus digestate (organic fertiliser). - **Fermentation:** Yeast-mediated conversion of sugars/starch into **ethanol**; used for sugarcane molasses or corn-based ethanol. **(iii) Physical/Mechanical Conversion:** - **Oil Extraction + Transesterification:** Oilseeds (Jatropha, soybean) are crushed; the crude oil is reacted with methanol (catalyst: NaOH) to produce **biodiesel (FAME)** + glycerol. **Examples Tabulated:** | Route | Process | Example | Product | |---|---|---|---| | Thermo | Combustion | Bagasse boiler | Steam / electricity | | Thermo | Gasification | Rice husk gasifier | Syngas | | Thermo | Pyrolysis | Wood pyrolysis | Bio-oil | | Bio | Anaerobic digestion | Cattle dung digester | Biogas | | Bio | Fermentation | Sugarcane molasses | Ethanol | | Physical | Transesterification | Jatropha oil | Biodiesel | > **Marks Distribution:** (a) Classification 7 marks (1 for intro + 5 categories ≈ 1 mark each + 1 for diagram/explanation); (b) Conversion routes 7 marks (Thermo 3 + Bio 3 + Physical 1). --- ### Question B (Module Internal Choice Option 2) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3, Set B]** *"A biomass fuel has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 45 %, H = 6 %, O = 38 %, N = 1 %, S = 0.5 %, Ash = 5 %, Moisture = 4 %. Calculate:* *(a) The stoichiometric air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of this fuel, and* *(b) The Higher Heating Value (HHV) using Dulong's formula. Also determine the Lower Heating Value (LHV)."* **(14 marks)** ### (a) Stoichiometric Air Calculation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Hydrogen available for combustion (corrected for fuel-bound oxygen):** $$ H_{\text{net}} = H - \dfrac{O}{8} = 6 - \dfrac{38}{8} = 6 - 4.75 = 1.25 \, \% $$ **Step 2 — Mass of O₂ needed per kg of each combustible:** $$ O_{2,\text{C}} = \dfrac{32}{12} \times \dfrac{C}{100} = 2.667 \times 0.45 = 1.200 \text{ kg} $$ $$ O_{2,\text{H}} = \dfrac{16}{2} \times \dfrac{H_{\text{net}}}{100} = 8.0 \times 0.0125 = 0.100 \text{ kg} $$ $$ O_{2,\text{S}} = \dfrac{32}{32} \times \dfrac{S}{100} = 1.0 \times 0.005 = 0.005 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3 — Total stoichiometric O₂ per kg of fuel:** $$ O_{2,\text{total}} = 1.200 + 0.100 + 0.005 = 1.305 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{/kg fuel} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to air (air is 23 % O₂ by mass):** $$ \text{Air}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{1.305}{0.23} = 5.674 \text{ kg air/kg fuel} $$ **Step 5 — If 30 % excess air is supplied (typical for boilers):** $$ \text{Actual air supplied} = 1.30 \times 5.674 = 7.376 \text{ kg air/kg fuel} $$ > **Marks:** 3 marks for H-O/8 correction, 2 marks for O₂ calculation, 2 marks for air calculation. --- ### (b) HHV and LHV Calculation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Apply Dulong's formula:** $$ \text{HHV} = 0.3491C + 1.1783H + 0.1005S - 0.1034O - 0.0151N - 0.0211A $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values (C=45, H=6, S=0.5, O=38, N=1, A=5):** $$ \text{HHV} = (0.3491 \times 45) + (1.1783 \times 6) + (0.1005 \times 0.5) - (0.1034 \times 38) - (0.0151 \times 1) - (0.0211 \times 5) $$ $$ \text{HHV} = 15.7095 + 7.0698 + 0.0503 - 3.9292 - 0.0151 - 0.1055 $$ $$ \text{HHV} = 18.78 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ **Step 3 — Compute LHV using moisture and hydrogen content:** $$ \text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 2.442 \times \left( \dfrac{9H + M}{100} \right) $$ $$ \text{LHV} = 18.78 - 2.442 \times \left( \dfrac{9 \times 6 + 4}{100} \right) $$ $$ \text{LHV} = 18.78 - 2.442 \times \left( \dfrac{54 + 4}{100} \right) $$ $$ \text{LHV} = 18.78 - 2.442 \times 0.58 $$ $$ \text{LHV} = 18.78 - 1.416 = 17.36 \text{ MJ/kg} $$ **Step 4 — Sanity check:** The HHV of 18.78 MJ/kg lies in the expected range for a typical lignocellulosic biomass (16–20 MJ/kg dry), validating the calculation. > **Marks:** 3 marks HHV calculation, 2 marks LHV setup, 2 marks final numerical substitution and answer. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:** > 1. **Forgetting the (H − O/8) correction** in air-fuel calculations. This is the most common error and typically costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Using 21 % O₂ by volume** instead of **23 % O₂ by mass** for air. Always re-check: the 23 % mass basis is used in Dulong's fuel formula; the 21 % volume basis is used for flue gas analysis. > 3. **Confusing HHV and LHV** — examiners explicitly test this with the latent heat term.
