Use $h_f = 2.442$ MJ/kg at 25 °C. > 4. **Not specifying "dry basis" or "as-received"** in numerical answers — leads to deduction of ½ mark. > 5. **Skipping units** in the final answer — strict KTU board policy: units are mandatory. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > Use this checklist 24 hours before the KTU examination for rapid revision. ### Core Definitions - ☐ Biomass = biodegradable organic matter usable as fuel. - ☐ Carbon-neutral = CO₂ absorbed in growth = CO₂ released in combustion. - ☐ 1st-generation biofuels = from food crops (sugarcane, corn, soybean). - ☐ 2nd-generation biofuels = from lignocellulosic / non-food biomass. - ☐ 3rd-generation biofuels = from algae. - ☐ 4th-generation biofuels = from genetically engineered organisms / electrofuels. ### Five Major Categories (MUST memorise) - ☐ Woody biomass → firewood, sawdust, pellets. - ☐ Agro-residues → rice straw, bagasse, rice husk, coconut shell. - ☐ Aquatic biomass → algae, water hyacinth. - ☐ Animal waste & MSW → cattle dung, poultry litter, kitchen waste. - ☐ Energy crops → Jatropha, sugarcane, switchgrass, Miscanthus. ### Analyses (MUST memorise the components) - ☐ **Proximate:** Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash (sum = 100 %). - ☐ **Ultimate:** C, H, O, N, S, Ash. ### Three Conversion Routes - ☐ **Thermochemical:** Combustion, Gasification, Pyrolysis. - ☐ **Biochemical:** Anaerobic Digestion, Fermentation. - ☐ **Physical:** Oil extraction + Transesterification. ### Critical Numerical Constants - ☐ Latent heat of vaporisation of water: $h_f = 2.442$ MJ/kg (at 25 °C). - ☐ Mass % of O₂ in air: **23 %**. - ☐ Volumetric % of O₂ in air: **21 %**. - ☐ Density of methane: 0.717 kg/m³ at NTP. - ☐ HHV of pure methane: 55.5 MJ/kg; LHV: 50.0 MJ/kg. - ☐ Biogas ≈ 60 % CH₄ + 40 % CO₂ → CV ≈ **21.5 MJ/m³**. ### Must-Know Formulas (Dulong's, AFR, LHV, Combustion efficiency) - ☐ Dulong's formula (refer to Section 2.3). - ☐ AFR = (11.6C + 34.8(H − O/8) + 4.35S) / 100 [kg air/kg fuel]. - ☐ LHV = HHV − 2.442 × (9H + M)/100. - ☐ Combustion efficiency = (T₁ − T₂)/(T₁ − Tₐ) × 100. ### Typical Energy Values (Quick-Reference) - ☐ Dry woody biomass: 18–20 MJ/kg. - ☐ Dry agricultural residue: 14–17 MJ/kg. - ☐ Cattle dung cake: 14–16 MJ/kg. - ☐ Biogas: 21.5 MJ/m³. - ☐ Ethanol: 26.8 MJ/kg. - ☐ Biodiesel (FAME): 37–40 MJ/kg. - ☐ Petrol (for comparison): 44 MJ/kg. ### KTU Board Frequently-Tested Pitfalls - ☐ The (H − O/8) correction in AFR — **never forget**. - ☐ Energy ratio > 1 is the sustainability test for any biofuel. - ☐ Photosynthetic efficiency of biomass = 1–2 % (low!) — explains why biomass requires large land area. - ☐ Cogeneration (combined heat & power) improves total efficiency to **> 70 %**. > [!TIP] > **Final Exam Tip (From the Examiner's Desk):** In 14-mark derivation questions, examiners always award 2 marks for a **correctly labelled block diagram**. Always include a flowchart of the conversion route (Combustion / Gasification / AD / Fermentation) even if not explicitly asked — it fetches easy marks. ---

topics.title: Biomass Conversion Technologies - Fixed Dome Type Biogas Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomass Conversion Technologies - Fixed Dome Type Biogas Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fixed Dome Type Biogas Plant** (also called a **Chinese Fixed Dome Plant** or its Indian variant, the **Deenbandhu Biogas Plant**) is a permanent, masonry-built, underground anaerobic digestion system in which the digester and the gas-holder share a single, structurally rigid dome. Unlike floating-drum designs, the gas storage space is an integral, immovable upper portion of the digester vessel itself. Biogas accumulates in the sealed upper dome and exerts pressure on the slurry below, displacing an equal volume of digested slurry upward into an attached **outlet / expansion / compensation tank**, which acts as the variable-volume gas-pressure regulator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Biomass Conversion):**
> Students must be able to draw and label the schematic of a Fixed Dome Biogas Plant, explain the hydraulic-displacement principle, list the merits/demerits versus floating-drum plants, and apply the standard design formulas for digester volume, hydraulic retention time (HRT), and gas yield.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Submerged Balloon in a Swimming Pool"

Imagine a rigid, sealed plastic ball weighted down into a backyard swimming pool so that its open neck points downward and connects, through a flexible pipe, to a smaller bucket placed at the pool's edge at a **higher level**. As you slowly blow air into the ball, the air pushes down on the water inside the ball. Because water cannot be compressed, exactly the same volume of water is forced out through the flexible pipe into the higher bucket. The air pressure inside the ball stays roughly constant because the *height difference* between the ball's water-level and the bucket's water-level never changes much.

This is precisely how a fixed-dome plant operates:

| Pool Analogy | Real Plant Component |
|---|---|
| Sealed rigid plastic ball | Fixed underground concrete / masonry dome |
| Air pumped in | Biogas generated by anaerobic bacteria |
| Water displaced | Digested slurry pushed out |
| Higher bucket | Outlet / Compensation tank |
| Flexible pipe | Slurry outlet pipe |
| Height difference | Determines gas pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> The gas pressure in a fixed dome plant is therefore **not constant** — it is proportional to the difference in slurry level between the digester and the compensation tank. This is the single most important conceptual distinction from a floating-drum (constant-pressure) plant.

## 1.3 Salient Technical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT):** $\mathbf{30}$ to $\mathbf{50 \text{ days}}$ for cattle dung at mesophilic conditions
- **Operating Temperature (Mesophilic):** $\mathbf{30 \,^{\circ}\text{C}}$ to $\mathbf{40 \,^{\circ}\text{C}}$
- **Optimum pH range:** $\mathbf{6.8}$ to $\mathbf{7.5}$
- **Carbon-to-Nitrogen (C/N) Ratio:** $\mathbf{20 : 1}$ to $\mathbf{30 : 1}$
- **Typical plant life:** $\mathbf{15 \text{ to } 20 \text{ years}}$
- **Standard family sizes (KVIC):** $\mathbf{1 \text{ m}^3}$, $\mathbf{2 \text{ m}^3}$, $\mathbf{4 \text{ m}^3}$, $\mathbf{6 \text{ m}^3}$ gas/day
- **Density of slurry:** approximately $\mathbf{1080 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ (water-rich dung slurry)
- **Design gas pressure:** $\mathbf{0.7 \text{ to } 1.2 \text{ m water column}}$ ($\approx \mathbf{7 \text{ to } 12 \text{ kPa}}$)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Constituent Components — Structured Breakdown

A complete Fixed Dome Biogas Plant consists of the following functional sub-units. Each one has a defined engineering role, and KTU questions frequently ask the student to *name the component and state its function*.

### A. Inlet Pipe / Charging Tank (Mixing Chamber)
- Positioned at ground level on one side of the plant.
- Receives the daily charge of fresh biomass (cattle dung, kitchen waste, agricultural residue, poultry droppings).
- Acts as a **mixing pit** where the substrate is homogenized with water in the correct ratio.
- **Slurry dilution ratio:** typically **1 : 1** (substrate : water by weight).

### B. Digester (Fermentation Chamber)
- The largest, sealed, underground, dome-shaped chamber where **anaerobic digestion** occurs.
- Built from brick masonry, stone masonry, or reinforced concrete — never steel (corrosion by $\text{H}_2\text{S}$).
- Shape: cylindrical lower portion topped with a hemispherical / spherical dome.
- Houses the active methanogenic bacterial consortium.
- Volume is sized for the chosen HRT.

### C. Gas Storage Dome (Fixed, Rigid Upper Section)
- The upper sealed portion of the same vessel — *not* a separate floating unit.
- Stores the biogas produced by bacterial action.
- Pressure here drives slurry outward whenever a cock is opened for gas use.

### D. Outlet Pipe / Displacement Pipe
- A pipe connecting the top region of the digester (just below the gas dome) to the compensation tank.
- Carries the displaced slurry upward and outward.
- Submerged water seal at its base prevents gas leakage.

### E. Compensation / Expansion / Displacement Tank
- An open-top, smaller tank located **at a higher elevation** than the digester's gas-dome crown.
- Receives the displaced slurry.
- Its water-level acts as the **variable-pressure regulator**.
- Also serves as the **slurry overflow** for plant operators to remove digested slurry for use as bio-manure.

### F. Gas Outlet Pipe with Valve
- Tapped from the topmost point of the fixed dome.
- Carries the biogas via a valve to the point of utilization (cooking stove, lamp, engine).

### G. Stirrer / Agitator (Optional)
- Manual or mechanical device to break the surface scum layer.
- Promotes uniform bacterial activity and prevents dead zones.

### H. Slurry Inspection / Manhole Port
- Sealed access port for periodic maintenance, cleaning, and inspection.

## 2.2 Working Principle — The Hydraulic Displacement Mechanism

The operating sequence of a fixed dome plant follows a strict cyclic logic:

1. **Charging:** Fresh biomass slurry of total mass $M_{in}$ is fed into the digester through the inlet pipe.
2. **Bacterial Action:** Inside the sealed anaerobic environment, a four-stage microbial cascade — **Hydrolysis $\rightarrow$ Acidogenesis $\rightarrow$ Acetogenesis $\rightarrow$ Methanogenesis** — converts the organic matter into biogas (approximately $\mathbf{55\%}$ to $\mathbf{65\%}$ $\text{CH}_4$, $\mathbf{30\%}$ to $\mathbf{40\%}$ $\text{CO}_2$, trace $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ and water vapor).
3. **Gas Accumulation:** Biogas rises and accumulates in the upper fixed dome, compressing the headspace.
4. **Slurry Displacement:** Because liquids are practically incompressible, the increasing gas volume displaces an **exactly equal volume** of digested slurry.
5. **Outward Flow:** The displaced slurry travels up the outlet pipe and into the compensation tank.
6. **Pressure Build-up:** Gas pressure $P_g$ is balanced by the hydrostatic pressure of the slurry column, given by:

$$P_g = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot \Delta h$$

where $\Delta h$ is the difference in slurry levels between the compensation tank and the digester's gas dome base.

7. **Gas Utilization:** When a consumer opens the gas cock, gas flows out, pressure drops, slurry flows back from the compensation tank into the digester — and the cycle repeats.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Fixed" Dome?** Because the dome is structurally rigid (concrete/brick), unlike the movable steel drum of the KVIC floating-gasholder plant. This eliminates all moving parts, prevents rust, and dramatically extends plant life.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Digester Volume | $V_{digester} = V_g \times HRT$ | $\text{m}^3$ | $V_g$ is daily gas volume |
| 2 | Daily Slurry Feed | $M_{feed} = \dfrac{V_{digester}}{HRT}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ | Total volume fed per day |
| 3 | Daily Gas Yield | $V_g = M_{feed} \times Y_g$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ | $Y_g$ = gas yield per $\text{m}^3$ slurry |
| 4 | Biogas Pressure | $P_g = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot \Delta h$ | $\text{Pa}$ | $\Delta h$ in metres |
| 5 | Methane Fraction | $f_{CH_4} \approx 0.55 \text{ to } 0.65$ | dimensionless | Empirical, depends on substrate |
| 6 | Methane Volume | $V_{CH_4} = f_{CH_4} \times V_g$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ | Useful energy carrier |
| 7 | Energy Output | $E = V_{CH_4} \times \rho_{CH_4} \times CV_{CH_4}$ | $\text{kJ/day}$ | $CV_{CH_4} \approx 55.5 \text{ MJ/kg}$ |
| 8 | HRT (typical cattle dung) | $HRT = 30 \text{ to } 50$ | $\text{days}$ | Mesophilic range |
| 9 | Slurry Dilution | $\dfrac{V_{substrate}}{V_{water}} \approx 1:1$ | ratio | By weight or volume |
| 10 | Optimum pH | $6.8 \le pH \le 7.5$ | — | Below 6.0 = acid failure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all KTU numerical problems, the substitution path is **always:**
> Daily gas requirement $\rightarrow$ Daily slurry mass $\rightarrow$ Digester volume (multiply by HRT). Reverse this and you will lose marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Rural Domestic Energy:** Cooking gas for individual households (typical 2–6 m³/day family plant).
- **Institutional Kitchens:** Hostels, temples, prisons, panchayat buildings.
- **Small-Scale Power Generation:** Dual-fuel engines running irrigation pumps (1 m³ biogas $\approx$ 0.6 L diesel equivalent).
- **Organic Fertilizer Co-Production:** Digested slurry as nitrogen-rich bio-manure (N-P-K $\approx$ 1.5-1.0-0.8).
- **Waste Management for Dairy Farms and Slaughterhouses.**
- **Sanitation & Public Health** when integrated with household toilets (e.g., DEWATS systems).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Digester Volume Formula

The fundamental sizing relationship for any anaerobic digester can be derived from a simple mass-balance argument on the slurry volume inside the digester.

**Step 1 — State the governing concept.**
The digester must retain each parcel of slurry long enough for the methanogenic bacteria to convert a usable fraction of its organic content into biogas. This required residence time is the **Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT)**, measured in days.

**Step 2 — Define HRT mathematically.**
If $V_{digester}$ is the working volume of the digester and $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate of fresh slurry entering (and equal volume leaving) per day, then:

$$HRT = \dfrac{V_{digester}}{Q} \quad \text{(in days)}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the digester volume.**

$$V_{digester} = Q \times HRT$$

**Step 4 — Express $Q$ in terms of the desired daily gas output.**
If $Y_g$ is the gas yield per cubic metre of slurry (typical value $Y_g \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10 \text{ m}^3 \text{ gas per m}^3 \text{ slurry}$ for cattle dung), then:

$$Q = \dfrac{V_g}{Y_g}$$

**Step 5 — Combine the two relations.**

$$V_{digester} = \dfrac{V_g \times HRT}{Y_g}$$

This is the **canonical KTU board formula** for sizing any fixed-dome biogas plant.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> **Problem (Model Question):**
> A household requires $\mathbf{2 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}}$ of biogas. The substrate is cattle dung with a gas yield of $\mathbf{0.06 \, \text{m}^3 \text{ gas per m}^3 \text{ slurry}}$. The chosen hydraulic retention time is $\mathbf{40 \text{ days}}$. Calculate:
> (a) The required daily slurry feed volume.
> (b) The volume of the digester.
> (c) The volume of methane produced per day (assume 60% methane content).

### Solution

**(a) Daily slurry feed volume $Q$:**

$$Q = \dfrac{V_g}{Y_g} = \dfrac{2 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}}{0.06 \, \text{m}^3 \text{ gas} / \text{m}^3 \text{ slurry}}$$

$$Q = 33.33 \, \text{m}^3 \text{ slurry/day}$$

**(b) Digester volume $V_{digester}$:**

$$V_{digester} = Q \times HRT = 33.33 \times 40$$

$$V_{digester} = 1333.33 \, \text{m}^3$$

**(c) Methane volume per day $V_{CH_4}$:**

$$V_{CH_4} = f_{CH_4} \times V_g = 0.60 \times 2 = 1.20 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students commit the dimensional error of dividing $V_g$ by $Y_g$ the wrong way around. Always check: $\dfrac{\text{m}^3 \text{ gas}}{\text{m}^3 \text{ slurry per m}^3 \text{ gas}} \rightarrow \text{m}^3 \text{ slurry}$. If your units do not cancel to give slurry volume, you have inverted $Y_g$.

---

## 3.3 Pressure Derivation in the Compensation Tank

The gas pressure inside a fixed dome depends on the **vertical height difference** between the slurry level in the compensation tank and the gas–slurry interface inside the digester.

**Step 1 — Consider a static fluid column.**
A vertical column of slurry of height $\Delta h$ exerts a hydrostatic pressure at its base given by Pascal's law:

$$P_{hydrostatic} = \rho \cdot g \cdot \Delta h$$

**Step 2 — Apply mechanical equilibrium at the gas–slurry interface.**
The internal gas pressure must exactly balance this hydrostatic head; otherwise, slurry would either flow into or out of the digester.

$$P_g = \rho_{slurry} \cdot g \cdot \Delta h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute standard numerical values.**
Take $\rho_{slurry} = 1080 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, $\Delta h = 1.0 \, \text{m}$ (typical):

$$P_g = 1080 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 \approx 10595 \, \text{Pa} \approx 10.6 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to "cm of water column" for engineering intuition.**
Since $1 \, \text{cm H}_2\text{O} = 98.1 \, \text{Pa}$:

$$\Delta h_{cm} = \dfrac{10595}{98.1} \approx 108 \, \text{cm of water column}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the compensation tank must be placed **at least 0.8–1.2 m above the digester crown**. If it is placed too low, the gas pressure is insufficient to push gas through domestic pipelines; if placed too high, the structural load on the dome increases.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Fixed Dome vs. Floating Drum

| Parameter | Fixed Dome Plant | Floating Drum Plant (KVIC) |
|---|---|---|
| Gas Holder | Rigid masonry dome (immovable) | Movable steel drum floating on water |
| Gas Pressure | Variable (depends on slurry level in compensation tank) | Constant (set by drum weight) |
| Moving Parts | None | Drum, guide frame, rollers |
| Corrosion Risk | Negligible | High (steel + $\text{H}_2\text{S}$) |
| Plant Life | 15–20 years | 5–10 years |
| Capital Cost | Lower (masonry) | Higher (steel fabrication) |
| Gas Yield Consistency | Slightly lower (initial 1–2 months slow) | Higher from day one |
| Maintenance Skill | Skilled mason required | Standard fabrication |
| Space Required | Underground — surface free | Above ground — large footprint |
| Construction Complexity | High (curved masonry) | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Schematic Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fixed Dome Biogas Plant

The Mermaid diagram below maps the functional flow of biomass, slurry, and gas through the plant. Each labelled node corresponds to a real physical component described in Section 2.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[FRESH BIOMASS\nCattle dung / kitchen waste] --> nodeB[INLET CHARGING TANK\nMixing pit 1:1 with water]
    nodeB --> nodeC[DIGESTER\nUnderground masonry chamber\nHRT 30 to 50 days]
    nodeC --> nodeD[ANAEROBIC BACTERIA\nHydrolysis to Methanogenesis]
    nodeD --> nodeE[FIXED GAS STORAGE DOME\nUpper sealed hemispherical space]
    nodeE --> nodeF[GAS OUTLET VALVE\nSupply to stove or engine]
    nodeC --> nodeG[OUTLET DISPLACEMENT PIPE\nUptake tube connecting dome to compensation tank]
    nodeG --> nodeH[COMPENSATION TANK\nOpen top tank placed at higher elevation]
    nodeH --> nodeI[DISPLACED SLURRY OVERFLOW\nBio manure for agriculture]
    nodeE -. pressure balances .-> nodeH
    nodeJ[STIRRER AGITATOR\nOptional manual mixer] -. breaks scum .-> nodeC
    nodeK[MANHOLE INSPECTION PORT\nSealed access for maintenance] -. access .-> nodeC
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Solid arrows** denote the *primary flow* of slurry, biomass, or gas.
- **Dotted arrows** denote *ancillary interactions* (pressure balance, agitation, maintenance access).
- The double relationship `nodeE <-> nodeH` captures the central hydraulic-displacement principle: gas produced in the dome pushes slurry up to the compensation tank; conversely, when gas is consumed, slurry returns under gravity.

## 4.2 Functional Flow Sequence

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[STEP 1 Fresh substrate charged] --> stepB[STEP 2 Slurry enters digester]
    stepB --> stepC[STEP 3 Bacteria digest anaerobically]
    stepC --> stepD[STEP 4 Biogas accumulates in fixed dome]
    stepD --> stepE[STEP 5 Dome pressure rises and pushes slurry up]
    stepE --> stepF[STEP 6 Slurry overflows into compensation tank]
    stepF --> stepG[STEP 7 Gas drawn off via valve to consumer]
    stepG --> stepH[STEP 8 Pressure drops and slurry flows back]
    stepH --> stepC
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and letter-prefixed (`nodeA`, `stepA`, etc.).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) used as standalone node names.
> - Inside double-quoted labels, only raw uppercase alphanumeric text and standard punctuation are used — no bold, italics, or HTML tables embedded.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar Model]**
*Explain the principle of operation of a Fixed Dome Biogas Plant with a neat sketch. List any two advantages of this plant over a floating-drum plant.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Principle (2 marks):** A fixed dome biogas plant operates on the principle of **hydraulic displacement of slurry**. Fresh biomass slurry is charged daily into the underground masonry digester, where anaerobic bacteria convert it into biogas. The accumulated biogas rises into the sealed, rigid upper dome and exerts pressure on the slurry. Because slurry is incompressible, an exactly equal volume of digested slurry is pushed up the outlet pipe into a **compensation tank** located at a higher elevation. Gas pressure is determined by the height difference of slurry levels.
- **Two advantages (1 mark):**
  1. No moving parts → no corrosion → plant life of **15–20 years**.
  2. Lower long-term cost and free of rust problems compared to the steel floating drum.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Similar Model]**
*Define Hydraulic Retention Time (HRT). What is its typical range for cattle-dung-based plants, and how does it affect digester sizing?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** HRT is the average number of days a unit volume of slurry remains inside the digester from entry to exit. $HRT = V_{digester}/Q$.
- **Range (1 mark):** For cattle dung in the mesophilic temperature range, HRT is typically **30 to 50 days**.
- **Effect on sizing (1 mark):** $V_{digester} = Q \times HRT$. A longer HRT demands a larger digester for the same daily gas output, increasing capital cost but improving gas yield stability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** Two alternative questions are provided below. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks

**(a)** With the help of a labelled schematic, explain the construction and working of a **Fixed Dome Biogas Plant**. Describe the role of each component in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small dairy farm needs $\mathbf{4 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}}$ of biogas. Using cattle dung with a gas yield of $\mathbf{0.05 \, \text{m}^3 \text{ gas per m}^3 \text{ slurry}}$ and an HRT of $\mathbf{40 \text{ days}}$, calculate: (i) the daily slurry volume required, (ii) the digester volume, and (iii) the daily methane output (assume 60% methane in biogas). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**

| Component | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Inlet pipe / charging tank | Receives and mixes fresh substrate with water in 1:1 ratio | 1 |
| Underground masonry digester | Sealed chamber for anaerobic digestion; sized using HRT formula | 1.5 |
| Fixed gas dome (upper rigid section) | Stores produced biogas; structurally rigid (concrete / brick) | 1 |
| Outlet / displacement pipe | Carries displaced slurry upward to compensation tank | 1 |
| Compensation tank (open top, at higher elevation) | Receives overflow slurry; sets gas pressure by its water level | 1.5 |
| Gas outlet valve and pipeline | Delivers biogas to consumer end-use | 0.5 |
| Stirrer / manhole (optional) | Breaks scum, enables maintenance | 0.5 |

**Working principle (4 lines, to be written in exam):** Fresh slurry enters the digester → anaerobic methanogens act for 30–50 days → biogas ($\approx$ 60% $\text{CH}_4$ + 40% $\text{CO}_2$ + trace $\text{H}_2\text{S}$) accumulates in the sealed upper dome → gas pressure $P_g = \rho g \Delta h$ pushes slurry up the outlet pipe into the compensation tank → gas is drawn off via valve → pressure drops → slurry returns under gravity.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

(i) **Daily slurry volume $Q$:**

$$Q = \dfrac{V_g}{Y_g} = \dfrac{4 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}}{0.05 \, \text{m}^3/\text{m}^3} = 80 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

(ii) **Digester volume:**

$$V_{digester} = Q \times HRT = 80 \times 40 = 3200 \, \text{m}^3$$

(iii) **Daily methane output:**

$$V_{CH_4} = 0.60 \times 4 = 2.4 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Compare **Fixed Dome** and **Floating Drum** biogas plants under the following heads: gas pressure behavior, plant life, capital cost, maintenance, corrosion resistance, and surface footprint. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship $V_{digester} = V_g \times HRT / Y_g$ step by step. A community of $\mathbf{50 \text{ families}}$ needs $\mathbf{2 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}}$ of biogas per family. With HRT $= 45$ days and $Y_g = 0.08$, find the total digester volume required for the community plant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Fixed Dome | Floating Drum |
|---|---|---|
| Gas pressure | Variable — depends on slurry level in compensation tank | Constant — set by drum weight |
| Plant life | 15–20 years | 5–10 years |
| Capital cost | Lower long-term (masonry) | Higher (steel fabrication) |
| Maintenance | Requires skilled mason for any crack repair | Standard welding and painting work |
| Corrosion resistance | Excellent (no metal exposed to gas) | Poor ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$ attacks steel drum) |
| Surface footprint | Negligible — entirely underground | Large — drum and guide frame above ground |
| Initial gas production | Slow start (1–2 months) | Immediate gas output |
| Construction skill | High (curved masonry) | Moderate |

(7 marks — award approx. 1 mark per row × 6 = 6, plus 1 mark for any additional correct remark.)

**Part (b) — Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Derivation (3 marks):**

From the definition of HRT:

$$HRT = \dfrac{V_{digester}}{Q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{digester} = Q \times HRT$$

From the definition of gas yield per unit slurry:

$$Y_g = \dfrac{V_g}{Q} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = \dfrac{V_g}{Y_g}$$

Substituting into the first equation:

$$V_{digester} = \dfrac{V_g}{Y_g} \times HRT = \dfrac{V_g \times HRT}{Y_g}$$

**Numerical (4 marks):**

Total daily gas requirement:

$$V_g = 50 \times 2 = 100 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

Digester volume:

$$V_{digester} = \dfrac{100 \times 45}{0.08} = \dfrac{4500}{0.08} = 56250 \, \text{m}^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Many students write $V_{digester} = V_g \times HRT$ *without* dividing by $Y_g$. This gives a dimensionally wrong answer (gives litres instead of cubic metres). Always include $Y_g$ in the denominator.
> 2. **Skipping the schematic in 7-mark questions:** A 7-mark Part (a) question *demands* a labelled diagram. Without it, a student typically loses 2–3 marks even with good text.
> 3. **Forgetting the compensation tank:** Examiners specifically look for whether you have explained *where the displaced slurry goes* and *how gas pressure is set*. A mere description of the digester alone is insufficient.
> 4. **Writing "HRT = 30" without justification:** Always quote the *range* (30–50 days) and state that it applies under mesophilic conditions with cattle dung.
> 5. **Confusing gas yield $Y_g$ with HRT:** $Y_g$ has units of $\text{m}^3 \text{ gas per m}^3 \text{ slurry}$; HRT has units of days. They are entirely different quantities.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A Fixed Dome Biogas Plant is an underground, masonry-built anaerobic digester with an integral, rigid gas-storage dome and a separate **compensation tank**.
- **Core Principle:** Hydraulic displacement — biogas pushes slurry out into the compensation tank.
- **Key Components (Mnemonic "I-D-O-C-G-S-M"):** **I**nlet pipe, **D**igester, **O**utlet pipe, **C**ompensation tank, **G**as outlet, **S**tirrer, **M**anhole.
- **Standard HRT:** 30–50 days (mesophilic, cattle dung).
- **Sizing Formula:** $V_{digester} = (V_g \times HRT) / Y_g$.
- **Methane Content:** 55–65% by volume.
- **Gas Pressure:** Variable; depends on slurry column height $\Delta h$ between digester and compensation tank.
- **Plant Life:** 15–20 years (much longer than floating drum).
- **No moving parts** → no corrosion → minimal maintenance.
- **Disadvantages:** High initial construction skill, slow initial gas output, gas leakage through dome cracks possible if masonry quality is poor.
- **Typical Use:** Rural household cooking, small institutions, dairy farms, community-level plants.
- **Must-draw in exams:** Labelled schematic showing inlet, digester, gas dome, outlet pipe, compensation tank, gas valve.
- **Variants:** Chinese Fixed Dome, Indian **Deenbandhu** (hemispherical, lower cost), and KVIC standardized designs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Urban Waste to Energy Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Urban Waste to Energy Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Urban Waste to Energy Conversion (UWtE)** — also called **Municipal Solid Waste to Energy (MSWtE)** — is defined as the systematic recovery of useful thermal, electrical, or mechanical energy from heterogeneous urban refuse through a chain of physical, thermochemical, and biochemical processes, while simultaneously reducing the waste volume destined for sanitary landfills.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework (OEEET613, Module 3), urban waste encompasses:

- **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** — household, commercial, institutional, and street-cleaning refuse.
- **Industrial Waste (non-hazardous)** — construction & demolition debris, paper-mill sludge.
- **Bio-medical Waste (BMW)** — handled under separate, regulated streams.
- **E-Waste** — electronic scrap containing recoverable metals.
- **Sewage Sludge** — semi-solid residue from wastewater treatment.

The three engineering pillars of UWtE are:

1. **Waste Minimization & Segregation at Source**
2. **Material Recovery (Recycling & Composting)**
3. **Energy Recovery (WTE)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Energy recovery from urban waste is recognized as a **Tier-2 Renewable Energy Source** because the bio-fraction (food, paper, yard waste) captures solar energy fixed via photosynthesis. The fossil-derived fraction (plastics, rubber) is technically **non-renewable**, but its diversion from landfill is environmentally mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stuffed Kitchen Bin" Intuition

Imagine your home kitchen waste bin: a soggy mix of vegetable peels (wet), paper tea-bags (carbon), a torn plastic wrapper (petroleum-based), and a dead battery (hazardous). If you simply **bury it** (landfilling), it rots anaerobically, producing methane — a greenhouse gas 25× more potent than CO₂ — and leaches toxic liquor into the groundwater.

Now, instead of burying it, you **sort it**:

- The wet kitchen waste is sent to a sealed, oxygen-free digester → microbes chew it → **biogas (CH₄ + CO₂)** lights your stove.
- The dry paper/plastic fraction, after shredding and drying, is fed to a high-temperature reactor → it **combusts/gasifies** → steam spins a turbine → electricity lights your bulb.
- The leftover ash is used in road construction or brick-making.

**This is, in essence, the Urban Waste-to-Energy concept: turning a city's "rotten apple" into a productive energy orchard.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard values must be memorized for KTU numerical problems:
> - **Density of MSW (loose, as-collected):** **150 – 250 kg/m³**
> - **Density of MSW (compacted in transfer truck):** **400 – 600 kg/m³**
> - **Density of RDF (Refuse Derived Fuel):** **500 – 800 kg/m³**
> - **Calorific value of raw MSW (Indian conditions):** **6 – 12 MJ/kg** (vs. 10–13 MJ/kg in Europe)
> - **Calorific value of RDF (after drying/pelletizing):** **14 – 18 MJ/kg**
> - **Methane (CH₄) Global Warming Potential (GWP₁₀₀):** **28–34** (per IPCC AR5)
> - **Energy content of methane:** **55.5 MJ/kg**
> - **Stoichiometric O₂ for CH₄ combustion:** **2 mol O₂ per mol CH₄**
> - **Air-to-fuel mass ratio (stoichiometric, CH₄):** **17.2 : 1**

## 1.4 Composition of Indian MSW (KNOW THIS)

| Component | % by Weight (Indian cities) | % by Weight (OECD average) |
|------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Biodegradable (food, yard) | **40 – 60 %** | 30 – 40 % |
| Paper & Cardboard | 6 – 12 % | 25 – 35 % |
| Plastics | 8 – 12 % | 10 – 15 % |
| Glass & Metals | 5 – 10 % | 10 – 15 % |
| Inerts (Rubble, Sand) | 15 – 25 % | 5 – 10 % |
| Moisture | **30 – 50 %** | 15 – 25 % |
| Calorific value (as-received) | **6 – 12 MJ/kg** | 9 – 13 MJ/kg |

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Indian MSW is hard to burn directly:** High moisture + high inert (rubble) content → low calorific value → incineration is self-sustaining only if moisture < 55% and inert < 15%. This is why **bio-methanation** dominates in India, unlike Europe where mass-burn incineration prevails.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 4R+1R Waste Hierarchy (Mandatory KTU Conceptual Knowledge)

Energy recovery sits at **Level 4** in the priority order:

1. **Refuse** (best — avoid generation)
2. **Reduce**
3. **Reuse**
4. **Recycle** (material recovery)
5. **Recover (energy)** ← *The WTE level — applied to non-recyclable fractions only*
6. **Residual disposal (landfill)** ← *Last resort*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect you to state: *"WTE is justified only after the 4Rs are exhausted; otherwise it competes with recycling."*

## 2.2 Major UWtE Process Routes — Logical Flow

Urban Waste is split into two fundamental physical streams for energy extraction:

### Route A — Thermochemical (Dry MSW, moisture < 30%)

- **A1. Incineration (Mass Burn / Combustion)** — Direct burning with excess air, T = 850–1100 °C, produces steam → Rankine cycle.
- **A2. Gasification** — Sub-stoichiometric O₂ (or steam) at T = 700–900 °C, produces **syngas** (CO + H₂).
- **A3. Pyrolysis** — No oxygen, T = 400–800 °C, produces **bio-char + bio-oil + syngas**.
- **A4. Plasma Arc Gasification** — T > 3000 °C, ultra-clean vitrified slag.

### Route B — Biochemical (Wet bio-fraction, moisture > 50%)

- **B1. Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** — Mesophilic (35–40 °C) or thermophilic (50–55 °C), wet/slurry, produces **biogas (60% CH₄, 40% CO₂)**.
- **B2. Landfill Gas (LFG) Recovery** — Passive or active extraction from engineered sanitary landfills, produces 40–60% methane gas.
- **B3. Fermentation (for ethanol/butanol)** — Mostly experimental for MSW.

### Route C — Mechanical/Physical Pre-treatment

- **Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF)** production: Shredding → Magnetic separation → Air-classification → Drying → Pelletization.
- **5–7 SRF (Solid Recovered Fuel)** is the EU's standardized pellet form.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Every formula below is a guaranteed KTU Part-B (14-mark) or Part-A (3-mark) question. Tables avoid `|` symbols using `\vert`.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Energy in MSW (mass basis) | $E_{MSW} = m_{MSW} \times CV_{MSW}$ | MJ; $m$ in kg, $CV$ in MJ/kg |
| 2 | Energy in MSW (per capita) | $E_p = P \times G \times CV_{waste}$ | MJ/day; $P$=population, $G$=waste generation (kg/cap/day) |
| 3 | Biogas yield (specific) | $V_{CH_4} = m_{VS} \times Y_{CH_4}$ | $Y_{CH_4}$ = **0.2 – 0.4 m³ CH₄ per kg VS** (volatile solids) |
| 4 | Theoretical CH₄ from Buswell equation | $C_nH_aO_bN_c + (n - a/4 - b/2 + 3c/4)H_2O \rightarrow (n/2 - a/8 + b/4 + 3c/8)CO_2 + (n/2 + a/8 - b/4 - 3c/8)CH_4 + cNH_3$ | Stoichiometric; assumes glucose C₆H₁₀O₅ basis |
| 5 | Calorific value (Dulong-like, for waste) | $CV = 337C + 1428(H - O/8) + 95S$ | kJ/kg; C, H, O, S in mass fractions |
| 6 | Stoichiometric O₂ for combustion | $O_{2,stoich} = 32C/12 + 32H/4 + 32(S/32) - 32O/16$ | kg O₂ per kg fuel |
| 7 | Stoichiometric air | $A_{stoich} = O_{2,stoich} / 0.232$ | kg air/kg fuel (air is 23.2% O₂ by mass) |
| 8 | Excess air ratio | $\lambda = A_{actual} / A_{stoich}$ | Dimensionless; incineration: $\lambda = 1.5 - 2.0$ |
| 9 | LFG generation (IPCC method) | $L_0 = MCF \times DOC \times DOC_f \times 16/12 \times F$ | kg CH₄ per kg waste; $F$ = fraction of DOC that finally degrades |
| 10 | Power from LFG/biogas | $P = V_{CH_4} \times \eta_{gen} \times CV_{CH_4} / 3600$ | kW (if V is in m³/h); $\eta_{gen}$ = 0.30–0.40 |
| 11 | Plant electrical efficiency (WTE) | $\eta_{el} = P_{el} / (m_{waste} \times CV_{waste})$ | %; typical MSW incineration: 18 – 25% |
| 12 | CHP / Cogeneration efficiency | $\eta_{CHP} = (P_{el} + Q_{th}) / E_{input}$ | %; biogas engine CHP: 80 – 88% |
| 13 | Combustion efficiency (CO/CO₂ ratio) | $\eta_{comb} = [CO_2]/([CO_2] + [CO]) \times 100\%$ | %; CPCB norm > 99% |
| 14 | Volume reduction | $R_V = 1 - V_{ash}/V_{waste,in}$ | %; incineration gives 90% volume reduction |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Application

- **Municipal Corporations** use UWtE to comply with **Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016 (India)** and to avoid 100+ year legacy landfill liability.
- **Swachh Bharat Mission 2.0** mandates legacy dump remediation, often via bio-mining + WTE plants.
- **Typical Indian UWtE plants** (operating): Okhla (Delhi, 25 MW), Timarpur-Okhla (23 MW), Narela (24 MW), Jalandhar (10 MW), Jabalpur (10 MW).
- **International benchmarks:** Copenhagen (Amager Bakke) — 100% CHP, ski-slope on roof; Shenzhen East — 5000 TPD, world's largest.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination tip:** Always quote a specific plant (e.g., Timarpur-Okhla) in long answers to demonstrate applied knowledge. Examiners reward this.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Generated Per Capita Per Day from MSW

This is the **most common KTU Part-A 3-mark question**, often expanded to a full 14-mark Part-B.

### Statement

Estimate the daily energy potential of municipal solid waste generated by a city of population **P**, with per-capita waste generation **g** (kg/cap/day) and average calorific value **CV** (MJ/kg).

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Total daily MSW mass:**

$$
m_{waste} = P \times g
$$

where:
- $P$ = population (number of persons)
- $g$ = per capita waste generation rate (kg/person/day)

**Step 2 — Total daily energy content of the waste:**

$$
E_{gross} = m_{waste} \times CV = P \times g \times CV
$$

**Step 3 — Apply conversion efficiency** (since not all chemical energy becomes electricity). For a typical Indian MSW incineration plant with Rankine cycle:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{combustion} \times \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{cycle} \times \eta_{generator}
$$

Using typical values: $0.95 \times 0.85 \times 0.30 \times 0.95 \approx 0.23$, so $\eta_{overall} \approx 23\%$.

**Step 4 — Net electrical energy output:**

$$
E_{el} = \eta_{overall} \times P \times g \times CV
$$

**Step 5 — Express in kWh/day** (using $1\,MJ = 0.2778\,kWh$):

$$
E_{el,kWh} = 0.2778 \times \eta_{overall} \times P \times g \times CV
$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Question:** A city has a population of 1,000,000. Per-capita MSW generation = 0.5 kg/cap/day. Calorific value = 10 MJ/kg. Combustion + Rankine efficiency = 22%. Compute: (a) Daily MSW mass, (b) Daily energy content, (c) Daily electrical energy, (d) Equivalent hours of household lighting (assuming 100 W bulb per household of 5).

**Step (a) — Daily MSW mass:**

$$
m_{waste} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.5 = 500{,}000\,kg/day = 500\,tonnes/day
$$

**Step (b) — Daily energy content (gross):**

$$
E_{gross} = 500{,}000 \times 10 = 5{,}000{,}000\,MJ/day
$$

**Step (c) — Net electrical energy:**

$$
E_{el} = 0.22 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = 1{,}100{,}000\,MJ/day
$$

Convert to kWh:

$$
E_{el,kWh} = 1{,}100{,}000 \times 0.2778 = 305{,}580\,kWh/day
$$

**Step (d) — Households served (assuming 5 persons/household, 100 W per household, 4 hrs/day):**

Energy per household per day = $0.1\,kW \times 4\,h = 0.4\,kWh$
Number of households served = $305{,}580 / 0.4 = 763{,}950$ households ≈ **7,64,000 homes** (each lit for 4 hours/day).

> [!TIP]
> **Show the unit conversion steps explicitly** in exams. Mark loss is most common at MJ → kWh conversions. Memorize: $1\,MJ = 0.2778\,kWh$ and $1\,kWh = 3.6\,MJ$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stoichiometric Air Requirement for MSW Combustion

Used in problems involving **flue gas analysis, air pollution control, and boiler design**.

### Statement

Compute the theoretical air required to completely combust 1 kg of MSW having ultimate analysis (by mass): C = 30%, H = 4%, O = 22%, S = 0.5%, N = 0.8%, Moisture = 25%, Ash (inert) = 17.7%.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Combustion reactions:**

$$
C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2
$$

$$
H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O
$$

$$
S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2
$$

**Step 2 — Oxygen required (per kg of dry fuel):**

For Carbon:
$$
O_{2,C} = \frac{32}{12} \times C = 2.667\,C
$$

For Hydrogen:
$$
O_{2,H} = \frac{32}{4} \times H = 8\,H
$$

For Sulphur:
$$
O_{2,S} = \frac{32}{32} \times S = 1\,S
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract oxygen already in fuel:**

$$
O_{2,stoich} = 2.667\,C + 8\,H + 1\,S - 1\,O
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in values (C=0.30, H=0.04, S=0.005, O=0.22):**

$$
O_{2,stoich} = (2.667 \times 0.30) + (8 \times 0.04) + (1 \times 0.005) - (1 \times 0.22)
$$

Compute each term:

$$
= 0.8001 + 0.32 + 0.005 - 0.22
$$

$$
= 0.9051\,kg\,O_2\,per\,kg\,dry\,fuel
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to air** (using 23.2% O₂ by mass in air):

$$
A_{stoich} = \frac{0.9051}{0.232} = 3.901\,kg\,air\,per\,kg\,dry\,fuel
$$

**Step 6 — Actual air with 60% excess** ($\lambda = 1.6$):

$$
A_{actual} = 1.6 \times 3.901 = 6.242\,kg\,air/kg\,dry\,fuel
$$

**Step 7 — Account for moisture in MSW.** If the as-fired moisture is 25%, then dry fuel = 75% of 1 kg as-fired = 0.75 kg. So per kg **as-fired MSW**:

$$
A_{actual,as-fired} = 6.242 \times 0.75 = 4.68\,kg\,air/kg\,as-fired\,MSW
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The moisture does **not** participate in combustion but it does absorb heat, lowering adiabatic flame temperature. This is the dominant reason why high-moisture Indian MSW refuses to burn without auxiliary fuel (e.g., diesel pilot).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Buswell Equation for Anaerobic Digestion (Theoretical CH₄ Yield)

The **Buswell equation** gives the stoichiometric maximum CH₄ yield from any organic compound $C_nH_aO_bN_c$.

### Statement

For the generic organic substrate $C_nH_aO_bN_c$, derive the maximum methane yield during anaerobic digestion.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Empirical formula of substrate** — take the generalized formula $C_nH_aO_bN_c$.

**Step 2 — Anaerobic reaction (no oxygen input):**

$$
C_nH_aO_bN_c + (n - a/4 - b/2 + 3c/4)\,H_2O \rightarrow (n/2 - a/8 + b/4 + 3c/8)\,CO_2
$$
$$
+ (n/2 + a/8 - b/4 - 3c/8)\,CH_4 + c\,NH_3
$$

**Step 3 — Atom balance check for Glucose C₆H₁₂O₆** (n=6, a=12, b=6, c=0):

CH₄ coefficient:

$$
\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} = 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3
$$

CO₂ coefficient:

$$
\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Write the balanced reaction:**

$$
C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3\,CH_4 + 3\,CO_2
$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical yield:** 1 mol glucose (180 g) produces 3 mol CH₄ (67.2 L at STP).

$$
Y_{CH_4,theor} = \frac{3 \times 22.4}{180} = 0.373\,m^3\,CH_4/kg\,glucose
$$

**Step 6 — Practical yield (with 80% biodegradability):**

$$
Y_{CH_4,practical} = 0.373 \times 0.80 = 0.299\,m^3\,CH_4/kg\,glucose
$$

**Step 7 — For kitchen waste with mixed composition** (volatile solids basis):

$$
V_{CH_4,plant} = m_{VS} \times Y_{CH_4,practical}
$$

For a 10 TPD kitchen-waste AD plant with 80% VS content:

$$
V_{CH_4,plant} = 10{,}000 \times 0.80 \times 0.30 = 2{,}400\,m^3\,CH_4/day
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the equation derivation:** The coefficients arise from simultaneously solving **C-balance, H-balance, O-balance, N-balance, and oxidation-state-balance** under the constraint that the oxygen in H₂O and substrate fully oxidizes carbon. KTU examiners may ask you to derive the glucose → 3CH₄ + 3CO₂ result directly. Show all four atom balances.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Overall Urban Waste to Energy Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["URBAN WASTE COLLECTION"] --> B["WASTE SEGREGATION PLANT"]
    B --> C["BIODEGRADABLE FRACTION 40-60 PERCENT"]
    B --> D["NON BIODEGRADABLE FRACTION 40-60 PERCENT"]
    B --> E["INERT FRACTION 15-25 PERCENT"]
    C --> F["ANAEROBIC DIGESTER MESOPHILIC 35-40C"]
    F --> G["BIOGAS 60 PERCENT CH4 40 PERCENT CO2"]
    F --> H["DIGESTATE SOLID LIQUID"]
    H --> H1["COMPOST FOR AGRICULTURE"]
    H --> H2["LIQUID FERTILIZER"]
    G --> I["GAS PURIFICATION H2S SILOXANE REMOVAL"]
    I --> J["GAS ENGINE OR BOILER CHP"]
    J --> K["ELECTRICITY TO GRID"]
    J --> L["HOT WATER DISTRICT HEATING"]
    D --> M["SHREDDER AND DRYER"]
    M --> N["REFUSE DERIVED FUEL RDF"]
    N --> O["INCINERATOR 850-1100C"]
    O --> P["BOILER STEAM GENERATION"]
    P --> Q["STEAM TURBINE RANKINE CYCLE"]
    Q --> K
    O --> R["FLUE GAS TREATMENT ESP SCR"]
    R --> S["STACK EMISSION COMPLIANT"]
    O --> T["BOTTOM ASH 10 PERCENT"]
    T --> U["ROAD CONSTRUCTION BRICKS"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]**

> Define Municipal Solid Waste (MSW). List the four major components of MSW with their typical Indian urban percentage ranges.

**Model Answer:**

**Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** is defined under the Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000 (superseded by SWM Rules, 2016) as the commercial and residential waste generated in a municipal area, excluding industrial hazardous waste, bio-medical waste, and e-waste.

The four major components of MSW (with Indian urban percentage by weight):

1. **Biodegradable / Organic** (food waste, yard waste, paper): **40 – 60 %**
2. **Recyclable** (plastics, glass, metals, textile): **15 – 25 %**
3. **Inert** (construction debris, sand, stones): **15 – 25 %**
4. **Moisture content** (intrinsic in the waste matrix): **30 – 50 %**

The high biodegradable and moisture content of Indian MSW makes it **unsuitable for direct incineration** and ideal for **anaerobic digestion / bio-methanation**, which is the preferred urban waste-to-energy route in India.

---

### Question 2 (3 marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

> What is Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF)? Mention any four steps in RDF production.

**Model Answer:**

**Refuse Derived Fuel (RDF)** is a fuel produced by shredding, drying, and densifying the combustible fraction of MSW (after removal of recyclables, inerts, and moisture) into a uniform, high-calorific-value solid fuel (typically pellets or fluff).

**Calorific Value:** 14 – 18 MJ/kg (vs. 6 – 12 MJ/kg for raw MSW).

**Four Steps in RDF Production:**

1. **Primary Shredding** — Reduces the bulky MSW to < 30 cm pieces using a rotary or hammer mill.
2. **Magnetic Separation** — Removes ferrous metals (Fe) using an overband magnetic separator.
3. **Air Classification / Trommel Screening** — Separates light organic fraction (paper, plastic) from heavy inerts (stones, glass) using aerodynamic drag and vibrating screens.
4. **Secondary Shredding & Drying** — Further size reduction (< 5 cm) followed by thermal drying to reduce moisture to < 20%.

The end product — **RDF pellets or fluff** — is co-fired in **cement kilns**, **fluidized bed combustors**, or **dedicated WTE boilers**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q7 (a) + (b)]**

> **(a)** Describe with a neat block diagram the process of **anaerobic digestion** of urban bio-waste for biogas production. Explain the four biochemical stages and the role of Methanogenic bacteria. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A metropolitan city generates **1500 tonnes/day** of MSW. If the biodegradable fraction is **55%** (volatile solids basis) and the practical methane yield is **0.30 m³ CH₄ per kg VS**, estimate: (i) the daily methane production, (ii) the electrical energy generated per day (assume generator efficiency 35%, CV of CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/m³), and (iii) the number of households (each consuming 5 kWh/day) that can be served. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Process of Anaerobic Digestion (AD):**

Anaerobic Digestion is the **biochemical breakdown of organic matter by microorganisms in the absence of oxygen**, producing a combustible gas called **biogas** (typically 60% CH₄, 40% CO₂) and a nutrient-rich residue called **digestate**.

```
Pre-treatment → Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis → Biogas + Digestate
```

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Bio-waste in] → [Shredder] → [Slurry Tank 5–10% TS] → [Digester 35–55°C, HRT 15–30 days]
    → [Biogas holder] → [H₂S scrubber] → [Gas engine / Boiler]
    → [Digestate dewatering] → [Compost] + [Liquid fertilizer]
```

**The Four Biochemical Stages:**

1. **Hydrolysis** — Complex biopolymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken down into simple monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids) by extracellular enzymes (amylase, protease, lipase) secreted by hydrolytic bacteria. **Retention time: 5–15 days.** This is the rate-limiting step for high-cellulosic waste.

2. **Acidogenesis (Fermentation)** — Acidogenic bacteria (e.g., *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*) convert monomers into volatile fatty acids (VFAs: acetic, propionic, butyric), alcohols (ethanol), H₂, and CO₂. Optimum pH: **5.5 – 6.5**.

3. **Acetogenesis** — Acetogenic bacteria (e.g., *Syntrophobacter*) convert VFAs and alcohols into acetic acid, H₂, and CO₂ — the direct precursors of methane. This stage is **thermodynamically unfavorable** and depends on syntrophic association with methanogens to keep H₂ partial pressure low.

4. **Methanogenesis** — Methanogenic archaea (e.g., *Methanobacterium*, *Methanosarcina*) convert acetate, H₂/CO₂, and formate into CH₄. Two main pathways:
   - **Acetoclastic:** $CH_3COOH \rightarrow CH_4 + CO_2$ (≈ 70% of CH₄)
   - **Hydrogenotrophic:** $CO_2 + 4H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 + 2H_2O$ (≈ 30% of CH₄)
   - Optimum pH: **6.8 – 7.4**; sensitive to ammonia toxicity and rapid pH changes.

**Role of Methanogenic Bacteria:**
- They are the **sole producers of methane** in the AD chain.
- They are **archaea** (not true bacteria), strictly anaerobic, slow-growing (doubling time 2–6 days), and highly sensitive to pH, temperature, and toxic compounds (NH₃, H₂S, heavy metals).
- The balance between acidogenesis (fast) and methanogenesis (slow) determines digester stability. Overloading causes VFA accumulation → pH drop → digester "souring" → methanogen inhibition → process failure.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Total MSW = 1500 TPD = $1{,}500{,}000\,kg/day$
- Biodegradable fraction (VS basis) = 55%
- Methane yield = $0.30\,m^3\,CH_4/kg\,VS$
- Generator efficiency $\eta_{gen} = 0.35$
- Calorific value of CH₄ = $55.5\,MJ/m^3$
- Household consumption = 5 kWh/day

**Step (i) — Daily methane production:**

Mass of VS:

$$
m_{VS} = 1{,}500{,}000 \times 0.55 = 825{,}000\,kg\,VS/day
$$

Volume of CH₄:

$$
V_{CH_4} = 825{,}000 \times 0.30 = 247{,}500\,m^3\,CH_4/day
$$

**Step (ii) — Daily electrical energy:**

$$
E_{th} = 247{,}500 \times 55.5 = 13{,}736{,}250\,MJ/day
$$

$$
E_{el} = 0.35 \times 13{,}736{,}250 = 4{,}807{,}687.5\,MJ/day
$$

Convert to kWh:

$$
E_{el,kWh} = 4{,}807{,}687.5 \times 0.2778 = 1{,}335{,}377\,kWh/day
$$

**Step (iii) — Number of households served:**

$$
N = \frac{1{,}335{,}377}{5} = 267{,}075 \text{ households}
$$

> **Final Answer:** (i) **2,47,500 m³ CH₄/day**, (ii) **13,35,377 kWh/day ≈ 13.35 lakh kWh/day**, (iii) **2,67,075 households** can be served.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3, Q8 (a) + (b)]**

> **(a)** Compare **Incineration, Gasification, and Pyrolysis** as WTE technologies in a tabular form. State typical operating temperatures, products, and net electrical efficiencies. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A WTE incinerator processes **500 TPD** of MSW with an average calorific value of **8.5 MJ/kg**. The plant has a Rankine cycle with steam conditions of **40 bar, 400 °C**, alternator efficiency **96%**, and isentropic turbine and pump efficiencies of **85%** and **80%** respectively. The boiler efficiency is **85%** and generator efficiency is **96%**. Estimate: (i) the thermal energy input per hour, (ii) the heat absorbed by the boiler per hour, and (iii) the net electrical power output (assume 5% auxiliary consumption). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| Parameter | Incineration | Gasification | Pyrolysis |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|-----------|
| **Operating Temperature** | 850 – 1100 °C | 700 – 900 °C | 400 – 800 °C |
| **Oxygen supply** | Excess air ($\lambda$ = 1.5 – 2.0) | Sub-stoichiometric ($\lambda$ = 0.2 – 0.4) or steam | None (inert N₂ atmosphere) |
| **Primary Products** | Flue gas (CO₂, H₂O, N₂), heat, ash | Syngas (CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂) | Bio-char, bio-oil, syngas |
| **Energy form** | Direct heat → steam | Chemical (syngas) → IC engine / gas turbine | Char (solid), oil (liquid), gas |
| **Net electrical efficiency** | 18 – 25% | 25 – 35% | 20 – 30% |
| **Air pollution** | High (NOx, dioxins) — needs APC | Moderate (tars) | Lowest (no combustion) |
| **Residue volume** | 25 – 30% ash | < 10% vitrified slag | 15 – 20% bio-char |
| **Suitable MSW** | Low moisture, high CV | Wide range, pre-dried | Wide range, pre-dried |
| **Capex** | Highest | Moderate | Lowest (in India) |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $m_{waste} = 500\,TPD = 500{,}000/24 = 20{,}833.33\,kg/hr$
- $CV = 8.5\,MJ/kg$
- $\eta_{boiler} = 0.85$
- $\eta_{gen} = 0.96$
- Auxiliary consumption = 5%

**Step (i) — Thermal energy input per hour:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m} \times CV = 20{,}833.33 \times 8.5 = 177{,}083.33\,MJ/hr
$$

Convert to MW:

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = 177{,}083.33 / 3600 = 49.19\,MW_{th}
$$

**Step (ii) — Heat absorbed by boiler (steam generation rate):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{boiler} = \eta_{boiler} \times \dot{Q}_{in} = 0.85 \times 49.19 = 41.81\,MW_{th}
$$

**Step (iii) — Net electrical power output:**

Gross electrical power:

$$
P_{gross} = \eta_{gen} \times \dot{Q}_{boiler} = 0.96 \times 41.81 = 40.14\,MW_e
$$

Net electrical power (after 5% auxiliary):

$$
P_{net} = 0.95 \times 40.14 = 38.13\,MW_e
$$

> **Final Answer:** (i) **49.19 MW thermal**, (ii) **41.81 MW absorbed by boiler**, (iii) **≈ 38.13 MW net electrical output**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mark-Loss Zones in UWtE Problems:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert kg/day → kg/hr, or MJ → kWh, is the #1 mark killer. Always state the unit conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Wet vs. Dry vs. As-Fired Basis:** When an ultimate analysis is given, verify whether it is on a **dry basis** or **as-fired basis**. If dry, you must scale by $(1 - moisture)$ to get as-fired values.
> 3. **Efficiency Stacking Order:** Apply efficiencies in the correct sequence: $\eta_{boiler}$ first, then $\eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{generator}$ for the electrical side. Reverse this and you lose 2 marks.
> 4. **MSW Composition ≠ Ultimate Analysis:** Indian MSW has 40–60% biodegradable wet weight, but the **elemental C-H-O-N composition** of that dry biodegradable fraction is required for combustion calculations. Many students confuse the two.
> 5. **Stoichiometric Air vs. Excess Air:** Stoichiometric O₂ ≠ Stoichiometric air. The factor is $1/0.232$ (or $1/0.21$ if using volume basis). Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **CHP vs. Electricity-Only:** If a problem mentions "CHP" or "cogeneration", you must account for the **thermal recovery credit**, not just the electrical output.
> 7. **Skip the diagram in (a) part at your peril:** A "neat block diagram" is worth 2–3 marks on its own in any 7-mark sub-question. Do not waste 20 minutes drawing it freehand — use labeled boxes with arrows and a caption.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — print this before exam:**

- **MSW in India:** 40–60% biodegradable, 30–50% moisture, CV = 6–12 MJ/kg → favors **anaerobic digestion** over incineration.
- **Standard Calorific Values to memorize:** MSW = 10 MJ/kg, RDF = 15 MJ/kg, CH₄ = 55.5 MJ/m³, Petrol = 44 MJ/kg, Coal = 25 MJ/kg.
- **Buswell Equation for glucose:** $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 3CH_4 + 3CO_2$ → 0.373 m³ CH₄/kg theoretical, ~0.30 m³/kg practical.
- **Anaerobic Digestion 4 stages:** Hydrolysis → Acidogenesis → Acetogenesis → Methanogenesis. **Methanogens are archaea, slow-growing, pH-sensitive.**
- **Incineration:** 850–1100°C, excess air λ = 1.5–2.0, electrical efficiency 18–25%, requires 99% combustion efficiency (CPCB norm).
- **Gasification:** 700–900°C, sub-stoichiometric, produces syngas (CO + H₂), higher efficiency, lower emissions.
- **Pyrolysis:** No oxygen, 400–800°C, produces bio-char + bio-oil + syngas in roughly 1:2:2 mass ratio (varies).
- **RDF production:** Shred → Magnetic separate → Air classify → Dry → Pellet. CV 14–18 MJ/kg. Used in cement kilns.
- **Air-to-fuel ratio:** Stoichiometric air = $O_{2,stoich} / 0.232$ (mass basis). Stoichiometric O₂ = $2.667C + 8H + S - O$ kg/kg dry fuel.
- **Excess air for MSW incineration:** 60–100% (i.e., $\lambda$ = 1.6 – 2.0) to maintain combustion stability.
- **LFG recovery:** Engineered landfills produce 40–60% CH₄ gas, recovered via vertical wells + blower; IPCC method gives $L_0 = MCF \times DOC \times DOC_f \times 16/12 \times F$.
- **Indian WTE plants to remember:** Timarpur-Okhla (Delhi, 23 MW), Narela (Delhi, 24 MW), Okhla Phase-3 (25 MW), Jalandhar (10 MW), Jabalpur (10 MW).
- **CPCB emission norms:** Particulate Matter < 50 mg/Nm³, HCl < 50 mg/Nm³, SO₂ < 200 mg/Nm³, NOx < 250 mg/Nm³, Dioxins < 0.1 ng TEQ/Nm³.
- **Conversion factor to remember:** $1\,MJ = 0.2778\,kWh$, $1\,kWh = 3.6\,MJ$, $1\,TPD = 1000/24 = 41.67\,kg/hr$.
- **4R hierarchy:** Refuse > Reduce > Reuse > Recycle > **Recover (energy)** > Landfill.
- **For 14-mark answers, ALWAYS include:** a labeled block diagram, the 4 AD stages, a comparative table, and a real Indian plant example.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Urban Waste to Energy Conversion**

topics.title: Biomass Gasification (Downdraft). Tidal Power: Tides and Waves as Energy Suppliers and Their Mechanics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Biomass Gasification (Downdraft). Tidal Power: Tides and Waves as Energy Suppliers and Their Mechanics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Biomass Gasification — Downdraft Type

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Biomass gasification is a *thermo-chemical conversion* process in which solid biomass feedstock (wood, agricultural residue, coconut shells, sawdust, etc.) is partially oxidized at high temperatures (**800 °C – 1200 °C**) under a *sub-stoichiometric* air supply to produce a combustible low-to-medium calorific value fuel gas called **Producer Gas** (or *syngas* when oxygen/steam is used).

A **Downdraft Gasifier** is a fixed-bed gasifier in which both the *air/oxidant supply* and the *product gas* flow **co-currently downward** through the reactor, in the same direction as gravity. The air is introduced through a tuyère (nozzle) located *below* the combustion zone, and the producer gas exits at the bottom of the reactor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Producer Gas Composition (Typical):** CO ≈ **18–22 %**, H₂ ≈ **12–18 %**, CH₄ ≈ **1–3 %**, CO₂ ≈ **10–12 %**, N₂ ≈ **50–55 %**. Calorific Value (CV) ≈ **4.5–5.5 MJ/Nm³**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Vertical Charcoal Kiln"
Imagine lighting a barbecue pit filled with coconut shells. You blow air from the *bottom* using a hand blower (the tuyère). The smoke (gas) does not rise upward like in a chimney — instead, it is forced to pass *through* the layer of glowing red-hot charcoal *below* the fire. As the hot smoke passes through this hot charcoal bed, the toxic smoke chemicals (tars and soot) are thermally "cracked" into cleaner, combustible gas. This is precisely how a downdraft gasifier works: the design exploits the hot reduction zone to *self-clean* the gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In an *updraft* gasifier, air enters at the bottom and gas exits from the top (gas flows up). Tar content is high. In a *downdraft* gasifier, both enter and exit from the bottom — the gas must pass through the hottest bed of charcoal, cracking tars. Result: **low-tar producer gas** (≤ 100 mg/Nm³) — ideal for direct firing of diesel engines.

---

## 1.2 Tidal Power — Tides and Waves as Energy Suppliers

**Formal Definition:**
Tidal power is the form of **Ocean Renewable Energy** derived from two distinct oceanographic phenomena:
1. **Tidal Energy** — extracted from the *potential energy* of the periodic rise and fall of seawater level caused by the *gravitational attraction* of the **Moon** (≈ 67 %) and the **Sun** (≈ 33 %), modulated by Earth's rotation.
2. **Wave Energy** — extracted from the *kinetic and potential energy* of wind-generated ocean surface waves propagating across the sea surface.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Breathing Sea and the Rolling Carpet"

- **Tides = The Breathing Sea:** Picture a giant bathtub full of water. The Moon's gravity slowly lifts one side of the water (creating *high tide*) while the opposite side bulges due to inertia. Twice a day, the water level in your bathtub rises and falls by 1–10 meters. If you place a turbine in the path of this rising/falling water, you can capture its energy — this is a *tidal barrage*.
- **Waves = The Rolling Carpet:** Strong winds blowing over hundreds of kilometers of ocean transfer their energy to the water surface, creating waves that travel thousands of kilometers with little loss. A 1-meter wave carries about **10 kW per meter of crest length** — equivalent to a strip of carpet 1 m wide moving with the energy of a small motorcycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tidal Cycle (Synodic Period):** T = **12 h 25 min** ≈ **44,712 s** (one high tide to the next). Therefore, **two full cycles occur per lunar day (≈ 24 h 50 min)**.
> - **Spring Tides:** Sun–Earth–Moon *aligned* (new/full moon) → *largest* tidal range.
> - **Neap Tides:** Sun–Moon at **90°** to Earth (quarter moon) → *smallest* tidal range.

> [!NOTE]
> The theoretical extractable tidal power per unit basin area scales with the **square of the tidal range** R. Hence sites with large R (e.g., Gulf of Kutch in Gujarat ≈ 8 m, Bay of Fundy in Canada ≈ 16 m) are the only viable locations for tidal barrages.

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for KTU 2024 Scheme

Both topics test:
- **CO1:** Knowledge of various renewable energy resources.
- **CO2:** Conversion mechanisms, performance indices, and selection criteria.
- **CO3:** Engineering analysis and design of renewable energy systems.

The downdraft gasifier and tidal/wave systems are *almost always* asked in **ESE Part B (14 marks)** questions of the KTU examination, given their practical engineering relevance to India (Kerala coast, Gujarat Gulf, Sundarbans).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Downdraft Gasifier — Zone-wise Analysis (Top → Bottom)

The downdraft gasifier has **four distinct thermodynamic zones** arranged vertically. Each zone performs a specific function.

### Zone 1 — Drying Zone (Temperature: 100 °C – 200 °C)
- Biomass enters the top, where it is dried by the **hot rising gases** from the lower zones.
- Moisture content (typically 10–35 %) is evaporated.
- No chemical reaction; only heat transfer (latent heat of vaporization).

### Zone 2 — Pyrolysis Zone (Temperature: 200 °C – 600 °C)
- Devolatilization of biomass occurs in the *absence of oxygen*.
- Biomass decomposes into **char (solid) + volatiles (gas + tar vapors)**.
- Endothermic process.
- Products: CO, CO₂, H₂, CH₄, water vapor, tars, acetic acid.

### Zone 3 — Oxidation (Combustion) Zone (Temperature: 800 °C – 1200 °C)
- Air (O₂) is injected through the **tuyère nozzles** placed just above the grate.
- Exothermic reactions supply the heat required to drive drying, pyrolysis, and reduction.

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$2\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \Delta H = -221.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### Zone 4 — Reduction Zone (Temperature: 600 °C – 800 °C)
- The hot gases (CO₂, H₂O) from the combustion zone pass through the *hot char bed* below.
- **Tar cracking** occurs here — long-chain hydrocarbons break into CO, H₂, CH₄.
- This is the *key advantage* of the downdraft design: low-tar gas.

$$\text{C} + \text{CO}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO} \quad \text{(Boudouard Reaction)} \quad \Delta H = +172.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$\text{C} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Water-Gas Reaction)} \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \text{ kJ/mol}$$
$$\text{C} + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_4 \quad \text{(Methanation)} \quad \Delta H = -74.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

### Zone 5 — Ash Collection (Bottom)
- Ash and unconverted char fall onto the grate; periodically removed.

### Key Design Parameters (KTU 2024 Standard Values)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Equivalence Ratio (ER) | **0.20 – 0.30** | dimensionless |
| Air-to-Fuel Ratio | **1.5 – 2.0** | kg/kg |
| Gas Yield | **2.0 – 2.5** | Nm³/kg biomass |
| Gasification Efficiency (cold gas) | **60 – 75 %** | % |
| Producer Gas CV | **4.5 – 5.5** | MJ/Nm³ |
| Tar Content | **≤ 100** | mg/Nm³ |
| Specific Gasification Rate | **100 – 500** | kg/m²·h |
| Biomass Particle Size | **10 – 50** | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalence Ratio (ER) = Actual Air Supplied / Stoichiometric Air Required.**  
> Optimum ER = **0.25** for downdraft gasifiers. Higher ER → more combustion, less gas. Lower ER → incomplete gasification, more tars.

---

## 2.2 Tidal Power — Theoretical & Engineering Formulation

### 2.2.1 Origin of Tides — Three Key Forces

1. **Gravitational Force of Moon** — strongest tide-generating force.
2. **Gravitational Force of Sun** — ~ 46 % the strength of Moon's tide force.
3. **Centrifugal Force** due to Earth-Moon revolution around their common center of mass.

### 2.2.2 Tidal Energy Conversion Schemes

| Scheme | Type of Energy | Captured By | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tidal Barrage | Potential | Bulb/kaplan turbines in dam | La Rance (France) |
| Tidal Stream | Kinetic | Underwater rotors | MeyGen (Scotland) |
| Tidal Lagoon | Potential (controlled) | Bulb turbines in impoundment | Swansea Bay (UK) |
| Dynamic Tidal Power (DTP) | Potential (continuous) | Long coastal dam with phase difference | Conceptual |

### 2.2.3 Tidal Range and Energy Potential

The *tidal range* R is the vertical distance between **High Tide (HW)** and **Low Tide (LW)** levels. At a site with basin surface area A, the water mass in the basin varies between full and empty states.

For a *single-basin single-effect* scheme:
- Water mass held at height R/2 above mean sea level holds potential energy.
- **Energy per tidal cycle** (one filling or one emptying):

$$E_{\text{single}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,R^2$$

- Since two cycles occur per day (one filling + one emptying), the **average daily energy**:

$$E_{\text{daily}} = 2 \times \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,R^2 = \rho\,g\,A\,R^2$$

- **Average power** (over 24 hours = 86,400 s):

$$\boxed{P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\rho\,g\,A\,R^2}{T_{\text{cycle}}}}$$

where T_cycle ≈ 44,712 s for one tidal cycle (12 h 25 min) or 86,400 s for daily averaging.

### 2.2.4 Tidal Stream (Kinetic) Power

For a current of velocity v (m/s) passing through a swept area A:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{stream}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^3 \cdot C_p}$$

where C_p = power coefficient (Betz limit = 0.593).

---

## 2.3 Wave Energy — Theoretical & Engineering Formulation

### 2.3.1 Origin of Ocean Waves
- Wind blowing over the ocean surface transfers energy via *friction* and *pressure fluctuations*.
- As waves propagate out of the generation area (*swell*), they can travel thousands of km with little dissipation.
- Energy density: P ∝ H²·T (height squared times period).

### 2.3.2 Wave Power Formulation

The total wave energy per unit horizontal surface area (sum of kinetic and potential energy for a sinusoidal wave of amplitude a = H/2):

$$E_{\text{per area}} = \frac{1}{8}\,\rho\,g\,H^2 \quad [\text{J/m}^2]$$

The group velocity (deep water) — the speed at which wave energy propagates:

$$c_g = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{g\,T}{4\pi} \quad [\text{m/s}]$$

Hence the **wave power per unit crest length**:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{wave}} = \frac{\rho\,g^2\,H^2\,T}{32\,\pi} \quad [\text{W/m of crest}]}$$

A widely used practical form (with ρ = 1025 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s²):

$$\boxed{P \approx 0.98\,H^2\,T \approx 0.5\,H^2\,T \quad [\text{kW/m, with H in m, T in s}]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "0.5" not "0.98"?** The empirical 0.5 factor (used in many textbooks) accounts for: irregular sea states, directional spreading, and the use of *significant wave height* H_s rather than peak H. For KTU exam purposes, the rigorous form ρg²H²T/32π is the *correct* one to use in derivations; the 0.5 form is acceptable for numerical substitution.

### 2.3.3 Wave Energy Conversion Devices

| Device Type | Mode of Operation | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Point Absorber | Heave motion of buoy → hydraulic pump | PowerBuoy (USA) |
| Attenuator | Multi-segment floating snake flexing with waves | Pelamis (UK) |
| Terminator | Oscillating water column (OWC) | LIMPET (Islay, Scotland) |
| Overtopping | Waves fill reservoir, water returns through turbine | Wave Dragon (Denmark) |

---

## 2.4 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit | Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{single tide}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho g A R^2$ | Energy per fill/empty | J | (1) |
| 2 | $P_{\text{tidal}} = \frac{\rho g A R^2}{T}$ | Average tidal power | W | (2) |
| 3 | $P_{\text{stream}} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A v^3 C_p$ | Tidal stream power | W | (3) |
| 4 | $E_{\text{wave, area}} = \frac{1}{8}\rho g H^2$ | Wave energy per unit sea area | J/m² | (4) |
| 5 | $c_g = \frac{gT}{4\pi}$ | Group velocity (deep water) | m/s | (5) |
| 6 | $P_{\text{wave}} = \frac{\rho g^2 H^2 T}{32\pi}$ | Wave power per crest length | W/m | (6) |
| 7 | $P \approx 0.5 H^2 T$ | Practical wave power formula | kW/m | (7) |
| 8 | $\text{ER} = \frac{\text{Air}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{Air}_{\text{stoich}}}$ | Equivalence Ratio | – | (8) |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{gasification}} = \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{gas}} \times \text{CV}_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{biomass}} \times \text{CV}_{\text{biomass}}}$ | Cold gas efficiency | % | (9) |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{\text{air, stoich}} = \frac{m_{\text{biomass}} \cdot \text{O}_{\text{req}}}{0.23}$ | Stoichiometric air (assuming biomass as CH₁.₄O₀.₆) | kg/h | (10) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU 2024 — Substitution rule:** In all tidal/wave problems, use **ρ (seawater) = 1025 kg/m³** and **g = 9.81 m/s²**. For biomass, **CV ≈ 17–20 MJ/kg** (dry).

| Downdraft Gasifier (1–5 MW) | Co-generation in rice mills, bagasse-to-power | Mature |
