# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Tidal Power from a Single Basin

> **Problem Context:** A tidal barrage impounds a basin of area A. At high tide, the water level inside the basin is at the *outer sea level*; the sluice gates are then closed. As the tide recedes, a head difference of R (tidal range) develops between basin and sea, and water flows through a turbine.

### Step 1 — Define the State of the Basin

Let the basin surface area be A (assumed constant for a rectangular basin — a valid engineering approximation when the basin is large and water level changes are small relative to basin dimensions).

At time t after gate closure, the water surface inside the basin is at height h(t) above some datum (low-tide sea level). The head difference available to the turbine is:

$$H(t) = h(t) - 0 = h(t)$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Volume Remaining in the Basin

The volume of water *still contained* in the basin at head h(t) is:

$$V(t) = A \cdot h(t)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Potential Energy Stored in the Basin Water

The center of mass of the water column lies at height h(t)/2 above the datum. The potential energy of the impounded water is:

$$E(t) = m \cdot g \cdot \frac{h(t)}{2} = \rho \cdot V(t) \cdot g \cdot \frac{h(t)}{2}$$

Substituting V(t) = A·h(t):

$$E(t) = \rho \cdot A \cdot h(t) \cdot g \cdot \frac{h(t)}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,h(t)^2$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Energy Released Per Emptying

At the start of emptying, h(t₀) = R (full basin). At the end, h(t_f) = 0. The energy extracted by the turbine as the basin empties from full to empty is the *change* in potential energy:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{empty}} &= E(t_0) - E(t_f) \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,R^2 - \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,(0)^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,R^2 \quad \text{[Joules]}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Account for Both Filling and Emptying

A tidal cycle produces **two energy extraction events**: one emptying (ebb generation) and one filling (flood generation). So the total daily energy:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{day}} &= 2 \times \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,R^2 \\
&= \rho\,g\,A\,R^2 \quad \text{[J/day]}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Average Power Over 24 Hours

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{day}}}{86{,}400 \text{ s}} = \frac{\rho\,g\,A\,R^2}{86{,}400} \quad \text{[W]}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{\text{avg}} \;\approx\; \frac{\rho\,g\,A\,R^2}{T_{\text{cycle}}}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 examiner expects the boxed form, with T being either the tidal period (44,712 s) or 24 h (86,400 s). The simpler form ρgAR²/4πT may also be accepted.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Wave Power Per Unit Crest Length

> **Problem Context:** A sinusoidal deep-water wave of height H and period T propagates across the ocean. We wish to compute the time-averaged power crossing a vertical plane of unit width (1 m of crest length) perpendicular to the propagation direction.

### Step 1 — Wave Amplitude and Assumptions

For a sinusoidal wave, the surface elevation is:

$$\eta(x,t) = a \cos(kx - \omega t), \quad a = \frac{H}{2}$$

where:
- $a$ = amplitude (m)
- $H$ = wave height (trough-to-crest) (m)
- $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ = wave number (rad/m)
- $\omega = 2\pi/T$ = angular frequency (rad/s)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)
- $T$ = wave period (s)

Deep water condition: depth d > λ/2.

### Step 2 — Total Wave Energy Density (Per Unit Sea Surface Area)

For linear (Airy) wave theory, the total energy per unit horizontal area is the *sum* of potential and kinetic energy densities. Time-averaged:

$$E_{\text{potential}} = \frac{1}{4}\,\rho\,g\,a^2 = \frac{1}{16}\,\rho\,g\,H^2 \quad [\text{J/m}^2]$$

$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = \frac{1}{4}\,\rho\,g\,a^2 = \frac{1}{16}\,\rho\,g\,H^2 \quad [\text{J/m}^2]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= E_p + E_k \\
&= \frac{1}{16}\,\rho\,g\,H^2 + \frac{1}{16}\,\rho\,g\,H^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{8}\,\rho\,g\,H^2 \quad [\text{J/m}^2]
\end{aligned}$$

> Note: Kinetic and potential energy densities of a sinusoidal wave are *equipartitioned* — each equals half the total.

### Step 3 — Wave Phase Velocity and Group Velocity

The phase velocity (crests travel at):

$$c = \frac{\lambda}{T} = \frac{g\,T}{2\pi} \quad [\text{m/s}] \quad \text{(deep water, using dispersion relation } \omega^2 = gk)$$

The **group velocity** (energy propagates at this speed):

$$c_g = \frac{c}{2} = \frac{g\,T}{4\pi} \quad [\text{m/s}]$$

> This is a crucial *deep-water* result: energy moves at half the speed of individual crests.

### Step 4 — Wave Power (Energy Flux) Per Unit Crest Length

Power = (energy per unit area) × (group velocity):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{wave}} &= E_{\text{total}} \times c_g \\
&= \frac{1}{8}\,\rho\,g\,H^2 \times \frac{g\,T}{4\pi} \\
&= \frac{\rho\,g^2\,H^2\,T}{32\,\pi} \quad [\text{W/m}]
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{\text{wave}} \;=\; \frac{\rho\,g^2\,H^2\,T}{32\,\pi} \;\approx\; 0.98\,H^2\,T \;\text{[W/m]}\;}$$

### Step 5 — Practical Engineering Form

Inserting ρ = 1025 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s²:

$$\frac{\rho\,g^2}{32\,\pi} = \frac{1025 \times (9.81)^2}{32\,\pi} = \frac{98{,}642}{100.53} \approx 981.2$$

Hence:

$$P \;\approx\; 981\,H^2\,T \;\; \text{W/m} \;\approx\; 0.98\,H^2\,T \;\; \text{kW/m}$$

The *commonly used* practical form (with H in m, T in s):

$$P \;\approx\; 0.5\,H^2\,T \quad \text{kW/m} \quad \text{[empirical, used widely in KTU problems]}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Many students use the formula for *potential* energy only (1/16 ρgH²) instead of the *total* energy (1/8 ρgH²). This halves your answer. Always remember **KE = PE** for a sinusoidal wave.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Downdraft Gasifier Sizing

> **Worked Problem Template (KTU Pattern):**
> "A downdraft gasifier is to be designed for an output of X kW thermal from producer gas having calorific value Y MJ/Nm³. Biomass of CV = 17 MJ/kg and moisture content M % is used. Air is supplied at 30 % equivalence ratio. Find (a) mass of biomass required per hour, (b) mass of air required per hour, (c) gas yield per kg biomass, (d) gasifier efficiency."

### Step-by-Step Solution Template

**Given:**
- Power output, $P = X$ kW (thermal)
- Producer gas CV, $\text{CV}_g = Y$ MJ/Nm³
- Biomass CV, $\text{CV}_b = 17$ MJ/kg
- Moisture content, $M$ (decimal)
- Equivalence ratio, $\text{ER} = 0.30$

**Step 1:** Energy balance on producer gas:

$$P = \dot{m}_g \times \text{CV}_g \implies \dot{m}_g = \frac{P}{\text{CV}_g} \;\; \text{[kg/s or Nm}^3/\text{s]}$$

**Step 2:** Mass of biomass required:

$$\dot{m}_b = \frac{\dot{m}_g \times \text{CV}_g}{\eta_g \times \text{CV}_b} \quad \text{with } \eta_g = 0.7 \text{ (typical)}$$

**Step 3:** Air required (stoichiometric):

Biomass approximated as $\text{CH}_{1.4}\text{O}_{0.6}$ requires $\approx 5.6$ kg O₂ per kg biomass. Air is 23 % O₂ by mass, so:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air, stoich}} = \frac{5.6}{0.23} \times \dot{m}_b \approx 24.3\,\dot{m}_b$$

**Step 4:** Actual air at ER = 0.30:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air, actual}} = \text{ER} \times \dot{m}_{\text{air, stoich}} = 0.30 \times 24.3\,\dot{m}_b \approx 7.3\,\dot{m}_b$$

**Step 5:** Gas yield:

$$\text{Gas yield} = \frac{\dot{m}_g}{\dot{m}_b} \quad \text{[Nm}^3/\text{kg biomass]}$$

**Step 6:** Cold gas efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{cold}} = \frac{\dot{m}_g \times \text{CV}_g}{\dot{m}_b \times \text{CV}_b} \times 100\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The student is expected to remember: Stoichiometric O₂ for cellulose-like biomass ≈ **5.6 kg O₂/kg fuel**; for CH₁.₄O₀.₆ stoichiometric air ≈ **6.0–6.5 kg/kg**.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Tidal vs. Wave vs. Biomass Gasifier

| Parameter | Downdraft Gasifier | Tidal Barrage | Wave Energy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Solid biomass (stored chemical) | Gravitational (kinetic + potential) | Wind-induced (solar) |
| Source Density | High (CV ≈ 17 MJ/kg) | Low (range ≈ 5–10 m) | Moderate (1–50 kW/m) |
| Power Density | 1–5 MW/ha (plant footprint) | 5–10 MW/km² | 10–60 kW/m crest |
| Capacity Factor | 60–80 % | 22–28 % | 25–35 % |
| Predictability | On-demand (fuel-stocked) | Highly predictable (astronomical) | Less predictable (weather) |
| Major Limitation | Tar handling, feedstock drying | High capital cost, few sites | Storm survival, grid connection |
| India Status | MNRE-supported (surplus biomass) | Gulf of Kutch/Cambay | Tamil Nadu, Kerala coast |
| Typical Payback | 3–5 years | 15–30 years | 8–15 years |
| Carbon Footprint | Near-zero (closed CO₂ loop) | Zero | Zero |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** Tidal = "PREDICTABLE but POOR" (predictable astronomy, poor capacity factor). Wave = "ABUNDANT but UNPREDICTABLE" (vast resource, weather-dependent). Gasifier = "STOCKABLE" (fuel inventory, on-demand).

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Downdraft Gasifier — Functional Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the four thermodynamic zones, the heat-mass flows, and the input/output streams of a downdraft gasifier.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Feedstock Hopper] --> B[Drying Zone 100 to 200 C]
    B --> C[Pyrolysis Zone 200 to 600 C]
    C --> D[Oxidation Combustion Zone 800 to 1200 C]
    D --> E[Reduction Zone 600 to 800 C]
    E --> F[Grate and Ash Collection]
    
    G[Air Blower] -->|Tuyere Injection| D
    F -->|Producer Gas Exit| H[Gas Cleaning Cyclone]
    H --> I[Cooling and Filtration Unit]
    I --> J[IC Engine or Burner]
    
    B -.Hot Rising Flue Gases.-> A
    D -.Heat Transfer.-> C
    D -.Heat Transfer.-> B
    E -.CO2 and H2O Reduced to CO and H2.-> H
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** denote mass flow (biomass, air, gas).
- **Dotted arrows** denote heat flow / energy feedback.
- Notice the **co-current downward flow** of biomass and air/gas — the *defining feature* of a downdraft design.
- Tar vapors from pyrolysis are forced through the hot combustion and reduction zones, achieving *in-situ tar cracking* — a major engineering advantage.

---

## 4.2 Tidal Barrage — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Open Sea with Tidal Range R] -->|Rising Tide| B[Sluice Gates CLOSED]
    A -->|High Tide Reached| C[Sluice Gates OPEN]
    C -->|Ebb Generation Head Difference| D[Bulb Kaplan Turbines]
    D -->|Spent Water| E[Tidal Basin Reservoir]
    E -->|Next Rising Tide| F[Sluice Gates OPEN Flood Generation]
    F -->|Reverse Flow| D
    D -->|Mechanical Shaft| G[Gearbox]
    G --> H[Synchronous Generator]
    H -->|Step Up Transformer| I[Power Grid 11 kV or 33 kV]
    
    J[Control Room SCADA] -.Monitors and Optimizes.-> C
    J -.Monitors and Optimizes.-> F
    K[Navigation Lock] --> A
    L[Marine Fish Ladder] --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The basin fills at **high tide** and empties at **low tide** — generating power in *both directions* (ebb + flood) for a **double-effect scheme**.
- The **bulb Kaplan turbine** is preferred for low-head (3–10 m), high-flow tidal applications.
- Fish ladders and navigation locks are environmental and social necessities — often **overlooked in student diagrams** (lose marks!).

---

## 4.3 Wave Energy Conversion — Oscillating Water Column (OWC)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Open Sea Surface Incoming Wave] --> B[Submerged Capture Chamber]
    B -->|Air Column Pushed Up| C[Air Turbine Wells or Impulse Type]
    C -->|Bidirectional Airflow| D[Generator Set]
    D -->|Step Up Transformer| E[Grid Connection 11 kV]
    
    B -.Wave Recedes Air Sucked Down.-> C
    F[Control Valves] -.Rectify Airflow.-> C
    G[Foundation Seabed Caisson] --> B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The Wells turbine is the workhorse for OWC devices because it rotates in the *same direction* regardless of airflow direction (bidirectional flow).
- This is the most commonly asked wave device in KTU papers.

---

## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram — Tidal vs. Wave Source Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A1[Astronomical Tidal Source]
        M1[Earth Moon Sun Gravitational System] --> T1[Primary Tide Semi Diurnal Period 12 h 25 min]
        T1 --> T2[Spring and Neap Modulation 14 day Cycle]
        T2 --> T3[Tidal Range R metres 1 to 15 m]
    end
    
    subgraph A2[Meteorological Wave Source]
        W1[Wind Stress on Sea Surface] --> W2[Wave Generation Fetch Limited]
        W2 --> W3[Wave Height H and Period T 1 to 10 s]
        W3 --> W4[Swell Propagation Thousands of km]
    end
    
    T3 --> O1[Tidal Power Conversion]
    W4 --> O2[Wave Power Conversion]
    
    O1 --> P1[Barrage Stream Lagoon DTP]
    O2 --> P2[Point Absorber Attenuator OWC Terminator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The fundamental physical distinction: **Tides** are driven by **gravitational potential** of celestial bodies (deterministic, predictable centuries ahead). **Waves** are driven by **meteorological wind** (stochastic, predictable hours to days ahead only).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
Define **Biomass Gasification**. What is the role of the *equivalence ratio* in a downdraft gasifier?

**Model Answer:**

Biomass gasification is the thermo-chemical conversion of solid biomass into a combustible producer gas (rich in CO, H₂, CH₄) by partial oxidation at high temperatures (800–1200 °C) under a sub-stoichiometric air supply.

The **equivalence ratio (ER)** is the ratio of the *actual air supplied* to the *stoichiometric air required* for complete combustion:

$$\text{ER} = \frac{\text{Air}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{Air}_{\text{stoichiometric}}}$$

For downdraft gasifiers, the optimum ER is **0.20–0.30**:
- ER < 0.20 → incomplete gasification, low temperature, high tar content.
- ER > 0.30 → excessive combustion, low gas yield, low CV producer gas.
- ER = 0.25 → optimum balance of gas yield, CV, and tar level.

*(Memorize: ER = 0.25 → CO ≈ 20 %, H₂ ≈ 15 %, CV ≈ 5 MJ/Nm³.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
Distinguish between **Spring Tides** and **Neap Tides**. Why is the Gulf of Kutch considered ideal for tidal power in India?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Spring Tides | Neap Tides |
|---|---|---|
| Configuration | Sun–Earth–Moon **aligned** (syzygy) | Sun–Moon at **90°** to Earth (quadrature) |
| Occurs at | **New Moon** and **Full Moon** | **First Quarter** and **Third Quarter** Moon |
| Tidal Range | **Maximum** (up to 1.5× average) | **Minimum** (≈ 0.5× average) |
| Tide-Generating Force | Moon + Sun forces **add** | Moon and Sun forces **partially cancel** |

**Gulf of Kutch (Gujarat)** is ideal for tidal power in India because:
- Average tidal range of **5–8 m** (among the highest in India).
- Narrow basin mouth (Rann of Kutch) — natural barrier reduces civil engineering cost.
- Estimated potential ≈ **7,000 MW** as per MNRE.
- The Gulf of Cambay (Khambhat) is even better with **R ≈ 10 m** and potential ≈ **7,000 MW**; the two Gulfs together are rated for ~ **14,000 MW**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of a **downdraft gasifier**. List the four zones and the temperature range in each zone. State the producer gas composition and calorific value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A downdraft gasifier is fed with **100 kg/h** of rice husk (CV = 15 MJ/kg, moisture 10 %). The gasifier produces **250 Nm³/h** of producer gas with CV = **5.0 MJ/Nm³**. Calculate:
   (i) The **cold gas efficiency** of the gasifier.
   (ii) The **heat output** in kW thermal.
   (iii) The **specific gasification rate** if the gasifier cross-section is **0.20 m²**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to (a)

A **downdraft gasifier** is a fixed-bed reactor in which both biomass and air/gas flow *co-currently downward*. The reactor has four distinct zones (top to bottom):

| Zone | Function | Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| **Drying** | Moisture evaporation | 100 – 200 °C |
| **Pyrolysis** | Devolatilization → char + gases + tars | 200 – 600 °C |
| **Oxidation (Combustion)** | Exothermic: C + O₂ → CO₂ | 800 – 1200 °C |
| **Reduction** | Tar cracking; CO₂ + C → 2CO | 600 – 800 °C |

Air is injected through a **tuyère** located just above the grate. The hot gases (containing tars from pyrolysis) pass through the *incandescent char bed* in the reduction zone, where tars thermally crack to CO and H₂. Producer gas exits at the bottom through the grate.

**Producer gas composition (typical):** CO ≈ 20 %, H₂ ≈ 15 %, CH₄ ≈ 2 %, CO₂ ≈ 11 %, N₂ ≈ 52 %.

**Calorific Value:** 4.5 – 5.5 MJ/Nm³.

**Key advantage:** Very low tar content (≤ 100 mg/Nm³) — gas can be directly fed to an IC engine without complex cleaning.

**Block diagram** — see SECTION 4.1 for the full Mermaid schematic.

---

#### Model Answer to (b)

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_b = 100$ kg/h
- $\text{CV}_b = 15$ MJ/kg
- $\dot{V}_g = 250$ Nm³/h
- $\text{CV}_g = 5.0$ MJ/Nm³
- Cross-section $A = 0.20$ m²

**(i) Cold Gas Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{cold}} = \frac{\dot{V}_g \times \text{CV}_g}{\dot{m}_b \times \text{CV}_b} \times 100\%$$

Numerator: $250 \times 5.0 = 1250$ MJ/h

Denominator: $100 \times 15 = 1500$ MJ/h

$$\eta_{\text{cold}} = \frac{1250}{1500} \times 100\% = 83.3\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{cold}} \approx 83.3\,\%}$$

**(ii) Heat Output (kW thermal):**

$$P = \frac{1250 \text{ MJ/h}}{3.6 \text{ MJ/kWh}} = 347.2 \text{ kW (thermal)}$$

Or directly:

$$P = 250 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{h} \times 5.0 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 \times \frac{1000 \text{ kW}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} \cdot \frac{\text{h}}{3600 \text{ s}}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 347 \text{ kW thermal}}$$

**(iii) Specific Gasification Rate (SGR):**

$$\text{SGR} = \frac{\dot{m}_b}{A_{\text{cross-section}}} = \frac{100 \text{ kg/h}}{0.20 \text{ m}^2} = 500 \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SGR} = 500 \text{ kg/m}^2\cdot\text{h}}$$

This is at the upper end of typical (100–500 kg/m²·h), indicating a *compact, high-throughput* design.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the **origin of ocean tides**. With neat diagrams, describe the working of a **single-basin single-effect tidal barrage** and a **single-basin double-effect tidal barrage**. Compare the two schemes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A tidal power plant is proposed at a site with:
- Basin area $A = 30 \text{ km}^2$
- Average tidal range $R = 7$ m
- Turbine-generator efficiency $\eta = 0.80$
- Density of seawater $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³

Compute:
  (i) The **energy available per tidal cycle** (one filling or one emptying).
  (ii) The **average daily energy** generated (in kWh/day), accounting for double-effect generation.
  (iii) The **average power output** in MW. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer to (a)

**Origin of Tides:**

Tides are the periodic rise and fall of sea level caused by the **gravitational attraction** of the **Moon** (primary, ≈ 67 % of the tide-raising force) and the **Sun** (secondary, ≈ 33 %), combined with the **centrifugal force** arising from the revolution of the Earth–Moon system around its barycenter.

The combined tide-raising force creates two *tidal bulges* — one on the side facing the Moon and one on the opposite side. As Earth rotates (period 24 h), a fixed location passes through both bulges in **~ 12 h 25 min** — the *semi-diurnal tidal period*.

**Tidal Barrage Types:**

**Single-Basin, Single-Effect (SBE-SSE):**
- Water is impounded at **high tide** by closing sluice gates.
- Gates remain closed as the tide ebbs.
- When the head difference (basin vs. sea) reaches ~ 1–2 m, water is released through the **turbine** to the sea.
- Power is generated only during **ebb tide** (one direction).
- Head available: up to full tidal range R.

**Single-Basin, Double-Effect (SBE-DSE):**
- Uses **reversible (bulb) turbines** that can generate on both **ebb and flood** tides.
- Ebb generation: basin empties to sea.
- Flood generation: sea fills basin (turbine runs in reverse direction).
- Energy yield ~ **40–50 % more** than single-effect.
- Civil works and turbine cost are higher, so cost-benefit must be evaluated.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Single-Effect | Double-Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Generation periods | Ebb only | Ebb + Flood |
| Turbine type | Unidirectional | Reversible/bulb |
| Energy per cycle | (1/2)ρgAR² | ≈ ρgAR² (full cycle) |
| Output per day | 1× cycle | 2× cycle |
| Civil cost | Lower | Higher (extra sluices) |
| Annual energy | Reference (1.0×) | ~ 1.4× |

---

#### Model Answer to (b)

**Given:**
- $A = 30 \text{ km}^2 = 30 \times 10^6$ m²
- $R = 7$ m
- $\eta = 0.80$
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Energy per Tidal Cycle (single fill or empty):**

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho\,g\,A\,R^2$$

Substituting:

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 10^6 \times (7)^2$$

Compute step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{cycle}} &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 10^6 \times 49 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1.47 \times 10^9 \\
&= 5026.125 \times 1.47 \times 10^9 \quad \text{(units: kg·m/s²·m² = J)} \\
&\approx 7.388 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} \\
&= 7.388 \times 10^9 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

Convert to kWh: divide by 3.6 × 10⁶:

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{7.388 \times 10^{12}}{3.6 \times 10^6} \approx 2.052 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh} = 2052 \text{ MWh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{cycle}} \approx 2052 \text{ MWh per cycle}}$$

**(ii) Daily Energy (Double-Effect):**

For double-effect, we extract energy during both ebb and flood — i.e., 2 cycles per day:

$$E_{\text{day}} = 2 \times E_{\text{cycle}} = 2 \times 2052 = 4104 \text{ MWh/day}$$

Apply turbine-generator efficiency:

$$E_{\text{net,day}} = \eta \times E_{\text{day}} = 0.80 \times 4104 = 3283 \text{ MWh/day}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{net,day}} \approx 3283 \text{ MWh/day}}$$

**(iii) Average Power Output (MW):**

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{net,day}}}{24 \text{ h}} = \frac{3283}{24} \approx 136.8 \text{ MW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{avg}} \approx 137 \text{ MW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting η in (iii)**: Many students calculate gross energy and forget to apply turbine-generator efficiency (0.80) — losing 2–3 marks. Always read carefully whether the question asks for *gross* or *net* energy.
> 2. **Single vs. Double Effect confusion**: A common trap is to multiply by 4 instead of 2. One day has 2 *cycles* (each cycle = 1 high + 1 low), so 4 events but only 2 *useful* generations in single-effect. In double-effect, all 4 events are used.
> 3. **Units trap**: ρgAR² gives *Joules* (not kWh). Always convert: 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ.
> 4. **Off-by-one in tidal cycle**: Tidal cycle is **12 h 25 min**, NOT exactly 12 h. For long-term energy assessment, use 12.42 h; for one-day assessment, 2 cycles = 24 h 50 min ≈ 24 h is fine.
> 5. **In gasifier problems, students forget to convert Nm³ to kg** for mass balance. Use ρ_gas ≈ 1.2 kg/Nm³ if needed.
> 6. **For wave power, students often forget that** $a = H/2$, leading to a factor-of-4 error. Always state this explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Additional Practice — Wave Energy Numericals (Frequently Asked)

### Quick-Fire: Wave Power

**Problem:** At a coastal site in Kerala, the average significant wave height is **H = 2 m** and the average wave period is **T = 8 s**. Compute the wave power per metre of crest length.

**Solution:**

$$P = \frac{\rho\,g^2\,H^2\,T}{32\,\pi}$$

$$P = \frac{1025 \times (9.81)^2 \times (2)^2 \times 8}{32\,\pi}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{1025 \times 96.24 \times 4 \times 8}{100.53} \\
&= \frac{3{,}156{,}864}{100.53} \\
&\approx 31{,}402 \text{ W/m} \approx 31.4 \text{ kW/m}
\end{aligned}$$

Using the practical form: $P \approx 0.5 \times (2)^2 \times 8 = 16$ kW/m (empirical, lower).

**Final answer (rigorous):** **P ≈ 31.4 kW/m** (per metre of crest length).

For a 10 km long coast, available power = $31.4 \times 10{,}000 = 314$ MW of *gross* theoretical power — illustrating the *enormous* potential of Kerala's coastline for wave energy.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Downdraft Gasifier
- [x] **Four zones in order (top to bottom):** Drying → Pyrolysis → Oxidation → Reduction → Ash.
- [x] **Co-current downward flow** of biomass, air, and gas — distinguishes it from updraft.
- [x] **Optimum Equivalence Ratio (ER) = 0.25** (range 0.20–0.30).
- [x] **Producer gas CV ≈ 4.5–5.5 MJ/Nm³**; composition dominated by N₂ (~50 %), CO (~20 %), H₂ (~15 %).
- [x] **Cold gas efficiency** = (gas energy out) / (biomass energy in) × 100 % — typically **60–75 %**.
- [x] **Tar content is LOW** because the gas must pass through the *hot reduction zone* — key advantage over updraft.
- [x] **Limitations:** Cannot process biomass with > 20 % moisture; particle size must be 10–50 mm; bed height must be sufficient.
- [x] **Applications:** Direct firing in IC engines (dual-fuel mode), rice husk to power, rural electrification.
- [x] **Stoichiometric air** for typical biomass ≈ **5.6–6.0 kg air per kg fuel** (assume CH₁.₄O₀.₆).

### Tidal Power
- [x] **Tidal period = 12 h 25 min** (semi-diurnal); two full cycles per lunar day.
- [x] **Spring tides** = Sun + Moon aligned (max R); **Neap tides** = Sun and Moon at 90° (min R).
- [x] **Power formula:** $P_{\text{avg}} = \rho g A R^2 / T$ — note R is *squared*, so doubling R quadruples power.
- [x] **Two scheme types:** Single-effect (ebb only) vs. Double-effect (ebb + flood). Double yields ~ 40 % more energy.
- [x] **Turbine type:** **Bulb Kaplan** (low-head, high-flow, reversible).
- [x] **Indian sites:** Gulf of Kutch (R ≈ 5–8 m), Gulf of Cambay (R ≈ 10 m). Combined potential ≈ 14,000 MW.
- [x] **Capacity factor is low** (~ 25–30 %) because generation is intermittent and predictable but not continuous.
- [x] **Constraint:** Coastal civil works, marine ecosystem impact, sedimentation in basin.

### Wave Power
- [x] **Wave power per unit crest length:** $P = \rho g^2 H^2 T / (32 \pi) \approx 0.5 H^2 T$ kW/m.
- [x] **Wave energy per unit area:** $E = (1/8) \rho g H^2$ — KE = PE for sinusoidal wave.
- [x] **Group velocity (deep water):** $c_g = gT/(4\pi)$ — energy travels at half the phase speed.
- [x] **Main device types:** Point absorber, attenuator, terminator (OWC), overtopping.
- [x] **Wells turbine** is the workhorse for OWC because it rotates in one direction for *bidirectional* airflow.
- [x] **Practical conversion efficiency** is low (10–30 %) due to wave randomness and device limitations.
- [x] **Indian potential:** Kerala coast, Tamil Nadu coast — yet to be commercially exploited.
- [x] **Wave climate must be characterized** by H, T, and direction (fetch, duration, wind speed).

### Top 5 Most-Likely KTU 2024 Questions on This Topic
1. **Derive the tidal power formula** $P = \rho g A R^2 / T$ with assumptions — guaranteed 7–10 mark question.
2. **Derive the wave power formula** $P = \rho g^2 H^2 T / (32 \pi)$ — guaranteed 7–10 mark question.
3. **Draw and explain downdraft gasifier zones** with reactions — guaranteed 14-mark question.
4. **Numerical on cold gas efficiency** of a downdraft gasifier — guaranteed 5–7 mark sub-question.
5. **Numerical on tidal energy per cycle** and average power — guaranteed 5–7 mark sub-question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** In Part B answers, *always* (i) state assumptions clearly, (ii) define every symbol, (iii) draw a labeled diagram (even a simple block diagram scores 2–3 marks), and (iv) box the final numerical answer with units. Marks are deducted for *missing units*, not for *wrong units with correct reasoning*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Characteristics of Tidal Power, Classification of Tidal Power Plants - Harnessing Tidal Energy, Advantages and Limitations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Characteristics of Tidal Power, Classification of Tidal Power Plants - Harnessing Tidal Energy, Advantages and Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Tidal Power

**Tidal Power** is a form of **hydropower** that converts the energy contained in the natural rise and fall of ocean tides (caused by the gravitational interaction between the Earth, Moon, and Sun) into useful electrical energy, primarily through the construction of a **tidal barrage** across a suitable coastal inlet or estuary.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Tidal energy is the gravitational potential energy stored in the mass of water displaced between high tide and low tide. It is a renewable, predictable, and site-specific form of ocean energy.

### 1.2 Fundamental Characteristics of Tidal Power

| Characteristic | Description | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Tidal Range (h)** | Vertical difference between successive high and low tides | **0.5 m – 16 m** |
| **Tidal Cycle (T)** | Time for one complete cycle (high → low → high) | **12 hours 25 minutes** (Semidiurnal) |
| **Tidal Period (M₂)** | Principal lunar semi-diurnal constituent | **12.42 hours** |
| **Energy Density** | Kinetic energy per unit mass of seawater | ~800× that of air |
| **Predictability** | Astronomical determinism | **Centuries-ahead** forecasting |
| **Site Restriction** | Economic viability threshold | **h ≥ 5 m** for barrage plants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tidal Range vs. Tidal Amplitude:** *Tidal amplitude* is the height of tide relative to mean sea level, while **tidal range** = 2 × amplitude = (High Tide − Low Tide). For design calculations, we use the **tidal range (h)**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coastal Battery" Model

Imagine a giant **bucket placed at the shore**, with a sluice gate and a small waterwheel (turbine) at the bottom:

1. As the tide rises (flood tide), seawater fills the bucket through the open gate.
2. At **high tide**, the gate closes — the bucket is now full of water raised to a height **h** above the low tide level.
3. When the tide outside starts falling, the water inside the bucket is held back by the gate, creating a **head difference** (similar to a battery storing potential).
4. The gate is then opened just enough for water to flow out through a **turbine**, generating electricity — the water "discharges" its stored potential energy, exactly like a battery discharging.
5. The cycle repeats every **12 hours 25 minutes** (with a small variation due to lunar declination).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike solar/wind, the "fuel" (tidal water) is delivered for **free** by gravitational forces, making the Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) very low once the plant is built. The trade-off is the **massive capital expenditure** for the barrage and civil works.

### 1.4 Astronomical Origin of Tides

The fundamental tidal-generating force per unit mass is given by:

$$ F_t = \frac{2 G M_r R_E}{d^3} \cos(2\theta) $$

where:
- $G$ = Universal Gravitational Constant = **6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg²**
- $M_r$ = Mass of the tide-raising body (Moon ≈ 7.35 × 10²² kg)
- $R_E$ = Radius of Earth = **6.371 × 10⁶ m**
- $d$ = Distance between Earth and Moon = **3.844 × 10⁸ m**
- $\theta$ = Zenith angle of the tide-raising body

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** The Moon contributes **~67%** and the Sun contributes **~33%** of the total tidal force, even though the Sun is far more massive — because tidal force depends on the **gradient** of gravity, which scales as $1/d^3$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Energy Stored in a Tidal Basin

Consider a basin of horizontal surface area **A** filled by a rising tide to a mean level **h** above the low-tide level. The water mass per unit rise is $\rho A \, dz$, located at a height $z$ above the outlet. The total potential energy stored is:

$$ E_{PE} = \int_0^h \rho g A z \, dz = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 \quad \text{(Joules)} $$

This is the **theoretical maximum extractable energy per tidal cycle** in Joules.

### 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step

- **Step 1:** At the start, the basin is at low tide (empty relative to the working head).
- **Step 2:** As tide rises to height $z$, an elemental slice of water $dm = \rho A \, dz$ is raised by $z$, gaining P.E. = $dm \cdot g \cdot z$.
- **Step 3:** Integrating from $z = 0$ to $z = h$ gives total stored energy.
- **Step 4:** On ebbing through the turbine, only a fraction of this can be recovered (≈ **80–90%** ideally; **20–30%** in practice due to head losses, kinetic losses, and incomplete filling).

### 2.3 Average Power Equation

The average power over a complete cycle of period $T$ is:

$$ P_{avg} = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 \cdot f $$

where $f = 1/T$ is the tidal frequency (≈ **2.31 × 10⁻⁵ Hz** for semidiurnal tides, i.e., 2 cycles per day).

### 2.4 Tidal Prism (Volume)

The volume of water exchanged per tidal cycle is called the **tidal prism**:

$$ V_p = A \times h \quad \text{(m}^3\text{)} $$

This represents the "discharge per cycle" if the basin filled and emptied completely.

### 2.5 Tidal Stream Power (Current-Based)

For tidal stream turbines (no barrage, just underwater rotors in a current):

$$ P_{stream} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A_{swept} v^3 C_p $$

where:
- $v$ = tidal current velocity (m/s)
- $C_p$ = Power coefficient (Betz limit = **0.593**, practical ≈ 0.35–0.45)
- $A_{swept}$ = rotor swept area (m²)

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2$ | Tidal Potential Energy | **J** |
| 2 | $V_p = A \cdot h$ | Tidal Prism (volume) | **m³** |
| 3 | $P_{avg} = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 f$ | Average Power Output | **W** |
| 4 | $f = 2/T_{day} \approx 2.31 \times 10^{-5}$ | Semidiurnal frequency | **Hz** |
| 5 | $P_{stream} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$ | Tidal Stream Power | **W** |
| 6 | $F_t \propto M_r / d^3$ | Tidal Force on Earth | **N/kg** |
| 7 | $C_p^{max} = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ | Betz Limit (turbines) | **—** |
| 8 | $Q = A h / T$ | Tidal Flow Rate | **m³/s** |
| 9 | $E_{year} = 705 \times E$ | Annual Energy Yield | **J** |
| 10 | $\text{CF} = \frac{P_{avg}}{P_{installed}}$ | Capacity Factor (~ **25–35%**) | **—** |

### 2.7 Physical Constants (Standard Values for KTU Problems)

- Density of seawater: $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (**bold**)
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (**bold**)
- Density of freshwater: $\rho_{fw} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Number of semidiurnal cycles per year: **705** (≈ 2 × 365.25, adjusted for lunar month)

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The above formulas are used in:
- **Feasibility studies** for sites like the Gulf of Kutch (India), Bay of Fundy (Canada), Rance River (France), and the Severn Estuary (UK).
- **Sizing of turbine generators** and **slab/turbine selection** (bulb, rim, straflo).
- **Design of civil works** — barrage length, sluice gate area, lock gates.
- **Estimation of annual energy yield** for project DPRs (Detailed Project Reports).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: Average Power from a Tidal Barrage

**Problem:** Derive an expression for the average power available from a single-basin, single-effect tidal power plant.

**Derivation:**

Let the basin have a uniform horizontal surface area $A$ (m²) and the tidal range be $h$ (m). At the start of the cycle, the basin is empty (low tide). As the tide rises over a time $T/4$, the basin fills to high tide level.

**Step 1:** The mass of water entering the basin up to height $z$:

$$ dm = \rho A \, dz $$

**Step 2:** Potential energy gained by this slice (raised by height $z$):

$$ dE = dm \cdot g \cdot z = \rho A g z \, dz $$

**Step 3:** Total energy stored when filled to height $h$:

$$ E_{stored} = \int_0^h \rho A g z \, dz $$

$$ E_{stored} = \rho A g \left[ \frac{z^2}{2} \right]_0^h = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 $$

**Step 4:** Average power over a full cycle of period $T$ (one ebb + one flood):

$$ P_{avg} = \frac{E_{stored}}{T} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 \cdot \frac{1}{T} $$

**Step 5:** With overall efficiency $\eta$ (typically 0.25–0.40):

$$ \boxed{P_{avg} = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 f} $$

where $f = 1/T$ is the tidal frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the efficiency low?** Because the head varies from $h$ at the start of discharge to 0 at the end. Turbines work best at a specific head, so much of the late-cycle flow is lost. Also, the basin is not completely filled/emptied.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Solution: KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A tidal barrage is proposed at a site with the following data:
- Basin area $A = 20 \text{ km}^2 = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- Mean tidal range $h = 6.5 \text{ m}$
- Overall efficiency of generation $\eta = 0.35$
- Number of effective cycles per day = 2 (semidiurnal)

**Calculate:**
1. The total energy available per tidal cycle
2. The average power output
3. The annual energy generation

**Given:**
- $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $A = 20 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$, $h = 6.5 \text{ m}$, $\eta = 0.35$

**Step 1 — Energy per cycle (theoretical):**

$$ E_{theory} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 $$

$$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times (20 \times 10^6) \times (6.5)^2 $$

$$ = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 2.0 \times 10^7 \times 42.25 $$

Let us compute stepwise:

$$ 0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5 $$

$$ 512.5 \times 9.81 = 5027.625 $$

$$ 5027.625 \times 2.0 \times 10^7 = 1.005525 \times 10^{11} $$

$$ 1.005525 \times 10^{11} \times 42.25 = 4.248 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} $$

$$ \boxed{E_{theory} \approx 4.248 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} \approx 4248 \text{ GJ}} $$

**Step 2 — Practical (extractable) energy per cycle:**

$$ E_{extract} = \eta \cdot E_{theory} = 0.35 \times 4.248 \times 10^{12} $$

$$ \boxed{E_{extract} \approx 1.487 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} \approx 1487 \text{ GJ}} $$

**Step 3 — Average power:**

The cycle time for semidiurnal tide: $T = 12.42 \text{ hours} = 44712 \text{ s}$

$$ P_{avg} = \frac{E_{extract}}{T} = \frac{1.487 \times 10^{12}}{44712} $$

$$ P_{avg} = 3.326 \times 10^7 \text{ W} $$

$$ \boxed{P_{avg} \approx 33.26 \text{ MW}} $$

**Step 4 — Annual energy yield:**

Number of cycles per year $N \approx 705$

$$ E_{year} = N \times E_{extract} = 705 \times 1.487 \times 10^{12} $$

$$ E_{year} = 1.048 \times 10^{15} \text{ J} $$

Converting to MWh: $1 \text{ MWh} = 3.6 \times 10^9 \text{ J}$

$$ E_{year} = \frac{1.048 \times 10^{15}}{3.6 \times 10^9} $$

$$ \boxed{E_{year} \approx 2.91 \times 10^5 \text{ MWh} = 291{,}000 \text{ MWh/year}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix Comparison** of different sites (for KTU viva context):

| Site | Tidal Range (m) | Area (km²) | Theoretical P (MW) | Practical P (MW) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rance, France** | 8.4 | 22 | 240 | **240 (actual)** |
| **Bay of Fundy (proposed)** | 16.0 | 200 | ~3,000 | ~1,200 (est.) |
| **Gulf of Kutch, India** | 6.0 | 35 | ~250 | ~80 (est.) |
| **Severn Estuary, UK** | 9.0 | 540 | ~6,000 | ~2,000 (est.) |
| **Our problem** | 6.5 | 20 | 95 | **33.3** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Tidal Power Plants

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root["TIDAL POWER PLANTS"]
    
    Root --> BARR["TIDAL BARRAGE"]
    Root --> STREAM["TIDAL STREAM"]
    Root --> LAG["TIDAL LAGOON"]
    Root --> DYN["DYNAMIC TIDAL POWER"]
    
    BARR --> B1["Single Basin - Single Effect"]
    BARR --> B2["Single Basin - Double Effect"]
    BARR --> B3["Double Basin System"]
    BARR --> B4["Pumping Scheme"]
    
    STREAM --> S1["Horizontal Axis Turbine"]
    STREAM --> S2["Vertical Axis Turbine"]
    STREAM --> S3["Oscillating Hydrofoil"]
    STREAM --> S4["Venturi Duct Augmentation"]
    
    LAG --> L1["Onshore Coastal Lagoon"]
    LAG --> L2["Offshore Enclosed Lagoon"]
    
    DYN --> D1["Long Coastal Dam TBM"]
    
    B1 --> B1A["Ebb Generation Only"]
    B1 --> B1B["Flood Generation Only"]
    
    B2 --> B2A["Ebb + Flood Bidirectional"]
    
    B3 --> B3A["High Pool + Low Pool"]
    B3 --> B3B["Continuous Power Output"]
    
    B4 --> B4A["Auxiliary Pump at Low Demand"]
    B4 --> B4B["Increases Head Differential"]
```

### 4.2 Tidal Barrage Working — Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["OCEAN TIDE - HIGH"] -->|Rising Water| B["SLUICE GATE OPEN"]
    B -->|Water Inflow| C["BASIN RESERVOIR"]
    C -->|Water Held| D["SLUICE GATE CLOSED AT HIGH TIDE"]
    D -->|Ebb Tide Outside| E["HEAD DIFFERENTIAL CREATED"]
    E -->|Low Tide Outside| F["TURBINE INLET VALVE OPENED"]
    F -->|Water Discharge| G["BULB / STRAFLO TURBINE"]
    G -->|Mechanical Rotation| H["GEAR BOX / COUPLING"]
    H -->|Shaft Rotation| I["SYNCHRONOUS GENERATOR"]
    I -->|AC Power| J["STEP-UP TRANSFORMER"]
    J -->|HV Transmission| K["GRID / LOAD CENTER"]
    G -->|Used Water| L["OUTLET TO SEA - LOW TIDE"]
    
    M["PUMP STATION OPTIONAL"] -.->|Reverse Pumping| C
    N["NAVIGATION LOCK"] --- C
```

### 4.3 Single-Basin Single-Effect — Operating Sequence (Ebb Generation)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OT as Ocean Tide
    participant SG as Sluice Gate
    participant BS as Basin
    participant TR as Turbine
    participant GR as Generator
    
    Note over OT,GR: Phase 1 - Flood Tide (Generation OFF)
    OT->>SG: Rising Sea Level
    SG->>BS: Open - Water Enters Basin
    
    Note over OT,GR: Phase 2 - High Tide (Holding)
    OT->>SG: Maximum Level Reached
    SG->>SG: Close Sluice Gate
    OT->>OT: Begin Receding
    
    Note over OT,GR: Phase 3 - Ebb Generation
    OT->>SG: Sea Level Drops Below Basin
    SG->>TR: Open Turbine Gates
    BS->>TR: Water Discharges Through Turbine
    TR->>GR: Mechanical to Electrical Energy
    GR-->>GR: Power to Grid (4-5 hours)
    
    Note over OT,GR: Phase 4 - Low Tide (Wait)
    OT->>SG: Equal Levels Reached
    SG->>SG: Close Turbine Gates
```

### 4.4 Turbine Types Used in Tidal Barrages (Functional Comparison Block)

```mermaid
graph TB
    T["TIDAL TURBINES"] --> T1["BULB TURBINE"]
    T["TIDAL TURBINES"] --> T2["RIM TURBINE"]
    T["TIDAL TURBINES"] --> T3["STRAFLO TURBINE"]
    T["TIDAL TURBINES"] --> T4["TUBULAR TURBINE"]
    
    T1 --> T1A["Generator Encased in Waterproof Bulb"]
    T1 --> T1B["Axial Flow - Most Common - Used in Rance"]
    
    T2 --> T2A["Generator on Rim of Rotor"]
    T2 --> T2B["Eliminates Bulb Obstruction"]
    
    T3 --> T3A["Straight Flow - Generator Outside"]
    T3 --> T3B["Direct Drive from Blades"]
    
    T4 --> T4A["Compact Inline Design"]
    T4 --> T4B["Low Head Applications"]
```

### 4.5 Schematic of a Double-Basin Tidal Plant (Textual Layout)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SYSTEM["DOUBLE BASIN TIDAL PLANT"]
        OCEAN["OCEAN"]
        H1["HIGH TIDE SLUICE A"]
        H2["LOW TIDE SLUICE B"]
        HIGH_POOL["HIGH POOL BASIN"]
        TURBINE_HOUSE["TURBINE HOUSE (Bidirectional)"]
        LOW_POOL["LOW POOL BASIN"]
        
        OCEAN -->|Flood| H1 --> HIGH_POOL
        HIGH_POOL -->|Ebb Through Turbine| TURBINE_HOUSE
        TURBINE_HOUSE -->|To Low Pool| LOW_POOL
        LOW_POOL -->|Drain| H2 --> OCEAN
    end
    
    GEN["GENERATOR UNIT"]
    TURBINE_HOUSE -.-> GEN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** In a double-basin scheme, the **high pool** is filled at high tide, the **low pool** is emptied at low tide, and the **turbine house** runs continuously as water flows from high → low pool. This addresses the **intermittency** problem of single-basin plants.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define **tidal range**. What is the minimum tidal range generally required for the economic operation of a tidal power plant?

**Model Answer:**
The **tidal range (h)** is defined as the vertical difference in seawater level between successive **high tide** and **low tide** at a given coastal location. Mathematically, $h = H_{HT} - H_{LT}$.

For economic operation of a tidal barrage power plant, the minimum tidal range required is approximately **5 m** (with optimal plants operating at ranges of 7 m or more). Below 5 m, the energy density per unit area becomes too low to justify the high capital cost of the barrage structure and turbines.

*(Examiner's note: Mention 5 m threshold; cite Rance (8.4 m) or Bay of Fundy (16 m) as examples for full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** List any **four advantages** of tidal power generation over other renewable sources.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Highly Predictable** — Tides can be predicted centuries in advance using astronomical data (unlike solar/wind).
2. **High Energy Density** — Seawater is ~800× denser than air, so even small tidal volumes yield significant energy.
3. **Zero Fuel Cost and Zero Carbon Emissions** during operation.
4. **Long Plant Life** — Tidal barrages have a lifespan of **75–100 years**, longer than most renewables.
5. **Low Operating Cost** once commissioned; minimal recurring expenses.
*(Any four points for 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In Part B derivations, students often forget the **efficiency factor (η)** when computing actual (practical) energy. Marks are specifically allocated for distinguishing **theoretical** vs **practical** energy. Also, ensure units are consistent — convert **km² to m²** and **hours to seconds** before substituting.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice Option**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the average power available from a **single-basin, single-effect** tidal power plant in terms of basin area, tidal range, and tidal period. [7 marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

Let:
- $A$ = effective basin area (m²) — assumed uniform
- $h$ = mean tidal range (m)
- $\rho$ = density of seawater = **1025 kg/m³**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $T$ = tidal period (s)
- $\eta$ = overall efficiency

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The mass of an elemental slice of water raised by a height $z$ above the basin floor:

$$ dm = \rho A \, dz $$

**Step 2:** Potential energy of this slice:

$$ dE = dm \cdot g \cdot z = \rho A g z \, dz $$

**Step 3:** Integrating from $z = 0$ (low tide) to $z = h$ (high tide):

$$ E = \int_0^h \rho A g z \, dz = \rho A g \cdot \left[ \frac{z^2}{2} \right]_0^h $$

$$ E = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 \quad \text{(Theoretical energy per cycle, in Joules)} $$

**Step 4:** Average power over the cycle of period $T$:

$$ P_{avg} = \frac{E}{T} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 \cdot \frac{1}{T} $$

**Step 5:** Including efficiency $\eta$:

$$ \boxed{P_{avg} = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 \cdot f} $$

where $f = 1/T \approx 2.31 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Hz}$ for semidiurnal tides.

---

**(b)** A single-basin tidal power plant is proposed at a site with basin area **30 km²** and mean tidal range **7.5 m**. The overall conversion efficiency is **0.30**. Calculate:
1. The energy generated per tidal cycle
2. The average power output
3. The annual energy yield in MWh

Assume $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. [7 marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A = 30 \text{ km}^2 = 30 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$
- $h = 7.5 \text{ m}$
- $\eta = 0.30$
- $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $T = 12.42 \text{ h} = 44712 \text{ s}$ (semidiurnal)
- $N = 705$ cycles/year

**Sub-step 1 — Theoretical energy per cycle:**

$$ E_{th} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 $$

$$ = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 10^6 \times (7.5)^2 $$

Computing stepwise:
- $0.5 \times 1025 = 512.5$
- $512.5 \times 9.81 = 5027.625$
- $5027.625 \times 30 \times 10^6 = 1.508 \times 10^{11}$
- $(7.5)^2 = 56.25$
- $1.508 \times 10^{11} \times 56.25 = 8.486 \times 10^{12} \text{ J}$

**Sub-step 2 — Practical (extractable) energy per cycle:**

$$ E_{practical} = \eta \times E_{th} = 0.30 \times 8.486 \times 10^{12} $$

$$ E_{practical} = 2.546 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} $$

$$ \boxed{E_{practical} \approx 2546 \text{ GJ per cycle}} $$

**Sub-step 3 — Average power:**

$$ P_{avg} = \frac{E_{practical}}{T} = \frac{2.546 \times 10^{12}}{44712} $$

$$ \boxed{P_{avg} \approx 5.69 \times 10^7 \text{ W} \approx 56.9 \text{ MW}} $$

**Sub-step 4 — Annual energy yield:**

$$ E_{annual} = N \times E_{practical} = 705 \times 2.546 \times 10^{12} $$

$$ E_{annual} = 1.795 \times 10^{15} \text{ J} $$

Converting to MWh: $\text{MWh} = \text{J} / (3.6 \times 10^9)$

$$ E_{annual} = \frac{1.795 \times 10^{15}}{3.6 \times 10^9} $$

$$ \boxed{E_{annual} \approx 4.99 \times 10^5 \text{ MWh} \approx 4.99 \times 10^5 \text{ MWh/year}} $$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice Option**

**(a)** Explain the various **methods of harnessing tidal energy** with a neat classification. Briefly describe the working of a **tidal stream system**. [7 marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Methods of harnessing tidal energy:**

1. **Tidal Barrage** (most mature technology) — A dam-like structure built across a tidal estuary. Water is trapped at high tide and released through turbines at low tide.
   - *Single-basin, single-effect:* Generates power only during ebb tide.
   - *Single-basin, double-effect:* Generates during both ebb and flood (bidirectional).
   - *Double-basin:* Uses two basins (high pool + low pool) for continuous power.
   - *Pumping scheme:* Adds energy at low-demand periods to enhance head.

2. **Tidal Stream (Current) Systems** — Underwater turbines placed in fast-flowing tidal channels. Operates like underwater wind turbines but uses the kinetic energy of moving water. No barrage needed.

3. **Tidal Lagoons** — Coastal lagoons enclosed by dams (similar to barrages but built in shallow seas). Lower environmental impact.

4. **Dynamic Tidal Power (DTP)** — A long dam (tens of km) built perpendicular to the coast. Exploits the natural phase difference along the coast to generate continuous power.

**Working of a Tidal Stream System:**

- Tidal streams are fast-moving (1–3 m/s) horizontal flows caused by tides in narrow channels.
- A **bidirectional axial-flow turbine** is mounted on a seabed tripod or monopile.
- The rotor blades are pitched to extract kinetic energy during both flood and ebb flows.
- The rotor drives a **permanent magnet synchronous generator** via a gearbox (or direct drive).
- Power is transmitted via a subsea cable to shore.
- Capacity factor typically **30–45%**, but capacity can be 1–2 MW per turbine.

**Mathematical basis:**

$$ P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p $$

where $v$ is the current velocity, $A$ is the swept area, and $C_p$ is the power coefficient (Betz limit = 0.593).

---

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working of a **single-basin, single-effect (ebb generation) tidal power plant**. List its **four major limitations**. [7 marks]

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (Ebb Generation Scheme):**

1. **Filling Phase (4–5 hours):** At rising tide, the **sluice gates** are opened. Seawater flows freely into the basin through the open sluices, with no generation (head is too small).

2. **Holding Phase (1–2 hours):** At **high tide**, the sluice gates are closed, and the basin is isolated. The sea level outside begins to drop due to the receding tide.

3. **Generation Phase (4–5 hours):** When the head difference across the barrage exceeds a threshold (typically 1–2 m), the **turbine gates** are opened. Water flows from the basin to the sea through **bulb turbines** (or straflo/rim turbines), spinning the generator and producing electricity.

4. **Waiting Phase (1–2 hours):** At low tide, the head difference becomes zero. Turbine gates are closed. The cycle waits for the next flood tide.

**Diagrammatic Representation (as per Mermaid Section 4.2):**
- Ocean ↔ Sluice gate ↔ Basin reservoir
- Basin ↔ Turbine ↔ Generator ↔ Grid
- Pump station (optional) and navigation lock (auxiliary)

**Four Major Limitations:**

1. **High Capital Cost** — Civil works (barrage, sluice gates, locks) are extremely expensive. LCOE is high compared to fossil fuels.

2. **Site-Specific** — Only locations with a high tidal range (>5 m) and suitable topography (narrow bay/estuary) are viable. Very few sites exist globally.

3. **Intermittent Power Output** — Generation occurs only 4–5 hours per cycle (≈ 8–10 hours/day). Continuous supply requires **double-basin** or grid backup.

4. **Environmental Impact** — Barrages alter tidal flushing, leading to:
   - Sediment accumulation (siltation)
   - Disruption of fish migration
   - Changes in salinity and ecosystem of the estuary
   - Effect on coastal sediment transport

5. **Long Gestation Period** — Construction can take 5–10 years.
6. **Navigation Disruption** — Barrages require locks, adding transit time and cost.

*(Any four well-explained points for 7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Tidal Power**

- **Tidal Energy Origin:** Gravitational pull of Moon (67%) + Sun (33%); proportional to $M/d^3$.
- **Tidal Range (h):** High tide − Low tide; economically viable if **h ≥ 5 m**.
- **Tidal Period (T):** 12 h 25 min (semidiurnal) → 2 cycles/day → **705 cycles/year**.
- **Master Formula:** $E = \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2$ (per cycle) → remember the **½ factor**!
- **Average Power:** $P_{avg} = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho g A h^2 \cdot f$; typical $\eta = 0.25$ to $0.40$.
- **Tidal Prism:** $V_p = A \cdot h$ — water volume exchanged per cycle.
- **Tidal Stream Power:** $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A v^3 C_p$, $C_p^{max} = 0.593$ (Betz limit).
- **Classification:** Barrage (4 sub-types) | Stream | Lagoon | Dynamic — know the working of each.
- **Turbine Types:** Bulb (most common), Rim, Straflo, Tubular — all axial-flow Kaplan variants.
- **Single-Basin Single-Effect:** Generation only in ONE direction (ebb OR flood); power output is intermittent.
- **Single-Basin Double-Effect:** Bidirectional generation; more energy but complex control.
- **Double-Basin:** Two reservoirs (high + low) → continuous power; highest capital cost.
- **Pumping Scheme:** Pumps water uphill during slack tide → increases head → boosts energy.
- **Standard Constants:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $N = 705$ cycles/year.
- **Capacity Factor of Tidal Plants:** typically **25–35%** (lower than baseload plants).
- **Predictability:** 100% (astronomical) — a key advantage over wind/solar.
- **Famous Examples:** Rance, France (240 MW); Bay of Fundy, Canada; Gulf of Kutch, India (potential); Severn Estuary, UK.
- **Lifespan:** 75–100 years (barrage) vs. 20–25 years (wind turbines).
- **Environmental Concerns:** Sedimentation, fish migration, salinity change, marine ecosystem disruption.
- **Unit Conversion Traps:** km² → m² (×10⁶), h → s (×3600), J → MWh (÷3.6 × 10⁹).
- **Always include η** in practical calculations; theoretical values alone are exam traps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)

### Formal Academic Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OTEC (Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion)** is a renewable energy technology that harnesses the **thermal gradient** between the **warm surface water** of tropical/sub-tropical oceans and the **cold deep ocean water** to drive a heat engine and produce useful electrical or mechanical work. According to KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, OTEC is a **non-conventional, low-grade, solar-derived thermal energy system** requiring a minimum temperature differential of **$\Delta T \geq 20^{\circ}\text{C}$** between the warm surface layer and the cold deep layer (typically below 1000 m depth).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of OTEC like a **"natural refrigerator"** that you can run in reverse to generate electricity. Imagine the ocean's surface as a giant solar collector that has been heated by the Sun for centuries, while the deep ocean acts as a massive cold-storage tank. OTEC works exactly like a conventional thermal power plant, except instead of burning coal to create hot and cold reservoirs, it cleverly uses two naturally existing reservoirs already present in tropical oceans. The bigger the **temperature gap** between the hot (sun-warmed surface) and cold (deep) water, the more efficiently you can run a heat engine between them — just like how a pressure cooker builds more pressure when there's a bigger temperature difference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact for KTU Exams:** OTEC was first conceptualized by **Jacques-Arsène d'Arsonval** (1881) and the first working cycle was proposed by his student **Georges Claude** (1930). The thermal efficiency of OTEC is inherently low (typically 3–6%) because of small temperature differentials, but it offers **24/7 base-load renewable power**.

---

## 1.2 Classification of OTEC Systems (KTU High-Yield Topic)

| OTEC Type | Working Fluid | Cycle Used | Key Advantage | Major Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Closed Cycle OTEC (CC-OTEC)** | Pure working fluid (Ammonia, R-134a) | Rankine Cycle | Compact heat exchangers, no vacuum needed | Working fluid toxicity (NH₃) |
| **Open Cycle OTEC (OC-OTEC)** | Seawater itself (direct) | Claude Cycle | Produces fresh water as by-product | Requires very large vacuum pumps |
| **Hybrid OTEC** | Combined — Seawater + ammonia | Rankine + Claude | Dual output (electricity + desalinated water) | Higher capital complexity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid OTEC** uses warm seawater to flash-evaporate, then uses the steam in a turbine, condenses using cold deep water, and uses the fresh water as a pre-heater for the closed-cycle ammonia loop. This is the configuration adopted in the **SCEEP plant (Hawaii, 1993)**.

---

## 1.3 Geothermal Energy — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geothermal Energy** is the heat energy stored within the Earth's interior, originating primarily from the original planetary accretion (primordial heat), radioactive decay of isotopes (U-238, Th-232, K-40), and exothermic chemical reactions in the mantle. The estimated average geothermal gradient of the Earth's crust is approximately **$25$–$30^{\circ}\text{C/km}$**, with hot spots reaching **$200^{\circ}\text{C/km}$**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of the Earth as a **"giant pressure cooker with a thick lid."** The deeper you go, the more the contents are squeezed and heated. If you drill a straw into a region where the heat is accessible (like Iceland or the Pacific Ring of Fire), steam and hot water naturally shoot up to the surface, ready to spin a turbine.

---

## 1.4 Green Energy & Fuel Cells — Quick Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Green Energy** refers to energy derived from natural processes that are **replenished at a rate equal to or faster than the rate of consumption**, with **near-zero net carbon emissions** during operation. KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes it under **clean, sustainable, and indigenous** energy sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Fuel Cell** is an **electrochemical energy conversion device** that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically **H₂**) and an oxidant (typically **O₂**) into electrical energy, with **water and heat** as by-products. It is **NOT a heat engine** — hence it is not bound by the Carnot limit. The theoretical efficiency can approach **80–90%**, with practical fuel cell systems achieving **40–60% electrical efficiency**.

### Intuition for Fuel Cell
A fuel cell is like a **"battery that never runs out"** — as long as you keep feeding it hydrogen and oxygen, it keeps producing electricity and water, silently, with no combustion, no moving parts (in the membrane), and no pollution. It is the **opposite of electrolysis**: where electrolysis uses electricity to split water, a fuel cell recombines hydrogen and oxygen to **make** electricity.

---

## 1.5 Hydrogen Energy — Syllabus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrogen Energy** refers to the use of **H₂ gas** as a **zero-carbon energy carrier** (not a primary energy source). Hydrogen is the **most abundant element in the universe (~75% by mass)** but must be **extracted** from compounds (CH₄, H₂O, biomass) using primary energy. It is classified as **green, blue, or grey** based on production route.

| Hydrogen Color | Production Method | Carbon Emissions |
|---|---|---|
| **Green Hydrogen** | Electrolysis powered by renewables | **Zero** |
| **Blue Hydrogen** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) with CCS | **Low** |
| **Grey Hydrogen** | Steam Methane Reforming without CCS | **High (~10 kg CO₂/kg H₂)** |
| **Pink Hydrogen** | Nuclear-powered electrolysis | Near-zero |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 OTEC — Principle of Working (Detailed)

The OTEC system operates as a **low-temperature differential heat engine**. The operating principle relies on **Carnot's theorem** to establish the **maximum theoretical efficiency**.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1: Cold & Warm Water Intakes**
- A **floating or shoreline OTEC plant** draws **warm surface seawater** (typically $25$–$30^{\circ}\text{C}$) through a surface intake (sub-surface pipe) at depths of **0–20 m**.
- **Cold deep seawater** (typically $4$–$7^{\circ}\text{C}$) is pumped up from depths exceeding **1000 m** through a long, high-density polyethylene (HDPE) cold-water pipe (CWP).

**Step 2: Heat Exchange in the Evaporator**
- The warm seawater transfers its heat to a working fluid (in CC-OTEC, **ammonia**) inside the **evaporator** (heat exchanger).
- Ammonia has a low boiling point ($\approx -33^{\circ}\text{C}$ at 1 atm), so it easily vaporizes.

**Step 3: Turbine Expansion**
- The high-pressure ammonia vapor expands through a **turbine**, producing mechanical work that drives a **generator** to produce electricity.
- The vapor pressure drops, temperature reduces.

**Step 4: Condensation Using Cold Water**
- The low-pressure ammonia vapor passes through the **condenser**, where cold deep seawater absorbs its latent heat.
- Ammonia returns to a liquid state.

**Step 5: Working Fluid Recirculation**
- The liquid ammonia is pressurized by the **working fluid pump** and re-circulated to the evaporator — closing the **Rankine loop**.

### Why $\Delta T = 20^{\circ}\text{C}$ is Critical
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Carnot efficiency** of a heat engine depends directly on the temperature difference between hot and cold reservoirs. Below $\Delta T = 20^{\circ}\text{C}$, the **net work output** becomes too small to overcome parasitic loads (pumping power for cold water, working fluid circulation), making the plant **economically and energetically non-viable**.

---

## 2.2 Closed Cycle OTEC (CC-OTEC) — Detailed Process

In CC-OTEC, the working fluid (typically **anhydrous ammonia NH₃** or **R-134a**) remains in a sealed loop.

**Process Flow:**
- Evaporator → Turbine → Condenser → Pump → Evaporator
- The heat source is the warm seawater, the heat sink is the cold seawater.
- Net electricity output requires **net work = Turbine work − Pump work − CWP pumping work**.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- CC-OTEC is **more commercially mature** than open cycle.
- Used in Japan's **Saga University 100 kW plant (1982)** and the **Mini-OTEC** (1979, Hawaii).
- Suitable for modular and offshore floating plants.

---

## 2.3 Open Cycle OTEC (OC-OTEC) — Detailed Process

In OC-OTEC (the **Claude Cycle**), seawater itself is the working fluid.

**Process Flow:**
1. Warm seawater enters a **flash evaporator** maintained at **partial vacuum (~0.035 atm)** corresponding to about $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ saturation.
2. Water flashes to **low-pressure steam**.
3. Steam drives a **low-pressure turbine** (specially designed for very low inlet pressure).
4. Spent steam enters a **surface condenser** cooled by deep ocean water, where it **condenses into fresh water** — a precious by-product.
5. The condensate is then pumped to the de-aerator to release dissolved gases.

### Real-World Utility
- The **SCEEP (Sea Solar Experimental Energy Plant)** in Hawaii (100 kW, 1993) demonstrated open cycle OTEC at sea.
- India's **Sagar Sampada** research vessel performed extensive OTEC trials in the **Lakshadweep Sea**.

---

## 2.4 Hybrid OTEC — Combined Cycle

The **hybrid OTEC** combines CC-OTEC and OC-OTEC:
- Seawater flash-evaporates to produce **low-pressure steam**.
- Steam is fed to an **evaporator** that boils ammonia for the closed cycle.
- Condensed fresh water from the open cycle is used to pre-heat the closed-loop fluid.
- **Dual Output:** Electricity + Fresh water.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Examiners frequently test this concept with a comparative cycle diagram — students must label **Open**, **Closed**, and **Hybrid** with their **working fluid, output, and pressure level** in the cycle.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (OTEC, Geothermal, Fuel Cell)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Tables:** All absolute values and modulus expressions use `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Expression | Variables & Units | Boundary Condition / Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Carnot (Maximum) Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | $T_H$, $T_C$ in **Kelvin (K)** | Idealized — actual $\eta \approx 3$–$6\%$ |
| 2 | **Net Power Output of OTEC** | $P_{\text{net}} = \eta \cdot Q_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{parasitic}}$ | $P$ in **kW**, $Q$ in **kW** | $\Delta T \geq 20^{\circ}\text{C}$ required |
| 3 | **Heat Input to Working Fluid** | $Q_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_p \cdot (T_H - T_{\text{evap}})$ | $\dot{m}_w$ in kg/s, $c_p \approx 4.18$ kJ/kg·K | Used in evaporator |
| 4 | **Heat Rejection in Condenser** | $Q_{\text{out}} = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{cond}} - T_C)$ | $\dot{m}_c$ in kg/s | $T_C$ is deep water temperature |
| 5 | **Cold Water Pipe (CWP) Diameter** | $D_{\text{CWP}} = \left(\dfrac{8 \dot{m}_c^2 f L}{\pi^2 \rho^2 \Delta P}\right)^{1/5}$ | $D$ in m, $L$ in m | Friction factor $f$ is critical |
| 6 | **Volumetric Flow of Seawater** | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{in}}}{c_p \rho \Delta T}$ | $\dot{V}$ in m³/s | Typical ratio $= 4$ m³ cold : 2 m³ warm per MWh |
| 7 | **Geothermal Power Output** | $P = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{\text{in}} - h_{\text{out}}) \cdot \eta_{\text{turb}}$ | $h$ in kJ/kg | Depends on resource type |
| 8 | **Geothermal Gradient** | $\dfrac{dT}{dz} = \alpha$ | $\alpha \approx 30^{\circ}\text{C/km}$ (crust) | Increases near tectonic boundaries |
| 9 | **Fuel Cell EMF (Nernst Equation)** | $E = E^{\circ} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\dfrac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$ | $E$ in V, $F = 96485$ C/mol | $n = 2$ for H₂ fuel cell |
| 10 | **Hydrogen Energy Density (HHV)** | $E_{H_2} = 142 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | MJ/kg | Compare with gasoline: 46 MJ/kg |
| 11 | **Hydrogen Heating Value (LHV)** | $E_{H_2,LHV} = 120 \text{ MJ/kg}$ | MJ/kg | Used when water is in vapor form |
| 12 | **Hydrogen Combustion Reaction** | $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O + 285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$ | kJ/mol | Highly exothermic, clean |

---

## 2.6 Geothermal Energy — Working Principle

### Heat Extraction Mechanism
1. **Geothermal Reservoir:** Hot rocks or magma chambers heat water underground, forming a **hydrothermal resource**.
2. **Production Well:** Drilled to bring **steam or hot water** to the surface.
3. **Steam Separator:** Separates steam from water (for **flash steam plants**).
4. **Turbine & Generator:** Steam drives turbine, producing electricity.
5. **Cooling Tower / Re-injection Well:** Spent fluid is **re-injected** into the reservoir to maintain pressure — a critical **closed-loop sustainability** principle.

### Types of Geothermal Resources
- **Hydrothermal (convective):** Naturally occurring steam/hot water reservoirs.
- **Hot Dry Rock (HDR) / Engineered Geothermal Systems (EGS):** Artificially fractured hot rocks with injected water.
- **Geopressured:** High-pressure, high-temperature brine with dissolved methane.

---

## 2.7 Classification of Geothermal Power Plants

| Type | Resource | Temperature Range | Working Fluid | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry Steam** | Natural steam | $> 200^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Direct steam | 10–20% |
| **Flash Steam** | Hot water > $150^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $150$–$300^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Flashed steam | 8–15% |
| **Binary Cycle** | Hot water $90$–$180^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $90$–$180^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Organic fluid (isobutane, isopentane) | 8–12% |
| **Enhanced/EGS** | HDR deep wells | $> 200^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Injected water | 5–10% |

---

## 2.8 Classification of Fuel Cells (High-Yield KTU Topic)

| Type | Full Name | Electrolyte | Operating Temperature | Fuel | Mobile Ion | Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AFC** | Alkaline Fuel Cell | KOH solution | $50$–$200^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Pure H₂ | $\text{OH}^-$ | 50–70% |
| **PEMFC** | Proton Exchange Membrane FC | Polymer membrane (Nafion) | $60$–$100^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Pure H₂ | $\text{H}^+$ | 40–60% |
| **PAFC** | Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell | H₃PO₄ | $150$–$220^{\circ}\text{C}$ | H₂ (with CO tolerant) | $\text{H}^+$ | 40–50% |
| **MCFC** | Molten Carbonate FC | Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃ | $600$–$700^{\circ}\text{C}$ | H₂, CO, CH₄ | $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ | 50–60% |
| **SOFC** | Solid Oxide Fuel Cell | YSZ (Yttria-Stabilized Zirconia) | $700$–$1000^{\circ}\text{C}$ | H₂, CO, CH₄ | $\text{O}^{2-}$ | 50–65% |
| **DMFC** | Direct Methanol Fuel Cell | Polymer membrane | $60$–$130^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Liquid CH₃OH | $\text{H}^+$ | 20–30% |

### Reactions in H₂ Fuel Cell (PEMFC Example)

**Anode (Oxidation):**

$$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

**Cathode (Reduction):**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For **SOFC**, the mobile ion is **$\text{O}^{2-}$** (oxide ion) moving from cathode to anode. For **MCFC**, mobile ion is **$\text{CO}_3^{2-}$** (carbonate ion). For **PEMFC & PAFC**, mobile ion is **$\text{H}^+$** (proton). The examiner's favorite trick is asking you to write the **half-reactions** for an unfamiliar fuel cell type.

---

## 2.9 Hydrogen Energy — Storage and Transport

### Hydrogen Storage Methods
1. **Compressed Gas (CGH₂):** Stored at 350–700 bar in composite tanks.
2. **Liquefied (LH₂):** Cryogenic storage at $-253^{\circ}\text{C}$ — high energy cost of liquefaction.
3. **Metal Hydrides:** Chemically bonded in $\text{MgH}_2$, $\text{LaNi}_5$ — safe but heavy.
4. **Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHC):** Reversible chemical bonding with toluene/methylcyclohexane.
5. **Underground Storage:** Salt caverns (used extensively in the US Gulf Coast).

### Hydrogen Production Methods
- **Electrolysis:** $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$ (renewable if powered by solar/wind).
- **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR):** $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_2$ (dominant method).
- **Biomass Gasification:** Produces syngas ($\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}$).
- **Photo-biological Splitting:** Algae-based, still in research stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Hydrogen Energy" as a primary energy source. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically classifies it as an **energy carrier (secondary)**, similar to electricity — it must be produced from a primary source (fossil, nuclear, or renewable).

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility — Integrated Perspective

| Domain | Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| **Tropical island nations** (Maldives, Lakshadweep) | OTEC + freshwater dual-purpose plants |
| **Pacific Ring of Fire** (Japan, Iceland, Indonesia) | Geothermal baseload electricity |
| **Space & Defense** (NASA) | AFC, PEMFC for space missions and submarines |
| **Automotive** (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo) | PEMFC hydrogen cars |
| **Industrial Heating** | Hydrogen replaces natural gas in steel/cement |
| **Telecom Towers** (India) | Green hydrogen for backup power |
| **Power-to-Gas** | Excess renewable electricity → H₂ → injected into gas grid |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Carnot Efficiency of an Ideal OTEC System

We begin with the definition of thermal efficiency for any heat engine:

$$\eta = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

By the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a cyclic heat engine:

$$W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}$$

Substituting into the efficiency expression:

$$\eta = \frac{Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

For a **reversible (Carnot) cycle**, the ratio $\dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ depends only on the absolute reservoir temperatures:

$$\frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

Substituting this reversible relationship:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

For a tropical OTEC site, with warm surface water at $T_H = 28^{\circ}\text{C}$ and cold deep water at $T_C = 5^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$T_H = 28 + 273.15 = 301.15 \text{ K}$$
$$T_C = 5 + 273.15 = 278.15 \text{ K}$$

Therefore:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{278.15}{301.15}$$

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - 0.9235$$

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 0.0765 = 7.65\%$$

**Interpretation:** Even an **ideal, lossless OTEC plant** can convert only **7.65%** of the absorbed solar energy into work. This is why OTEC plants must be **large-scale** and **co-located with useful heat sinks/sources** (e.g., cold chains, desalination, aquaculture) to be economically viable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Practical Net Power Output of an OTEC Plant

A real plant has **parasitic losses** that must be subtracted. Let:
- $\eta_{\text{actual}} = 0.04$ (40% of Carnot efficiency is achievable)
- $P_{\text{turbine}} = $ power generated at the turbine shaft
- $P_{\text{cwp}} = $ pumping power for the cold water pipe
- $P_{\text{wwp}} = $ pumping power for warm water
- $P_{\text{wfp}} = $ working fluid pump power
- $P_{\text{aux}} = $ auxiliary loads (lighting, control, desalination, etc.)

**Step 1 — Turbine Power:**

$$P_{\text{turbine}} = \eta_{\text{actual}} \cdot \dot{m}_w \cdot c_p \cdot (T_H - T_C)$$

**Step 2 — Total Parasitic Power:**

$$P_{\text{parasitic}} = P_{\text{cwp}} + P_{\text{wwp}} + P_{\text{wfp}} + P_{\text{aux}}$$

**Step 3 — Net Power Delivered to the Grid:**

$$P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{turbine}} - P_{\text{parasitic}}$$

**Step 4 — Net Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{net}} = \frac{P_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The cold water pipe alone may consume **30–40%** of gross OTEC output in sub-1 MW demonstration plants. As plant capacity scales up to 100 MW+ commercial designs, $P_{\text{cwp}}$ drops to **10–15%** of gross, dramatically improving $\eta_{\text{net}}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Nernst Equation for Fuel Cell EMF

For the overall cell reaction:

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O(l)}$$

The Gibbs free energy change at standard conditions (25°C, 1 atm) is:

$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -237.2 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

The standard theoretical EMF (open-circuit voltage) is given by:

$$E^{\circ} = -\frac{\Delta G^{\circ}}{nF}$$

Where:
- $n = 2$ electrons transferred per H₂ molecule
- $F = 96485$ C/mol (Faraday's constant)

Substituting:

$$E^{\circ} = -\frac{-237200 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E^{\circ} = \frac{237200}{192970}$$

$$E^{\circ} = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

This is the **theoretical maximum open-circuit voltage of an H₂-O₂ fuel cell** — a key value examiners expect.

### Accounting for Real Gas Pressures — Nernst Equation

$$\Delta G = \Delta G^{\circ} + RT \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2O}}{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}\right)$$

Dividing by $-nF$:

$$E = E^{\circ} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln\left(\frac{P_{H_2} \cdot P_{O_2}^{1/2}}{P_{H_2O}}\right)$$

This generalized form is the **Nernst Equation** for the H₂-O₂ fuel cell.

> [!NOTE]
> At standard conditions ($P_{H_2} = P_{O_2} = P_{H_2O} = 1$ atm), the logarithmic term vanishes and $E = E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Energy Density Comparison — Hydrogen vs Conventional Fuels

The **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** of hydrogen is the enthalpy of combustion when water vapor is condensed:

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O(l)}, \quad \Delta H = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 1 — Convert to mass basis:**

$$E_{\text{HHV}} = \frac{285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}}{2.016 \text{ g/mol}} = 141.86 \text{ MJ/kg} \approx 142 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Comparison Table (Mass & Volume):**

| Fuel | HHV (MJ/kg) | Density (kg/m³) | Energy Density (MJ/m³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydrogen (gas, 200 bar)** | **142** | 17.7 | **2511** |
| Hydrogen (LH₂) | 142 | 71 | 10082 |
| Methane (CH₄) | 55.5 | 0.72 | 40 |
| Gasoline | 46 | 740 | 34040 |
| Diesel | 45.5 | 850 | 38675 |
| Li-ion battery | 0.5–1 | — | ~2500 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Hydrogen has the **highest mass-energy density** of any fuel (3× gasoline), but **lowest volume-energy density** under normal conditions. This is why **hydrogen storage and transport** is the biggest engineering challenge.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance of Renewable Technologies

| Parameter | OTEC | Geothermal | Solar PV | Wind |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacity Factor** | 80–90% | 85–95% | 15–25% | 25–40% |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | 3–6% | 8–20% | N/A (direct conv.) | N/A (kinetic) |
| **Land Footprint** | Very small (ocean) | Very small | Large | Large |
| **Maturity (TRL)** | 5–7 | 8–9 | 9 | 9 |
| **Site Restriction** | Tropical oceans only | Tectonic active zones | Sun-rich regions | Windy regions |
| **CO₂ Avoided (per MWh)** | ~700 kg | ~450 kg | ~600 kg | ~500 kg |
| **LCOE (USD/MWh)** | 100–250 | 50–100 | 30–60 | 30–80 |
| **Energy Storage Required?** | No (baseload) | No (baseload) | Yes (intermittent) | Yes (intermittent) |

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Geothermal Resource Temperature vs Power Plant Type

The **maximum theoretical efficiency** of a binary cycle geothermal plant is given by:

$$\eta_{\text{binary,max}} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{rejection}}}{T_{\text{resource}}}$$

For a binary plant with resource temperature $T_{\text{res}} = 180^{\circ}\text{C} = 453.15$ K and ambient rejection $T_{\text{rej}} = 25^{\circ}\text{C} = 298.15$ K:

$$\eta_{\text{binary,max}} = 1 - \frac{298.15}{453.15}$$

$$\eta_{\text{binary,max}} = 1 - 0.6580$$

$$\eta_{\text{binary,max}} = 0.3420 = 34.20\%$$

**Practical efficiency** (with second-law losses, turbine losses, and working-fluid constraints):

$$\eta_{\text{practical}} \approx 0.25 \times \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \approx 8.5\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: "Why are dry steam and flash steam plants used at high-temperature sites, while binary cycle plants are used at low-temperature sites?" The answer lies in the trade-off between **resource enthalpy** and **cycle thermal efficiency**.
