# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: OTEC — Definition & Minimum $\Delta T$
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q:** Define Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC). Why is a minimum temperature difference of 20°C required for an OTEC plant to operate?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) is a renewable energy technology that converts the thermal energy stored in the temperature difference between the warm surface seawater of tropical oceans (~$25$–$30^{\circ}\text{C}$) and the cold deep seawater (~$4$–$7^{\circ}\text{C}$) into electrical energy using a heat engine.

> **Reason for $\Delta T \geq 20^{\circ}\text{C}$ (1.5 marks):** The Carnot efficiency of an OTEC plant is given by $\eta = 1 - T_C / T_H$. If $\Delta T < 20^{\circ}\text{C}$, the gross work produced is too small to overcome the parasitic loads (cold water pumping, working fluid circulation, auxiliaries), resulting in **negative net power output**. Hence, a 20°C gradient is the **thermodynamic and economic threshold** for commercial viability.

---

### Question 2: Fuel Cell — Mobile Ions
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q:** List any three types of fuel cells and write the mobile ion responsible for charge transfer in each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Fuel Cell Type | Mobile Ion |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **PEMFC** (Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell) | $\text{H}^+$ (Proton) |
| 2 | **SOFC** (Solid Oxide Fuel Cell) | $\text{O}^{2-}$ (Oxide ion) |
| 3 | **MCFC** (Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell) | $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ (Carbonate ion) |

> In each case, the ion migrates through the electrolyte to complete the internal circuit, while electrons flow through the external circuit to perform useful work.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): Full Question on OTEC and Geothermal**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model Paper]**

**Q (a)** Explain the **principle of working** of a **Closed Cycle OTEC plant** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its **advantages and limitations** compared to the Open Cycle OTEC. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Working Principle (3 marks):**
A Closed Cycle OTEC plant operates on the **Rankine cycle** using a low-boiling-point working fluid (typically **anhydrous ammonia**, $\text{NH}_3$, which boils at $\approx -33^{\circ}\text{C}$ at 1 atm). The process is:
- **Warm surface seawater** ($T_H \approx 28^{\circ}\text{C}$) flows through the **evaporator**, transferring heat to the working fluid, which vaporizes at high pressure.
- The **high-pressure ammonia vapor** expands through a **turbine** coupled to a **generator**, producing electricity.
- The expanded, low-pressure vapor enters the **condenser**, where it is cooled and condensed by **cold deep seawater** ($T_C \approx 5^{\circ}\text{C}$).
- The **liquid ammonia** is pressurized by the **working fluid pump** and re-circulated to the evaporator.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**

The cycle can be represented as:
$$\text{Evaporator} \rightarrow \text{Turbine} \rightarrow \text{Condenser} \rightarrow \text{Pump} \rightarrow \text{Evaporator}$$
with warm seawater supplying heat at the evaporator and cold seawater extracting heat at the condenser.

**Step 3 — Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | CC-OTEC | OC-OTEC |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Ammonia/R-134a | Seawater |
| Cycle | Rankine | Claude (flash) |
| Output | Electricity only | Electricity + fresh water |
| Vacuum needed | No | Yes (0.035 atm) |
| Toxicity risk | Yes (NH₃) | No |
| Maturity | Higher | Lower |

**Advantages of CC-OTEC:** Compact heat exchangers, no large vacuum systems, suitable for floating plants, more commercially demonstrated.
**Limitations of CC-OTEC:** Toxic ammonia, no fresh water by-product, working fluid cost.

---

**Q (b)** What is **Geothermal Energy**? Explain the **different types of geothermal power plants** with their working temperature ranges. Briefly discuss the **environmental advantages** of geothermal energy. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1.5 marks):**
Geothermal energy is the heat energy contained within the Earth's interior, originating from **primordial heat, radioactive decay (U-238, Th-232, K-40)**, and **exothermic mantle reactions**. The Earth's average geothermal gradient is **$25$–$30^{\circ}\text{C/km}$** of depth.

**Step 2 — Types of Geothermal Power Plants (4 marks):**

| Type | Resource | Temperature | Working Fluid | Site Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry Steam** | Natural steam reservoir | $> 200^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Direct steam | The Geysers, California; Larderello, Italy |
| **Flash Steam** | Hot water $150$–$300^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $150$–$300^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Flashed steam (1, 2, or 3 stage) | Wairakei, NZ; Cerro Pabellón, Chile |
| **Binary Cycle** | Hot water $90$–$180^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $90$–$180^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Organic fluid (isobutane/isopentane) | Chena Hot Springs, Alaska; Soultz, France |
| **EGS (Enhanced Geothermal System)** | Hot dry rock at $4$–$6$ km depth | $> 200^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Injected water | Cooper Basin, Australia |

**Step 3 — Environmental Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- **Zero carbon emissions** during operation (~45 g CO₂/kWh vs 1000 g for coal).
- **Small land footprint** (1–8 acres per MW).
- **Baseload capability** — provides 24/7 power unlike solar/wind.
- **No fuel cost**, low operating cost, minimal water consumption (binary cycle recycles water).
- **No combustion, no particulates, no SO₂/NOₓ emissions.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice on Fuel Cells & Hydrogen**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**Q (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**. Write the **half-cell and overall cell reactions**. State **any two advantages** and **any two limitations** of PEMFC. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Principle (2 marks):**
A PEMFC consists of a **proton-conducting polymer membrane (Nafion)** sandwiched between two platinum-catalyzed electrodes (anode and cathode). Pure hydrogen is supplied to the anode, and oxygen (or air) to the cathode.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (1 mark):**

$$\text{H}_2 \text{ in} \rightarrow \text{Anode} \rightarrow \text{PEM} \rightarrow \text{Cathode} \rightarrow \text{O}_2 \text{ in}$$

External circuit: Anode → Load → Cathode (electron flow).

**Step 3 — Reactions (2 marks):**

**Anode (Oxidation — Hydrogen splits into protons and electrons):**

$$\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$$

**Cathode (Reduction — Protons, electrons, and oxygen combine to form water):**

$$\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**

$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy} + \text{Heat}$$

The theoretical open-circuit voltage is $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V.

**Step 4 — Advantages (1 mark):**
- High power density, suitable for **transportation** (cars, buses).
- **Low operating temperature** ($60$–$100^{\circ}\text{C}$) → fast start-up.
- Modular, scalable, zero local emissions (only water).

**Step 5 — Limitations (1 mark):**
- **Expensive platinum catalyst.**
- Highly sensitive to **CO poisoning** (CO levels > 10 ppm degrade performance).
- **Membrane dehydration** at high temperatures limits operation to ~100°C.
- Requires **pure hydrogen** — costly storage and distribution.

---

**Q (b)** What is **Hydrogen Energy**? Explain in detail the **different methods of hydrogen production**, classifying them according to the color-coded convention (Green, Blue, Grey, Pink hydrogen). Discuss **two major challenges** in hydrogen storage and transport. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Hydrogen is a **zero-carbon energy carrier** (NOT a primary energy source) that can be used to store, transport, and deliver energy. It has the **highest mass-energy density (142 MJ/kg)** of any fuel, about 3× gasoline. The element is the most abundant in the universe (~75% by mass).

**Step 2 — Color-Coded Classification of Hydrogen (4 marks):**

| Color | Production Method | Reaction / Process | Carbon Emissions |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Grey** | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{H}_2 + \text{CO}_2$ | High (~10 kg CO₂/kg H₂) |
| **Blue** | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS) | Same as grey, with CO₂ captured | Low (10–20% of grey) |
| **Green** | Electrolysis powered by renewables | $2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2$ | **Zero** |
| **Pink** | Electrolysis powered by nuclear | Same as green, with nuclear electricity | Near-zero |
| **Turquoise** | Methane Pyrolysis | $\text{CH}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2 + \text{C (solid)}$ | Low (solid carbon) |
| **White** | Natural geological deposits | Extracted from underground reserves | Variable |

**Step 3 — Hydrogen Storage & Transport Challenges (2 marks):**

**Challenge 1 — Low Volumetric Energy Density:** At ambient conditions, 1 kg of H₂ occupies ~11 m³. Solutions include **compressed gas (350–700 bar)**, **cryogenic liquid ($-253^{\circ}\text{C}$)**, and **metal hydrides** ($\text{MgH}_2$, $\text{LaNi}_5$).

**Challenge 2 — Hydrogen Embrittlement:** H₂ molecules diffuse into metals, causing **embrittlement, cracking, and leakage** in pipelines and storage tanks. Specialised coatings, composite tanks, and alloy selection are required, raising costs.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (Optional 0 marks):**
Hydrogen is a **pillar of the future clean energy economy** — its value lies in decarbonizing hard-to-abate sectors (steel, cement, heavy transport) and providing long-duration energy storage.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this Module:**
> 1. **Confusing "OTEC efficiency" and "Carnot efficiency"** — OTEC actual efficiency is **3–6%**, NOT the Carnot value (~7.65%). Always state **Carnot (theoretical max)** vs **actual (practical)**.
> 2. **Writing only the Carnot equation without specifying temperatures in Kelvin** — convert °C to K first or lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Calling hydrogen a "primary energy source"** — it is an **energy carrier**. Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong mobile ion in fuel cells** — PEMFC = $\text{H}^+$, SOFC = $\text{O}^{2-}$, MCFC = $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$. Mixing these is a common error.
> 5. **Forgetting half-cell reactions** in fuel cell questions — always write anode (oxidation) and cathode (reduction) separately.
> 6. **OTEC environmental impacts** — students often miss the **deep-water discharge's impact on marine ecology** and the **biogenic CO₂ release** from deep water. These are favourite KTU examiner topics.
> 7. **Geothermal plant types** — students mix up flash steam and binary cycle; remember: **Flash** uses **water/steam**; **Binary** uses **organic fluid**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 4 Quick-Recall Summary:**

### **OTEC Essentials**
- ⭐ OTEC = **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion**; uses $\Delta T \geq 20^{\circ}\text{C}$ between surface and 1000 m deep water.
- ⭐ Carnot efficiency: $\eta = 1 - T_C / T_H$ (temperatures in **Kelvin**).
- ⭐ **CC-OTEC**: Ammonia working fluid, Rankine cycle, electricity only, **Mini-OTEC 1979 (Hawaii)**, **Saga 1982 (Japan)**.
- ⭐ **OC-OTEC (Claude)**: Seawater as fluid, vacuum flash, **electricity + freshwater**, **SCEEP 1993 (Hawaii)**.
- ⭐ **Hybrid OTEC**: Combines CC + OC; **dual output** (electricity + fresh water + cooling).
- ⭐ **Major OTEC environmental impacts**: deep-water discharge affecting marine life, CO₂ release from deep water, biofouling, possible seismic activity from large-scale cold water pipe.
- ⭐ **Notable OTEC sites**: Hawaii, Japan, Tahiti, India (Lakshadweep via Sagar Sampada), Indonesia, Cuba.
- ⭐ **Cold Water Pipe (CWP)** is the critical engineering component — made of HDPE, can be 1000+ m long, 10+ m diameter.

### **Geothermal Energy Essentials**
- ⭐ Geothermal gradient: **$25$–$30^{\circ}\text{C/km}$** in crust; higher near tectonic boundaries.
- ⭐ **Dry steam** (T > 200°C), **Flash steam** (150–300°C), **Binary cycle** (90–180°C), **EGS** (HDR at 4–6 km).
- ⭐ Binary cycle uses **organic Rankine cycle (ORC)** with isobutane/isopentane.
- ⭐ **The Geysers, California** is the world's largest geothermal complex (>1.5 GW).
- ⭐ Iceland generates **~30% of electricity** from geothermal.
- ⭐ **Baseload** renewable — capacity factor 85–95%.
- ⭐ **Re-injection wells** are mandatory to maintain reservoir pressure and ensure sustainability.

### **Green Energy Essentials**
- ⭐ Green Energy = renewable + low/zero carbon emissions.
- ⭐ KTU examples: solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, OTEC, tidal, wave, fuel cells, hydrogen.
- ⭐ Distinguished from "Clean Energy" (which may include nuclear) by its **sustainability and renewability**.

### **Fuel Cells Essentials**
- ⭐ Fuel cell = **electrochemical device**, not a heat engine — **not bound by Carnot limit**.
- ⭐ Theoretical efficiency: **80–90%**, practical: **40–60%**.
- ⭐ **AFC** = alkaline (KOH), mobile ion $\text{OH}^-$, used in **Apollo spacecraft**.
- ⭐ **PEMFC** = polymer membrane (Nafion), $\text{H}^+$ mobile, used in **Toyota Mirai**, $60$–$100^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- ⭐ **SOFC** = solid ceramic electrolyte (YSZ), $\text{O}^{2-}$ mobile, $700$–$1000^{\circ}\text{C}$, used in **stationary power**.
- ⭐ **MCFC** = molten carbonate (Li₂CO₃/K₂CO₃), $\text{CO}_3^{2-}$ mobile, $600$–$700^{\circ}\text{C}$, used in **large-scale CHP**.
- ⭐ **PAFC** = phosphoric acid (H₃PO₄), $\text{H}^+$ mobile, $150$–$220^{\circ}\text{C}$, used in **commercial CHP**.
- ⭐ **DMFC** = direct methanol, $\text{H}^+$ mobile, used in **portable electronics**.
- ⭐ Overall reaction: $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electricity} + \text{Heat}$.
- ⭐ Theoretical OCV: $E^{\circ} = 1.229$ V; practical operating voltage: $0.6$–$0.8$ V per cell.

### **Hydrogen Energy Essentials**
- ⭐ Hydrogen is an **energy carrier**, not a primary energy source.
- ⭐ **HHV = 142 MJ/kg**, **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** — highest gravimetric energy density.
- ⭐ **Green H₂** = electrolysis with renewables = **zero carbon**.
- ⭐ **Grey H₂** = SMR = **high carbon**.
- ⭐ **Blue H₂** = SMR + CCS = **low carbon**.
- ⭐ Storage methods: **CGH₂, LH₂, metal hydrides, LOHC, underground caverns**.
- ⭐ Major challenge: **hydrogen embrittlement** of metals and **low volumetric energy density**.
- ⭐ Applications: **fuel cell vehicles, industrial heating (steel, ammonia), power generation, energy export**.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEET613 - RENEWABLE ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operating Principles, Zero-Energy Concepts. Benefits of Hydrogen Energy, Hydrogen Production Technologies (Electrolysis Method Only), Hydrogen Energy Storage, Applications of Hydrogen Energy, Problems Associated with Hydrogen Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hydrogen Energy — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Energy** is a form of chemical energy stored in the bonds of the diatomic hydrogen molecule (H₂). Because hydrogen does not exist freely in nature in usable quantities, it is classified as an **energy carrier** (or *energy vector*) rather than a primary energy source. Energy must first be spent to *produce* it (e.g., via electrolysis of water), it can then be *stored* and *transported*, and finally *converted* back into useful work (electricity via fuel cells, or heat via combustion).

In KTU 2024 terminology, hydrogen sits at the heart of the **Hydrogen Economy** — an end-to-end value chain encompassing production → storage → transport → end-use, intended to replace fossil fuels in sectors that are hard to electrify directly (steel, heavy transport, shipping, aviation).

## 1.2 Zero-Energy Concept — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Zero-Energy (or Zero-Carbon / Net-Zero) Concept:** A building, system, or process is called *zero-energy* when the total amount of energy it consumes annually is matched (or offset) by an equal or greater amount of **renewable energy** it generates on-site or procures from clean sources.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Self-Sustaining Wallet"
Think of your monthly expenses versus your monthly income. A *Zero-Energy Home* is like a household that earns (via rooftop solar, small wind, micro-hydro, or solar-driven hydrogen production) **exactly as much money as it spends** over a year. The "wallet" never runs into debt against the grid. Hydrogen complements this idea by acting as the *savings account* — when solar produces excess during the day, that excess electricity is used in an electrolyzer to make H₂, which is stored and then reconverted to electricity in a fuel cell at night, balancing the daily mismatch between generation and demand.

### Standard Metrics You Must Know

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂** = **141.9 MJ/kg** (≈ **39.4 kWh/kg**)
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂** = **119.9 MJ/kg** (≈ **33.3 kWh/kg**)
- **Energy density by mass of H₂** ≈ **3× gasoline** (gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg)
- **Energy density by volume** = **8.5 MJ/L** (cryogenic liquid H₂ at –253 °C)
- **Faraday's constant** = **F = 96,485 C/mol**
- **Universal Gas Constant** = **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is hydrogen's mass-energy density so high, but volume-energy density so low?**
> Because H₂ is the *lightest and smallest* molecule on Earth. You get enormous energy per kilogram, but at ambient conditions it is an extremely dilute gas (≈ 0.0108 MJ/L) — which is precisely *why* storage engineering is hard.

---

## 1.3 Operating Principle of Hydrogen as an Energy Carrier

The principle is a **closed chemical loop**:

1. **Input energy** (renewable electricity) splits water → H₂ + O₂ (Electrolysis).
2. H₂ is **compressed, liquefied, or chemically bound** for storage/transport.
3. At point of use, H₂ is **recombined with oxygen**:
   - In a **Fuel Cell** → electricity + water + heat (electrochemical, silent).
   - In a **Combustion Engine/Turbine** → mechanical work + water + NOₓ (thermal, noisier).
4. The only by-product from a fuel cell is **pure water** — hence "zero emission at point of use".

The full cycle is often called the **"Hydrogen Economy Loop"** or **Power-to-Gas-to-Power (P2G2P)** when coupled with renewables.

---

## 1.4 Benefits of Hydrogen Energy

| # | Benefit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Highest mass-energy density** of any fuel (~120–142 MJ/kg) | Ideal for aviation, rockets, heavy vehicles where weight matters |
| 2 | **Zero CO₂ emission** at point of use (in fuel cells) | Decarbonizes transport, industry |
| 3 | **Storable for long durations** (months–years) without losses | Solves the *intermittency* problem of solar/wind |
| 4 | **Sector-coupling fuel** — bridges electricity, heat, mobility, chemicals | Replaces natural gas, diesel, coal in many sectors |
| 5 | **Producible from any primary energy** (renewable, nuclear, fossil + CCS) | Energy-independence / flexibility |
| 6 | **Abundant raw material** — water is the universal feedstock | No geopolitical fuel-supply risk |
| 7 | **Scalable** from kW (home) to GW (grid) | Suitable for distributed and centralized systems |
| 8 | **Can be converted back to electricity** at efficiencies of 40–60% (PEMFC) | Useful for stationary power, backup |

---

## 1.5 Hydrogen Production Technologies — Classification

Hydrogen is colour-coded by its production pathway:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Grey Hydrogen** — from natural gas (Steam Methane Reforming, SMR); emits CO₂.
> - **Blue Hydrogen** — Grey H₂ + **Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS)**.
> - **Green Hydrogen** — from **renewable electricity + electrolysis of water**; the *only truly zero-carbon* route at source. This is the route mandated in KTU Module 4.

Per the KTU syllabus scope, **only the Electrolysis Method** is treated in depth here.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrolysis — Operating Principle

**Electrolysis of water** is an electrochemical process in which a DC voltage is applied across two electrodes immersed in a water-based electrolyte, splitting water into hydrogen (at the cathode) and oxygen (at the anode).

### 2.1.1 Overall Reaction (Universal)

$$2\,H_2O_{(l)} \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,H_{2\,(g)} \;+\; O_{2\,(g)}$$

Enthalpy change: **ΔH° = +285.8 kJ/mol** (endothermic, requires energy input)
Gibbs free-energy change: **ΔG° = +237.2 kJ/mol** (at 25 °C, 1 atm)

### 2.1.2 Half-Cell Reactions

**(a) Alkaline Electrolyzer** (KOH or NaOH electrolyte)

$$\text{Cathode (reduction):}\quad 2H_2O + 2e^- \;\longrightarrow\; H_2 + 2OH^-$$

$$\text{Anode (oxidation):}\quad 2OH^- \;\longrightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^-$$

**(b) PEM (Proton Exchange Membrane) Electrolyzer**

$$\text{Cathode:}\quad 2H^+ + 2e^- \;\longrightarrow\; H_2$$

$$\text{Anode:}\quad H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^-$$

**(c) Solid Oxide Electrolyzer (SOEC)** — high-temperature (700–850 °C)

$$\text{Cathode:}\quad H_2O + 2e^- \;\longrightarrow\; H_2 + O^{2-}$$

$$\text{Anode:}\quad O^{2-} \;\longrightarrow\; \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2e^-$$

---

## 2.2 Thermodynamic Voltages — Theoretical Minimums

| Quantity | Definition | Formula | Value at 25 °C |
|----------|------------|---------|----------------|
| Reversible (minimum) voltage | Minimum voltage below which no reaction occurs | $E_{rev}=\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ | **1.229 V** |
| Thermoneutral voltage | Voltage at which the cell neither absorbs nor releases heat | $E_{th}=\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{nF}$ | **1.481 V** |
| Actual cell voltage | Always > 1.481 V due to irreversibilities | $V_{cell}=E_{rev}+\eta_{act}+\eta_{ohm}+\eta_{conc}$ | 1.8–2.5 V (typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **n = 2** electrons per molecule of H₂ produced. **F = 96,485 C/mol**.

### 2.2.1 Why two voltages? — The Hidden Logic

- **ΔG** is the *useful electrical work* obtainable. So **E_rev = 1.229 V** is the *thermodynamic floor* — apply less and water simply will not split.
- **ΔH** is the *total heat + electrical* energy required. The extra **(ΔH − ΔG) = T·ΔS ≈ 48.6 kJ/mol** is supplied as **heat** at high temperature (this is why SOECs are so efficient — they harvest free thermal energy).
- In practice, **kinetic, ohmic, and concentration overpotentials (η)** push V_cell to **1.8–2.5 V**, reducing overall efficiency.

---

## 2.3 Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis — The Heart of the Topic

### First Law (1833)
> The mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the charge passed through the electrolyte.

### Second Law (1834)
> The mass of substance liberated is proportional to its equivalent weight (M/n).

### Combined Mathematical Form (KTU Must-Know)

$$m \;=\; \frac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F} \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

where:
- $m$ = mass of substance deposited/liberated (grams)
- $Q = I \cdot t$ = total charge passed (Coulombs)
- $I$ = current (Amperes)
- $t$ = time (seconds)
- $M$ = molar mass of substance (g/mol)
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per molecule
- $F$ = **96,485 C/mol** (Faraday's constant)

### Specialization for Hydrogen (KTU High-Yield Shortcut)

For H₂: $M = 2.016$ g/mol, $n = 2$

$$m_{H_2} \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t \cdot 2.016}{2 \times 96485} \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t}{95733} \;\text{(grams, approx.)}$$

Equivalent form (volumetric, at STP):

$$V_{H_2} \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t \cdot V_m}{n \cdot F} \;=\; \frac{I \cdot t \times 22{,}414}{2 \times 96485} \;\text{L}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim — every KTU board question on electrolysis derives from these equations.**

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Mass of H₂ produced | $m = \dfrac{I\,t\,M}{nF}$ | kg or g | Faraday's 1st + 2nd Law |
| 2 | Theoretical H₂ volume (STP) | $V_{H_2}=\dfrac{I\,t\,V_m}{nF}$ | m³ or L | $V_m=22.414$ L/mol at STP |
| 3 | Reversible voltage | $E_{rev}=\dfrac{\Delta G^\circ}{nF}$ | V | 1.229 V at 25 °C |
| 4 | Thermoneutral voltage | $E_{th}=\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{nF}$ | V | 1.481 V at 25 °C |
| 5 | Cell efficiency | $\eta_{cell}=\dfrac{E_{rev}}{V_{cell}}$ | % | < 100% always |
| 6 | Faraday (current) efficiency | $\eta_F=\dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}}$ | % | Accounts for parasitic losses |
| 7 | Overall electrolyzer efficiency | $\eta_{sys}=\dfrac{HHV_{H_2}}{V_{cell}\times I \times t / m_{H_2}}$ | % | Energy-in vs energy-out |
| 8 | Specific energy consumption | $E_s=\dfrac{nF\,V_{cell}}{M}$ | kWh/kg H₂ | ≈ 47–55 kWh/kg in practice |
| 9 | Energy stored in H₂ | $E_{H_2}=m\times HHV$ | MJ or kWh | HHV = 141.9 MJ/kg |
| 10 | Compressor work (isothermal ideal) | $W_{comp}=nRT\,\ln(P_2/P_1)$ | J | Used for storage compression |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Electrolyzer Equation

The specific energy consumption formula
$$E_s \;=\; \frac{nF\,V_{cell}}{M}$$
is the **single most-asked relation in KTU papers**. It tells the engineer: "for every kilogram of H₂ I want, how many kWh of electricity must I buy?" With V_cell = 2.0 V (typical), $E_s = (2 \times 96485 \times 2.0)/0.002016 \approx$ **53.6 kWh/kg H₂**. This single number drives project economics — electrolyzer CAPEX, renewable electricity tariffs, and H₂ selling price are all chained to it.

---

## 2.6 Hydrogen Energy Storage

Hydrogen can be stored physically or chemically. The KTU syllabus expects the student to differentiate these.

### (a) Compressed Gas (most mature)
- Stored at **350 bar (Type III) or 700 bar (Type IV)** in composite tanks.
- Energy density ≈ **4.7 MJ/L at 700 bar**.

### (b) Liquid Hydrogen (Cryogenic)
- Cooled to **–253 °C**; density = **70.8 kg/m³**.
- Boil-off losses ≈ **0.1–1% per day**.

### (c) Underground Storage
- Salt caverns, depleted gas fields, aquifers.
- Used for **TWh-scale seasonal storage**.

### (d) Metal Hydrides & Chemical Carriers
- H₂ absorbed in metals (e.g., LaNi₅H₆, MgH₂) or converted to **ammonia (NH₃)**, **liquid organic hydrogen carriers (LOHC)**, or **methanol (CH₃OH)**.
- Safer, higher volumetric density than compressed gas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, just remember the **four classes** above and one numeric example for each: 700 bar, –253 °C, salt caverns, LOHC.

---

## 2.7 Applications of Hydrogen Energy

1. **Transportation** — Fuel-cell electric vehicles (FCEVs): cars (Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo), buses, trucks, trains (Coradia iLint), ships.
2. **Stationary Power Generation** — Backup fuel cells (1 kW – 10 MW) for hospitals, data centres.
3. **Grid-Scale Energy Storage** — Power-to-Gas-to-Power balancing variable renewables.
4. **Industrial Feedstock** — Ammonia (NH₃) for fertilizers; methanol; steel making via **direct reduced iron (DRI)** with green H₂.
5. **Heating** — H₂ blending (≤ 20%) or 100 % in natural-gas networks; H₂ boilers & H₂-ready heat pumps.
6. **Aerospace** — Rocket propellant (LH₂ + LOX) — NASA, ISRO, SpaceX Starship upper stages.
7. **Portable Power** — PEMFCs for laptops, defence, remote sensors.

---

## 2.8 Problems Associated with Hydrogen Energy

| # | Problem | Engineering Root Cause |
|---|---------|-----------------------|
| 1 | **Low volumetric energy density** at ambient conditions | Lightest molecule → need compression/liquefaction |
| 2 | **Hydrogen Embrittlement** | H atoms diffuse into metal lattices, making steel pipelines brittle |
| 3 | **High production cost** of Green H₂ | $E_s$ ≈ 50 kWh/kg × renewable tariff ≈ $3–6/kg |
| 4 | **Storage & transport losses** | Boil-off, compression energy, leakage (H₂ is the smallest molecule) |
| 5 | **Lack of refuelling infrastructure** | Building pipelines and 700-bar stations is capital intensive |
| 6 | **Safety — flammability range 4–75 % in air** | Odourless, colourless, highly flammable; leak detection is hard |
| 7 | **Low round-trip efficiency** (P2G2P ≈ 30–45 %) | Electrolysis + storage + fuel-cell losses at each step |
| 8 | **Water consumption** | ~9 L of purified water per kg of H₂ |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **To the student:** KTU typically asks two derivations in this module: (i) Faraday's mass equation from first principles, and (ii) the cell-voltage / efficiency derivation. Both are reproduced below in full, with *every* algebraic step exposed.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Faraday's Combined Law of Electrolysis

### Statement (First Law)
> *"The mass $m$ of a substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge $Q$ that passes through the electrolyte."*

### Statement (Second Law)
> *"The mass $m$ of a substance liberated by a given charge is proportional to the chemical equivalent weight (M/n) of the substance."*

### Proof / Derivation

**Step 1 — Express the amount of substance in moles.**
If $Q$ coulombs of charge pass through the electrolyte, and $F$ coulombs liberate 1 mole of a *univalent* substance, then for a substance needing $n$ electrons per atom:

$$\text{Number of moles of substance} \;=\; \frac{Q}{n\,F}$$

**Step 2 — Convert moles to mass.**
Mass = moles × molar mass:

$$m \;=\; \frac{Q}{n\,F} \times M \;=\; \frac{Q\,M}{n\,F}$$

**Step 3 — Replace $Q$ by $I \cdot t$ (since $Q = I\,t$ for constant current):**

$$\boxed{\,m \;=\; \frac{I\,t\,M}{n\,F}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Verification for hydrogen.**
$M_{H_2}=2.016$ g/mol, $n=2$. For 1 A·hr = 3600 C:

$$m_{H_2} \;=\; \frac{1 \times 3600 \times 2.016}{2 \times 96485} \;=\; 0.0376 \;\text{g}$$

Sanity check: To produce 1 kg H₂ you need ≈ **26,600 A·hr** — correct, this matches industry data.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Energy Consumption & Overall Efficiency of an Electrolyzer

**Step 1 — Energy input to the electrolyzer (electrical).**
For current $I$ flowing for time $t$ at cell voltage $V_{cell}$:

$$E_{in} \;=\; V_{cell}\,I\,t \quad [\text{Joules}]$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical minimum energy (from Gibbs free energy).**
At 25 °C, $\Delta G^\circ = 237.2$ kJ per mole of H₂.

$$E_{min} \;=\; \Delta G^\circ \times \text{moles of H}_2 \;=\; \frac{\Delta G^\circ \cdot I\,t}{n\,F}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical voltage (reversible).**
Divide minimum energy by total charge $Q = I\,t$:

$$E_{rev} \;=\; \frac{E_{min}}{I\,t} \;=\; \frac{\Delta G^\circ}{n\,F} \;=\; \frac{237{,}200}{2 \times 96{,}485} \;=\; 1.229 \;\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Voltage efficiency.**

$$\eta_{V} \;=\; \frac{E_{rev}}{V_{cell}} \;=\; \frac{1.229}{V_{cell}}$$

**Step 5 — Faraday (current) efficiency.**
Some current is "wasted" on parasitic reactions (e.g., corroding electrodes).

$$\eta_{F} \;=\; \frac{I_{useful}}{I_{total}} \;=\; \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}}$$

**Step 6 — Overall electrolyzer energy efficiency (using HHV):**

$$\eta_{sys} \;=\; \frac{m_{H_2} \times HHV_{H_2}}{V_{cell}\,I\,t}$$

In percent:

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{sys}\,(\%) \;=\; \frac{m_{H_2} \times HHV_{H_2}}{V_{cell}\,I\,t} \times 100\,}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the mass relation $m = I t M/(nF)$ to obtain specific energy:**

$$E_s \;=\; \frac{V_{cell}\,I\,t}{m_{H_2}} \;=\; \frac{V_{cell}\,I\,t}{I\,t\,M/(nF)} \;=\; \frac{V_{cell}\,nF}{M}$$

In kWh/kg:

$$\boxed{\,E_s \;=\; \frac{nF\,V_{cell}}{M \times 3600} \;\text{kWh/kg H}_2\,}$$

Plug in $n=2$, $F=96485$, $M=0.002016$ kg/mol, $V_{cell}=2.0$ V:

$$E_s \;=\; \frac{2 \times 96485 \times 2.0}{0.002016 \times 3600} \;\approx\; 53.2 \;\text{kWh/kg H}_2$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 pattern):** An alkaline electrolyzer operates at 2.1 V and 500 A for 8 hours. Calculate: (a) mass of H₂ produced, (b) volume of H₂ at STP, (c) specific energy consumption, (d) overall efficiency using HHV. (Assume Faraday efficiency = 95 %).

### Given Data
- $V_{cell} = 2.1$ V, $I = 500$ A, $t = 8 \times 3600 = 28{,}800$ s
- $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- $HHV = 141.9$ MJ/kg, $\eta_F = 0.95$

### (a) Theoretical Mass of H₂

$$m_{th} \;=\; \frac{I\,t\,M}{nF} \;=\; \frac{500 \times 28{,}800 \times 2.016}{2 \times 96{,}485} \;\text{g}$$

$$m_{th} \;=\; \frac{29{,}030{,}400}{192{,}970} \;\approx\; 150.46 \;\text{g}$$

Actual mass:

$$m_{act} \;=\; \eta_F \times m_{th} \;=\; 0.95 \times 150.46 \;\approx\; 142.94 \;\text{g} \;\approx\; 0.143 \;\text{kg}$$

### (b) Volume at STP

$$V_{H_2} \;=\; \frac{m_{act}}{M} \times 22{,}414 \;\text{L/mol} \;=\; \frac{142.94}{2.016} \times 22{,}414 \;\approx\; 1{,}589{,}000 \;\text{L} \;\approx\; 1589 \;\text{m}^3$$

(Sanity-check alternative via Faraday equation:
$V = I t V_m/(nF) = (500 \times 28800 \times 22.414)/(2 \times 96485) \times 0.95 \approx 1589$ m³ — matches.)

### (c) Specific Energy Consumption

$$E_s \;=\; \frac{V_{cell}\,nF}{M \times 3600} \;=\; \frac{2.1 \times 2 \times 96485}{0.002016 \times 3600} \;\approx\; 55.85 \;\text{kWh/kg}$$

### (d) Overall Efficiency

Electrical energy input:

$$E_{in} \;=\; V_{cell}\,I\,t \;=\; 2.1 \times 500 \times 28800 \;=\; 30{,}240{,}000 \;\text{J} \;=\; 8.4 \;\text{kWh}$$

Energy stored in H₂ (using HHV):

$$E_{H_2} \;=\; m_{act} \times HHV \;=\; 0.143 \;\text{kg} \times 141.9 \;\text{MJ/kg} \;=\; 20.29 \;\text{MJ} \;\approx\; 5.636 \;\text{kWh}$$

$$\eta_{sys} \;=\; \frac{5.636}{8.4} \times 100 \;\approx\; 67.1\,\%$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Electrolyzer Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix appears almost verbatim in KTU Board Part B questions.**

| Parameter | Alkaline (ALK) | PEM | Solid Oxide (SOEC) |
|-----------|----------------|-----|--------------------|
| Electrolyte | KOH / NaOH liquid | Polymer membrane | YSZ / GDC ceramic |
| Operating Temperature | 60–90 °C | 50–80 °C | 700–850 °C |
| Cell Efficiency (HHV) | 63–70 % | 65–80 % | 75–85 % |
| Current Density | 0.2–0.6 A/cm² | 1–2 A/cm² | 0.3–1 A/cm² |
| Dynamic Operation | Slow, hard to ramp | **Fast — ideal for renewables** | Slow |
| Cost (2024) | $500–1000/kW | $1000–1800/kW | $2000+/kW (early stage) |
| Stack Life | 60,000–90,000 h | 20,000–40,000 h | < 20,000 h |
| Maturity | **Most mature (industrial)** | Commercial | R&D / Pilot |
| Best Application | Large-scale steady | **Coupled with variable RE** | Industrial waste-heat integration |

---

## 3.5 Zero-Energy System Equation (Conceptual Derivation)

A Zero-Energy system satisfies, over an annual cycle:

$$E_{gen} \;-\; E_{cons} \;\geq\; 0$$

For a hydrogen-buffered zero-energy building:

$$E_{gen} \;=\; E_{PV} \;+\; E_{wind} \;+\; \left(\frac{m_{H_2} \cdot HHV}{\eta_{FC}}\right)$$

$$E_{cons} \;=\; E_{load,\,annual}$$

Solving for required H₂ buffer mass:

$$m_{H_2}^{\,req} \;=\; \eta_{FC} \cdot \frac{\left(E_{cons} - E_{PV} - E_{wind}\right)}{HHV}$$

where $\eta_{FC}$ ≈ 50 % (PEM fuel cell electrical efficiency). This is a **must-practice derivation** for Part B.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hydrogen Economy — End-to-End Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RES[Renewable Sources<br/>Solar PV, Wind, Hydro]
    ELECPower[DC Electricity<br/>via Rectifier]
    ELECT[Electrolyzer<br/>Electrolysis of H2O]
    COMP[Compression<br/>or Liquefaction]
    STORE[Hydrogen Storage<br/>Tanks, Caverns, LOHC]
    TRANS[Transport<br/>Pipeline, Truck, Ship]
    END[End-Use Conversion<br/>Fuel Cell or Engine]
    OUT[Outputs<br/>Electricity, Heat, Water]

    RES --> ELECPower --> ELECT
    ELECT -- H2 gas --> COMP
    COMP --> STORE --> TRANS --> END
    END --> OUT
    ELECT -- O2 byproduct --> OUT

    classDef source fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#A0E0FF,stroke:#00688B,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#B5E8B5,stroke:#2E8B57,color:#000

    class RES,ELECPower source
    class ELECT,COMP,STORE,TRANS,END process
    class OUT output
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Yellow blocks = primary energy source / electricity conditioning.
- Blue blocks = core hydrogen value chain (production → storage → transport → use).
- Green block = outputs (electricity + heat + pure water; emissions only at the electrolyzer when fed by renewables are zero).

---

## 4.2 Electrolyzer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DCSource[DC Power Supply<br/>Renewable-derived]
    StackCell[Electrolyzer Stack<br/>Multiple Cells in Series]
    Cathode[Cathode Chamber<br/>H2 Evolution]
    Anode[Anode Chamber<br/>O2 Evolution]
    MemSeparator[Membrane / Diaphragm Separator]
    H2Out[H2 Outlet<br/>to Purifier and Dryer]
    O2Out[O2 Outlet<br/>Vent or Capture]
    H2OPump[Water Feed Pump<br/>De-ionized H2O In]
    Purifier[Purifier<br/>and Compressor]
    H2Storage[H2 Storage Tank]

    DCSource --> StackCell
    StackCell --> Cathode
    StackCell --> Anode
    StackCell --> MemSeparator
    H2OPump --> StackCell
    Cathode --> H2Out
    Anode --> O2Out
    H2Out --> Purifier
    Purifier --> H2Storage
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **DC source** must be *low-ripple*; renewable inputs go through a rectifier + filter.
- **Water feed** is high-purity de-ionized (resistivity > 1 MΩ·cm) to prevent fouling.
- **H₂ and O₂ streams must be strictly separated** — otherwise the mixture becomes explosive (the *Lower Explosive Limit* is just **4 % H₂ in air**).
- After the stack, a **purifier (PSA or catalytic de-oxo)** removes residual O₂ and moisture before the compressor.

---

## 4.3 Hydrogen Storage Methods — Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2Store[Hydrogen Storage Methods]
    H2Store --> Phys[Physical Storage]
    H2Store --> Chem[Chemical Storage]

    Phys --> CG[Compressed Gas<br/>350 or 700 bar]
    Phys --> LH2[Cryogenic Liquid<br/>minus 253 degC]
    Phys --> UGS[Underground<br/>Salt Caverns]

    Chem --> MH[Metal Hydrides<br/>LaNi5, MgH2]
    Chem --> LOHC[LOHC<br/>Dibenzyltoluene]
    Chem --> NH3[Ammonia NH3<br/>Easier to Liquefy]
    Chem --> MeOH[Methanol CH3OH<br/>Liquid at Room Temp]

    classDef root fill:#FFE7B0,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000
    classDef phys fill:#D4F0FF,stroke:#00688B,color:#000
    classDef chem fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000

    class H2Store root
    class Phys,CG,LH2,UGS phys
    class Chem,MH,LOHC,NH3,MeOH chem
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Physical storage** keeps H₂ as a molecule — needs only pressure or temperature change.
- **Chemical storage** *bonds* H₂ to a carrier — safer and denser, but requires a "release reactor" to recover H₂ at the point of use.

---

## 4.4 Fuel Cell — Operating Principle (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2In[H2 Inlet<br/>from Tank]
    O2In[Air / O2 Inlet]
    AnodeFC[Anode<br/>H2 minus 2e minus yields 2H plus]
    CathodeFC[Cathode<br/>2H plus plus 2e minus plus 0.5 O2 yields H2O]
    ElectrolyteFC[Electrolyte<br/>PEM or OH minus]
    ELOut[External Circuit<br/>Loads and Inverter]
    ByProd[By Products<br/>Water and Heat]

    H2In --> AnodeFC
    O2In --> CathodeFC
    AnodeFC <--> ElectrolyteFC
    CathodeFC <--> ElectrolyteFC
    AnodeFC -- electrons --> ELOut
    ELOut -- electrons --> CathodeFC
    CathodeFC --> ByProd
    AnodeFC --> ByProd
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- This is the **reverse of electrolysis** — H₂ is *consumed* to release electricity.
- The **electrolyte** (PEM, alkaline, or solid oxide) determines the operating temperature and charge carrier (H⁺ vs OH⁻ vs O²⁻).
- **Water + heat** are the only by-products when pure H₂ is used.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Part A answers must be precise, between 80–120 words, and may include one labeled equation/diagram. Avoid essay-style writing — *brevity + clarity* = full marks.

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]`
**Explain the "Zero-Energy Concept" with reference to a hydrogen-buffered renewable system.**

**Model Answer:**
A zero-energy system is one in which the net annual energy consumed equals the net annual energy generated from on-site renewable sources. A hydrogen-buffered zero-energy building achieves this by routing excess solar/wind electricity through an **electrolyzer** to produce H₂, storing it (compressed/liquid), and reconverting via a **fuel cell** when generation falls short. Mathematically,

$$E_{PV} + E_{wind} + \eta_{FC} \cdot m_{H_2}\cdot HHV \;\geq\; E_{load}$$

Hydrogen's role is to provide **long-duration seasonal storage** that batteries cannot economically offer, closing the annual energy balance.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three benefits and three problems of hydrogen energy.**

**Model Answer:**

*Benefits:*
1. Highest **mass-energy density** (~141.9 MJ/kg HHV) — ~3× gasoline.
2. **Zero CO₂ emission** at point of use (fuel-cell pathway).
3. Long-duration, **seasonal storage** capability — solves solar/wind intermittency.

*Problems:*
1. **Low volumetric energy density** at ambient conditions → expensive compression/liquefaction.
2. **Hydrogen embrittlement** of steel pipelines and storage vessels.
3. **High cost** of green hydrogen (USD 3–6/kg) due to electrolyzer CAPEX + renewable electricity tariff.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Format Rule:** Each Part B carries 14 marks, broken into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (e.g., 7 + 7). Marks distribution within each sub-part is typically: *diagram 2 + explanation 3 + numerical 2*.

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **electrolysis method of hydrogen production**. Discuss the half-cell reactions in alkaline and PEM electrolyzers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An alkaline electrolyzer operates at **2.2 V** and draws **800 A** for **10 hours**. Given $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol, $n = 2$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, and Faraday efficiency = 96 %, compute:
  (i) Mass of H₂ produced.
  (ii) Volume of H₂ at STP.
  (iii) Overall electrolyzer efficiency using HHV = 141.9 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### (a) Electrolysis Method — Theory + Block Diagram [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):** Electrolysis is an electrochemical process in which DC electrical energy is used to split water into hydrogen and oxygen. Water is fed into an electrolyzer cell comprising two electrodes (cathode and anode) separated by an electrolyte (alkaline liquid, polymer membrane, or ceramic).

**Half-cell reactions (3 marks):**

*Alkaline electrolyzer:*
$$\text{Cathode:}\; 2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 + 2OH^-$$
$$\text{Anode:}\; 2OH^- \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2O + 2e^-$$

*PEM electrolyzer:*
$$\text{Cathode:}\; 2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2$$
$$\text{Anode:}\; H_2O \rightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^-$$

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw a labelled flowchart — Renewable DC source → Power conditioning → Electrolyzer stack (with cathode/anode/membrane compartments) → H₂ purification & drying → Compressor → Storage. (See Section 4.2 diagram of these notes for reference.)

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Given:** $V_{cell}=2.2$ V, $I=800$ A, $t = 10 \times 3600 = 36{,}000$ s, $M=2.016$ g/mol, $n=2$, $F=96{,}485$ C/mol, $\eta_F = 0.96$.

**(i) Mass of H₂ produced (3 marks)**

Theoretical mass:

$$m_{th}=\frac{I\,t\,M}{nF}=\frac{800 \times 36{,}000 \times 2.016}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$

$$m_{th}=\frac{58{,}060{,}800}{192{,}970} \approx 300.88 \;\text{g}$$

Actual mass:

$$m_{act}=\eta_F \cdot m_{th}=0.96 \times 300.88 \approx 288.85 \;\text{g}\;\approx\;0.289\;\text{kg}$$

**(ii) Volume of H₂ at STP (2 marks)**

$$V_{H_2}=\frac{m_{act}}{M}\times 22{,}414 = \frac{288.85}{2.016}\times 22{,}414 \approx 3{,}211{,}400\;\text{L}$$

$$\boxed{V_{H_2}\approx 3211\;\text{m}^3}$$

**(iii) Overall efficiency (2 marks)**

Electrical input energy:

$$E_{in}=V_{cell}\,I\,t=2.2 \times 800 \times 36{,}000 = 63{,}360{,}000\;\text{J} = 63.36\;\text{MJ}$$

Energy stored in H₂:

$$E_{H_2}=m_{act}\times HHV=0.28885\;\text{kg}\times 141.9\;\text{MJ/kg}\approx 41.0\;\text{MJ}$$

$$\eta_{sys}=\frac{41.0}{63.36}\times 100 \approx 64.7\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{sys}\approx 64.7\,\%}$$

---

### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]` (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Discuss the **different methods of hydrogen storage** with their relative merits and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A zero-energy residential complex requires a hydrogen buffer of **50 kg**. The fuel cell operates at 55 % electrical efficiency. Compute:
  (i) Total electrical energy that can be recovered from the buffer (use HHV = 141.9 MJ/kg).
  (ii) If 30 % of this recovered energy is consumed by electrolyzer + storage losses, find the **net usable electrical energy**.
  (iii) How many litres of water are consumed in producing this 50 kg H₂ (assuming 9 L per kg H₂)? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### (a) Hydrogen Storage Methods [7 marks]

**1. Compressed Gas (2 marks):** Most mature; H₂ stored at **350–700 bar** in composite cylinders. Simple, fast refuelling. **Limitation:** low volumetric density (~4.7 MJ/L at 700 bar) and high compression energy.

**2. Liquid Hydrogen (2 marks):** Cooled to **–253 °C**; density 70.8 kg/m³. **Limitation:** ~30 % liquefaction energy penalty; daily boil-off losses of 0.1–1 %.

**3. Underground Storage (1.5 marks):** Salt caverns / depleted reservoirs — only method suitable for **TWh-scale, seasonal** storage. Geographically constrained.

**4. Chemical Storage — Metal Hydrides / LOHC / NH₃ (1.5 marks):** H₂ chemically bonded to a carrier → high volumetric density, safer transport. **Limitation:** heavy carriers, thermal energy needed for H₂ release, slow kinetics.

---

#### (b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]

**Given:** $m_{H_2}=50$ kg, $\eta_{FC}=0.55$, HHV = 141.9 MJ/kg, water consumption = 9 L/kg.

**(i) Total recoverable electrical energy (3 marks)**

Chemical energy stored:

$$E_{chem}=50 \times 141.9 = 7095 \;\text{MJ}$$

Electrical output from fuel cell:

$$E_{elec}=\eta_{FC}\times E_{chem}=0.55 \times 7095 \approx 3902\;\text{MJ}$$

In kWh:

$$E_{elec}=\frac{3902}{3.6}\approx 1083.9\;\text{kWh}$$

**(ii) Net usable electrical energy after 30 % loss (2 marks)**

$$E_{net}=E_{elec}\times (1 - 0.30) = 3902 \times 0.70 = 2731.4\;\text{MJ}$$

$$\approx 758.7\;\text{kWh}$$

**(iii) Water consumed (2 marks)**

$$V_{water}=50 \times 9 = 450\;\text{L}$$

$$\boxed{V_{water}=450\;\text{litres}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing HHV with LHV.** KTU uses HHV by default (141.9 MJ/kg). If a question gives "LHV = 119.9 MJ/kg", use it — *do not silently substitute HHV*.
> 2. **Forgetting Faraday efficiency** in numerical problems. If $\eta_F$ is given (e.g., 0.95), the actual mass is $\eta_F \times m_{th}$. Skipping this costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing units** — Coulombs vs A·hr, MJ vs kWh. Always state conversions explicitly. KTU examiners *deduct* for sloppy units.
> 4. **Reversible vs Thermoneutral voltage** — at 25 °C, $E_{rev}=1.229$ V but the electrolyzer *must* run at $\geq 1.481$ V (thermo-neutral) for adiabatic operation. Memorize both.
> 5. **Half-cell reactions** — many students write the *overall* reaction for half-cells, losing 2 marks. Memorize cathode (reduction, –) and anode (oxidation, +) separately for both alkaline and PEM.
> 6. **Neglecting $\eta_{FC}$** in zero-energy derivations — students often equate HHV energy directly to electrical energy, ignoring the 40–60 % fuel-cell conversion loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Checklist — Pin this on your wall.**

- [x] **Hydrogen = energy carrier, NOT a primary source.** Always produced from another energy form.
- [x] **HHV = 141.9 MJ/kg**, **LHV = 119.9 MJ/kg**. Use HHV unless told otherwise.
- [x] **Faraday's combined law:** $m = \dfrac{I\,t\,M}{nF}$ — non-negotiable equation.
- [x] For H₂: $n = 2$, $M = 2.016$ g/mol.
- [x] **Reversible voltage** = 1.229 V (from $\Delta G^\circ = 237.2$ kJ/mol).
- [x] **Thermoneutral voltage** = 1.481 V (from $\Delta H^\circ = 285.8$ kJ/mol).
- [x] Practical V_cell = 1.8–2.5 V → efficiency 60–80 %.
- [x] **Specific energy consumption** = $\dfrac{nF\,V_{cell}}{M}$ ≈ **50–55 kWh/kg** for green H₂.
- [x] **Four storage methods:** compressed gas, cryogenic liquid, underground, chemical carriers (MH / LOHC / NH₃ / MeOH).
- [x] **Fuel cell** = reverse of electrolysis → electricity + water + heat.
- [x] **Zero-energy concept:** annual $E_{gen} \geq E_{cons}$; H₂ buffer enables seasonal balancing.
- [x] **Key benefits:** zero CO₂ at use, high mass-energy density, long-duration storage, sector coupling.
- [x] **Key problems:** low volumetric density, embrittlement, cost, leakage, flammability (4–75 % in air), water use.
- [x] **Applications:** FCEVs, grid storage, ammonia/methanol feedstock, green steel, rockets, heating.
- [x] **Electrolyzer types:** Alkaline (mature, cheap), PEM (fast, RE-coupled), SOEC (high efficiency, R&D).
- [x] Remember the **round-trip P2G2P efficiency** ≈ 30–45 % — examiners love asking this!
